The patient, a female with no significant past medical history, was admitted for intravenous heparin therapy due to a carotid dissection identified on an MRI/MRA. She reported a history of bronchitis with violent coughing and fatigue, as well as a left frontal headache a month prior to admission. Following the resolution of these symptoms, she developed pulsatile tinnitus in her right ear for three days, which then shifted to her left ear. This symptom persisted and was more noticeable when she was lying in bed at night, corresponding to her heartbeat. She had no recent neck pain, trauma, heavy lifting, chiropractic manipulations, hair appointments, etc. She frequently lifts her young children but reported no recent head or neck strain. She also reported no headache, visual changes, speech changes, dysphagia, facial droop, weakness, sensory changes, incoordination, bowel or bladder changes, or recent fevers, chills, shortness of breath, chest pain, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation.

Her past medical history includes myomectomies for fibroids, a cesarean section, a history of ectopic pregnancy, Asherman's syndrome, and a history of supraventricular tachycardia during pregnancy. Her family history includes her mother, who died with dementia and aortic sclerosis, and her father, who died of T cell leukemia and had hypertension.

Upon physical examination, she was awake, cooperative, and showed no signs of distress. Her vital signs were stable and within normal limits. Her neurological examination was normal, with no evidence of apraxia or neglect. Her cranial nerves were intact, and her motor and sensory functions were normal. Her deep tendon reflexes were normal, and her coordination was intact. Her gait was not tested.

Her admission labs showed an abnormal MCHC value of 35.5 and an abnormal RBC count of 15 in her urine. An MRI/MRA of her head revealed an abnormal appearance of the proximal intracranial portion of the left internal carotid artery, representing a focal dissection of this vessel. There was no occlusion, definite pseudoaneurysm, or evidence of embolic infarction in its vascular distribution. A CT angiogram of her head/neck confirmed the dissection and also revealed a small mucous retention cyst in the left maxillary sinus and a 4mm left thyroid lobe nodule.

During her hospital stay, she was initiated on IV heparin therapy and received her first dose of coumadin on her second hospital day. A CT angiogram was obtained to better visualize the extent of her carotid dissection and evaluate for the possibility of a pseudoaneurysm. The patient was informed of the small left thyroid lobe nodule and it was recommended that she should have an ultrasound of her thyroid done over the next weeks. She was discharged in a stable condition with instructions to follow up with her primary care physician within one week and to return to her PCP's office in two days for a follow-up INR check and titration of warfarin dose. She was also advised to return in three weeks for an outpatient follow-up CTA of head/neck to ensure that the aneurysm does not grow in size. Her medication upon admission was a daily multivitamin.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a left-handed woman who has a recent history of left internal carotid artery dissection and is currently on coumadin. She presented with a 15-second episode of dizziness. The onset of her symptoms began with a headache following a violent coughing episode. She subsequently developed pulsatile tinnitus and was discovered to have a dissection of the petrous portion of the left internal carotid artery. Initial treatment with heparin was transitioned to coumadin. Since her discharge, she has been doing well with only mild headaches responsive to tylenol and a decrease in the intensity of the pulsatile tinnitus. 

On the day of evaluation, she experienced a sudden onset of profound dizziness and a sensation of her head being in a vacuum. She denies vertigo and likens the dizziness sensation to lightheadedness. She recalls her heart pounding prior to symptom onset. The symptom was associated with anxiety and a sense of impending doom. It spontaneously resolved within 15 seconds. She denies chest discomfort prior to symptom onset. She has had decreased fluid intake and denies new medications other than the anticoagulants. 

Her past medical history includes dissection of petrous portion L ICA (attributed to coughing spells), myomectomies for fibroids, c section, ectopic pregnancy, Asherman's, and SVT during pregnancy. Her family history includes her mother who died with dementia and aortic disease, and her father who died of T cell leukemia and had hypertension. 

Upon admission, her physical exam was normal. Her neurologic examination was also normal. Her lab results showed an abnormal MCHC of 35.2 and 3 RBCs in her urine. Her CTA Head and Neck, CXR, and MRA and MRI brain results were normal except for the unchanged appearance of the left carotid dissection in the distal cervical region. 

During her hospital stay, she had several brief episodes of tachycardia seen on telemetry with HR in the 120's. No other cardiac arrhythmia was seen on either telemetry or EKG. The etiology of her dizziness is not clear. It was recommended that she undergo outpatient evaluation by a cardiologist. The dizziness was possibly anxiety-related. Her INR was subtherapeutic so she was restarted on a lovenox bridge. 

Upon admission, she was on coumadin and multivitamin. Her discharge diagnosis was dizziness. Her mental status was clear and coherent, she was alert and interactive, and she was ambulatory - independent with no neurologic deficits.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted for surgery following a right tibia fracture sustained while ice skating. This was an isolated injury and the patient denied any head strike or loss of consciousness, as well as any numbness or paresthesias. Her past medical history includes dissection of the petrous portion of the left internal carotid artery (attributed to coughing spells), myomectomies for fibroids, a cesarean section, an ectopic pregnancy, Asherman's syndrome, and supraventricular tachycardia during pregnancy. She has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. Her family history is non-contributory.

Upon physical examination, the patient was in no acute distress and had unlabored breathing. Her abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended. The incision was clean, dry, and intact with no erythema or discharge, and minimal ecchymosis. She was in an aircast boot on her right lower extremity. Her right lower extremity dorsalis pedis pulse was 2+ with distal digits warm and well perfused.

An X-ray of the tibia/fibula showed steps related to the fixation of a spiral fracture of the distal tibia, with placement of a tibial intra medullary rod with associated proximal and distal interlocking screws. Additional screws transfix a longitudinal fracture through the posterior aspect of the distal tibia, which extends to the tibiotalar articular surface. A fibular neck fracture was also noted.

During her hospital stay, the patient underwent surgery for a right tibia intramedullary nail, which she tolerated well. Post-surgery, she was initially given IV fluids and IV pain medications, and progressed to a regular diet and oral medications by post-operative day 1. She was given antibiotics and anticoagulation per routine. Her home medications, Simvastatin 20mg daily and ASA 81mg daily, were continued throughout her hospitalization. The operative splint was taken down on post-operative day 1 and she was transitioned to an aircast boot. Her hospital course was otherwise unremarkable.

At the time of discharge, the patient's pain was well controlled with oral medications, incisions were clean/dry/intact, and she was voiding/moving bowels spontaneously. She was non-weight bearing in the right lower extremity and was discharged on Lovenox for DVT prophylaxis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. Her activity status was independent ambulation. Her discharge diagnosis was a right tibia fracture.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to progressive left hip pain. After conservative management failed, he underwent a left total hip arthroplasty. His past medical history includes paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, dyslipidemia, likely obstructive sleep apnea, and osteoarthritis. He has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Aspirin, Doxazosin, Fluticasone Propionate nasal spray, Hydrocodone-Acetaminophen for pain, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Finasteride, and Flecainide Acetate. 

The physical examination revealed that the patient was in no acute distress, with stable vital signs and well-controlled pain. The incision from the surgery was healing well with staples and scant serosanguinous drainage. The thigh was full but soft, with no calf tenderness. Neurologically, the patient was intact with no focal deficits. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (4.10, 3.81, 4.20), Hgb (13.4, 12.4, 13.4), Hct (39.1, 36.0, 39.6), MCH (32.6, 32.4), Glucose (109), and UreaN (32). 

Postoperative course was remarkable for sciatic pain, which was not affected by cyclobenzaprine but showed good effect with diazepam, lidoderm patch, and gabapentin. Pain was initially controlled with a PCA and then transitioned to oral pain medications. The patient received lovenox for DVT prophylaxis. The surgical dressing was changed and the incision was found to be clean and intact. The patient was seen daily by physical therapy and labs were checked throughout the hospital course. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with left hip osteoarthritis. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory but required assistance or aid. The patient was discharged to home with services in stable condition.
label: controls

summary:
The male patient, with a past medical history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, dyslipidemia, likely obstructive sleep apnea, and osteoarthritis, was admitted due to recurrent episodes of dizziness and lightheadedness that had acutely worsened over the last 72 hours. The patient reported that these episodes had been increasing in frequency over the last month, occurring without any discernible triggers and lasting for minutes at a time. He denied syncope, chest pain, edema, and orthopnea throughout this entire period. He had been taking all his medications as prescribed, which included aspirin, doxazosin, finasteride, flecainide acetate, and metoprolol succinate. 

Upon admission, the patient's heart rate was notably low at 41 bpm. Initial treatment in the emergency department included 1 mg IV glucagon, Zofran, and 500 ml NS IVF, which improved his heart rate to the 50s. The patient was then admitted for further monitoring. 

The patient's family history includes his mother having a myocardial infarction and hypertension, and his father having a stroke. There were no early unexplained deaths or sudden cardiac death in the family. 

Physical examination upon admission and discharge showed mild bradycardia with no murmurs, gallops, or rubs. The patient was well developed, well nourished, and oriented. No significant skin lesions or rashes were noted. 

Abnormal lab results upon admission included glucose at 105, urea nitrogen at 30, mean corpuscular hemoglobin at 34.8, and platelet count at 144. Monocytes and eosinophils were also elevated at 14.4 and 8.6 respectively. 

Imaging studies showed increased infrahilar opacity in the right lung, concerning for pneumonia in the right clinical setting. There was also moderate elongation of the descending aorta and moderate retrocardiac atelectasis. 

During the hospital stay, the patient underwent placement of a dual chamber permanent pacemaker for sick sinus syndrome, which resolved his symptoms. Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with symptomatic bradycardia, sick sinus syndrome, and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the neurosurgery department with a chief complaint of headache. He was transferred from an outside hospital where a CT scan revealed a subarachnoid hemorrhage. The patient reported experiencing an electric shock sensation traveling up his spine to his head while at work, which was followed by a progressively severe headache, nausea, and vomiting. He was evaluated by his primary care physician who ordered a head CT at the ER, which confirmed the subarachnoid hemorrhage. The patient was then transferred for neurosurgical evaluation. 

The patient's past medical history includes cardiac stenting and coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG). His family history is significant for multiple cerebrovascular accidents (CVAs) in his sister, father, and mother. There is no family history of polycystic kidney disease or Ehlers Danlos syndrome. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was awake, alert, and cooperative. His vital signs were stable and his neurological examination was normal. An angiogram was performed as a major surgical or invasive procedure during his hospital stay. 

The patient's lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.73), HGB (12.4), HCT (34.8), MCH (33.2), MCHC (35.6), NEUTS (75.2), and LYMPHS (17.8). A head CT and CTA revealed hyperdensity anterior to the brainstem, a small degree of hydrocephalus, and no obvious aneurysm or arteriovenous malformation (AVM). 

During his hospital stay, the patient continued to experience headaches but remained neurologically stable. He developed a low-grade fever and blood cultures were sent, which were still pending at the time of discharge. An MRI of the C/T spine was negative for AVM but showed spinal stenosis. 

The patient's medications upon admission included lopressor, lipitor, ASA, lisinopril, fish oil, and a multivitamin. 

Upon discharge, the patient was neurologically stable. His discharge instructions included taking his pain medication as prescribed, limiting exercise to walking, increasing fluid and fiber intake, and avoiding anti-inflammatory medications unless directed by his doctor. He was also advised to contact his surgeon immediately if he experienced new onset of tremors or seizures, any confusion or change in mental status, any numbness, tingling, or weakness in his extremities, or pain or headache that was continually increasing or not relieved by pain medication.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a left-handed female, was admitted due to intermittent headaches and right neck and shoulder pain, similar to her previous episode of vertebral artery dissection. She has a past medical history of atraumatic right vertebral artery dissection. The patient reported that her symptoms began four days prior to admission, with no history of trauma or unusual activities. She initially managed her discomfort with NSAIDs (ibuprofen) but her pain progressed, leading to her decision to seek medical attention. She also experienced a transient episode of right eye blurring while working on the computer, which resolved spontaneously. The patient denied experiencing diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, lightheadedness, vertigo, tinnitus, hearing difficulty, difficulties producing or comprehending speech, focal weakness, numbness, parasthesiae, bowel or bladder incontinence or retention, and difficulty with gait. She also denied recent fever, chills, cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, tightness, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, abdominal pain, change in bowel or bladder habits, dysuria, arthralgias, myalgias, and rash.

The patient's family history is notable for a maternal grandfather with multiple strokes at an old age, but no known history of arterial dissection or connective tissue disease. She is allergic to codeine. Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be in mild discomfort, with tenderness along the right paraspinals, trapezi, and scapula, and very limited head turn to the right. Neurological examination was largely normal.

Lab results showed an abnormal RBC count of 3.64 and an abnormal Eosinophil count of 0.9. MRI and MRA of the brain and neck, as well as CT and CTA of the head and neck, showed no evidence of hemorrhage, edema, masses, infarction, stenosis, occlusion, or aneurysm formation. The patient's hospital course was uneventful, with no new dissection found on MRA. Her neck and shoulder pain was determined to be likely muscular in origin and was managed with valium and tizanidine. The patient was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and able to ambulate independently. Her discharge diagnosis included a history of atraumatic vertebral dissection and musculoskeletal neck and shoulder pain. The patient was advised to follow a heart-healthy diet, engage in daily exercise, and practice stress reduction techniques. She was instructed to follow up with her primary care physician in the next few weeks.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a white male, was admitted due to experiencing pain between his shoulder blades during exertion over the past several months. The pain was occasionally severe. A stress test was negative, but a CT scan revealed a thoracic aortic aneurysm. The patient is hypertensive and a smoker. He was started on Losartan. A connective tissue disorder was considered but ruled out. 

His past medical history includes hypercholesterolemia, thoracic aortic aneurysm, hypertension, and erectile dysfunction. His family history is significant for his grandfather who died from a ruptured aneurysm. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Losartan 50 mg daily, Toprol XL 50 mg daily, Cialis as needed, and Ambien 10 mg as needed at bedtime. 

The patient underwent a valve-sparing aortic root replacement procedure. Post-procedure, he was transferred to the CVICU in stable condition on Phenylephrine and Propofol drips. Cefazolin was used for surgical antibiotic prophylaxis. 

During his hospital stay, the patient developed a fever. C-difficile toxin was negative on two occasions and he reported frequent diarrhea episodes related to diet. This resolved with one Imodium tablet. Urinalysis was negative. White blood cell count remained normal and sternal incision showed no signs of infection. Fevers resolved. 

Abnormal lab results include RBC-2.90*, Hgb-9.4*, Hct-26.5*, MCH-32.5*, MCHC-35.6*, RBC-2.86*, Hgb-8.8*, Hct-25.4*, and K-3.2*, Glucose-110*, Na-132*, Cl-93*. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with aortic root aneurysm, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, and erectile dysfunction. He was alert and oriented, ambulating with a steady gait, and his sternal pain was managed with Dilaudid as needed.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female of unspecified age, was admitted due to pleuritic pain. She has a history of intermittent chest tightness for the past year, which was found to be due to a moderate-sized right pneumothorax. The patient has had five episodes of chest discomfort, usually right-sided, accompanied by a feeling of tightness/muscle spasm, with pain upon inspiration occasionally associated with wheezing and dyspnea. The discomfort is typically on the front of the right chest, though it radiates to the back at times. She has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions.

Her past medical history includes left shoulder pain, irritable bowel syndrome, dysmenorrhea, female infertility, elevated prolactin level, visual changes, bilateral retinal lattice degeneration, and vitreous syneresis. Her family history includes her father having a stroke and her mother having a thyroid disorder.

Upon admission, she was not taking any preadmission medications. During her hospital stay, she underwent a right pleural chest tube placement and VATS right upper lobe and right lower lobe superior segment wedge resections, diaphragmatic biopsy with primary repair, and mechanical and chemical 0.5 g doxycycline pleurodesis.

Her physical exam revealed marked joint hyper-mobility of the wrist and hyper-extendable elbows. She was thin but not marfanoid appearing. Her vitals were stable and her pain was controlled with IV Tylenol and Dilaudid. Her chest tube drain put out a modest amount of tan, bloody fluid and had no air leak. Her post pull chest xray showed no pneumothorax. Her room air saturations were 95%.

Lab results showed WBC-7.2, RBC-4.07, HGB-12.1, HCT-36.3, MCV-89, MCH-29.7, MCHC-33.3, RDW-13.1, RDWSD-42.6, PLT COUNT-334, PTT-29.3, GLUCOSE-88, UREA N-14, CREAT-0.8, SODIUM-139, POTASSIUM-5.3, CHLORIDE-105, TOTAL CO2-23, ANION GAP-11. A CT scan of the chest revealed a moderate right pneumothorax with moderate right middle lobe atelectasis, with no underlying mechanism identified. There was no CT evidence for tension pneumothorax.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of spontaneous right pneumothorax. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent. Her discharge medications included Acetaminophen, Cyclobenzaprine, Docusate Sodium, Milk of Magnesia, and OxyCODONE. She was instructed to continue using her incentive spirometer, check her incisions daily, and avoid ibuprofen for the next two weeks, among other instructions. She was scheduled for a follow-up appointment in the Thoracic Clinic in two weeks.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to shortness of breath. She has a history of left shoulder pain, irritable bowel syndrome, dysmenorrhea, female infertility, elevated prolactin level, visual changes, bilateral retinal lattice degeneration, and vitreous syneresis. Her family history includes her father having a stroke and her mother having a thyroid disorder. Upon admission, she was taking Cyclobenzaprine 10 mg PO TID as needed for spasms.

The patient had previously undergone VATS RUL and RLL wedge and mechanical and chemical pleurodesis for spontaneous pneumothorax, and biopsy of some pigmented diaphragmatic lesions. She presented with recurrent right basilar PTX. She experienced right-sided lower pleuritic type pain, worse on inspiration, and shortness of breath. Pathology from the VATS had shown fibroelastotic scarring and fibrin/hemorrhage suggestive of prior pneumothorax, with the diaphragmatic lesions consistent with hemosiderin deposition. There was some concern of pulmonary endometriosis given the temporal association with the patient's menses, however, this was not demonstrated on biopsy.

During her hospital stay, she underwent CT guided placement of right pleural pigtail catheter, intrapleural Doxycycline pleurodesis, and VATS pleurodesis, blebectomy, diaphragmatic repair. Post-procedure, her pain was managed with an epidural catheter and dPCA. Her diet was advanced to regular with no issues. Her PCA was discontinued on POD2 and her chest tubes were removed later that day. Repeat chest x-ray after chest tube removal revealed only a trace pneumothorax. Her epidural catheter was subsequently discontinued and she was transitioned to oral pain medications.

Abnormal lab results upon admission included MCHC at 31.6. Upon discharge, abnormal lab results included RBC at 3.71, Hgb at 11.0, MCHC at 31.9, Glucose at 105, and AnGap at 9. Radiographs showed a significant interval reduction in the right pneumothorax following the insertion of the right basal pigtail pleural drainage catheter. However, a repeat CXR revealed worsening of the pneumothorax which now had a fluid component.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of recurrent right pneumothorax. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a right-handed female who presented for further evaluation of recurrent nocturnal events. These episodes began a year ago, following a systemic, viral-like illness with low-grade fevers and malaise. The patient described waking from sleep with a sensation of muscle tension and 'discharging excess energy from her fingers.' She would then extend her back and extremities into the bed, with episodes typically lasting 3 seconds. She reported no auras, loss of consciousness, tongue biting, urinary incontinence, or postictal symptoms such as confusion. The longest episode-free interval was a 6-week period.

The patient's past medical history includes scoliosis, hyperlipidemia, mild aortic dilation, and sinus problems post-surgical repair of 'impaction'. She has undergone an MRI of her spine, which showed chronic spinal disease and scoliosis. An EEG, PSH, and brain MRI were reportedly normal. A sleep study was also conducted but was not diagnostic. The patient's family history includes a son with a seizure disorder, but no other neurological diseases were reported in the family.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be in no apparent distress, lying in bed comfortably. Her vital signs were stable and within normal limits. The neurological examination was largely unremarkable, with the patient being awake, alert, and oriented. Her motor and sensory functions were intact, and her gait was normal.

Lab results showed a slightly low RBC count at 4.04. All other lab results, including WBC, HGB, HCT, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, PLT COUNT, GLUCOSE, UREA N, CREAT, SODIUM, POTASSIUM, CHLORIDE, TOTAL CO2, ANION GAP, CALCIUM, PHOSPHATE, MAGNESIUM, ALT(SGPT), AST(SGOT), and ALK PHOS, were within normal limits.

The patient was admitted to the epilepsy service for EEG LTM. Her interictal EEG showed intermittent left greater than right temporal/parietal theta slowing, as well as frequent left temporal/parietal sharp transients, likely epileptiform in nature. During an event, she had multiple myoclonic jerks, primarily of the right shoulder/arm, sometimes involving the entire body, and at times associated with dystonic posturing of the arms. The EEG during this event showed an arousal pattern. Given the stereotyped nature of the events and the abnormal interictal EEG, it was suspected that these events are ictal in origin.

Upon admission, the patient was on Lipitor 80 mg QHS and Metoprolol 6.25 mg BID. The patient was diagnosed with nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent at the time of discharge.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy, severe anxiety, depression, scoliosis, hyperlipidemia, mild aortic dilation, sinus problems post surgical repair of 'impaction', episcleritis, and nephrolithiasis, was admitted to the neurology service due to increased anxiety, sleep difficulties, and nocturnal events. She has had previous psychiatric admissions due to her severe anxiety and depression, which are possibly related to her epilepsy. Her symptoms began several years ago and were characterized by recurrent nocturnal events involving arousal from sleep, a poorly characterized energy sensation, core tightening, and bilateral tonic extension of the arms and legs lasting several seconds, as well as daytime right shoulder myoclonic jerks. 

Her EEG demonstrated left temporal/parietal slowing and frequent interictal epileptiform discharges in the same region. An MRI showed some nonspecific subcortical T2/FLAIR hyperintensities, but no concerning lesions. There were a couple small lesions in the left frontal lobe, one near the motor strip, that could potentially explain some of her symptoms; however, no definite source was present. She has undergone trials of carbamazepine and clonazepam, with no clear improvement in her symptoms, and is now off both. 

She had worsening and severe anxiety that ultimately led to inpatient psychiatric admission where she was started on Gabapentin (now at 200mg BID) and Nortriptyline 25mg BID, which initially seemed to help get her anxiety under good control. However, her anxiety worsened again. In terms of seizure control, her event frequency had decreased significantly, but she developed a new event that has been occurring nightly: sudden awakening from sleep, followed by irresistible urge to move/rock her legs back and forth. This is associated with intense sensation of fear and dread. 

During her hospital stay, she had several episodes of waking from sleep usually in the morning hours, with feeling anxious and variably being impelled to move her feet. There were no EEG changes associated with these episodes, which may last for an hour or so. After discussion with her outpatient neurologist, Lamotrigine 12.5mg po BID was started, without any rashes noted. Gabapentin was continued, and may be tapered as outpatient. 

Her family history includes a son with developmental disabilities and seizure disorder, and a maternal cousin with nocturnal events beginning with leg twitches. Her medications upon admission included Gabapentin 200mg TID, Fluvoxamine 200mg daily, Nortriptyline 25mg BID (tapering down), and Atorvastatin 80mg daily. Her lab results showed abnormal RBC count of 3.95 and MCH of 32.1, and Eosinophils of 5.5.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with no significant past medical history, presented with persistent and worsening neck pain, which began upon waking up. He also reported a change in tongue sensation, specifically an inability to feel cold when drinking from a water cooler. He was unsure if this sensation was localized to one side of the tongue or involved the entire tongue, and whether this sensation persisted to the present day. He had a history of a remote gymnastic-related neck injury more than a few years ago, which did not result in any neurological deficits but did cause severe neck pain. He also reported a recent hiking incident where he slipped slightly, but denied any significant fall or whiplash event. 

The patient's past surgical history includes hydrocele and varicocele repair, wisdom teeth removal, tonsillectomy, and hernia repair as an infant. He has no known allergies except for iodinated contrast media - IV dye. His family history is positive for diabetes and hypertension, but no connective tissue disease or genetic diseases involving stretchy skin or hypermobile joints were reported.

Upon physical examination, the patient was awake, cooperative, and showed no signs of distress. His vital signs were stable and within normal limits. His neurological examination was largely unremarkable, with normal mental status, cranial nerve function, motor function, sensory function, deep tendon reflexes, coordination, and gait. 

Laboratory results showed a hemoglobin level of 12.2, PTT of 28.5, and INR of 1.1. Both serum and urine drug screens were negative, and urinalysis was negative for infection. Radiologic data from an MRI/MRA of the head and neck showed no acute intracranial abnormality or acute infarct, and a normal MRA of the brain. However, there was a short segment of high signal surrounding the V3 segment of the left vertebral artery, suggestive of a focal dissection.

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with a left vertebral artery dissection, likely due to trauma. No brainstem or posterior circulation ischemia was seen. His tongue symptoms resolved and he was placed on a heparin drip in the emergency department, which was later transitioned to aspirin 81mg monotherapy. He was discharged in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and able to ambulate independently. His discharge diagnosis was vertebral artery dissection.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female with a history of depression since her early years. She has been on several medications, none of which have been effective. She has been under the care of various psychiatrists over the years. A few months ago, she was recommended Electroconvulsive Therapy (ECT) due to her refractory depression. However, her parents were hesitant until she had more medication trials. The patient's depression has been more acute over the past month, and she has been unable to work. She reports increased sensitivity and irritability, especially with the onset of colder, darker weather. She has passive suicidal ideation but insists she would never harm herself due to her supportive family. She feels hopeless about her future, seeing others progressing in life while she feels stuck.

The patient's past medical history includes hypothyroidism and a workup for temporal lobe epilepsy during her childhood, which did not conclusively link her psychiatric issues to a neurological basis. She has also been tested for certain syndromes, all of which were negative. She has a history of substance use, including alcohol and tobacco, but denies drug use. She currently consumes one cup of coffee a day.

Her family history includes her paternal grandfather with severe depression and alcoholism, a paternal uncle who led an isolated life, and her mother and brother with ADHD. Her father has mild depression.

Upon physical examination, her temperature was 96.4, blood pressure 121/76, heart rate 125, and respiratory rate 17. Her mental status examination revealed a depressed mood, sad affect with occasional smiles, and linear thought form. She denied any abnormal perceptions or suicidal/homicidal ideation. Her insight and judgment were assessed as limited/fair.

The patient was admitted to the inpatient psychiatric unit. Her outpatient psychiatrist was contacted, and in line with his recommendation, she was started on a trial of Remeron after tapering and discontinuing Parnate. She received two ECT treatments during her stay and will continue these treatments as an outpatient after discharge. She reported an improvement in her mood and a decrease in her daily crying spells after starting ECT. She regularly attended group sessions and had no behavioral issues.

Upon admission, her medications included Lorazepam 1mg twice daily and Tranylcypromine (Parnate) 10mg twice daily. She was prescribed this medication three times daily but could not tolerate it. Her discharge diagnosis included Major Depression and hypothyroidism. Her condition at discharge was stable with an improvement in mood since admission.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female under the OBSTETRICS/GYNECOLOGY service, was admitted due to leakage of fluid. She had a history of hypothyroidism and was previously evaluated for temporal lobe epilepsy during her childhood, but no definitive conclusion was made that her psychiatric problems were neurologically based. She had been tested for certain syndromes in the past, with negative results. 

Upon admission, she was experiencing mild cramping but no painful contractions. She reported no fever, chills, dysuria, or abdominal trauma. She underwent a normal vaginal delivery of twins during her hospital stay. Her postpartum course was uneventful. 

Her family history includes a paternal grandfather with severe depression and alcoholism, a paternal uncle who led an isolated life, a mother and brother with ADHD, and a father with mild depression. 

On physical examination, her vital signs were stable. Her abdomen was soft and gravid, with no tenderness. Fetal heart tones for both twins were normal, with moderate variability and positive accelerations. The patient was found to have grossly ruptured membranes, with pooling of clear fluid and a positive nitrazine test. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC count (3.41), hemoglobin (11.0), hematocrit (31.9 and 29.0), and mean corpuscular hemoglobin (32.3). Her white blood cell count was within normal limits. 

Upon admission, she was on Levothyroxine Sodium 50 mcg PO DAILY and Venlafaxine XR 450 mg PO DAILY. She was discharged in stable condition with instructions to call or return if she experienced fever, excessive bleeding, pain, or depressive symptoms. She was also advised to avoid any vaginal activity for 4 weeks.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female of undisclosed age, was admitted to the Obstetrics/Gynecology service due to elevated blood pressures observed during a prenatal visit at 33 weeks and 6 days gestation. This is her second pregnancy, with a history of preterm premature rupture of membranes (PPROM) and preterm delivery (PTD) of twins at 34-35 weeks during her first pregnancy. She has a history of heavy menses, abnormal pap smear results (LGSIL, CIN1 on colposcopy), but has had normal results since. She denies any history of sexually transmitted infections, fibroids, cysts, or heavy bleeding. 

Her past medical history includes depression and anxiety, for which she is on multiple medications and has a history of electroconvulsive therapy (ECT). She also has temporal lobe epilepsy, managed with lamotrigine, and hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine. She has a history of elevated blood pressures, but no history of chronic hypertension. She has been evaluated for a connective tissue disorder, but the work-up was negative. 

Her family history includes depression, alcoholism, ADHD, hypertension in both parents, and a connective tissue disorder in her father and sister. She denies any family history of aneurysms, uterine or organ rupture. 

During her hospital stay, she reported significant fatigue, but denied loss of fluid, vaginal bleeding, contractions, headache, visual changes, shortness of breath, chest pain, epigastric pain, and abdominal pain. She was experiencing constipation and was advised to take Colace 100 mg BID, Miralax as needed, prune juice, increase dietary fiber intake, and follow a bisacodyl suppository regimen. 

Her physical examination was largely unremarkable, with no edema or tenderness noted in her extremities. Her vitals were within normal limits, with blood pressure readings ranging from 119-142/77-91. 

Lab results showed abnormal red blood cell count (RBC-3.66*, 3.47*), hemoglobin (Hgb-10.8*), and hematocrit (Hct-32.3*). Other lab results, including white blood cell count, creatinine, ALT, AST, uric acid, and urine protein:creatinine ratio, were within normal limits. 

She was diagnosed with gestational hypertension after her pregnancy-induced hypertension labs, urine protein:creatinine ratio, and 24-hour urine protein were found to be within normal limits. She was discharged home with precautions and close follow-up. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including levothyroxine, venlafaxine XR, lamotrigine, bupropion, prenatal vitamins, folic acid, aspirin, and acetaminophen. She also had several medications prescribed as needed for constipation and pain, including docusate sodium, acetaminophen, calcium carbonate, milk of magnesia, and bisacodyl.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female of undisclosed age, was admitted for prenatal care due to elevated blood pressure readings in the clinic. She has a known diagnosis of gestational hypertension (gHTN) and reported elevated home blood pressure readings. She denies any symptoms such as loss of fluid, vaginal bleeding, contractions, fetal movement, headache, visual changes, shortness of breath, chest pain, epigastric pain, and abdominal pain. 

Her past medical history includes a prior preterm premature rupture of membranes (PPROM) and preterm delivery of twins, depression and anxiety treated with multiple medications and electroconvulsive therapy (ECT), temporal lobe epilepsy managed with lamictal, hypothyroidism, and a question of connective tissue disorder/mitral valve prolapse. She also has a history of heavy menses and an abnormal pap smear (LGSIL, CIN1 on colposcopy) but has had normal paps since. She denies any history of sexually transmitted infections, fibroids, cysts, or heavy bleeding. 

Her family history includes depression, alcoholism, ADHD, hypertension (both parents), and a connective tissue disorder (father, sister). She denies any family history of aneurysms, uterine or organ rupture. 

Upon admission, her blood pressure readings were elevated, and she was asymptomatic. Her physical examination was unremarkable. Her blood work showed abnormal red blood cell count (3.84*), hemoglobin (11.0*), hematocrit (33.8*), and PTT (23.0*). Her magnesium level was also elevated (5.2*). 

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with preeclampsia with severe blood pressures. She developed severe range blood pressures requiring short-acting antihypertensive medications. Induction of labor was carried out, and she had a spontaneous vaginal delivery of a liveborn female infant. She received IV magnesium throughout her intrapartum course and started on labetalol for blood pressure control. She continued on her home medications of venlafaxine, buproprion, and lamictal for depression, and levothyroxine for hypothyroidism. 

Her medications upon admission included acetaminophen, bupropion (sustained release), lamotrigine, levothyroxine sodium, and venlafaxine XR. 

Her postpartum course was uncomplicated, and she was deemed stable for discharge with a plan set for postpartum follow-up. Her discharge diagnosis was preeclampsia with severe features at 35 weeks gestation.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a male with a history of bipolar disorder and hepatitis C. He was admitted with symptoms of meningismus and was evaluated for possible intracranial hemorrhage. He had a fever of 103 and photophobia for two days, along with fatigue and a diffuse headache. His blood cultures grew MSSA, and his antibiotics were switched to Oxacillin. A thorough endocarditis work-up was conducted, including TTE and TEE, which were negative for endocarditis. A CT scan showed inflammation of the R lower quadrant mesentary and trace free fluid without free air, and a 1.8 cm indeterminant lesion in the spleen. The patient complained of the worst headache of his life, became decorticate, and had a blown L pupil. He was emergently intubated and transferred without head imaging for further work-up. 

Upon arrival to the ICU, a non-contrast head CT was obtained which showed a L subdural hematoma and 2 areas of ICH in the occipital lobe. His past medical history includes bipolar disorder, hepatitis C, and a possible coarctation of the aorta. His family history is unknown. 

Abnormal lab results include WBC-11.3*, RBC-4.59*, Hgb-13.2*, Hct-38.4*, Neuts-89*, Lymphs-4*, ALT-142*, AST-89*, LD(LDH)-398*, CK(CPK)-404*, cTropnT-0.06*, and Triglyc-212*. 

Imaging studies showed new bilateral frontal intraparenchymal hemorrhage as well as left subdural hemorrhage with 1.1 cm of midline shift. There was effacement of the basal cisterns. There was no definite tonsillar herniation. The aortic arch was partially visualized, and was tortuous, buckled, and located high in the mediastinum, with a possible coarctation or aortic pseudo-coarctation. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was found to have MSSA bacteremia with positive blood cultures. He had multiple lesions in the brain, with questionable spleen and kidney lesions suggestive of systemic septic emboli from an unknown source. He was found to have an aortic aneurysm of 4.7 cm by CT scan/TEE. He was evaluated by cardiothoracic surgery who recommended semiurgent repair as an outpatient. He was also found to have 3+ aortic regurgitation on TEE. He was noted to have an erythematous, nonpainful blanching rash with superimposed vesicles on dependent areas of his body. His mental status changed and he was noted to have increasing somnolence and worsening strength on the left side. He was transferred to the MICU. His labs were notable for a sodium of 124 and a WBC of 19. He was pan-cultured. 

His medications upon admission were Lithium 30 mg PO BID and Paxil 30 mg PO daily. During his stay, he was given IV Oxacillin, Lithium 30 mg PO BID, and Paxil 30 mg PO daily.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of mild asthma and panic attacks, was admitted with a chief complaint of pneumothorax. He presented with right-sided chest pain that occurred while walking and worsened when bending down. The pain was pleuritic and associated with shortness of breath. The patient's chest X-ray showed a small apical pneumothorax. He had no history of pneumothorax in the past and had not taken any pain medication. He last smoked marijuana two days prior to admission and reported no trauma. 

The patient's family history includes his grandmother's history of coronary artery disease and his grandfather's history of stroke. His physical examination was largely unremarkable, with normal temperature, heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, and oxygen saturation. 

Lab results showed an abnormal mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) of 35.1. Other lab results, including white blood cell count, red blood cell count, hemoglobin, hematocrit, mean corpuscular volume, mean corpuscular hemoglobin, red cell distribution width, platelet count, glucose, urea nitrogen, creatinine, sodium, potassium, chloride, bicarbonate, and anion gap were all within normal ranges.

During his hospital stay, the patient's pneumothorax remained stable and he was not in respiratory distress. It was decided not to insert a chest tube or pigtail. His pain was managed with oral medication. Despite a small increase in the size of the pneumothorax noted by the radiologist, the patient was doing well, maintaining 100% oxygen saturation on room air, and was comfortable at rest. He was deemed appropriate for discharge with instructions for close follow-up and a chest X-ray prior to his clinic visit. 

Upon admission, the patient was not on any medications. His discharge diagnosis was spontaneous pneumothorax. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory at the time of discharge.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), mild asthma, and a previous spontaneous pneumothorax, presented to the emergency department with chest pain. He reported experiencing pain during inspiration and some shortness of breath, but denied having difficulty breathing. A chest x-ray revealed a right-sided apical pneumothorax. The patient had been evaluated by pulmonology for dyspnea on exertion since his last admission, with normal pulmonary function tests (PFTs) and cardiopulmonary exercise testing (CPET).

During his physical examination, decreased breath sounds were noted in the right upper chest and a soft systolic murmur was detected over the left intercostal space. The patient underwent a right pleural catheter placement due to the pneumothorax. Subsequent chest x-rays showed an increasing right pneumothorax, now moderate in size, and a small residual right apical pneumothorax after the placement of the chest tube. There was no evidence of acute pneumonia or vascular congestion.

The patient was evaluated by the Thoracic Surgery service and surgery was recommended due to this being his second pneumothorax on the right side. However, the patient preferred to wait on surgery. A repeat chest x-ray showed an enlarging pneumothorax, leading to the placement of a pleural catheter in the emergency room. After transfer to the surgical floor, the patient's chest tube was placed on suction, with no air leak noted. Following a waterseal trial, a tiny right apical pneumothorax was observed. The tube was removed and a post-pull film showed a stable right apical pneumothorax. The patient's room air saturations were 96% and his pain was minimal, controlled with Tylenol.

The patient's medication upon admission was lorazepam 0.5 mg PO Q8H as needed for anxiety. There is no known family history of pulmonary disease, pneumothorax, or any genetic connective tissue disorders. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of spontaneous right pneumothorax. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory upon discharge.
label: controls

summary:
The male patient was admitted to the orthopedics service with a chief complaint of left hip osteoarthritis (OA). He underwent a left total hip arthroplasty (L THA) without complications. His past medical history is significant for coronary artery disease (CAD), for which he underwent coronary artery bypass grafting (CABGx4) with left internal mammary artery to left anterior descending artery (LIMA->LAD), right internal mammary artery to left anterior descending artery diagonal 1 (RIMA->LADD1), radial artery to obtuse marginal 2 (Rad a->OM2), and saphenous vein graft to posterior descending artery (SVG->PDA). He also had percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (PTCA) of the left circumflex artery (LCx) and PDA graft. He has a history of hyperlipidemia, depression, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) with severe esophagitis and gastritis, erectile dysfunction, and joint pains with Lipitor, Zocor, and Lescol. He has had right knee surgery and multiple fractures of both elbows, as well as a left patellar fracture. 

The patient has allergies to Compazine, Erythromycin Base, Norvasc, Lipitor, Pravastatin, and Ondansetron. His family history is positive for heart disease, pulmonary fibrosis, and stomach cancer. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Plavix 75, Nexium BID, Dilaudid, Lisinopril 20, and Metoprolol 100. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's blood work showed abnormal results, including a low red blood cell count (RBC-2.96*, 3.19*, 3.40*), low hemoglobin (Hgb-9.3*, 9.6*, 10.3*), low hematocrit (Hct-26.6*, 28.3*, 30.3*), and high glucose (Glucose-135*). 

The patient was seen daily by physical therapy and labs were checked throughout the hospital course and repleted accordingly. At the time of discharge, the patient was tolerating a regular diet and feeling well. He was afebrile with stable vital signs. His hematocrit was stable, and his pain was adequately controlled on a PO regimen. The operative extremity was neurovascularly intact and the wound was benign. The patient was discharged to home with services or rehabilitation in a stable condition. His weight-bearing status was 50% partial weight bearing (PWB).
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to left hip pain. His past medical history includes coronary artery disease (CAD), for which he underwent coronary artery bypass grafting (CABGx4: LIMA->LAD, RIMA->LADD1, Rad a->OM2, SVG->PDA). He also had percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (PTCA) of the left circumflex artery (LCx) and posterior descending artery (PDA) graft. He has a history of hyperlipidemia, depression, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) with severe esophagitis and gastritis, erectile dysfunction, and joint pains associated with Lipitor, Zocor, and Lescol. He has undergone right knee surgery and has had multiple fractures of both elbows and a left patellar fracture. He has a family history of heart disease, pulmonary fibrosis, and stomach cancer.

During his stay, the patient underwent a left total hip arthroplasty. Postoperatively, he was initially treated with a patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) followed by oral pain medications. He received intravenous antibiotics for 24 hours postoperatively, as well as Lovenox for deep vein thrombosis (DVT) prophylaxis. His surgical incision was found to be clean, dry, and intact without erythema or purulent drainage. He had one episode of chest pain, but his EKG was without significant changes, and myocardial infarction was ruled out.

Lab results showed abnormal values for red blood cell count (3.46*#), hemoglobin (10.6*#), hematocrit (30.6*#), and glucose (143*). The patient was seen daily by physical therapy and labs were checked throughout the hospital course and repleted accordingly. At the time of discharge, the patient was tolerating a regular diet and feeling well. His hematocrit was stable, and his pain was adequately controlled on an oral regimen.

Upon admission, the patient was on Tylenol, Aspirin, Lisinopril 20, Metoprolol 100 tid, Nexium 40, nitroglycerin spray and patch, Plavix 75, Zantac 150, and Zocor. The patient was discharged in a stable condition with instructions to return to the emergency department or notify his doctor if he experiences severe pain not relieved by medication, increased swelling, decreased sensation, difficulty with movement, fevers >101.5, shaking chills, redness or drainage from the incision site, chest pain, shortness of breath, or any other concerns. He was also instructed to follow up with his primary care physician regarding this admission and any new medications and refills.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a male with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, impaired fasting glucose, severe coronary artery disease (with a history of myocardial infarction, coronary artery bypass grafting, and multiple percutaneous coronary interventions), and distal length-dependent axonal sensory polyneuropathy. He was transferred from an outside hospital after presenting with sudden onset visual changes, chest pain radiating to the left shoulder and jaw, and difficulty speaking, all of which have since resolved. The patient reported similar episodes in the past, with the most recent one occurring two weeks prior. He also reported dyspnea and fatigue, which have worsened over the past two months. 

The patient's past medical history also includes peripheral vascular disease, depression, possible fibromyalgia, erectile dysfunction, right knee surgery, multiple fractures of both elbows, left patellar fracture, bilateral hip replacements, skin burns on his left leg, and a history of violent outbursts while in the hospital. His family history includes renal failure in his mother and coronary artery disease and a seizure disorder in his father. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was awake, cooperative, and not in acute distress. His vitals were stable, and his neurological examination was largely unremarkable, with the exception of impaired stereognosis bilaterally. His sensory examination revealed decreased pinprick and temperature sensation at C3-4, impaired light touch bilaterally, and impaired vibratory sense and proprioception at the great toes bilaterally. 

Lab results showed abnormal red blood cell count (4.23), hemoglobin (13.0), hematocrit (37.0), mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (35.1), and cholesterol (220). His HbA1c was 6.6 and estimated average glucose was 143, indicating impaired glucose control. His triglycerides were also elevated at 221. Urine drug screen was positive for opiates. 

Imaging studies, including CT of the head, CTA of the chest, and MRI/A of the brain and neck, did not reveal any acute abnormalities. An EEG was normal, and an echocardiogram showed mild global left ventricular hypokinesis with an ejection fraction of XX%. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was monitored for possible stroke or transient ischemic attack, as well as myocardial ischemia. However, EKG and cardiac enzymes were negative for myocardial ischemia, and imaging studies did not show evidence of acute stroke. An EEG did not show epileptiform activity, and an echocardiogram did not reveal any clots or patent foramen ovale. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Plavix, Nexium, Ranitidine, Simvastatin, Carvedilol, Aspirin, Calcium with Vitamin D, a multivitamin, Nitrolingual spray, a Nitro patch, and Coenzyme Q10.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, coronary artery disease (CAD) post coronary artery bypass graft (CABG), peripheral vascular disease, depression, possible fibromyalgia, erectile dysfunction, and distal length-dependent severe axonal sensory polyneuropathy, was admitted due to chest pain. He has had multiple admissions for chest pain since his CABG, without documented acute coronary syndrome. The patient reported a gradual onset of pain two days prior to admission, described as bilateral chest tightness with some pain radiating to the left arm. The pain was non-exertional, nonpositional, and occasionally associated with shortness of breath, diaphoresis, and nausea. The patient's initial vital signs were stable and physical exam was unremarkable. 

The patient's lab results showed a white blood cell count of 8.1, hematocrit of 37.6, creatinine of 1.0, and troponin of less than 0.01. Other abnormal lab results included a red blood cell count of 4.18, hemoglobin of 12.9, hematocrit of 37.6, and glucose of 101. The patient's bicarbonate level was also low at 20. 

The patient underwent a Persantine stress test with nuclear imaging due to his significant cardiac history. The test revealed atypical mild chest pain with no ischemic changes on EKG and an appropriate hemodynamic response to pharmacologic stress. The nuclear study revealed a severe fixed perfusion defect on the inferior wall, unchanged from a prior study, with a calculated left ventricular ejection fraction of 50%. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Carvedilol, Plavix, Esomeprazole, Losartan, Metformin XR, SLNTG, Pravastatin, Tramadol, Ranitidine, ASA, and a multivitamin. 

The patient's family history is significant for renal failure in his mother and a strong history of CAD in his father, three brothers, and grandfather. One brother died of gastric cancer and another has pulmonary issues. 

Upon discharge, the patient's primary diagnosis was chest pain, with secondary diagnoses of coronary artery disease and gastroesophageal reflux disease. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: controls

summary:
The male patient was admitted due to chest pain, vision changes, and bilateral leg weakness. He reported that his symptoms began three days prior to admission, with vision changes similar to his previous transient ischemic attacks (TIAs). He also experienced left arm pain, which then traveled down to his left leg, and left-sided 'crushing' chest pain radiating into his left arm. The patient has a history of coronary artery disease (CAD) with four coronary artery bypass grafts (CABGs), myocardial infarction (MI), 15 percutaneous coronary interventions (PCIs), hypertension (HTN), hyperlipidemia (HLD), peripheral vascular disease (PVD), depression, possible fibromyalgia, erectile dysfunction, right knee surgery, multiple fractures of both elbows, left patellar fracture, bilateral hip replacements, skin burns on his left leg, and distal length-dependent severe axonal sensory polyneuropathy. 

The patient's family history includes father, grandfather, and two brothers with CAD, father with epilepsy, one brother with cancer, another brother with pulmonary fibrosis, and a sister with a brain aneurysm. His mother has renal failure. 

Upon admission, the patient's vitals were recorded and labs were conducted. The labs showed mild normocytic anemia (hgb 13.6), mildly low phosphate (2.5), and troponin < 0.01. A head CT scan and chest X-ray did not show any acute process. Neurology was consulted due to the patient's symptoms. 

The patient's past medications include Albuterol Inhaler, Carvedilol, Clopidogrel, Losartan Potassium, MetFORMIN XR, Nitroglycerin Patch, nitroglycerin, Omeprazole, Pravastatin, Ranitidine, TraMADOL, Aspirin, Calcium 600 + D(3), DiphenhydrAMINE, and Multivitamins. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's visual disturbance and lower extremity weakness were evaluated by neurology, who suggested the possibility of atypical migraines. His chest pain was evaluated with serial EKGs and troponins, which were negative. A pharmacologic, nuclear stress test showed no reversible ischemic changes and a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 43%. The patient's emotional lability was also noted, with a suggestion of anxiety contributing to his symptoms. 

The patient's discharge diagnoses include vision changes, most likely an atypical panic attack, and atypical chest pain, ruled out for acute coronary syndrome. His secondary diagnoses include CAD, history of multiple TIAs, Type II diabetes mellitus, HTN, and HLD. The patient was advised to follow up with his primary care doctor and neurologist.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a male with a history of paroxysmal atrial flutter and reported ventricular tachycardia (VT). He presented with new onset dyspnea on exertion and a 12-pound weight gain over the past week. He was transferred from another facility with atrial fibrillation and new decompensated heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) with a reported ejection fraction (EF) of 15%. His cardiac history dates back to when he was younger, with a sudden onset of palpitations and a diagnosis of atrial flutter. He had an exercise tolerance test (ETT) with exercise-induced VT that was asymptomatic. He was discharged on a beta blocker, which he self-discontinued due to side effects. He had a history of ruptured appendicitis and a prolonged hospitalization that was reportedly complicated by intermittent VT. He also had a knee replacement, which was complicated by intermittent VT. He was recommended to undergo a VT ablation, but he refused. He was prescribed metoprolol, which he discontinued due to side effects. 

Five weeks prior to the current presentation, he noticed dyspnea on exertion, chest tightness, and a dry cough. His symptoms worsened over the past week, with abdominal distention, leg swelling, and new orthopnea. He was found to be in atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response, which responded to IV metoprolol. An echo showed global left ventricular hypokinesis with an EF of 15%. He was started on a nitro drip and transferred for further care. 

His past medical history includes acoustic neuroma, nephrolithiasis, osteoarthritis, paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia, tubular adenoma of the colon, knee arthroplasty, and cervical laminectomy. His home medications include vitamin C, multivitamin, turmeric, and fish oil. His family history includes a brother with arrhythmia post-ablation, another brother with unknown heart disease, a father with coronary artery disease (CAD)/peripheral vascular disease (PVD), lung cancer, and COPD, and a mother with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), breast cancer, thyroid disorder, and CAD.

On physical examination, he had an irregularly irregular rhythm, S3 present, a holosystolic murmur, crackles at bilateral bases, distended abdomen, and 1+ edema of bilateral extremities. His labs were notable for normal CBC, HCO3 20, otherwise normal electrolytes, and trop < 0.01. His discharge labs showed WBC-10.6, RBC-5.39, Hgb-15.6, Hct-46.9, MCV-87, MCH-28.9, MCHC-33.3, RDW-16.1, RDWSD-50.1, Plt, Glucose-93, UreaN-24, Creat-1.0, Na-141, K-4.0, Cl-99, HCO3-25, AnGap-17, ALT-61, AST-39, AlkPhos-70, TotBili-1.9, Calcium-8.8, Phos-4.0, Mg-2.0, and proBNP-2479.

He underwent cardiac angiography and right heart catheterization. The differential for the etiology of his cardiomyopathy is broad, but it is likely secondary to poorly controlled atrial fibrillation. He was given IV lasix and captopril and transitioned to PO lasix and lisinopril for discharge. He underwent a transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE) and direct current cardioversion (DCCV) with successful conversion to normal sinus rhythm (NSR). He was started on metoprolol XL. He was asymptomatic and euvolemic on discharge. He was also started on amiodarone for new atrial fibrillation. His discharge medications include amiodarone, apixaban, furosemide, lisinopril, metoprolol succinate XL, ascorbic acid, fish oil, and multivitamins. His discharge diagnoses include acute decompensated heart failure with systolic dysfunction and atrial fibrillation.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of paroxysmal atrial flutter, atrial fibrillation, and congestive heart failure (CHF) with a reported ejection fraction (EF) of 15%, presented with intermittent lightheadedness and dyspnea on exertion. He had been recently discharged and started on lisinopril, apixaban, and amiodarone. Despite discontinuing lisinopril due to low blood pressure, the patient continued to experience episodes of lightheadedness. He also reported a recent fall while mowing the lawn in high heat, resulting in pain in the left lower rib cage upon deep breathing, moving, and coughing. 

Initial vitals in the emergency department (ED) showed tachycardia and an EKG revealed sinus rhythm with a QTc of 459 and ST depression in V4. Lab results showed an elevated white blood cell count (WBC) of 11.4 and a B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) level of 3540. A CT chest scan revealed an increase in the size of bilateral pleural effusions, new extensive multifocal airspace opacities in the left upper lobe, a nondisplaced fifth left rib fracture, and contrast seen only in the right heart with reflux of contrast into the inferior vena cava (IVC) and hepatic veins, likely due to poor cardiac output. 

The patient's past medical history includes acoustic neuroma, nephrolithiasis, osteoarthritis (OA), paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia, tubular adenoma of the colon, knee arthroplasty, and cervical laminectomy. His home medications include vitamin C, multivitamin, turmeric, and fish oil. His family history includes a brother with arrhythmia post-ablation, another brother with unknown heart disease, a father with coronary artery disease (CAD), peripheral vascular disease (PVD), lung cancer, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and a mother with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), breast cancer, thyroid disorder, and CAD.

During his hospital stay, the patient reported not being short of breath at rest, but experiencing shortness of breath with movement and difficulty catching his breath. He reported left-sided rib pain at the location of his fracture that worsened with coughing, and a feeling of fluid in his chest. He also reported abdominal bloating, decreased appetite, slight swelling in his legs, and a weight gain of 5 lbs since his recent discharge. He denied current dizziness, lightheadedness, shortness of breath at rest, abdominal pain, urinary symptoms, diarrhea/constipation, and pain in extremities. 

Admission labs showed elevated neutrophils (74.4), lymphocytes (11.6), monocytes (12.8), eosinophils (0.4), absolute neutrophils (8.51), and absolute monocytes (1.46). Urea nitrogen was also elevated at 24. ProBNP was significantly elevated at 3540. Discharge labs showed elevated RDWSD (48.6), urea nitrogen (22), and anion gap (19). 

The patient was diagnosed with atrial flutter status post direct current cardioversion, paroxysmal non-sustained ventricular tachycardia, acute on chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, and lung lesion of unknown significance. He was discharged with instructions to weigh himself daily, follow up with his primary care physician for further evaluation of his lungs, and take his medications as directed. His discharge medications included lisinopril 2.5 mg daily, furosemide 40 mg daily, amiodarone 400 mg twice daily, apixaban 5 mg twice daily, ascorbic acid, fish oil, metoprolol succinate XL 25 mg daily, and a multivitamin.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to chest pain and shortness of breath. He has a history of paroxysmal atrial flutter, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) with an ejection fraction of 24%, acoustic neuroma, nephrolithiasis, osteoarthritis, paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia, tubular adenoma of the colon, and knee arthroplasty. He had previously undergone a cervical laminectomy. His home medications include Vitamin C, Multivitamin, Turmeric, and Fish oil. 

Upon admission, the patient reported worsening shortness of breath and midsternal chest tightness. He had a history of ventricular tachycardia and was discharged with a life vest. He was also on amiodarone BID and eliquius. In the emergency department, he received Lasix 40mg IV. His initial vitals showed tachycardia and he required supplemental oxygen to maintain saturation. His labs showed elevated WBC, potassium, proBNP, and lactate levels. His chest X-ray showed mild pulmonary edema, small pleural effusions, and upper lobe opacities suggestive of multifocal pneumonia. 

During his hospital stay, the patient reported an ongoing cough, orthopnea, leg swelling, and dyspnea. His physical examination revealed tachycardia, irregular heart rhythm, rales bilaterally to the inferior scapula, and 2+ bilateral edema to the shins. His discharge labs showed elevated WBC, glucose, urea nitrogen, and reduced potassium levels. 

The patient was diagnosed with acute on chronic exacerbation of HFrEF due to atrial fibrillation. He was diuresed with Lasix and transitioned to oral torsemide. His Lisinopril was discontinued and he was started on spironolactone. He also had a fifth rib fracture from a previous syncopal event and multifocal airspace opacities in the left upper lobe. His amiodarone was discontinued due to concerns of toxicity. 

The patient's family history includes a brother with arrhythmia and another with unknown heart disease, a father with coronary artery disease, peripheral vascular disease, lung cancer, and COPD, and a mother with systemic lupus erythematosus, breast cancer, thyroid disorder, and coronary artery disease. 

Upon discharge, the patient was prescribed Potassium Chloride, Spironolactone, and Torsemide. His Lisinopril, Amiodarone, and Furosemide were discontinued. His discharge condition was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a male with a history of paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia in the 1990s, non-sustained ventricular tachycardia, paroxysmal atrial flutter/fibrillation, and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) with an ejection fraction of 10%. He also has a history of acoustic neuroma, nephrolithiasis, osteoarthritis, tubular adenoma of the colon, knee arthroplasty, and cervical laminectomy. He has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. 

He was admitted for a scheduled cavo-tricuspid isthmus (CTI) line and pulmonary vein isolation (PVI) procedure due to his atrial fibrillation. Post-procedure, he became hypotensive, requiring pressure support. He was transferred to the coronary care unit (CCU) but was quickly able to move to the floor. He underwent medication titration on the floor and diuresis. He converted back into atrial fibrillation likely due to fluid overload. Once euvolemic, he was cardioverted again and stayed in sinus rhythm. 

The patient has had three recent hospitalizations. The first was for acute decompensated heart failure and atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. He had a cardiac catheterization with no signs of coronary artery disease. He was cardioverted, returned to sinus rhythm, and started on amiodarone. The second hospitalization was for acute decompensated heart failure and recurrent atrial flutter. He was diuresed and underwent successful cardioversion with restoration of sinus rhythm. His Lasix was increased from 20 to 40 mg daily. The third hospitalization was for chest pain, shortness of breath, and atrial fibrillation with a heart rate in the 90-110s. He was diuresed with Lasix and transitioned to Torsemide. His amiodarone was discontinued due to concerns for lung toxicity. 

During his current admission, he was found to be hypotensive after the CTI line and PVI procedure. He was given 160 mg of Lasix and 35 mg of Protamine during the procedure. Post-procedure, he became hypotensive with a systolic blood pressure (SBP) and mean arterial pressures (MAPs) that were not specified in the note. He was given Phenylephrine IV per anesthesia. His SBP transiently increased back to baseline but returned back to lower levels with MAPs. A Phenylephrine drip was started. 

On arrival to the CCU, he was feeling well and had no complaints. His physical exam was largely unremarkable, with the exception of 1+ pitting edema in bilateral feet. His family history is notable for a brother with arrhythmia status post ablation, a father with coronary artery disease, lung cancer, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and a mother with systemic lupus erythematosus, breast cancer, and thyroid disease.

His admission labs showed several abnormalities including elevated neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, absolute neutrophils, absolute lymphocytes, absolute monocytes, absolute eosinophils, absolute basophils, white blood cell count, red cell distribution width, red cell distribution width standard deviation, albumin, phosphate, proBNP, ALT, AST, glucose, urea nitrogen, creatinine, and a low PO2. His chest x-ray showed multifocal opacities scattered throughout the lungs, which may represent amiodarone toxicity or multifocal pneumonia. He also had a small left pleural effusion and no significant change in the cardiomegaly and central pulmonary vascular congestion. His discharge labs showed an elevated RDWSD and ALT. 

The patient was discharged with a primary diagnosis of acute on chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, non-ischemic cardiomyopathy, and atrial fibrillation/flutter. His secondary diagnoses were pulmonary scarring and acute kidney injury. His discharge medications included Amiodarone 200 mg twice daily for 4 weeks, Lisinopril 2.5 mg daily, Metoprolol Succinate XL 12.5 mg daily, Torsemide 20 mg as needed, Apixaban 5 mg twice daily, Ascorbic Acid, Fish Oil (Omega 3) 1000 mg daily, and Multivitamins 1 tablet daily. Spironolactone 12.5 mg daily was held and not to be restarted until talking to the cardiologist.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to recurrent left pleural effusion. His medical history includes systolic heart failure, atrial fibrillation/flutter, pulmonary infiltrates, acoustic neuroma, nephrolithiasis, osteoarthritis, tubular adenoma of the colon, knee arthroplasty, and cervical laminectomy. He underwent a left thoracoscopy with pleural biopsies, talc pleurodesis, and chest tube placement. The patient reported feeling tired and experiencing pain with movement or coughing, but denied other symptoms such as chest pain, shortness of breath, headache, nausea, or vomiting.

The patient's family history includes a brother with arrhythmia post-ablation, a father with coronary artery disease, lung cancer, and COPD, and a mother with systemic lupus erythematosus, breast cancer, and thyroid disease.

Upon admission, the patient's vital signs were stable and he was alert and interactive. Physical examination revealed a left chest tube in place with serosanguinous fluid in both tubes. No other significant findings were noted. Discharge physical exam showed good air movement throughout both lungs, with crackles in the left lower base.

Abnormal lab results upon admission included a high white blood cell count of 17.4, and elevated MCHC and RDWSD. Glucose was also elevated at 124. Pleural fluid analysis showed elevated total protein count and lymphocyte count. Discharge labs showed a decrease in white blood cell count, red blood cell count, hemoglobin, and hematocrit. 

Imaging revealed stable position of the left chest tube, unchanged extent of the left pleural effusion, minimally improved retrocardiac atelectasis, and stable moderate cardiomegaly. No pulmonary edema was observed. Microbiology results showed no growth in the pleural fluid and tissue cultures.

The patient was discharged with a drainage system to be managed by a home service. His medications upon admission included Amiodarone, Apixaban, Losartan Potassium, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Torsemide, Ascorbic Acid, Multivitamins, and Fish Oil. These were continued upon discharge. The patient was discharged home with service, in a clear and coherent mental state, and was ambulatory and independent.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of hypothyroidism, 'sports induced' asthma, hormonal migraines, and a motor vehicle accident resulting in intracranial hemorrhage and contusion, was admitted due to sudden onset right neck pain. The pain was triggered by violent coughing, which the patient attributes to a post-cold cough exacerbated by her asthma. She also reported experiencing right pulsatile tinnitus at the onset of her neck pain. The patient denied any recent traumatic injury, motor vehicle accident, neck strain, or heavy lifting that could have caused the pain. She was initially diagnosed with muscle spasm at an urgent care center and was prescribed diazepam. 

Upon admission, the patient was found to have right internal carotid artery (ICA) dissection and right vertebral artery narrowing on a CT angiography of the head and neck. She also developed a right Horner's syndrome, characterized by drooping of the right eyelid. The patient denied any loss of vision, blurred vision, diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, lightheadedness, vertigo, or hearing difficulty. She also denied any difficulties with speech, focal weakness, numbness, parasthesiae, bowel or bladder incontinence or retention, and difficulty with gait. 

The patient's family history includes an uncle who had a stroke at a young age and another stroke later in life. He was a smoker. There is no known family history of fibromuscular dysplasia, polycystic kidney disease, or connective tissue disease.

Upon physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented. She had right ptosis and miosis, but her pupils were briskly reactive to light. Her motor strength was intact, and she had no sensory deficits. Her deep tendon reflexes were normal, and she had no coordination deficits. Her gait was not tested due to pain with movement. 

Laboratory results showed abnormal potassium levels of 7.3 and 6.0 on the day of admission, and an abnormal total CO2 level of 21. Her PTT levels were also elevated at 53.2 and 92.3 on the day of admission, and 107.9 and 51.9 on the second day. Her neutrophil count was elevated at 73.5, and her eosinophil count was low at 0.6. 

Imaging studies confirmed the right ICA dissection and right vertebral artery narrowing. There were no acute infarcts, mass effect, or hydrocephalus noted on MRI. 

During her hospital stay, the patient was started on warfarin 5mg/day with an INR goal, and bridged with IV heparin until her INR reached a therapeutic range of 2.2. Her neck pain was treated with acetaminophen and tramadol. Her EKG showed normal sinus rhythm. 

Upon discharge, the patient's mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent. Her discharge diagnosis was right internal carotid dissection. Her discharge medications included Levothyroxine Sodium 88 mcg PO daily for hypothyroidism, ProAir HFA (albuterol sulfate) 90 mcg/actuation inhalation PRN for asthma, and Warfarin 5 mg PO daily.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted to the neurology department with a chief complaint of headache. He has a history of hyperlipidemia and diabetes mellitus, with possible hypertension. He has no history of atrial fibrillation, prior cerebrovascular accident/transient ischemic attack/intracerebral hemorrhage. He has allergies to penicillins, clindamycin, and Dilaudid. His family history includes hypertension and cancer (pancreatic, breast) on his mother's side, but no known history of hyperlipidemia, diabetes mellitus, cerebrovascular accident/transient ischemic attack, coronary artery disease/peripheral vascular disease, intracerebral aneurysms/arteriovenous malformation, or connective tissue disorders.
