No definite soft tissue gas was seen, but a plantar calcaneal spur was noted. 

The patient's condition improved significantly after being administered vancomycin in the Emergency Department. The podiatry team did not recommend any operative intervention aside from the bedside debridement. The patient was advised to continue with oral antibiotics for a certain number of days. Due to her numerous drug allergies, the Infectious Disease team recommended linezolid. The patient was advised to follow up with podiatry in a week and to continue daily betadine dressings to the left toe. 

The patient's discharge labs showed persistently abnormal values for Hemoglobin (10.4), Hematocrit (31.9), MCV (80), Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH - 25.9), and Glucose (134). Blood cultures were pending at the time of discharge and will need to be followed up. 

The patient's medication upon admission included cilostazol, clopidogrel, insulin lispro, levothyroxine sodium, lisinopril, tacrolimus, insulin glargine, methylprednisolone, diphenoxylate-atropine, everolimus, and loperamide. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of foot ulcer and cellulitis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. However, she required assistance or aid for ambulation. She was advised to continue daily betadine dressings to her toe ulcer until her follow-up with podiatry.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of metastatic neuro-endocrine (carcinoid) tumor, type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) complicated by a recent toe ulcer/cellulitis, and multiple drug allergies, presented to the emergency department (ED) with malaise. She reported feeling extremely fatigued and had difficulty getting out of bed. She also reported a decrease in appetite and some loose stools that self-resolved. She denied experiencing headache, neck stiffness, cough, vomiting, abdominal pain, or dysuria. She was found to be hypotensive at an outpatient allergist visit and was referred to the ED. 

The patient's past medical history includes arthritis, Bell's palsy, cigarette smoking, gastrointestinal issues, hypertension, right axillary lymphadenopathy, thyroid nodule, peripheral vascular disease, back pain, dermatofibroma, leg pain, rash, and a history of chickenpox. She has undergone multiple surgeries including ulnar nerve reconstruction, multiple ear surgeries, tonsillectomy, partial hysterectomy, carpal tunnel syndrome surgery, ganglion removal, and cholecystectomy. 

The patient's family history includes heart disease, transient ischemic attack, eye loss, diabetes type I in her mother, myocardial infarction in her father, valve prolapse and hearing loss in her sister, sudden cardiac death in another relative, pneumonia and obesity in her daughter, fluctuating blood pressure and gout in her son, Crohn's disease and hearing problems in her granddaughters, myocardial infarction in a 3-week-old relative, and stroke in her aunt.

Upon admission, the patient's vitals were 98.1 115 110/62 10 95% RA. Her labs showed a WBC of 11.6, Hgb 11.5, plt 231, AST 55, Lipase 87, CK 118, Cr of 1.1, lactate 1.0, Mg 1.5. Her UA was negative for infection and her Trop was <0.01. Her CXR showed no acute process. Her CT head showed no acute intracranial process, a partially visualized right parietal mass with surrounding edema, and a previously seen enhancing right hypothalamic lesion. Her EKG showed sinus rhythm, low voltage, precordial leads, anteroseptal infarct, old, with no significant change compared to previous ECG. 

During her stay in the ED, the patient's Tmax increased to 102 and she became encephalopathic. She was broadened to bacterial meningitis dosing and admitted for further care. During the time that she was febrile, she was hypoxic to 88%RA, but her CXR was unchanged. 

The patient's medications upon admission included cilostazol, clopidogrel, diphenoxylate-atropine, levothyroxine sodium, lisinopril, loperamide, methylprednisolone, tacrolimus, hydrocerin, alprazolam, glargine, ondansetron ODT, and cetirizine. 

The patient's discharge diagnoses were cellulitis and diabetic foot ulcer. Her secondary diagnoses included adrenal insufficiency, hypertension, hypomagnesemia, type 2 diabetes, hypothyroidism, peripheral arterial disease, and metastatic carcinoid tumor.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, thyroid nodule, arthritis, right axillary lymphadenopathy, history of chickenpox, and previous surgeries including cholecystectomy, hysterectomy, carpal tunnel surgery, and ulnar nerve reconstruction, was admitted due to a gangrenous left foot hallux. She reported dropping a dish on her foot several months prior, which led to darkened discoloration and worsening pain. She was given a one-week course of Clindamycin, which improved the erythema at the base of her left hallux. She denies any fever, chills, nausea, or vomiting. 

During her stay, she underwent a left digit amputation and a lower left extremity angioplasty. Her physical exam revealed a gangrenous left hallux without any bogginess or fluctuance, localized erythema at the base of the left hallux, and no tenderness to palpation. Radiology reports indicated severe end-stage osteoarthritic changes involving the first MTP joint, distal SFA versus popliteal disease on the left, and likely tibial disease. There was no radiographic evidence for osteomyelitis. 

Her lab results showed abnormal RDW and glucose levels on admission and discharge, and an abnormal Vancomycin level on discharge. Her home medications, including amlodipine, atorvastatin, lantus, levothyroxine, and lisinopril, were continued during her stay. 

Post-surgery, she remained afebrile with a normal WBC count and was able to tolerate regular oral intake without any problems. She was discharged with instructions to remain partial weight-bearing to her left heel in a surgical shoe, physical therapy, two weeks of post-operative antibiotics, and daily dressing changes. Her discharge diagnosis was a left gangrenous hallux.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted for elective angioplasty of her left lower extremity due to a non-healing ulcer on her left toe and progressive diffuse stenosis within a previously placed stent in her left lower extremity. The stenosis was associated with a drop in the left ankle-brachial index to 0.62. The patient underwent angioplasty of the left distal SFA stent in-stent stenosis and popliteal artery. Postoperatively, she was stable with no issues and her creatinine level was 0.7, which was at her baseline. She was discharged home in good condition with instructions to follow up in one month's time with a duplex of her left lower extremity.

Her past medical history includes type 2 diabetes, hypertension, a thyroid nodule, arthritis, right axillary lymphadenopathy, a history of chickenpox, and surgeries including cholecystectomy, hysterectomy, carpal tunnel surgery, and ulnar nerve reconstruction. She also has a history of right axillary pain and Bell's palsy. 

Upon admission, her medications included lantus 58 qam, lisinopril 40', levothyroxine 25' M through F, 50' on weekends, and chlorthalidone 12.5 every other day. She has allergies to penicillins, sulfa antibiotics, ciprofloxacin, Mavik, Diovan, adhesive tape, aspirin, nifedipine, cyclobenzaprine, chlorthalidone, and has a pseudocholesteine deficiency.

Her physical exam upon discharge showed normal vital signs, with no organomegaly and normal pulses. However, her blood glucose level was high at 330 and her chloride level was low at 95. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of a left toe ulcer with distal SFA instent stenosis post stent and popliteal angioplasty. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted for bilateral lower extremity claudication. She underwent a percutaneous transluminal angioplasty (PTA) of bilateral popliteals. The procedure was well-tolerated and she was extubated and transferred to the PACU in stable condition. She remained in bedrest for two hours, her diet was advanced, and she was restarted on her aspirin and clopidogrel therapy. Overnight, she did well with no new events. She was discharged in stable condition.

Her past medical history includes type 2 diabetes, hypertension, thyroid nodule, arthritis, right axillary lymphadenopathy, history of chickenpox, and she has undergone cholecystectomy, hysterectomy, carpal tunnel surgery, and ulnar nerve reconstruction. She also has a history of right axillary pain and Bell's palsy. She has no known family history of any significant diseases.

On physical examination at the time of discharge, she was alert and oriented times three. Her vitals were stable with a temperature of 98.4, heart rate of 86, blood pressure of 134/88, respiratory rate of 20, and oxygen saturation of 99% on room air. Her cardiac examination revealed a regular rate and rhythm. Pulmonary examination was clear to auscultation bilaterally. Her abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended. There was no evidence of hematoma or bruising in both groins and her pulses were palpable.

Her medications upon admission included Acetaminophen, Chlorthalidone, Clopidogrel, Docusate Sodium, Levothyroxine Sodium, Glargine, and Lisinopril. She also had a nicotine lozenge for nicotine withdrawal. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of bilateral lower extremity claudication and diabetes. Her discharge condition was stable with an activity status of ambulatory - independent. Her level of consciousness was alert and interactive and her mental status was clear and coherent.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted with a chief complaint of a swollen left knee and a fever. He reported waking up with a markedly swollen and tender knee, which he could barely flex. He also reported a fever of 100.1 and 101.8 on two separate occasions. The patient had previously injured the same knee in a softball game but did not seek medical attention at the time. He had no recent trauma to the knee, no scrapes or broken skin, and no recent illnesses. He works in a job where he spends about 70% of his time at job sites but could not recall any recent injury to the knee. 

Upon presenting to the ED, the joint was aspirated and no organisms were seen. He was discharged with Keflex. However, the culture grew GPCs (speciation pending) and he was called to return to the ED. His knee was clinically improved with less pain and improved range of motion. He was evaluated by ortho in the ED who recommended Vanco IV for possible septic bursitis. 

The patient's past medical history includes kidney stones. He is adopted and therefore, his family history is unknown. His physical exam showed erythematous and warm left knee joint with mild discomfort on passive range of motion. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for WBC-2500*, HCT-4*, POLYS-66*, BANDS-1*, SED RATE-63*, and CRP-188.6*. The joint fluid culture showed MSSA. Knee films showed extensive soft tissue swelling, but no acute fracture or dislocation was identified. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with septic bursitis. He was initially treated with Keflex which resulted in resolution of erythema. He returned to the hospital once joint cultures had small growth MSSA. Ortho believed it to be septic bursitis and not septic arthritis given adequate range of motion. The patient was placed on IV vancomycin while awaiting sensitivities, with continued improvement on knee, and remaining afebrile. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Keflex. His discharge diagnosis was septic bursitis and his condition upon discharge was good.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted due to lung nodules. He had developed a cough and a subsequent Chest CT showed a 5mm RML nodule. He has been followed yearly for this and in an undisclosed time, bilateral pulmonary nodules were documented. He underwent a CT guided biopsy of a LLL nodule which was non-diagnostic. He also had a CT of the abdomen and pelvis which showed some liver lesions and other unspecified lesions. The etiology of these lesions is unclear but the differential included infection, neoplasm, and vasculitis. He had a colonoscopy which was normal. His pulmonary symptoms include cough and some mid chest pressure when he feels more congestion. He denies shortness of breath, dyspnea on exertion, weight loss, recent upper respiratory infection/pneumonia, foreign travel, etc. He smoked for a short time about an undisclosed number of years ago but none since. He works every day in a family business and is exposed to lots of dust, sewerage, etc.

His past medical history includes kidney stones, RML lung nodule, and MRSA in the left knee and nose. He has also undergone bilateral hernia repairs. He has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. His family history is unknown as he is adopted.

During his hospital stay, he underwent a VATS RML wedge resection x 2 and RLL wedge resection x 1. He recovered well post-surgery, with minimal chest tube output, good oxygenation on room air, stable vital signs, and independent ambulation. His chest X-ray showed no pneumothorax or consolidation. His pain was well controlled on oral pain meds. He was discharged home and will follow up in clinic in 2 weeks.

Upon admission, he was taking over-the-counter cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) and a daily multivitamin. His physical exam was largely unremarkable. However, his lab results showed an elevated glucose level of 127* and a slightly elevated creatinine level of 1.3*. His chest X-ray prior to chest tube pull showed no appreciable pneumothorax, right pleural drainage tube in place, no appreciable pleural effusion, moderately extensive atelectasis and biopsy artifact persist in the right mid and lower lung zones, and moderate atelectasis has developed on the left. Left and right upper lobes are clear. Cardiomediastinal silhouette is unremarkable.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a past medical history of spontaneous abortion, hypothyroidism, Vitamin D deficiency, possible GERD, and vasovagal syncope in childhood, presented with progressive sensory changes. She had recently experienced a miscarriage and underwent a D&C procedure. Post-procedure, she noticed odd paresthesias to light touch over her lower legs and feet, which later extended to include her thighs, waist, and eventually ascended to nipple level. She reported no abnormality at rest and was only aware of the difference when she touched her skin. An urgent MRI scan was arranged which showed several enhancing lesions in the dorsal cord, as well as several lesions that did not enhance. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented, with normal muscle bulk and tone throughout. She reported an odd sensation to light touch, which included the legs and extended up the trunk to the level of ~T4 bilaterally. She believed that the right side was perhaps more affected. 

Admission labs showed an elevated absolute neutrophil count of 7.46 and a glucose level of 112. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis showed 1 RBC. Imaging revealed multiple T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions throughout the cervical and thoracic spinal cord, suggestive of demyelinating disease. There were also scattered white matter lesions in the bilateral cerebral hemispheres, as well as the right cerebellar hemisphere. 

During her hospital stay, the patient reported improvement in her symptoms. A lumbar puncture showed no evidence of infection. The presence of both enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on her MRI was highly suggestive of a neurological condition. The patient was diagnosed with multiple sclerosis (MS) using the McDonald criteria. 

Upon discharge, the patient's mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. Her neuro exam showed decreased light touch (mixed with dysesthesia) in the medial legs and bilateral torso. Her discharge medications included Levothyroxine Sodium 75 mcg PO DAILY. The patient was discharged home with a primary diagnosis of multiple sclerosis.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted following an accident where she was struck by an automobile while crossing the street. She lost consciousness and woke up in the ambulance. She was then transported to the hospital for further management. The patient has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She is allergic to Penicillins and Tetracycline. 

The primary complaint was due to the accident. She underwent an Open Reduction Internal Fixation (ORIF) procedure for a mandible fracture. Postoperatively, she was initially sleepy, likely due to the anesthesia and narcotics. She had difficulties navigating stairs, which was a concern as her apartment has 35 stairs. However, she was eventually cleared for home with services by Physical Therapy. 

Lab results showed abnormal glucose levels at 137, potassium at 3.0, total CO2 at 20, hematocrit at 35.3, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) at 80, and lymphocytes at 43.9. A head CT scan revealed no acute intracranial process, a left frontal/supra-orbital scalp soft tissue hematoma without skull fracture, and sinus disease. A mandibular series was conducted but the right mandibular ramus fracture was not clearly identified on the images provided due to the patient's dizziness. 

Upon discharge, the patient was hemodynamically stable, tolerating a regular diet, and her pain was adequately controlled. Her discharge diagnoses included pedestrian struck by auto, mandible fracture, lip and facial lacerations, and knee abrasions. She was given wire cutters and instructions on a full liquid and soft diet, and increasing her protein intake. She was advised to drink at least 6 cans daily of Boost Plus/Ensure Plus for proper nutrients and vitamins to aid in the healing process. She was also instructed to cut the wires on both corners of her teeth and go to the Emergency room immediately in case of an emergency such as shortness of breath, nausea, and/or vomiting.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for an elective right total knee replacement due to progressive right knee pain with activity. His past medical history includes osteoarthritis, a tonsillectomy, knee arthroscopy, and prostate cancer treated with XRT. He had no medications upon admission. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a successful right total knee replacement procedure without any complications. He was closely monitored throughout the procedure and remained hemodynamically stable. Post-operatively, he was transferred to the PACU and floor for further recovery. He remained hemodynamically stable and his pain was well controlled. However, he required a transfusion due to expected postoperative anemia. He made progress with physical therapy to improve his strength and mobility. 

Upon physical examination at discharge, the patient was afebrile with all vital signs stable. He was alert and oriented, with no acute distress. His right lower extremity was partially weight bearing. The incision was intact with no swelling, erythema, or drainage. The dressing was clean, dry, and intact. Extensor/flexor hallicus longus was intact, sensation was intact to light touch, neurovascular was intact distally, capillary refill was brisk, and 2+ pulses were present. 

The patient was discharged in a stable condition with a diagnosis of osteoarthritis. His discharge instructions included monitoring for any shortness of breath, new redness, increased swelling, pain, or drainage, or a temperature over 101. He was advised to bear weight on his right leg and use crutches for ambulation. He was also instructed to keep his incision/dressing clean and dry, and to continue his Lovenox injections as prescribed to help prevent blood clots.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for an elective L4-S1 lumbar fusion due to lumbar spondylosis. His chief complaint upon admission was right leg pain. His past medical history includes Chol and RUL opacity on CT. The patient underwent a major surgical procedure, L4-S1 posterior lumbar interbody fusion with pedicle screws and bone marrow aspirate. Post-operatively, he was transferred to the floor from the PACU. His immediate post-op pain was managed with a PCA, which was later discontinued and replaced with oral agents. The patient was able to void independently after his Foley was discontinued. Physical therapy evaluated him and cleared him for home. 

Post-op x-rays showed proper alignment and no evidence of hardware complication. His surgical wound was covered with steri strips and was clean, dry, and intact. The patient's drain was discontinued and there were no issues with drainage. His Ancef was discontinued. He had a Tmax for 24 hrs of ___, a UA was ordered and IS was recommended. He was ambulating independently. The UA was negative and he was asked to call the office to follow up on the culture. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Oxycodone PRN. His mental status upon discharge was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was able to ambulate independently. The patient was discharged home with a diagnosis of lumbar spondylosis. His discharge instructions included wound care, activity restrictions, pain management, and signs of complications to watch for. He was also instructed to call the office for urine culture results and to continue using his Incentive spirometer.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a male of undisclosed age who was admitted due to severe back and leg pain that had been worsening over the course of 18 months. His pain was so severe that it was poorly controlled with IV opioids and was affecting his ability to work. He had previously tried conservative management for his pain with little relief. He was scheduled for surgical repair of a ruptured L4-L5 disc in the coming weeks. The patient denied having diabetes mellitus, intravenous drug use, recent back procedures, urinary incontinance, urinary retention, or fecal incontinance. His past medical history includes gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). He was not on any medications upon admission and has no known drug allergies. His social history includes rare alcohol consumption and smoking, but no illicit drug use.

Physical examination revealed the patient was alert and oriented, but in severe discomfort. Sensory and motor examinations were normal. His upper extremities were clean and intact with no signs of tenderness, deformity, erythema, edema, induration, or ecchymosis. His arms and forearm compartments were soft and he had no pain with passive motion. His radial pulses were 2+.

During his hospital stay, the patient was considered for surgery for his herniated lumbar disc. However, due to surgical emergencies, the operation was delayed. In the meantime, his pain was managed with IV decadron, and oral oxycontin and oxycodone. As the patient became comfortable and was able to ambulate on oral pain medications, he was discharged home. He was instructed to avoid heavy lifting and significant twisting or bending. His condition upon discharge was good. His discharge diagnosis was a herniated lumbar disc.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female who was admitted due to worsening depressive symptoms and suicidal ideation. She has a history of depressive episodes but no previous psychiatric admissions or suicide attempts. She reported persistent thoughts of suicide over the last few weeks, along with symptoms of major depression such as loss of interest in activities, inability to see or invest in the future, lack of appetite, trouble concentrating, hopelessness, and a generalized feeling of being 'lost.' She also has a history of sexual trauma. The patient reported a three-month worsening of depressive symptoms, including anhedonia, poor concentration, low motivation, fatigue, diminished appetite, and suicidal ideation. She denied having any supports in the community and cited school and financial stressors as contributing to her worsening mood. 

The patient has a past medical history of asthma and Achilles tendonitis. She has no known drug allergies but is allergic to alcohol. She was not on any medications upon admission. She denies any family psychiatric history. 

Physical examination was largely unremarkable. Neurological examination was also normal. The patient was awake and alert, oriented, and able to register and recall words. Her speech was low volume with brief answers. Her mental status on discharge was improved, with a better mood congruent with a euthymic, reactive, mildly constricted affect. She denied suicidal ideation and demonstrated fair insight and good judgment.

Lab results showed an abnormal MCHC of 30.5 and an abnormal RDWSD of 47.1. Eosinophils were also slightly low at 0.7. All other lab results were within normal limits.

During her hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with an unspecified depressive disorder. She was provided with individual and group therapy focusing on meditation, self-awareness, and coping skills. She was encouraged to participate in various groups and milieu therapy opportunities offered by the unit. She was also provided with over-the-counter analgesics for her Achilles tendonitis. 

Upon discharge, the patient was not at an acutely elevated risk of self-harm nor danger to others. She was discharged home with a diagnosis of unspecified depressive disorder. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was in a good mood congruent with euthymic, reactive affect, and denied suicidal ideation. She was not prescribed any medications upon discharge.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with no significant past medical history, was referred for ERCP due to a biliary tumor. He was recently diagnosed with cholangiocarcinoma when he presented with jaundice and pruritis. He has a 3 cm complex hilar biliary stricture and has undergone two ERCPs. He was scheduled for a curative Whipple procedure, which was aborted due to the discovery of multiple peritoneal implants. This was a surprise to the patient and his family as previous CT scans did not show this. The patient has regained some weight since the placement of a plastic stent during ERCP. His initial jaundice has resolved. 

The patient underwent major surgical procedures including ERCP with metal stent, percutaneous cholecystostomy tube placement, and PICC placement. He reported pain at the site of the laparoscopy wounds and in his shoulder after the procedure. He also experienced a 7 lbs weight loss over a month and chest pain that resolves with Ativan. He also reported right shoulder pain post laparoscopy. 

The patient's family history includes his maternal grandfather and grandmother dying from heart disease. His uncle had diabetes and died of heart disease, and another uncle died of lung cancer. 

Upon admission, the patient was on OxycoDONE (Immediate Release) 5 mg PO Q4H for pain and Lorazepam 0.5 mg PO Q6H for anxiety. 

Abnormal lab results include WBC-16.8*, RBC-4.49*, MCH-32.1*, and AlkPhos-319*. The patient's ERCP revealed a complex tight 3cm hilar stricture. The right anterior and posterior intrahepatics were visualized, but the left intrahepatic duct could not be seen. A sphincterotomy was performed and two 8cm by 8mm uncovered metal stents were successfully placed in the right anterior and posterior intrahepatic ducts. 

CT scan showed diffuse intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation, central hepatic vascular stenosis and effacement with a central poorly defined hepatic lesion suggestive of cholangioCA/ Klatskin tumor. Blood culture revealed ESCHERICHIA COLI. 

The patient's hospital course was complicated by acute cholangitis and E. coli septicemia, as well as portal vein thrombus and evidence of liver infarct. He was started on Zosyn/Cipro and a PTC was placed. He was transitioned to Imipenem and his blood cultures were cleared. He was discharged on Meropenem 1g IV q8 for at least 2 weeks. He was also initiated on Lovenox 60mg SQ q12 hrs, indefinitely, but for at least several months. 

The patient's discharge diagnoses include metastatic cholangiocarcinoma, with metastases to peritoneum and liver, bile obstruction with acute cholangitis and E. coli septicemia, and portal vein thrombosis.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male of unspecified age, was admitted due to increasing right upper quadrant pain. He has a history of cholangiocarcinoma, for which he has had multiple stents placed to relieve biliary obstruction. He has also undergone a laparotomy which revealed peritoneal metastases, ruling out a previous plan for a Whipple procedure. He has been receiving palliative chemotherapy with gemcitabine and cisplatin, with the last infusion two weeks prior to this admission. He presented with fevers, worsening right upper quadrant pain, and increasing icterus. He also reported whiter stools and darker urine. He has managed his pain with minimal oxycodone, but reported an increase in pain beyond his baseline. He denied shortness of breath, chest pain, palpitations, dizziness, lightheadedness, diarrhea, numbness, tingling, and dysuria. A CT scan revealed no progression of his tumor, but laboratory assessment showed leukocytosis and a rising alkaline phosphatase (>600). 

During his stay, he developed acute fever and subsequent epigastric abdominal pain. Blood cultures returned positive for GNR, despite being on Unasyn. He was changed to Zosyn/Cipro and a CT scan showed dilatation of his left hepatic lobe biliary system, and possibly early abscess formation. A PTC was placed, which improved his symptoms. Nearly pan-resistant E. coli was grown, and he was transitioned to Imipenem. His blood cultures were cleared and a PICC line was placed. He was discharged on Meropenem 1g IV q8 for at least 2 weeks. 

His family history includes his maternal grandfather and grandmother dying from heart disease. His uncle had diabetes and died of heart disease. Another uncle died of lung cancer. 

Upon admission, his vitals were stable and physical examination revealed mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant. His discharge physical was largely unremarkable, with mild tenderness to palpation in the right upper quadrant. 

Admission labs showed abnormal values for WBC (15.9), RBC (3.55), Hgb (11.8), Hct (35.4), MCV (100), MCH (33.2), RDW (16.6), Neuts (78.0), PTT (37.5), Glucose (101), ALT (58), AST (50), AlkPhos (661), TotBili (3.5), and Albumin (3.3). Discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.41), Hgb (11.2), Hct (34.3), MCV (101), MCH (32.8), RDW (15.7), AlkPhos (522), and Phos (5.1). 

Imaging showed no progression of his tumor, and ERCP revealed obstruction of 2 stents in the biliary tree with evidence of pus behind the stents. Two metal stents were placed to relieve obstruction. 

During his hospital stay, he was diagnosed with acute cholangitis, metastatic cholangiocarcinoma to liver/peritoneum, portal vein thrombosis/liver infarct, and asymptomatic bradycardia. He was discharged with a primary diagnosis of acute cholangitis and secondary diagnosis of metastatic cholangiocarcinoma. 

Upon admission, he was on Lorazepam, OxycoDONE, Docusate Sodium, Senna, and Enoxaparin Sodium. He was discharged on Docusate Sodium, Enoxaparin Sodium, Lorazepam, OxycoDONE, Senna, Meropenem, and Amoxicillin-Clavulanic Acid.
label: controls

summary:
The male patient, with a past medical history of Stage IV Cholangiocarcinoma, presented with symptoms of nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain. He reported a fever of 101.3 which resolved on its own within a few hours, and a recurrent fever of 102.5 the next day. He also reported significant upper abdominal pain unresponsive to his usual dose of oxycodone 5mg. He has a history of cholangitis and had previously been treated for the same. His ERCP showed obstruction of 2 stents in the biliary tree with evidence of pus behind the stents. 2 metal stents were placed to relieve obstruction and biliary duct aspirate was sent for culture. Final culture results showed resistant E. Coli, pansensitive E. coli and Enterococcus. 

Upon admission, the patient was febrile with a temperature of 103.7. His labs showed a normal WBC and lactate, but an obstructive pattern on LFTs. His CXR showed no acute process. He complained of left calf tenderness, but ultrasound showed no DVT. CT of the abdomen showed no intra-abdominal process and the biliary stents appeared patent. He received vancomycin, cefepime and ~ 4L NS in the ED. Despite this, he continued to be hypotensive with SBPs in the 90s and was admitted to the ICU. 

In the ICU, the patient remained stable on antibiotics and fluids. He received only 1L of LR with a stable blood pressure not requiring pressors. He was transferred to the ___ in preparation for ERCP. 

His admission physical exam showed tenderness in the epigastrium and right upper quadrant. His admission labs showed abnormal RBC (3.66), Hgb (12.2), Hct (35.3), MCH (33.4), Neuts (89.1), Lymphs (7.4), Monos (1.6), Glucose (104), ALT (105), AST (94), AlkPhos (396), and TotBili (2.6). 

The patient underwent ERCP where 2 metal stents placed in the biliary duct were found in the major papilla. Both stents were partially occluded from the food debris. Multiple balloon sweeps were performed on the L side with a moderate amount of pus and debris coming out. Attempts were made multiple times to access either RA or RP hepatic ducts on the R side but were not successful. There was no contrast opacifying the R biliary system. 

The patient's hospital course was complicated by acute cholangitis with bile obstruction and metastatic cholangiocarcinoma to liver/peritoneum. He was treated with antibiotics per most recent culture data showing resistant E coli and enterococcus (meropenem 1g IV q8hrs, vancomycin 1g q12h). He also has a history of portal vein thrombosis/liver infarct for which he takes Lovenox as an outpatient. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID, Enoxaparin Sodium 100 mg SC Q24H, Lorazepam 0.5 mg PO Q6H:PRN anxiety, OxycoDONE (Immediate Release)  5 mg PO Q4H:PRN pain, Senna 1 TAB PO BID constipation, Ondansetron 8 mg PO Q8H:PRN nausea/vomiting, and Prochlorperazine 10 mg PO Q6H:PRN nausea/vomiting. 

The patient's family history includes his maternal grandfather and grandmother dying from heart disease. His uncle had diabetes and died of heart disease. Another uncle died of lung cancer.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of stage IV cholangiocarcinoma and portal vein thrombosis, was admitted for evaluation of abdominal pain, diarrhea, and fevers. He reported increasing abdominal pain, nausea, decreased oral intake, fevers, and diarrhea over the past two days. He denied any chest pain, dyspnea, vomiting, or dysuria. His last chemotherapy treatment was three days prior to admission. 

Upon admission, his vitals were recorded and labs showed a WBC count of 11.1K with 87% neutrophils, bicarbonate of 18, ALT of 57, AST 46, AP 218, total bilirubin 0.4. Lactate was 1.1. Urinalysis showed 3 WBC, with no bacteria, 6 RBC, small blood. He was administered ciprofloxacin and metronidazole, as well as hydromorphone 2 mg IV x 2. Blood cultures x 2 and urine culture were sent. CT A/P preliminarily showed a liver lesion with narrowing of the left portal vein and focal narrowing of the main portal vein, similar to prior, and stable biliary stents. The small and large bowel loops were without evidence of obstruction, but fluid-filled suggesting possible gastroenteritis. 

The patient has a history of two ERCP procedures, during which biliary stents were placed. Cytology was initially inconclusive but on follow up study showed adenocarcinoma. He was referred for surgical exploration and planned resection, but the procedure was aborted as peritoneal metastases were identified intraoperatively. Biopsy confirmed the finding of metastatic adenocarcinoma morphologically consistent with pancreaticobiliary primary. His postoperative course was complicated by E. coli bacteremia.

The patient's mother has hypertension. His father died with NASH cirrhosis. His brother has no health concerns. He has three children, a son with irritable bowel syndrome and a daughter with a disease. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent PTBD replacement, ERCP, and PICC placement. His abdominal pain, diarrhea, and fever were evaluated with a differential diagnosis of gastroenteritis, cholangitis, biliary infection, and abscess. His condition improved after PTBD replacement, but worsened within 24 hours of the procedure, likely due to instrumentation and spread of infectious source/post-procedure cholangitis. These symptoms resolved within 24 hours of the procedure. Bile cultures during this admission grew 2 strains of E.coli, including one very resistant strain, and sparse enterococcus. Vancomycin was started given concern for PTBD site infection. Blood cultures from admission remain negative. 

His creatinine levels increased to 1.6 from 1.0, likely due to decreased oral intake and large output from biliary drain that is connected to bowel. This improved to 1.2 with IVF and capping of his PTBD. His vancomycin dose was adjusted according to his renal function. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with MDR E. coli Cholangitis. He was prescribed Docusate Sodium, Enoxaparin Sodium, Lorazepam, Ondansetron, OxycoDONE (Immediate Release), Prochlorperazine, Senna, Piperacillin-Tazobactam, and Vancomycin. He was instructed to continue taking IV antibiotics at home, with Vancomycin through a specified date and Zosyn through another specified date. He was discharged home with service.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Stage IV cholangiocarcinoma, was admitted due to fever and abdominal pain. He has been treated with gemcitabine/cisplatin and has had three hospitalizations for cholangitis caused by resistant E. coli bacteremia in the past year. He reported constant right upper quadrant (RUQ) abdominal pain, fevers up to 103.8'F, paler stools, and darker urine. His vitals were stable upon admission. His laboratory studies showed anemia with hemoglobin at 7.8, MCV 106, and elevated liver enzymes with ALT 47, AST 49, LDH 350, and AP 319. A liver ultrasound showed some biliary radicals but no abscesses or fluid collections.

The patient underwent a percutaneous cholangiogram with replacement of the internal-external bile drain. His past medical history includes portal vein thrombosis. His family history includes his mother with hypertension, his father who died from NASH cirrhosis, a son with irritable bowel syndrome, and a daughter with an unspecified disease.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Docusate Sodium, Enoxaparin Sodium, Lorazepam, Ondansetron, Oxycodone, Prochlorperazine, and Senna. He was allergic to Demerol.

Abnormal lab results included a drop in hemoglobin to 7.2, MCV of 103, MCH of 33.9, RDW of 16.5, ALT of 47, AST of 49, LDH of 350, AP of 319, albumin of 3.4, calcium of 7.7, iron of 20, calTIBC of 242, ferritin of 918, TRF of 186, and neutrophils of 73.5. Urine analysis showed RBC of 8 and hyaline of 3. Microbiology results showed mixed bacterial flora in the bile and wound culture, including Enterococcus sp., Klebsiella oxytoca, and Escherichia coli. No growth was observed in the urine and blood cultures.

The patient was diagnosed with recurrent cholangitis. His bile drain was found to be clogged and was replaced. His liver function tests remained normal on the day of discharge, with AP decreasing to 240 from 319 on admission. He was treated with Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5g IV every 8 hours. His anemia was likely due to chronic inflammation, with macrocytosis caused by reticulocytosis. His portal vein thrombosis was managed with Enoxaparin. His cholangiocarcinoma was managed with gemcitabine/cisplatin. He was discharged home with instructions to continue antibiotic therapy until a specified date.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to abdominal pain. He has a history of stage IV cholangiocarcinoma and is currently on gemcitabine/cisplatin. He was previously admitted for cholangitis and was treated with zosyn. During that admission, a cholangiogram showed a clogged biliary drain which was replaced. The patient reported that his pain never fully resolved and has worsened over the past few days, with associated fevers reaching 101.5 at home and nausea without vomiting. 

The patient's past medical history includes cholangiocarcinoma, stage IV, and portal vein thrombosis. His oncologic history reveals that he initially presented with diarrhea, but then became jaundiced, prompting medical evaluation. He underwent two ERCP procedures, during which biliary stents were placed. Cytology was initially inconclusive but follow-up study showed adenocarcinoma. He was referred for surgical exploration and planned resection, but the procedure was aborted due to the identification of peritoneal metastases. His postoperative course was complicated by E. coli bacteremia.

The patient's family history includes his mother with hypertension, his father who died with NASH cirrhosis, and a brother with no health concerns. He has three children, one of whom has irritable bowel syndrome and another with an unspecified disease.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Ursodiol, Docusate Sodium, Ondansetron, Senna, Enoxaparin Sodium, Lorazepam, OxycoDONE (Immediate Release), and Prochlorperazine. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent an IR guided bile duct change catheter procedure. His labs showed several abnormal results including glucose at 104*, ALK PHOS at 173*, WBC at 12.1*#, RBC at 2.99*, HGB at 10.2*, HCT at 31.4*, MCV at 105*, MCH at 34.0*, PLT COUNT at 118*#, and a PTT of 30.9. 

The patient's hospital course included abdominal pain due to a working bile duct catheter, flushing and not obstructed, and stable LFTs. He underwent an IR guided catheter-based balloon sweep of the right hepatic duct stent and exchange of perc bil drain. He was also given antibiotics due to a fever and a WBC count of 12. His cholangiocarcinoma was not involved at this time during hospitalization and he was advised to see his doctor as an outpatient. His portal vein thrombosis was managed with lovenox. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with cholangitis, bile duct cancer, and cholangiocarcinoma. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. He was instructed to follow up with his doctor.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to severe post-prandial abdominal pain. He has a significant medical history of stage IV cholangiocarcinoma metastatic to the peritoneum, for which he had been receiving cisplatin/gemcitabine chemotherapy. His treatment course was complicated by cholangitis, for which he had biliary stents and an external-internal PTBD drain, and right hydronephrosis, for which he had a ureteral stent. He also has a history of portal vein thrombosis and is currently on lovenox. 

The patient reported that the abdominal cramping started three weeks ago, is diffuse, and occurs several hours after meals. He also experienced one episode of vomiting three weeks ago, but now primarily experiences nausea. The pain also wakes him from sleep and is not always clearly associated with eating. He has had no diarrhea or fever, no dark urine or pale stools, and no significantly worsening right upper quadrant pain. He has been using more oxycodone at home, but it is not providing much relief. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be tender in the right upper quadrant but showed no guarding or rebound. A biliary drain was in place with no drainage. His vitals in the clinic were BP: 114/78, Heart Rate: 68, and T 99.1. Lab results showed neutropenia. 

The patient's family history includes a mother with hypertension, a father who died with NASH cirrhosis, a son with irritable bowel syndrome, and a daughter with an unspecified disease. 

Abnormal lab results include: WBC-3.2*, RBC-3.25*, Hgb-10.7*, Hct-33.3*, MCV-103*, MCH-32.9*, MCHC-32.1, RDWSD-50.4*, RBC-3.37*, Hgb-11.6*, Hct-35.0*, MCV-104*, MCH-34.4*, Neuts-25.3*, Lymphs-55.0*, Monos-14.3*, Baso-1.1*, AbsNeut-0.88*, Monos-14.7*, and AlkPhos-163*. 

A right upper quadrant ultrasound showed no significant biliary dilatation or gallstones, but did show a soft tissue lesion at the gallbladder fundus, which may represent local or metastatic disease. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's abdominal pain was thought to be related to small bowel spasm due to metastatic implants or gastritis/peptic ulcer disease. His neutropenia resolved without growth factor support. His chemotherapy was held due to neutropenia. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Enoxaparin Sodium, Ondansetron, OxyCODONE SR, OxycoDONE (Immediate Release), Prochlorperazine, Ursodiol, Docusate Sodium, Polyethylene Glycol, and Senna. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with gastritis or duodenitis, abdominal pain, and cholangiocarcinoma. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to right upper quadrant (RUQ) pain and fever. He has a history of metastatic cholangiocarcinoma and has been experiencing these symptoms for a few days, along with occasional night sweats. He has a chronic percutaneous biliary drain in place, which has remained capped recently. He also reported one episode of non-bloody vomiting the day prior to admission and a mild cough productive of white sputum, which he attributes to allergies. 

The patient underwent two percutaneous cholangiograms with biliary drain exchange procedures. His past medical history includes stage IV cholangiocarcinoma and portal vein thrombosis. His family history includes his mother with hypertension, his father who died with NASH cirrhosis, and a son with irritable bowel syndrome. 

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam showed tenderness to touch in the RUQ, and his biliary drain was in place and capped. His vital signs were stable. His admission labs showed elevated ALT (61), Alk Phos (286), and Total Bilirubin (2.8), along with low Albumin (3.3) and low RBC (2.81), HGB (9.4), HCT (29.1), MCV (104), MCH (33.5), and Platelet count (72). His monocytes were also elevated (20.1). 

The patient's bile culture showed growth of Proteus species, Klebsiella oxytoca, and Enterobacter cloacae complex. An anaerobic culture showed sparse growth of Clostridium perfringens. 

The patient's discharge labs showed continued low RBC (3.60), HGB (12.0), HCT (37.2), MCV (103), MCH (33.3), and RDWSD (54.4), along with elevated Urea Nitrogen (23) and LDH (263). 

The patient's medications upon admission included Enoxaparin Sodium, Lactulose, Lorazepam, Ondansetron, Oxycodone (Immediate Release), Pantoprazole, Prochlorperazine, Ursodiol, Docusate Sodium, Polyethylene Glycol, Senna, and Oxycodone SR (OxyconTIN). 

The patient's primary diagnosis upon discharge was Cholangitis and Biliary Drain Obstruction, with secondary diagnoses of Metastatic cholangiocarcinoma and Portal vein thrombosis. The patient was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to abdominal pain. He has a history of stage 4 cholangiocarcinoma, which includes peritoneal metastases causing colonic narrowing and hydronephrosis requiring ureteral stenting. He has undergone fifty cycles of palliative gemcyabine/cisplatin over the years. He recently had a colonoscopy and an attempted palliative colonic stent, which was unsuccessful. Post-colonoscopy, he experienced cessation of stool and gas, progressive nausea/vomiting, progressive epigastric and RUQ pain, and developed gross hematuria. He placed a drain on his normally capped biliary drain to help treat the abdominal pain. His vitals in the ED were notable for hypokalemia and Cr elevation, decreased platelets. A CT scan of his abdomen showed a distended stomach and no small bowel obstruction.

His past medical history includes portal vein thrombosis, PE, and colonic compression. His family history includes hypertension in his mother, cirrhosis in his father, and throat cancer in his uncle. His medications upon admission included Ondansetron, Prochlorperazine, LORazepam, Lactulose, Docusate Sodium, Senna, Ursodiol, Enoxaparin Sodium, OxyCODONE (Immediate Release), OxyCODONE SR (OxyconTIN), and Pantoprazole.

Abnormal lab results included RBC-2.91*, Hgb-9.4*, Hct-27.5*, MCH-32.3*, RDWSD-49.1*, Plt Ct-37*, Glucose-101*, Creat-1.3*, K-2.7*, AlkPhos-135*, Mg-1.2*, Calcium-7.6*, Phos-2.4*, Blood-MOD*, Protein-100*, Urobiln-2*, Leuks-SM*, RBC->182*, WBC->182*, Bacteri-FEW*, and Color-Red*. Imaging showed a distended stomach suggesting gastric outlet obstruction, sigmoid colon wall thickening, stable intrahepatic biliary dilation with unchanged position of biliary stents, and stable mild right hydronephrosis with unchanged ureteral stent position.

During his hospital stay, he was diagnosed with partial gastric outlet obstruction, abdominal pain, CoNS bacteremia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, thrombocytopenia, hematuria, pyuria, and stage 4 cholangiocarcinoma. He was treated with vancomycin for the CoNS bacteremia, which was later discontinued as it was thought to be a contaminant. His hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia were treated with aggressive repletion and eventually, PO intake. His thrombocytopenia was thought to be due to recent chemotherapy and he was on argatroban gtt while antibodies were pending. He received 1 plt transfusion prior to ERCP but otherwise his platelets rose rapidly without intervention during hospitalization. His hematuria and pyuria were initially treated for possible UTI/pyelonephritis, but it became clear he did not have a UTI when UCx returned negative. His abdominal pain was managed by palliative care who recommended an increase in his Oxycontin to 40mg BID. He was also started on standing qHS Ativan per palliative care recommendations for his insomnia and irritability. His discharge diagnoses included gastric outlet obstruction, colonic stenosis, possible colitis, thrombocytopenia, metastatic cholangiocarcinoma, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, and biliary ductal obstruction.
label: controls

summary:
The male patient was admitted due to abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and abdominal bloating which he attributed to constipation. He had not had a bowel movement for about a week despite taking his home bowel regimen. He has a history of metastatic cholangiocarcinoma to the peritoneum, complicated by multiple biliary obstructions, episodes of cholangitis, and bacteremia. His past medical history also includes portal vein thrombosis, PE, and colonic compression. He has undergone two ERCP procedures, biliary stent placements, and a surgical exploration that was aborted due to the identification of peritoneal metastases. He has been on gemcitabine/cisplatin systemic chemotherapy. 

Upon admission, his vitals were stable and labs were notable for 11.7, 9.3>----<190, 36.0, Na 142, K 3.4, HCO3 27Cr 0.9, and BUN 19. His physical exam showed severe temporal wasting and sunken eyes, dry mucous membranes with multiple missing upper teeth, thin and supple neck, no increased WOB, RRR, no MRG, distended and soft abdomen, cool extremities with pitting edema to knee bilaterally, and dry skin. 

During his stay, he had his biliary T tube replaced and underwent a paracentesis which drained 1100cc. A repeat CT scan showed worsening ascites and anasarca in addition to a developing ileus. His abdominal distention continued with KUB demonstrating air fluid levels. Surgery was consulted but stated there was no surgical option and that he did not have an obstruction. Palliative care was then consulted. 

His lab results showed abnormal levels of WBC-11.0*, RBC-2.65*, Hgb-8.1*, Hct-25.8*, MCHC-31.4*, RDW-15.6*, RDWSD-54.3*, Neuts-72.1*, Lymphs-16.4*, Eos-0.2*, AbsLymp-0.75*, AbsEos-0.01*, Glucose-122*, UreaN-39*, and AlkPhos-151*. 

His medications upon admission included Lactulose, LORazepam, MethylPHENIDATE (Ritalin), Metoclopramide, Ondansetron, OxyCODONE SR (OxyconTIN), OxyCODONE (Immediate Release), Pantoprazole, and Ursodiol. 

The patient's family history includes hypertension in his mother, cirrhosis in his father, and throat cancer in his uncle.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to failure to thrive. His medical history includes stage IV extrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma metastatic to the peritoneum, biliary obstruction, cholangitis, E. coli bacteremia, and portal vein thrombosis. He has undergone two ERCP procedures where biliary stents were placed and cytology showed adenocarcinoma. He was referred for surgical exploration and planned resection, but the procedure was aborted due to the identification of peritoneal metastases. He has been on several chemotherapy regimens including gemcitabine/cisplatin and mFOLFOX. He also had an appendectomy scheduled, but it was not completed due to the extent of the disease. He was recently admitted for coffee ground emesis and leaking L PTBD, found to have non-bleeding esophageal varices. He was started on TPN and improved but this was not continued at home. 

Upon admission, he was on several medications including Bisacodyl, Calcium Carbonate, Folic Acid, Hydromorphone, Methadone, Polyethylene Glycol, Ranitidine, Senna, Thiamine, Lorazepam, Methylphenidate, Ondansetron, Pantoprazole, Ursodiol, and Multivitamins. 

His physical exam revealed cachexia, temporal wasting, discomfort in the RUQ and epigastric area, and generalized weakness. His lab results showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, RDW, RDWSD, Glucose, UreaN, AlkPhos, and Albumin. His Calcium was also low. A CT scan showed a multiloculated, rim-enhancing, fluid collection in hepatic segments IVb/V abutting the collapsed gallbladder, highly concerning for hepatic abscess, possibly secondary to ruptured cholecystitis. There was also mild bilateral hydronephrosis, new nodularity in the dependent portions of the lower lobes, and small volume ascites. 

During his hospital stay, he was transitioned to comfort measures only due to worsening symptoms and overall poor prognosis. His pain regimen was switched to PCA to good effect, and he was discharged on Dilaudid PCA on home hospice. His discharge medications included Dexamethasone, Dronabinol, Haloperidol, Hydromorphone, Lorazepam, Ondansetron, Bisacodyl, Calcium Carbonate, Methylphenidate, Polyethylene Glycol, Ranitidine, and Senna. 

His family history includes his mother with hypertension, his father who died with NASH cirrhosis, and a son with irritable bowel syndrome and a daughter with an unspecified disease.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to left hip pain following a bicycle accident. He was found to have a left hip fracture. His past medical history includes hypertension, mitral regurgitation, hypothyroidism, and prostate cancer. No family history was provided. 

Upon admission, the patient was alert and oriented. His cardiac rhythm was regular, lungs were clear, abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended. He experienced pain in his left hip with range of motion. A physical examination upon discharge showed similar results, with the addition of an incision that was clean, dry, and intact. 

The patient underwent a left hip pinning procedure under spinal anesthesia. Post-procedure, he was extubated and transferred to the recovery room, and then to the floor. He was seen by physical therapy to improve his strength and mobility and was cleared for discharge home with services. 

Lab results showed several abnormal findings. His RBC count was 4.53* and 3.59*, Hgb was 13.0* and 10.5*, Hct was 37.9* and 30.0*, and MCHC was 35.1*. His glucose levels were 110* and 128*, UreaN was 25*, and Calcium and Phos were 7.9* and 1.8* respectively. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Flomax 0.4mg daily, Synthroid, K-Dur 20meq daily, Triamterene/HCTZ daily, Vitamin B12, and MVI. 

The patient was discharged in stable condition with instructions to continue to be touchdown weight bearing on his left leg, continue his lovenox injections for a total of 4 weeks after surgery, and resume his home medications as prescribed by his doctor. He was also advised to seek medical attention if he noticed any increased redness, drainage, or swelling, or if he had a temperature greater than 101.5. His discharge diagnosis was a left hip fracture post a bicyclist accident.

summary:
The patient is a male with a history of hypertension (HTN), hyperlipidemia (HLD), hypothyroidism, prostate cancer, and a recently diagnosed lung mass. He presented with a chief complaint of headache, described as a 'head pressure' that has been ongoing for months but has worsened in the last few weeks. Associated symptoms include memory loss, increased confusion, poor mobility, trouble swallowing, loss of appetite, bilateral arm paresthesias, and unintentional 8-lb weight loss over the last few weeks. He had previously presented to outpatient providers after having several months of night sweats. A chest X-ray showed a mass, and a subsequent chest CT showed a spiculated mass within the right middle lobe with central necrosis. A PET-CT showed FDG avid mass consistent with malignancy. 

The patient underwent an EBUS with biopsy. He also had a brain MRI scheduled due to his headache symptoms. His sodium level was found to be low and he was instructed to limit fluid intake to >1L a day and to increase sodium liquids. The patient reported that this low PO intake has caused him to lose weight. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was alert, oriented x3, and appeared chronically ill. Temporal wasting was noted. His vitals were stable and no focal deficits were noted. 

Lab results showed a low sodium level of 130, which increased to 131 after IVF. His WBC was 4.3, Hgb 11.6, Hct 33.7, and PLT 205. His urine osmolality was 316 and blood osmolality was 274. His RBC was 4.10, Hgb 11.4, Hct 34.4, and glucose 114. 

The patient's CT head showed no acute intracranial changes. His MRI brain showed no evidence of intracranial metastatic disease or acute infarct or intracranial hemorrhage. His CXR showed a right lower lung mass. 

The patient's family history includes a mother with CVA and a father with MI. His medications upon admission included Levothyroxine Sodium, Zolpidem Tartrate, amLODIPine, lisinopril-hydrochlorothiazide, Simvastatin, and Escitalopram Oxalate. 

The patient's lung mass is likely due to new lung cancer, as evidenced by his weight loss, night sweats, and FDG-avid lung mass and adenopathy on right. His hyponatremia is likely due to SIADH, worsened by thiazide use. His chronic problems include HTN, HLD, hypothyroidism, major depression, and insomnia. His biopsy results are pending on discharge.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of right lower lobe organizing pneumonia, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, hypothyroidism, prostate cancer, depression, GERD, and mitral regurgitation, presented with altered mental status and confusion. He had been on high-dose steroids following a diagnosis of cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (COP). He had also been experiencing hand tremors and personality changes, including anxiety, obsessive-compulsive disorder, and confusion. His wife reported that he had become quieter, more obsessive, and had trouble keeping track of tasks. He had lost interest in hobbies and had difficulty using a telephone due to tremors. He also had gait instability. His mental status changes and tremors were thought to be due to the high-dose prednisone. 

During a prior hospital stay, he had a MRI head scan that showed no evidence of intracranial metastatic disease, acute infarct, or intracranial hemorrhage. In the emergency department, his vital signs were stable and he was well-appearing with no focal deficits. He was not oriented to time but was oriented to location and person. His labs showed a hemoglobin and hematocrit of 12 and 37.4 respectively, and a platelet count of 124. His chest X-ray showed no evidence of pneumonia but did show an improving opacification within the right mid lung, representing an underlying lung mass with resolving post biopsy hemorrhage. His EKG showed a normal sinus rhythm with occasional bigeminy. 

Upon admission, the patient was able to complete full sentences but seemed hesitant to disclose answers. He reported feeling 'delusional,' 'can't remember,' 'can't think correctly,' and that 'everything hurts.' He also reported a burning pain with urination and generalized weakness. His physical exam showed no jaundice, full strength in all extremities, and a tremor that worsened with activation. He was anxious and depressed. 

His lab results showed a white blood cell count of 8.0, red blood cell count of 4.17, hemoglobin of 12.1, hematocrit of 37.4, and platelet count of 124. His neutrophils were 81.7, lymphocytes were 11.0, and eosinophils were 0.0. His glucose was 115 and urea nitrogen was 36. His imaging showed resolving right middle lobe consolidation, and his EEG showed a mildly slow background with occasional bursts of generalized slowing, indicative of a mild to moderate encephalopathy affecting both cortical and subcortical structures. His MRI brain scan showed no acute intracranial abnormality and no enhancing mass. His cerebrospinal fluid studies showed a total protein of 100 and 111, and a glucose of 87. His ANCA, HIV antibody, and IgG were all negative. His complement C3 was 85 and C4 was 20. His discharge labs showed a white blood cell count of 4.6, red blood cell count of 3.74, hemoglobin of 11.4, hematocrit of 33.5, and platelet count of 124. His glucose was 116 and urea nitrogen was 31. 

The patient's mental status changes were initially thought to be due to steroid-induced psychosis with underlying neuropsychiatric issues. However, he also had a component of delirium given his lack of attention and fluctuating symptoms. His steroids were tapered down and he was started on seroquel with improvement in behavior, but not memory loss. His tremor was thought to be due to the steroids and was expected to improve with reduction in steroids. His hypertension was controlled and his hypothyroidism was managed with levothyroxine. He was discharged to an extended care facility with a diagnosis of steroid-induced psychosis and other encephalopathy of unclear etiology. He was instructed to follow up for outpatient neuropsychiatric testing.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a male with a history of COPD, prostate adenocarcinoma (treated with external beam treatment and Lupron), seropositive rheumatoid arthritis (on Methotrexate and sulfsalazine), degenerative joint disease in both shoulders (received a cortisol shot a month ago), schizophrenia (on risperidal and prozac), recurrent UTIs post prostate cancer radiation (on daily Macrobid), left lower lobe nodules (followed by Pulm and serial CTs), and GERD (on omeprazole). He presented with increased oxygen saturation requirement, despite not usually requiring O2. He reported fatigue but denied shortness of breath, fevers/chills, or increased cough. He also reported a 50lbs weight gain over the past year and occasional drenching night sweats over the past two months. 

Upon admission, the patient's vitals were Temp F98.6, BP118/58, HR96, R20, O2-sat 88-91% 4LNC. Physical examination revealed decreased breath sounds over the right lung fields, +rhonchi at b/l bases, +wheezes over b/l upper lung fields, no dullness to percussion, and mild clubbing in hands. 

Lab results showed abnormal WBC-11.2*, RBC-4.04*, Hgb-13.6*, Hct-39.4*, MCH-33.7*, Neuts-87.6*, Lymphs-6.0*, and Glucose-112*. Imaging revealed right lower lobe pneumonia and bibasilar atelectasis. A video swallow evaluation showed no evidence of aspiration during eating. 

The patient was initially thought to have hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP) and was placed on broad coverage with cefepime, vancomycin, and levofloxacin. However, repeat CXR showed rapid resolution of infiltrate, suggesting transient mucus plugging with chronic cough/ oropharyngeal aspiration as the more likely cause. This was confirmed by a CTA which showed multifocal bilateral consolidations consistent with aspiration. 

The patient was treated for a COPD exacerbation with a five-day prednisone taper of 40mg daily, a seven-day course of levofloxacin, duonebs, and advair. His respiratory status improved and supplemental oxygen was weaned to 1 L. 

Other chronic issues include lung nodules and RLL infiltrate, rheumatoid arthritis, GERD, right shoulder pain, schizophrenia/depression, recurrent UTI, and a history of adenocarcinoma. 

Upon discharge, the patient's primary diagnoses were mucus plugging and COPD exacerbation, with secondary diagnoses of rheumatoid arthritis, schizophrenia, recurrent UTIs, history of prostate cancer, and GERD. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was able to get out of bed with assistance to a chair or wheelchair. His discharge medications included docusate sodium, finasteride, fluticasone-salmeterol, fluoxetine, risperidone, ranitidine HCl, sulfasalazine, guaifenesin, levofloxacin, methotrexate sodium, Claritin, Macrobid, folic acid, Vitamin D-3, and azelastine.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a male with a past medical history of prostate cancer, rheumatoid arthritis, schizophrenia, and emphysema. He presented with fever, cough, and dyspnea. He reported chills and dyspnea at his nursing home prior to admission. He was found to be febrile and hypoxic, so oxygen was started. He reported having sick contacts at his nursing home and a cough productive of yellow sputum. He also reported increasing pain from urination for the past two months, located in his penis and around his prostate. He denied hematuria but reported feeling nauseous without vomiting and a mild headache. 

In the ED, his vitals showed a temperature of 100.7, heart rate of 125, and oxygen saturation of 91% on 6L Nasal Cannula, which improved prior to admission. His chest X-ray showed a right lower lobe infiltrate and his EKG showed sinus tachycardia with no ischemia. His white blood cell count was elevated at 11.6 with 95% neutrophils and his lactate was 2.5. He was given 1L of normal saline, nebulizer treatments, levofloxacin 750, and vancomycin 1 gram and was admitted to the floor. 

His past medical history includes prostate adenocarcinoma, seropositive rheumatoid arthritis, COPD, schizophrenia, recurrent UTIs after prostate cancer radiation, left lower lobe nodules, and GERD. His family history includes diabetes mellitus but no cancers. 

Upon admission, his physical exam showed clear lungs with good air movement and crackles at the right base, a non-displaced PMI with tachycardia but regular rhythm, and no suprapubic tenderness. His extremities showed trace edema in both legs without redness or swelling, badly deformed hands and feet with ulnar deviations, and chronic bilateral elbow bursitis. 

His admission labs showed an elevated lactate of 2.1, glucose of 137, urea nitrogen of 22, white blood cell count of 11.6, red blood cell count of 3.97, MCV of 104, MCH of 35.6, and neutrophils of 95.2. His urine showed large leukocytes, 18 red blood cells, and few white blood cell clumps. His urine culture showed yeast and Corynebacterium species. His chest X-ray showed recurrent right upper and bilateral lower lobe pneumonia. 

During his hospital stay, he was diagnosed with bacterial pneumonia and was started on empiric antibiotics. His lactate trended down and his oxygen requirement resolved. He also had microscopic hematuria and bacteriuria but no UTI. His hypoxemia was attributed to his pneumonia in the setting of baseline emphysema. His rheumatoid arthritis was managed with tramadol and Tylenol for pain control. His prostate cancer is currently in remission with undetectable PSA levels. His hyperlipidemia was managed with aspirin and atorvastatin. His schizophrenia and depression were managed with fluoxetine and risperidone. His macular degeneration was managed with multivitamins and his GERD was managed with omeprazole. 

Upon discharge, his mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was able to get out of bed with assistance to a chair or wheelchair. His discharge diagnoses were pneumonia, rheumatoid arthritis, and COPD. His discharge instructions included completing a 5-day course of antibiotics and getting a repeat chest X-ray in 6 weeks to ensure his pneumonia has resolved. He was also instructed to take all his medications as prescribed and keep all follow-up appointments.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of COPD, presented to the ED from a SNF with an increased cough, mucous production, and a fever of 100.6. He reported that his symptoms were similar to the pneumonia he was hospitalized for last year, but not as severe. He was given IV steroids, nebulizers, Vanco, and ZOsyn in the ED. His past medical history includes prostate adenocarcinoma, for which he underwent external beam treatment and Lupron a few years ago, seropositive rheumatoid arthritis, degenerative joint disease in both shoulders, schizophrenia, recurrent UTIs after prostate cancer radiation, left lower lobe nodules, and GERD. His family history includes diabetes mellitus, but no cancers.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to have occasional diffuse expiratory wheezing, good air movement, and a cough triggered by coughing or exertion. His hands showed severe distortion from known rheumatoid arthritis. His vital signs were T: 97.9, BP: 142/83, HR: 94, RR: 20, O2sat: 94%/3L NC.

Lab results showed an elevated glucose level of 121, and abnormal RBC, HGB, HCT, MCV, MCH, NEUTS, and LYMPHS values. A chest X-ray indicated recurrent lower lobe pneumonia.

During his hospital stay, the patient's pneumonia and COPD exacerbation improved significantly with levofloxacin 750 mg, steroids, and an increase in guaifenison and nebulizer treatments. His rheumatoid arthritis, prostate cancer, hyperlipidemia, schizophrenia, depression, and GERD were managed with their respective medications.

Upon admission, the patient was on a variety of medications including Risperidone, Tamsulosin, TraMADOL, Ipratropium Bromide Neb, Albuterol, Acapella, Atorvastatin, Senna, Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus, azelastine, Azo Cranberry + Probiotic, Docusate Sodium, Guaifenesin, Omeprazole, Methotrexate, Aspirin, BuPROPion, Acetaminophen, Finasteride, Fluoxetine, FoLIC Acid, Nitrofurantoin Monohyd, Polyethylene Glycol, Tiotropium Bromide, Cyanocobalamin, and Vitamin D.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of bacterial pneumonia and an acute exacerbation of COPD. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive, but required assistance or aid for ambulation. He was discharged to an extended care facility.

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of COPD, schizophrenia, rheumatoid arthritis (RA), prostate cancer, and sleep apnea, was admitted due to progressive dyspnea and hypoxia. He has a history of recurrent pneumonias and was recently treated for pneumonia with azithromycin. The patient reported continued progression in dyspnea and increased oxygen requirement. He denied fevers/chills but reported a productive cough, poor appetite, and fatigue. He was sent in by his nurse practitioner due to dyspnea because a recent echocardiogram showed an ejection fraction (EF) of approximately 10% with a probable apical clot. 

Upon admission, the patient was given vancomycin, levofloxacin, methylprednisolone, and started on a heparin drip. A chest x-ray showed a large right-sided opacity. An EKG showed sinus rhythm with premature atrial contractions (PACs) and premature ventricular contractions (PVCs), left axis deviation, and ST depressions/T wave inversions in lateral leads. The patient was transferred to the floor due to sustained hypoxia in the setting of intense coughing and sputum production. He was given 40mg IV lasix and started on duonebs. 

The patient's past medical history includes hypertension, prostate adenocarcinoma, seropositive rheumatoid arthritis, COPD, schizophrenia, recurrent UTIs after prostate cancer radiation, left lower lobe nodules, and GERD. His family history does not include early myocardial infarction, arrhythmia, cardiomyopathies, or sudden cardiac death. 

Abnormal lab results include RBC count of 4.09, Hgb of 13.6, MCV of 110, MCH of 33.2, MCHC of 30.1, Neuts of 81.6, Lymphs of 9.3, Glucose of 130, HCO3 of 37, proBNP of 7623, and PTT of 150. The patient's arterial blood gas showed a pO2 of 68, pCO2 of 72, pH of 7.36, and calTCO2 of 42. 

The patient underwent coronary catheterization and thoracentesis. The coronary angiography showed normal coronary arteries. The patient's pleural effusions were tapped and appeared transudative, likely due to heart failure. The patient was diuresed to euvolemia and a chest tube was placed with adequate drainage and improvement in dyspnea. 

The patient was diagnosed with acute systolic heart failure, COPD, mucus plugging, hypoxia, and rheumatoid arthritis. He was started on metoprolol XL 50 QDay, furosemide 40 QDay, lisinopril 5mg QDay, and warfarin at 2mg. The patient was discharged to extended care.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) requiring oxygen, schizophrenia, Rheumatoid Arthritis (RA), prostate cancer, and sleep apnea, was admitted due to hypoxemic respiratory failure and shortness of breath. He had multiple previous admissions due to pneumonia. In the emergency department, he presented with shortness of breath and no urine output for three days. Initial vitals showed hypoxemia and a blood gas analysis revealed abnormal values. Lab results showed elevated BUN/Cr (68/1.7), WBC (12 with 96% Neutrophils), and lactate (4.5). Chest x-ray showed extensive consolidation in the right lung, indicative of pneumonia. He was given a dose of Vancomycin, Cefepime, and Levofloxacin, and was intubated due to worsening respiratory status. A bronchoscopy showed thick sputum.

The patient's past medical history includes hypertension, prostate adenocarcinoma (treated with external beam treatment and Lupron), seropositive rheumatoid arthritis (treated with Methotrexate and sulfsalazine), COPD, schizophrenia (treated with Abilify and Prozac), recurrent urinary tract infections (UTIs) after prostate cancer radiation (treated with Macrobid), left lower lobe nodules, and Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD, treated with Omeprazole). There is no family history of early myocardial infarction, arrhythmia, cardiomyopathies, or sudden cardiac death.

Upon admission, the patient was intubated and sedated. Physical examination revealed decreased breath sounds at bilateral bases, regular heart rate and rhythm, and a soft, non-distended abdomen. The patient was on multiple medications upon admission, including Acetaminophen, Aripiprazole, Aspirin, Bupropion, Cyanocobalamin, Docusate Sodium, Finasteride, Fluoxetine, Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus, Folic Acid, Guaifenesin ER, Ipratropium-Albuterol Neb, Lorazepam, Nitrofurantoin Monohyd, Omeprazole, Polyethylene Glycol, Senna, Tamsulosin, Tiotropium Bromide, Tramadol, Vitamin D, Warfarin, Acidophilus Probiotic, Azelastine, CertaVite with Lutein, Cranberry, Fleet Enema, Fluticasone Propionate NASAL, Loratadine, Methotrexate, Sulfazine EC, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Lisinopril, Furosemide, Atorvastatin, and Tadalafil.

During his hospital stay, the patient was found to be in septic shock with E.coli growing in sputum and urine. Despite maximal support, antibiotics, and therapy, the patient unfortunately passed away. His hospital course was complicated by hypoxemic respiratory failure due to pneumonia and possibly exacerbated by severe COPD and heart failure. He also had septic shock due to E.coli UTI and pneumonia. His renal function worsened, likely due to hypotension and Acute Tubular Necrosis (ATN), and he was started on Continuous Venovenous Hemofiltration (CVVH). He also had elevated INR and atrial fibrillation, likely due to chronic antibiotic use and multiple medication interactions. His INR was managed with IV Vitamin K and heparin.

summary:
The patient, a white male, was admitted for surgical procedures including excision of a scar, left inguinal hernia repair with mesh, umbilical hernia repair open, and incisional hernia repair with mesh x 2. These procedures were performed due to his history of open colectomy for diverticular disease. His past medical history includes diverticulitis. No family history was noted in the report. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking herbal supplements and valtrex. During his hospital stay, an epidural was placed for pain management, which was discontinued on post-operative day 1 and replaced with demerol PCA. The patient was initially kept on sips and then advanced to clears. His pain management was later switched to oral percocet. 

The patient's physical examination showed normal temperature, heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, and oxygen saturation. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended with positive bowel sounds. His abdominal dressings were clean, dry, and intact. No pertinent results were noted in the report. 

The patient experienced some nausea when his diet was progressed from sips to clears. His discharge diagnosis was open incisional hernia repair and his condition upon discharge was good. He was instructed to refrain from driving or operating machinery due to the drowsiness caused by his pain medications. He was also advised to contact his doctor or return to the emergency room if he experienced any new or worsening symptoms. 

The patient was advised to avoid heavy lifting for 6 weeks and to resume moderate exercise at his discretion, excluding abdominal exercises. He was instructed to shower only, avoid tub baths or swimming, and to cover any clear drainage from his incisions with clean, dry gauze. He was also advised to remove any remaining steri-strips a certain number of days after surgery and to contact the doctor if he experienced increased pain, swelling, redness, or drainage from the incision sites. A follow-up appointment with Dr. ___ was scheduled for a couple of weeks after discharge.
