The patient also reported left upper quadrant pain, chronic constipation, and urinary frequency. He had a single instance of blood streaking in his cough on the first day of symptoms. The patient's initial vital signs in the Emergency Department (ED) were notable for tachypnea and diffuse wheezing/coarse crackles. Lab results showed an elevated Cr of 1.6, anion gap of 14, and a potassium level of 3.7 after initial gross hemolysis. The flu swab was negative. Imaging showed mild peribronchial cuffing consistent with small airway disease and possible left lower lobe congestion. The patient received Duonebs, 60 mg prednisone, 2 g magnesium, and ceft/azithro.

The patient's family history includes his father who had a myocardial infarction and his brother who has heart failure. The patient's physical exam upon admission showed tachycardia, diffuse inspiratory and expiratory wheezing, and trace bilateral lower extremity edema. The patient's discharge physical exam showed interval improvement in wheezing over the last 24 hours.

Admission labs showed abnormal values for urea nitrogen (24), Cr (1.6), potassium (6.8), total CO2 (21), anion gap (21), and hemoglobin (13.4). Discharge labs showed elevated white blood cell count (10.5, 12.7, 12.3), red blood cell count (4.40, 4.43, 4.30), hemoglobin (12.9, 12.8, 12.7), hematocrit (39.3, 39.9, 39.0), glucose (123, 105, 115), urea nitrogen (33, 36, 32, 33, 29), and Cr (1.7, 1.4, 1.5, 1.5, 1.4). Imaging studies showed mild peribronchial cuffing and no evidence of pneumonia on chest X-ray. CT chest without contrast showed moderate bronchial wall thickening, likely inflammatory, and mild mucous plugging in the lower lungs. There were also a few small nodules in the right lower lobe, with the largest measuring 0.6 cm.

The patient was treated for a suspected Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) exacerbation given bronchitis. He was placed on prednisone and azithromycin with standing ipratropium-albuterol nebulizers. Due to minimal improvement, he was transitioned to IV methylprednisolone, which he received for three days with significant improvement. He was also initiated on Advair, received saline nebulizers, standing Mucinex, and Tessalon Perles. His peak flow improved from 160 to 190 on discharge with some residual wheeze. The patient also had acute renal failure on top of his CKD, with a Cr of 1.6 upon presentation, which improved with oral fluids and discontinuation of Lasix. His home Lasix was discontinued as the indication was for lower extremity edema and given his CKD, the risk was felt to outweigh the benefit.

The patient's medications upon admission included Carbidopa-Levodopa, Simvastatin, Fluticasone Propionate nasal spray, Furosemide, Omeprazole, Entacapone, and Artificial Tears. The patient was discharged home with services from a facility. His discharge diagnosis included asthma exacerbation vs acute bronchitis, acute renal failure on CKD, Progressive Supranuclear palsy, and Gastroesophageal reflux disease.
label: controls

summary:
The male patient was admitted to the neurosurgery service following a motor vehicle accident (MVA) that resulted in a left frontal laceration and a black eye. A few weeks after the accident, he noticed left periorbital swelling, which was initially treated with erythromycin drops. However, the swelling recurred, accompanied by proptosis and left retroorbital pain. He denied any fever, nausea/vomiting, loss of vision, blurry or double vision. His past medical history was unremarkable and family history was non-contributory. 

Upon admission, the patient was awake, alert, and cooperative. He had left periorbital swelling and erythema, mild proptosis, and chemosis. His pupils were equally round and reactive to light, and visual fields were full to confrontation. Extraocular movements were intact except for a discrete limitation in extreme upward gaze on the left. Facial strength and sensation were intact and symmetric. Motor examination showed normal bulk and tone bilaterally, full power, and no abnormal movements or tremors. Sensation was intact to light touch bilaterally. 

The patient underwent a left frontal craniotomy for orbital/retro-orbital exploration and mass resection/decompression. An MRI revealed a 2.8 x 2.7 x 2.1-cm lesion within the left retroorbital space and middle cranial fossa, adjacent soft tissues, laterally and into infratemporal fossa, and preseptal soft tissues, with osseous destruction involving the sphenoid wing and intraorbital extension causing mass effect upon the rectus muscles, optic nerve, and globe. 

Lab results on admission showed an elevated MCV (81) and MCHC (36.0). At 02:25AM, the patient's NEUTS were elevated (72.3), HCT was low (38.9), MCV was elevated (81), MCHC was elevated (36.0), and CRP was elevated (14.4). His glucose level was also high (135). On discharge, the patient's RBC (3.58), Hgb (10.5), Hct (29.1), MCV (81), MCHC (36.0), PTT (21.0), and INR (2.0) were all abnormal. 

Imaging studies showed post-surgical changes in the left retro-orbital region, with pneumocephalus and a thin rim of hyperdense material consistent with blood products. There was a small subdural hemorrhage in the left frontal and parietal regions. There was no significant intraparenchymal hemorrhage, edema, or mass effect. Ventricles, sulci, and cisterns were unchanged in size and appearance. There was no shift of normally midline structures. Residual osseous destruction was seen involving the left lateral orbit and left frontal bone. 

The patient was diagnosed with a left retroorbital mass and ethmoid fracture. Preliminary pathology reports suggested Langerhans cell histiocytosis. The patient was also diagnosed with a right upper extremity deep vein thrombosis (DVT), for which he was started on aspirin and heparin. His PICC line was discontinued. 

The patient's medications upon admission included antibiotics, possibly ciprofloxacin.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the orthopedics department due to left midfoot arthritis and forefoot deformity. The patient underwent a major surgical procedure which included a left Strayer, left midfoot fusion, left MTP fusion, and left hammertoe corrections. The patient's hospital course was uneventful, with no issues reported on the day of discharge. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Lisinopril 10 mg, BuPROPion XL 300 mg, Dolutegravir 50 mg, emtricitab-rilpivir-tenofo ala 200-25-25 mg, and Citalopram 40 mg, all taken orally daily. The patient has a known allergy to Penicillins.

The physical examination revealed that the patient's splint was clean, dry, and intact. The patient was able to move all toes and showed no abnormal symptoms. The patient's toes were warm with good perfusion.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of left foot deformity. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. However, he required assistance or aid for ambulation, such as a walker or cane. The patient was instructed to keep the splint/dressing on until follow-up, keep the splint clean and dry at all times, and to wean off of narcotics. He was also advised to take aspirin for 2 weeks. Physical therapy was recommended, with instructions to remain non-weight bearing on the left lower extremity and to leave the splint on until follow-up. 

The patient's family history was not provided in the clinical note.
label: controls

summary:
The male patient was admitted due to drainage from multiple incision sites on his left foot. He had previously undergone a left Strayer procedure, left midfoot fusion, left MTP fusion, and left hammertoe corrections, but experienced multiple areas of wound breakdown. The patient's medical history includes sleep apnea, HIV infection, Hepatitis C, and depression. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Bupropion XL, Citalopram, Dolutegravir, Emtricitab-rilpivir-tenofo ala, Ibuprofen, Lisinopril, Oxycodone, and Ranitidine. He is allergic to Penicillins. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a left foot I&D and removal of hardware due to post-operative infection in his left foot. Post-surgery, he was started on prophylactic IV Vancomycin, and later switched to Flagyl PO and Ancef 2g IV. He also received Aspirin for DVT prophylaxis. His pain was managed with oral medications. 

Lab results showed abnormal levels of Hgb (9.9), Hct (33.8), MCV (67), MCH (19.5), MCHC (29.3), RDW (19.9), NRBC (0.2), AbsLymp (3.78), CD3 (3428), CD8 (2883), CD4/CD8 (0.19), UreaN (36), Cl (109), and HIV1 VL (1.6). The patient's tissue culture tested positive for Staph Aureus Coag. 

The patient was discharged in stable condition with a diagnosis of left foot infection. He was advised to be non-weight bearing on the operative extremity. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He required assistance or aid for ambulation.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of dementia, coronary artery disease status post remote coronary artery bypass graft, chronic kidney disease, hypertension, noninsulin dependent diabetes mellitus, and hypercholesterolemia, was admitted to the neurosurgery service following a fall. He had multiple recent falls as per family reports. A CT scan of the brain without contrast revealed a left-sided acute on chronic subdural hematoma with a 4mm midline shift. He was intubated for transport and brought in for neurosurgical evaluation. 

Upon admission, the patient's Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) was 9T. He was intubated, opened his eyes to voice, made no sounds, and localized to painful stimuli. His physical exam showed multiple abrasions on his knees, decreased bulk and normal tone bilaterally, bilateral tremors, and purposeful movement of all extremities but he did not follow commands. 

The patient underwent a craniotomy for evacuation of the subdural hematoma. Post-operative CT scans showed expected changes and a stable right hemispheric subdural hematoma. The patient self-extubated and maintained his airway well. His neurological exam was noted to be stable to improving each day following extubation, though limited by baseline dementia with superimposed delirium. 

The patient's dementia was managed with home medications Namenda and Depakote. He intermittently required hydralazine to maintain systolic blood pressure below 160 while NPO. Once he tolerated oral intake, he was restarted on home medication Losartan. He was evaluated for dysphagia and his diet was advanced from NPO to nectar/puree with aspiration precautions/ 1:1 assist. 

Upon discharge, the patient was oriented to self only, had briskly reactive pupils bilaterally, and showed purposeful movement with upper extremities but brisk withdrawal to noxious stimuli with lower extremities. He had expressive aphasia. 

The patient's medications upon admission included ASA 81mg, Citalopram 20mg daily, Dilvalproex, Atorvastatin 20mg daily, Colace, Januvia 50mg daily, Losartan 50mg daily, Memantine5mg QHS, and Metoprolol 25mg daily. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of left subdural hematoma with cerebral compression. His mental status was confused and he required maximum assistance for out of bed activities. He was discharged to an extended care facility for rehabilitation.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female of undisclosed age, was admitted to the orthopedics department due to a chronic dislocation and ulcer in her right ankle. The patient had injured her ankle a year prior and developed a small ulceration that grew into a large wound with white drainage. She had been treated with a walking boot and a course of Keflex, and her wound care included Betadine. She had been using a wheelchair for the past year and reported no fevers or chills. 

Her past medical history includes diabetes mellitus (DM), hyperlipidemia (HLD), hypertension (HTN), and gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD). She is a nonsmoker. Upon physical examination, she was found to be in no acute distress (NAD) with normal work of breathing (WOB). Her right lower extremity was in a knee immobilizer with an incision that was clean, dry, and intact. Sensation was intact over her proximal tibia and thigh. 

Lab results showed abnormal white blood cell count (10.4), red blood cell count (2.57), hemoglobin (8.0), hematocrit (25.0), and red cell distribution width standard deviation (53.2). 

During her hospital stay, she underwent a right below-knee amputation (BKA) and was stable post-operation. She was initially given IV fluids and IV pain medications, and progressed to a regular diet and oral medications by post-operative day 1. She was also given antibiotics and anticoagulation as per routine. Her home medications were continued throughout her hospitalization. 

Upon admission, her medications included simvastatin, metformin, amlodipine, atenolol, lisinopril, and Prilosec OTC. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of chronic dislocation and ulcer in her right ankle. At the time of discharge, her pain was well controlled with oral medications, her incisions were clean, dry, and intact, and she was voiding and moving bowels spontaneously. She was non-weight bearing on her right lower extremity and was to be discharged on Lovenox for deep vein thrombosis (DVT) prophylaxis. She was deemed ready for discharge and was to follow up with her doctor as per routine.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of vitamin D deficiency and seasonal allergies, was admitted due to right hip pain. She had fallen while roller-skating five days prior to admission, landing on her right hip. The patient reported progressive pain in the right hip and had been using crutches. She had been taking Oxycodone and Ibuprofen for pain management. No paresthesias were reported. The patient's past medical history also includes a tonsillectomy. Her family history was noncontributory.

Upon physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented with vitals of 97.0 80 116/72 18 100%. Both upper extremities were intact with no deformity, erythema, edema, induration, or ecchymosis. The right lower extremity showed pain with active and passive range of motion (AROM/PROM) of the hip, while the left lower extremity was soft and non-tender with full, painless AROM/PROM of hip, knee, and ankle.

The patient underwent a closed reduction with percutaneous pinning of the right femoral neck due to a fracture. Post-operation, the patient was given IV fluids and IV pain medications, and progressed to a regular diet and oral medications by post-operative day 1. The patient's home medications were continued throughout the hospitalization. The patient's hospital course was otherwise unremarkable.

At the time of discharge, the patient's pain was well controlled with oral medications, incisions were clean/dry/intact, and the patient was voiding/moving bowels spontaneously. The patient was touch-down weight-bearing in the right lower extremity.

The patient was not on any medications upon admission. However, during her stay, she was prescribed Acetaminophen, Bisacodyl, Docusate Sodium, Enoxaparin Sodium, OxycoDONE (Immediate Release), and Senna.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of a right femoral neck fracture. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was able to ambulate independently. The patient was discharged home and was instructed to follow up with her primary care doctor and surgeon's team.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female with a history of bipolar disorder and spinal stenosis. She was admitted to the psychiatry unit following self-inflicted facial wounds during a fight with her daughter. The patient reported feeling distressed and contemplated suicide, but decided against it, reflecting on her father's suicide years ago. She lives with her chronically ill daughter and granddaughter, which she finds stressful. The patient's daughter reported that her mother's mental health has been on a downward slope, with increased agitation and depressive symptoms. 

The patient's past medical history includes hypertension, esophagus condition, hypothyroidism, sleep apnea, spinal stenosis, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), glaucoma, hearing loss, and chronic episodic chest pain. She had been diabetic but this resolved with her weight loss. She has allergies to Klonopin, Neurontin, Penicillins, Pravastatin, Statins-Hmg-Coa Reductase Inhibitors, and Tricor. 

The patient's family history includes her father's suicide and sisters with depression. Her daughter also suffers from anxiety. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus, Aspirin, Buspirone, Cyclobenzaprine, Fish Oil, Levothyroxine Sodium, Lithium Carbonate SR, Lorazepam, Omeprazole, Quetiapine Fumarate, and Valacyclovir. 

The patient's physical exam revealed scratches on her face, and she was alert and attentive but appeared anxious and dysphoric. Her cognitive exam showed some errors in attention and memory tasks. 

Lab results showed an abnormal glucose level of 116 and a TSH level of less than 0.02. Other lab results including sodium, potassium, chloride, CO2, BUN, creatinine, calcium, magnesium, WBC, HGB, HCT, MCV, RDW, and platelet count were within normal ranges. Urine tests for various substances were negative. 

During her hospital stay, the patient's mood and affect stabilized, and she denied depressive symptoms and suicidal ideation. Her medication regimen was adjusted, and she responded well to the changes. Her thyroid dysfunction was considered a possible contributing factor to her mood and affect, and her synthroid dose was adjusted. She was also given a lidocaine patch for pain management due to her spinal stenosis and omeprazole for her esophagus condition. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with bipolar disorder, current episode mixed. She was instructed to follow up with all outpatient appointments, continue all medications as directed, avoid abusing alcohol and drugs, and contact her outpatient psychiatrist or other providers if she had any concerns.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female with a past medical history significant for dementia, hypertension, cholangitis caused by klebsiella bacteremia, and a history of mastectomy for breast cancer, colon cancer treated surgically, and bilateral hip replacements. She was admitted due to abdominal pain and was found to have acute cholecystitis. She had a percutaneous cholecystostomy placement and mechanical intubation as major surgical procedures during her stay. 

Upon admission, her vitals were T98.6 HR70 BP106/63 RR14 O292% RA and labs showed a WBC count of 17.7 and elevated BNP. She was intubated in the Emergency Department due to hypoxia and altered mental status. A right internal jugular central venous line was also placed. A percutaneous cholecystostomy drain was placed under ultrasound guidance, which was notable for difficult placement but did return bilious drainage. 

Her physical exam on admission showed she was intubated and sedated, with clear lungs, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops in the cardiovascular system, and a soft, non-distended abdomen with a percutaneous cholecystostomy drain in place. Her skin showed no rash and she was sedated neurologically. 

Lab results on admission showed elevated WBC, Neuts, Bands, AbsNeut, AbsLymp, AbsMono, Glucose, UreaN, Creat, and Lactate levels. Albumin and HCO3 were low. On discharge, her RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCH, MCHC, RDW, RDWSD, Glucose, and Creat were abnormal. 

Imaging studies showed low lung volumes with bibasilar atelectasis, distended, thick-walled gallbladder with a small amount of pericholecystic fluid, findings compatible with acute cholecystitis, and hepatic steatosis. The tip of the NG tube was in the distal esophagus, and there was appropriate position of the percutaneous cholecystostomy tube with interval decompression of the gallbladder. There was also interval increase of bibasal subsegmental atelectasis. 

During her hospital stay, she presented with acute cholecystitis and sepsis, received broad-spectrum antibiotics and fluid resuscitation with norepinephrine, and developed hypoxia which required intubation. She also had a single episode of atrial fibrillation with RVR while in the ICU, which resolved spontaneously. Her creatinine levels fluctuated due to transient hypoperfusion injury and aggressive diuresis. 

Upon discharge, her mental status was confused and her level of consciousness was alert and interactive. She was able to get out of bed with assistance to a chair or wheelchair. Her primary diagnoses were cholecystitis, complicated by septic shock, and hypoxemic respiratory failure. Secondary diagnoses included pulmonary edema, atelectasis, toxic-metabolic encephalopathy, dementia, acute kidney injury, prerenal, hypertension, atrial fibrillation, and aspiration. 

Her medications upon admission included Atenolol, Escitalopram Oxalate, Memantine, Amlodipine, Vitamin D, Cyanocobalamin, and Ascorbic Acid. There is no family history of gallbladder disease.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of dementia, hypertension, cholangitis caused by Klebsiella bacteremia, and acute cholecystitis, was admitted due to altered mental status (AMS) and gait issues. She had previously undergone an ERCP with sphincterotomy and percutaneous cholecystostomy placement. The patient's family reported two days of AMS with visual hallucinations, hypomania, slurred speech, and right leg dragging. A head CT scan was negative, and a CT abdomen showed the biliary drain intact. A chest X-ray suggested possible pneumonia. The patient denied fevers, chills, changes in urinary function, trauma, chest pain, shortness of breath, and toxic ingestion. 

Upon admission, the patient was not oriented to the year but was aware of her identity and location. Her vital signs were stable, and labs were notable for a glucose level of 102, BUN 13, Cr 0.8, and Ca 9.1. Her WBC count was 6.7, and RBC 4.27. A urinalysis was negative. An EKG showed sinus bradycardia, prolonged PR interval, RBBB, left axis deviation, and an old inferior infarct. 

The patient's past medical history includes dementia, a history of mastectomy for breast cancer, a history of colon cancer treated surgically, and bilateral hip replacements. There is no family history of gallbladder disease. 

During her hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with pneumonia and cholecystitis. Her mental status improved after treatment for pneumonia. Her hypertension was managed with home medications Norvasc and atenolol, and her dementia was managed with escitalopram and memantine. 

Upon discharge, the patient was alert and interactive but sometimes confused. She required assistance or aid for ambulation. Her discharge diagnoses were pneumonia and cholangitis, with secondary diagnoses of dementia, hyperlipidemia, and pernicious anemia. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Amlodipine, Acetaminophen, Ciprofloxacin HCl, MetRONIDAZOLE (Flagyl), Docusate Sodium, Senna, Sarna Lotion, Hydrocortisone (Rectal), Escitalopram Oxalate, Vitamin D, Memantine, Atenolol, Ascorbic Acid, and Cyanocobalamin. 

Abnormal test results included Hgb-10.9*, MCH-25.5*, MCHC-30.4*, RDW-16.3*, and RDWSD-49.2*. Imaging showed left lower lobe findings suggesting either atelectasis or pneumonia, enlarged LV, and low lung volumes. A T-Tube cholangio showed normal passage of contrast from the gallbladder to the duodenum with no evidence of obstruction. Microbiology results showed >= 3 bacterial colony types in urine culture, negative blood culture, and negative C. diff.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of dementia, hypertension, cholangitis caused by Klebsiella bacteremia, and acute cholecystitis, was admitted due to abdominal pain. She had previously undergone an ERCP with sphincterotomy and percutaneous cholecystostomy placement, which was removed a month later. The patient was transferred from another facility where imaging suggested possible cholecystitis. She was given IVF and Zosyn before being transferred for evaluation for possible percutaneous cholecystostomy placement. 

Upon admission, the patient's vitals were stable, but her abdomen was tender to touch, especially in the right upper quadrant. Lab results showed an elevated white blood cell count of 13.2, with 90.2% PMNs. Other notable lab results included a glucose level of 160, HCO3 of 20, and a proBNP of 2417. Imaging showed gallstones in a mildly distended gallbladder, pneumobilia, and diffuse intrahepatic biliary dilation. The patient received Acetaminophen 650 mg and Zosyn 4.5g. 

The patient's condition worsened on the floor, where she was not responding to verbal cues and was somnolent. Her family reported that she had been more somnolent than usual for the last month. She had no other symptoms such as fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, cough, or shortness of breath. 

The patient's past medical history includes dementia, a history of mastectomy for breast cancer, a history of colon cancer treated surgically, bilateral hip replacements, and likely heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. There is no family history of gallbladder disease. 

During her hospital stay, the patient underwent a percutaneous cholecystostomy placement. Her condition improved with treatment of infection. Her mental status improved, and her pain and leukocytosis resolved, suggesting source control. Her creatinine level increased from 0.8 on admission to 1.3-1.4 at discharge, possibly indicating a new baseline. She also experienced hypoxia, likely due to obstructive sleep apnea, bilateral atelectasis, and undiagnosed COPD. Her oxygen requirement was weaned to 1L at rest and 2L with exertion. She was prescribed albuterol PRN on discharge. 

The patient's discharge medications included Albuterol Inhaler, CefePIME 1 g IV Q24H, MetroNIDAZOLE 500 mg PO TID, Ascorbic Acid, Cyanocobalamin 500 mcg PO DAILY, DICYCLOMine 10 mg PO QID:PRN abd distress, Escitalopram Oxalate 40 mg PO DAILY, Melatin (melatonin) 5 mg oral QHS, Memantine 10 mg PO BID, and Vitamin D 1000 UNIT PO DAILY. Her home medications of amLODIPine 10 mg PO DAILY and Atenolol 25 mg PO DAILY were held and not to be restarted until instructed by her primary care physician. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of acute cholecystitis, sepsis, hypoxia, likely atelectasis and COPD, and dementia. She was instructed to continue intravenous and oral antibiotics through a specified date and to have her drain evaluated in late to make a plan for exchange or removal. She was also instructed to use 1L of oxygen while at rest and 2L while sleeping or exerting herself.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is an elderly female with a medical history of dementia, mastectomy for breast cancer, colon cancer treated surgically, and bilateral hip replacements. She was admitted due to abdominal pain and fevers. The patient was found to be febrile with a temperature of 101.8, and labs showed elevated levels of ALT (387), AP (147), AST (755), Dbili (1.5), TBili (2.2), and TProt (6.7). She was suspected to have cholangitis and was transferred for an ERCP procedure. 

Upon physical examination, she was found to have mild RUQ tenderness but no rebound or guarding. Her neurological examination showed she was oriented to person but not to place, and unaware of details of her medical care. 

A scan showed mild eccentric gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic edema, and possible cholangitis. Other findings included an indeterminate small focal hepatic lesion, uncomplicated diverticulosis, a fat-containing umbilical hernia, and small probable simple renal cysts. A CT scan of the head showed no evidence of infarction, hemorrhage, edema, or mass, but did show atrophy, probable small vessel ischemic changes, and atherosclerotic vascular disease. 

Blood tests showed elevated levels of WBC (9.2), RBC (4.47), Hgb (12.0), Hct (35.0), MCV (78), MCH (26.8), Glucose (113), ALT (518), AST (831), AlkPhos (141), TotBili (2.9), and Lactate (2.1). Blood cultures were positive for gram-negative rods. An ultrasound of the RUQ showed no biliary duct dilation, gallstones, wall thickening, and trace pericholecystic fluid within a nondistended gallbladder. 

The ERCP procedure showed no definite filling defects consistent with stones in the CBD or CHD. A biliary sphincterotomy was made and copious dark bile and sludge was removed. 

The patient was diagnosed with cholangitis with sepsis, hypertension, and dementia. She was given Zosyn prior to transfer and Unasyn during her stay. Her LFTs normalized and she was discharged on ciprofloxacin for a total 14-day course. She also developed hypertensive urgency and was given IV hydralazine and oral amlodipine. 

Her medications upon admission included Atenolol, Escitalopram Oxalate, Ascorbic Acid, Cyanocobalamin, Memantine, and Vitamin D. There is no family history of gallbladder disease.
label: controls

summary:
The male patient was admitted to the orthopaedics service due to swelling and pain in his left index finger, which was found to be infected following a cat bite. The patient underwent an irrigation and debridement (I&D) procedure on his left index finger to treat flexor tenosynovitis. The procedure was well-tolerated and the patient was stable post-operation. During his hospital stay, he was initially given IV fluids and IV pain medications, which were later transitioned to a regular diet and oral medications. The patient was also administered antibiotics and anticoagulation as per routine. His home medications were continued throughout the hospitalization. 

Upon physical examination, the patient appeared well, with no acute distress. His cardiovascular system was regular and he was breathing room air comfortably. The incision site on his left upper extremity showed minimal bloody drainage and improved erythema. The patient was able to fire extensors of the index finger and had sensation, grip, and light touch in the median, radial, and ulnar distribution. His fingers were within normal limits. 

At the time of discharge, the patient's pain was well controlled with oral medications, his incisions were clean, dry, and intact, and he was voiding and moving bowels spontaneously. He was weight bearing as tolerated in the left upper extremity. 

The patient has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. He was not taking any preadmission medications. His past medical history was unremarkable and his family history was noncontributory. 

The patient was diagnosed with left index finger pyogenic flexor tenosynovitis. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent. 

The patient was discharged home with instructions to follow weight bearing precautions, take all prescribed medications, and perform range of motion exercises as discussed. He was also advised to watch for any danger signs such as increasing pain, redness, swelling, drainage, numbness, tingling, loss of sensation, fever, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, and any other medical concerns. He was scheduled for a follow-up appointment with his hand surgeon and primary care doctor.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL), Extrapulmonary Tuberculosis (TB) treated with a 6-month course of rifampin, INH, ethambutol, and pyrazinamide, Hypothyroidism, and Osteoarthritis (OA), was admitted due to a fever of up to 101.9 for two days. She was on her second cycle of FCR (Fludarabine, Cytoxan, and Rituxan) treatment. She also complained of lightheadedness, nausea, and poor appetite, but denied cough, diarrhea, chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, dysuria, and changes in bowel or bladder habits. She had a bronchoscopy and lumbar puncture performed during her stay.

Upon admission, her medications included Acyclovir, Allopurinol, Fluconazole, Hydrochlorothiazide, Levothyroxine, Lorazepam, and Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole. 

Her lab results showed several abnormal findings including low WBC, RBC, HGB, HCT, NEUTS, LYMPHS, PLT COUNT, and high RDW, ATYPS, and GLUCOSE. Her LD(LDH) and AlkPhos were also elevated. Her proBNP was high and her Albumin, Calcium, and Phos were low. Her pO2, pCO2, and calTCO2 were also abnormal. 

Radiology reports indicated no significant change in nodal size and massive splenomegaly. There was increasing opacification in the right lower lobe which may be due to an infection. No pathology was identified to explain the patient's source of fever. There was an interval development of moderate right and small left pleural effusion and interval development of ascites. Ultrasound of the right upper extremity showed no evidence of DVT but did show subcutaneous edema in the area of swelling in the right forearm. 

Cardiology reports indicated sinus tachycardia and low voltage on EKG. Echo showed normal left atrium size, left ventricular wall thickness, cavity size, and global systolic function. There was mild mitral regurgitation and mild pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. 

Lumbar puncture and bronchoscopy were negative for malignant cells. 

During her hospital stay, she was treated for febrile neutropenia and potential pneumonia. She had an episode of hypotension that responded to IV fluids. She was transferred to the ICU due to sepsis but stabilized and was transferred back to the floor. Her counts recovered with recovery from infection. Her hypertension was stable during her stay and her hypothyroidism was managed with levothyroxine. 

Upon discharge, her condition was good and she was ambulating without assistance. Her discharge diagnoses included Pneumonia, Febrile Neutropenia, Pleural Effusion, Pericardial Effusion, CLL, and Hypothyroidism. She was instructed to call her oncologist or primary doctor immediately if she had any fever, chest pain, shortness of breath, increasing cough, or other concerning symptoms. She was advised not to resume taking Hydrochlorothiazide until seen by her primary doctor.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL), was admitted due to a fever. She had previously undergone two cycles of RCVP treatment for CLL, which normalized her white count but did not significantly reduce her lymphadenopathy or splenomegaly. She had an extended hospitalization for fever and night sweats, during which a bone marrow biopsy confirmed her CLL. A lymph node biopsy ruled out disease transformation but indicated a caseating granuloma concerning for Tuberculosis (TB). She was started on a TB medication regimen with rifampin, INH, ethambutol, and pyrazinamide, which was later adjusted due to poor tolerability. She completed a six-month course of her TB medicines. Her white blood count has been slowly rising and her platelet count has been depressed. She recently started treatment with the FCR regimen.

During her current admission, she reported a fever but denied other symptoms such as headache, visual changes, congestion, sore throat, shortness of breath, cough, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, dysuria, rash, numbness, weakness, and tingling. She was treated with 1L NS and 500mg Levaquin after blood cultures were drawn. 

Her past medical history includes CLL, extrapulmonary TB treated with a 6-month course of rifampin, INH, ethambutol, and pyrazinamide, hypothyroidism, and osteoarthritis. She has no known drug allergies. Her family history is not significant. 

Physical examination revealed mild cervical and supraclavicular lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and a black tooth posteriorly on the right. 

CT scans showed diffuse bilateral centrilobular nodules, stable lymph nodes, unchanged splenomegaly and hepatomegaly, and left thyroid lobe enlargement. Gallium scan showed patchy opacity in the superior segment of the right lower lobe and bulky bilateral lymphadenopathy. HIDA scan showed normal gallbladder filling. Ultrasound showed gallstones, minimal gallbladder wall thickening, trace pericholecystic fluid, hepatomegaly, splenomegaly, and multiple enlarged lymph nodes at the porta hepatis. MRI of the abdomen showed massive hepatosplenomegaly, gallstones, ascites, and significant periportal and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. Liver biopsy showed prominent CLL infiltrate, rare non-necrotizing granuloma, moderate steatosis, and increased portal, periportal and sinusoidal fibrosis.

During her hospital stay, she was treated for neutropenic fevers of unknown etiology, possible extrapulmonary TB, potential cholecystitis, and a possible dental infection. Her CLL was monitored and her hypothyroidism was managed with levothyroxine. Her medications upon admission included acyclovir, allopurinol, fluconazole, a multivitamin, levothyroxine, and TMP/SMZ DS. 

Upon discharge, her condition was fair and she was afebrile for several days off all antibiotics. Her discharge diagnoses were fever of unknown origin, CLL, and hypothyroidism.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL), was admitted for further investigation of fevers and night sweats. She had previously completed two cycles of R-CVP and was found to have extrapulmonary tuberculosis (TB) for which she completed a six-month treatment. She had also received the FCR regimen for cytoreduction and had recently started on CHOP-R chemotherapy. The patient reported feeling more fatigued, experiencing shortness of breath, and nausea. She had a fever of 101.0 and was found to be neutropenic. She denied any other symptoms including cough, chest pain, dyspnea, chills, vomiting, and dysuria. 

Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism and osteoarthritis. She had a history of extrapulmonary TB diagnosed and treated with a four-drug therapy including rifampin, INH, ethambutol, and pyrazinamide. She also had a history of CLL with persistent bulky lymphadenopathy and massive splenomegaly. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Levothyroxine 150ucg daily. Her physical examination was largely unremarkable. However, her lab results showed several abnormalities including elevated CK(CPK)-5*, GLUCOSE-140*, LD(LDH)-89*, and decreased RBC-2.89*, HGB-8.9*, HCT-25.2*, MCHC-35.4*, NEUTS-5*, LYMPHS-93*, and PLT COUNT-99*. 

A CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed a notable decrease in mediastinal, hilar, and axillary lymphadenopathy, and a decrease in size of the spleen and splenic lesions. There was no evidence of an infectious process in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis. 

During her hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with neutropenic fever. Her infectious workup was unrevealing and she was started on cefepime. Her ANC gradually increased and she was no longer neutropenic at the time of discharge. Her CLL was stable throughout the hospital course. 

Upon discharge, the patient's primary diagnoses were neutropenic fever and non-cardiogenic syncope. Her secondary diagnoses were CLL and hypothyroidism. Her condition at discharge was good.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to right upper quadrant (RUQ) and epigastric pain that radiated to her back. The pain started after dinner and progressively worsened, accompanied by nausea and one episode of vomiting. She denied having fevers, chills, or any changes in her bowel movements. She was aware of her cholelithiasis but denied any history of biliary colic.

Her past medical history includes Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) for which she had undergone multiple rounds of chemotherapy, the last one being Campath. Despite the treatment, she continued to have bulky lymphadenopathy and splenomegaly. She also had a history of extrapulmonary tuberculosis, hypothyroidism, and osteoarthritis. She had been on a tuberculosis medication regimen with rifampin, INH, ethambutol, and pyrazinamide, which was later switched due to poor tolerability. She completed a six-month course of her TB medicines. She was also on Levothyroxine and Vitamin D upon admission.

Her physical examination was largely unremarkable. However, her lab results showed several abnormalities including elevated ALT (134), AST (148), Alkaline Phosphatase (138), Amylase (1756), Lipase (2693), and IgG (597). Her albumin was low at 3.3. Her complete blood count showed low WBC (1.2), RBC (2.94), Hemoglobin (9.2), Hematocrit (27.1), and Platelet count (46). An abdominal ultrasound revealed cholelithiasis with mild intrahepatic biliary dilatation, fatty infiltration of the liver, and multiple pathologically enlarged lymph nodes at the porta hepatis consistent with her known CLL.

During her hospital stay, she was made NPO and underwent ERCP with sphincterotomy and sludge/stone removal from the common bile duct. She was given IV Dilaudid for pain and started on IV Zoysn. Her diet was gradually advanced and she was started on Neupogen for leukopenia. Her total granulocyte count improved and IV antibiotics were discontinued. She was discharged home on Augmentin with instructions to follow up for further labs to determine if further Neupogen dose was needed.

Her discharge diagnosis was gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis, along with CLL post multiple rounds of chemotherapy. She was advised to call her doctor or return to the Emergency Department for any new or worsening symptoms. She was also instructed to resume all regular home medications, rest, ambulate several times per day, drink adequate amounts of fluids, and avoid lifting heavy weights.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female with a history of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) who has undergone multiple therapies, most recently Campath. She also has a history of gallstone pancreatitis and has undergone ERCP and cholecystectomy. She presented with fever and diarrhea, which she had been experiencing for the past week. She also reported nausea, poor appetite, and intermittent sharp, predominantly right-sided abdominal pain. She received a dose of zosyn and had a CT torso without contrast. She has a history of intermittent neutropenia for which she receives intermittent GSCF, but she has not received this medication in the past week.

Her past medical history includes two cycles of R-CVP for CLL, which did not have a significant response. She continued to have bulky lymphadenopathy and massive splenomegaly. She was hospitalized for further workup for fever and night sweats. A bone marrow biopsy confirmed CLL and a lymph node biopsy ruled out transformation of her disease. She was diagnosed with extrapulmonary TB and started on a TB medication regimen. She completed a six-month course of her TB medicines. She also has hypothyroidism and osteoarthritis. 

Upon admission, her labs showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, and Plt Ct. Her UricAcd was also abnormal. Her discharge labs showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, Plt Ct, Neuts, Lymphs, Eos, Glucose, UreaN, Cl, Calcium, and Phos. Her stool test was positive for C. difficile. Her CT scan showed new regions of soft tissue attenuation within the right paracolic gutter and within the left upper quadrant, lateral to the spleen. These are of uncertain etiology or significance, but may be postoperative in nature. 

During her hospital stay, her fevers and diarrhea were likely secondary to C. difficile colitis. She was initially treated empirically with zosyn and flagyl. Surgery was consulted and did not feel that the patient required emergent surgery. Her diarrhea decreased and she was able to tolerate POs. Her ANC gradually decreased over the course of her hospitalization to 530. Neupogen was deferred given the patient's known bulky disease and splenomegaly. 

Her medications upon admission were Levothyroxine 137 mcg daily, Omeprazole 20 mg daily, and Vitamin D2 50,000 units twice monthly.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female with a history of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) who has undergone multiple therapies, most recently Campath. She also has a history of gallstone pancreatitis and has undergone ERCP and cholecystectomy. She was recently admitted with fever and diarrhea and was diagnosed with C. difficile. She was discharged 5 days prior to this admission. Since then, she has had intermittent low fevers, most recently to 100.9. Her diarrhea has resolved and she is having normal bowel movements. She continues to have a poor appetite and poor PO intake with intermittent nausea, but no vomiting. She denies abdominal pain, but does feel bloated. She has been taking flagyl for her C. difficile infection. She also has some minor throat pain, but no other localizing symptoms. She has a history of intermittent neutropenia for which she receives intermittent GSCF, but she has not received this medication in the past two weeks. 

Her past medical history includes hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, and a history of C. difficile. She was diagnosed with extrapulmonary TB and completed a six-month course of TB medicines. She also has a history of a slowly rising white blood cell count and a depressed platelet count. Her CT scans have overall been stable, but she remained with persistent bulky disease above and below the diaphragm with massive splenomegaly. 

Upon admission, her vitals were T 98.1, BP 117/70, HR 102, RR 16, 96% on room air. She was evaluated by surgery who recommended continued fever work-up in the absence of colitis on CT scan. She received 1L NS and was sent to the floor. Her physical exam showed dry MM with patches of white exudate on the tongue, TTP in the epigastrium, periumbilically, and on the right side, no rebound or guarding. 

Her admission labs showed abnormal WBC-2.3*, RBC-3.28*, Hgb-9.8*, Hct-29.2*, Neuts-36.8*, Lymphs-54.3*, and UreaN-5*. Her discharge labs showed abnormal WBC-1.5*, RBC-2.92*, Hgb-9.1*, Hct-27.3*, Neuts-36*, Lymphs-44*, Monos-13*, Eos-7*, and Calcium-8.1*. Her CT scan showed unchanged widespread lymphadenopathy consistent with known history of CLL, unchanged splenomegaly, unremarkable surgical site in the midline abdomen, and slight increase in size of soft tissue nodule in the right paracolic gutter. Her CXR showed focal areas of opacification consistent with atelectasis, though a developing pneumonia cannot be excluded.

During her hospital stay, she remained afebrile without any diarrhea. Her intermittent nausea may be a side effect of the flagyl. Repeat CT scan showed no evidence of colitis. Although CXR could not definitively rule out pneumonia, the patient had no respiratory symptoms. Urine analysis and culture were negative for infection. She was given IV fluids overnight for dehydration and was initially continued on flagyl. However, the following morning she was switched to oral vancomycin as it was felt that she has reduced ability to clear a C. difficile infection due to her immunocompromised state from her CLL and chemotherapy. 

Her medications upon admission included Levothyroxine 137 mcg daily, Omeprazole 20 mg daily, Vitamin D2 50,000 units twice monthly, and Metronidazole 500 mg PO Q8H.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL), was admitted due to shortness of breath. She had previously undergone two cycles of treatment for CLL, but did not show a significant response, although her white count did normalize. She continued to have bulky lymphadenopathy and massive splenomegaly. She had been diagnosed with extrapulmonary tuberculosis and had completed a course of treatment with rifampin, INH, and moxifloxacin. She also had a history of hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis for which she had undergone ERCP with sphincterotomy and cholecystectomy, and a history of Clostridium difficile.

During her current admission, she reported fevers, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds over the past month. She was given Cefepime and Ipratropium in the ED. She had an episode of tachycardia and chest pain with dyspnea and had a CTA that was negative for PE. 

Her admission labs showed abnormal CK(CPK)-7* and LD(LDH)-186. Imaging showed marked progression of interstitial opacities, extensive lymphadenopathy in the chest, multifocal areas of opacities throughout the lungs bilaterally, and splenomegaly. Bronchoalveolar lavage was positive for Pneumocystis jirovecii (carinii) and Staph aureus coag +. 

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with PCP pneumonia and was treated with intravenous bactrim for 6 days and then transitioned to a PO Bactrim DS regimen to complete a 21 day course. She was also given a 10 day steroid taper. She improved drastically on this regimen. Fluconazole was added for prophylaxis while she is taking steroids. 

She also experienced pancytopenia, likely secondary to infection, which improved with neupogen and blood transfusions. Her CLL was managed as per her primary oncologist's plan. She did not have any symptoms of diarrhea and was continued on oral vancomycin suppressive therapy for her history of C. diff. Her hypothyroidism was managed with continued synthyroid. 

Upon admission, she was on Levothyroxine 137 mcg daily, Omeprazole 20 mg qd, and Vancomycin 250 mg Capsule daily. Her discharge diagnosis included PCP pneumonia, CLL, history of Clostridium difficile, hypothyroid, anemia, and neutropenia. She was stable upon discharge.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female with a history of refractory Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL), extrapulmonary tuberculosis, and Clostridium difficile. She had recently been discharged on a 21-day course of Bactrim DS and a 10-day prednisone taper for Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP). She presented with fevers and headaches, which were later identified as symptoms of Bactrim induced aseptic meningitis. She also reported loss of appetite, leg cramps, and sweats. A lumbar puncture was performed, which showed no indication of any acute infection. The Bactrim was discontinued and she was started on once daily IV pentamidine, which she poorly tolerated. She was then switched to atovaquone to complete her 21-day antibiotic course. 

Her past medical history includes two cycles of R-CVP for CLL, with a persistent predominance of lymphocytes and bulky lymphadenopathy. She also had a history of extrapulmonary TB, for which she completed a six-month course of TB medicines. She has hypothyroidism and osteoarthritis, and has undergone ERCP with sphincterotomy for gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis, and cholecystectomy. 

Upon admission, her medications included Levothyroxine, Omeprazole, Vancomycin, Bactrim DS, Folic acid, and Fluconazole. Abnormal lab results included a low platelet count of 99, neutrophils at 34, lymphocytes at 65, monocytes at 1, an elevated RDW of 15.7, and an elevated TSH of 8.3. Her ALT was also elevated at 41. 

During her hospital stay, she received 2 doses of Kayexalate for high potassium levels, which returned to normal. Her TSH was initially elevated at 8.3, but was within normal limits on discharge without any changes in her thyroid replacement regimen. Her oral vancomycin suppressive therapy for C. difficile was continued throughout her hospital admission. 

Upon discharge, her diagnosis included drug induced aseptic meningitis secondary to Bactrim, CLL, hypothyroidism, arthritis, C. difficile on vancomycin suppression, and PCP pneumonia. Her family history was noncontributory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female with a history of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) who has undergone multiple rounds of chemotherapy, including Campath and fludarabine-based therapy (CVP, CHOP). She has persistent bulky lymphadenopathy and splenomegaly. She also has a history of recurrent Clostridium difficile colitis, hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, and extrapulmonary tuberculosis (TB) for which she completed a six-month course of rifampin, INH, and moxifloxacin. She has undergone an ERCP with sphincterotomy for gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis, and a cholecystectomy. 

The patient presented with fever, neutropenia, and hypotension. She had been monitoring her temperature due to recent hospitalizations for infections and noted a temperature of 102.2. She denied experiencing chills, night sweats, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, chest pain, diarrhea, neck stiffness, headaches, or photophobia. In the emergency department, she was given 3L of IV fluids, started on vancomycin and cefepime, and given dexamethasone 10 mg. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, RDW, Neuts, Lymphs, Glucose, UreaN, Cl, HCO3, CK(CPK), Calcium, Phos, and Hapto. Blood, CSF, and urine cultures were negative. A bone marrow biopsy was performed. 

During her hospital stay, she experienced transient hypotension, acute anemia, a heart murmur, tachycardia, and hyponatremia. She was transfused 2 units of PRBC's. Hemolysis labs were negative and decreased production was considered as a possible explanation. A parvo virus PCR was ordered and is pending result, and a folate level was normal. 

Upon admission, her medications included Levothyroxine, Omeprazole, Vancomycin, Bactrim DS, Folic acid, Fluconazole, Atovaquone, and Dexamethasone. Her family history is non-contributory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female with a history of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) who has undergone treatment with Campath, and has a history of recurrent c.diff colitis. She was recently hospitalized for PCP pneumonia, which was complicated by readmission for aseptic meningitis thought to be related to bactrim. She also had a neutropenic fever complicated with SIRS, during which no source of infection was found. She presented for admission due to neutropenic fever. She has a history of extrapulmonary TB that was treated with multidrug therapy. She has been experiencing decreased oral intake for 1.5 months. She denies chills and nightsweats, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, chest pain, diarrhea, neck stiffness, headaches or photophobia, dysuria, hematuria, and hematochezia. Her vital signs were pertinent for tachycardia. Blood and urine cultures were collected prior to starting empiric treatment of infection with Cefepime.

She has a history of hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, and has undergone ERCP with sphincterotomy for gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis, and cholecystectomy. She also has a history of C. difficile. Her family history is noncontributory. 

Upon physical examination, she appeared well with no acute distress. Her vital signs were stable. Her abdomen was soft, non-distended, and non-tender. Neurologically, she was alert and oriented with intact motor strength and sensation. 

Lab results showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, RDW, Plt Ct, Neuts, Lymphs, Monos, and LD(LDH). All blood cultures, fungal cultures, and viral antibody serologies were negative except for EBV which was positive at 80. CT Abdomen/Pelvis showed unchanged extensive lymphadenopathy/hepatosplenomegaly related to CLL and slight increase in ascites tracking in pelvis and paracolic gutters. Renal U/S showed moderate, predominantly pelvic ascites without evidence of hydronephrosis, stone or mass.

During her hospitalization, she continued to spike fevers up to 101.8. Her neutropenia improved with neupogen. She developed sweats during her fevers when she took Tylenol. She did not develop sweats if she did not take Tylenol. She had increasing abdominal distension. An U/S of the abdomen showed moderate pelvic ascites. During the course of her stay she developed acute renal failure. Prerenal etiology was ruled out by urine electrolytes. An abdominal u/s showed no hydronephrosis or urinary stones. 

Her WBC had been declining prior to Neupogen. The etiology was uncertain as she had not received chemotherapy for her CLL since ___. Recent BM biopsy revealed possibility of myelodysplastic syndrome. She received several blood transfusions to maintain an Hct >25. There was some concern for hemolysis as her haptoglobin <20. However, bilirubin and LDH were wnl. Her Hct always raised appropriately before slowly trending down. Stool was guaic negative. 

Her medications upon admission included Atovaquone, Vancomycin, Levothyroxine, Folic Acid, Omeprazole, Vitamin D, and Hydrochlorothiazine.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL), was admitted due to fevers and new urinary incontinence. She has a history of CLL treatment with Campath, recurrent c.diff colitis, and hospitalization for PCP pneumonia complicated by readmission for aseptic meningitis thought to be related to Bactrim. Her most recent hospitalization was for neutropenic fever complicated with SIRS, during which no source of infection was found. She also has a history of extrapulmonary TB, hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, and gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis. 

Upon admission, she reported daily fevers, shaking chills, weakness, decreased appetite, vomiting, left flank pain, urinary incontinence, and a cough when lying flat. She denied diarrhea, constipation, dysuria, hematuria, tingling, numbness, back pain, orthopnea, worsening lymphadenopathy, upper respiratory congestion, rhinorrhea, recent illness, sore throat, chest pain, shortness of breath, further vomiting, rashes, bleeding, or bruising. 

Her physical exam revealed afebrile, VSS, well appearing adult female, no acute distress, no anterior cervical, supraclavicular, submandibular or submental LAD, CTA b/l, no w/r/r, but decreased BS over LLL, no tenderness to palpation over C,T,L spine, +CVA tenderness on L, none on right, normal S1, S2, RRR, ___ murmur non-radiating left upper sternal border, NABS, very distended but compressible abdomen, +ttp over epigastric area, no R/G, Typanic to percussion, +ascites, HSM difficult to assess given abdominal distention, warm extremities, with +1 non-pitting edema bil ___, 2+ DP pulses bilat, alert and oriented, CN ___ intact, motor strength 4+/5 in extensors and flexors of upper and lower extremities bil, sensation intact and equal in the bil LEs, no rash or other lesions noted on exam.

Abnormal lab results included WBC-1.7*#, RBC-2.97*, Hgb-9.2*, Hct-26.8*, RDW-17.8*, Plt Ct-75*, Neuts-81.2*, Lymphs-10.8*, Glucose-117*, Creat-1.2*, LD(LDH)-258*, AlkPhos-122*, Albumin-2.7*, Calcium-7.7*, UricAcd-9.3*, RBC-7*, WBC-19* in urine, and WBC-0.6*, RBC-2.57*, Hgb-7.9*, Hct-23.5*, Neuts-72*, Lymphs-16*, Na-132*, HCO3-21*, Albumin-2.6*, Calcium-7.7*, UricAcd-9.1* at discharge. Urine cultures were negative, blood cultures had no growth, and CMV viral load was undetectable. 

Imaging showed a retrocardiac opacity on CXR, diffuse signal abnormality of the bone marrow on MRI L-spine, and increasing left pleural effusion, extensive unchanged lymphadenopathy and severe splenomegaly, and interval increase in ascites on CT Chest. 

During her hospital stay, she was treated with IV Cefepime for possible urinary tract infection and non-cirrhotic spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, and her fevers improved. Her urinary incontinence improved and was thought to be due to either mass effect of her bulky disease compressing her bladder or pressure from her ascites pressing on her bladder. An MRI of the L-spine showed no evidence of cord compression. Her CLL was monitored and she was considered for treatment with bendamustine. Her creatinine, which was elevated on admission, normalized by hospital day #1. Her anemia was monitored closely and she did not require any transfusions during this admission. She was started on allopurinol for hyperuricemia. 

Upon discharge, her condition was stable and afebrile, and her urinary incontinence had resolved. Her discharge medications included Levothyroxine, Lorazepam, Prochlorperazine Maleate, Ergocalciferol (Vitamin D2), Acyclovir, Allopurinol, Oxycodone, Ciprofloxacin, Hydrochlorothiazide, Folic Acid, and Pentamidine. She was instructed to seek immediate medical attention if she developed fever >100.4F, chills, inability to tolerate food or water, pain with urination, blood in the stool, black stool, increasing abdominal pain, chest pain, shortness of breath, or any new concerning symptom.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female with a history of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL), recurrent c.diff colitis, and hospitalization for PCP pneumonia complicated by readmission for aseptic meningitis thought to be related to Bactrim. She has also been treated for neutropenic fever complicated with SIRS, and was recently discharged from the hospital after being admitted for recurrence of fevers and positional urinary incontinence. She has undergone two cycles of RCVP (Rituxan, Cytoxan, vincristine, prednisone) and two cycles of FCR (fludarabine, Cytoxan, and Rituxan) as part of her treatment for CLL. She also has a history of extrapulmonary TB, hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, and gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis for which she underwent ERCP with sphincterotomy and cholecystectomy. 

The patient was admitted for pain control and radiation therapy due to abdominal pain, vomiting, and fevers. She reported difficulty laying flat due to shortness of breath. She was given a lidocaine patch and 1L NS for pain relief and hydration. 

On physical examination, she appeared comfortable but had a very distended abdomen. Her vital signs were stable and she had no rash or other skin lesions. Her lab results on admission showed abnormal values for WBC (1.0*#), RBC (3.04*), Hgb (9.3*), Hct (27.6*), RDW (17.8*), Plt Ct (84*), Creat (1.2*), Calcium (8.3*), and UricAcd (7.1*). Imaging showed a small-to-moderate left pleural effusion and medium-sized ascites in all four quadrants of the abdomen. 

During her hospital stay, she received palliative radiation therapy to the spleen and underwent an abdominal paracentesis, which resulted in the successful drainage of approximately 4 liters of peritoneal fluid. Her fevers responded to Tylenol and her pain was controlled with a lidocaine patch, oxycodone, and oxycontin. Her renal function, which was initially abnormal, normalized after increasing her oral fluid intake and holding her HCTZ medication. 

Her medications upon admission included Levothyroxine, Lorazepam, Prochlorperazine Maleate, Ergocalciferol (Vitamin D2), Acyclovir, Allopurinol, Oxycodone, Ciprofloxacin, Hydrochlorothiazide, Folic Acid, and Pentamidine. 

Upon discharge, her abdominal distention had significantly decreased and her pain was controlled. Her discharge diagnoses were Chronic Lymphoblastic Leukemia and Abdominal Ascites. Her discharge medications were similar to those upon admission, with the exception of Ciprofloxacin, which was stopped. She was instructed to seek immediate medical attention if she developed any new concerning symptoms. She was scheduled for follow-up with radiation oncology and her primary care physician within one week of discharge.
label: controls

summary:

She has a history of recurrent c.diff colitis and hospitalization for PCP pneumonia, complicated by readmission for aseptic meningitis thought to be related to Bactrim. She was also previously admitted for neutropenic fever complicated with SIRS, during which no source of infection was found. Her most recent discharge was after being admitted for recurrence of fevers, severe abdominal distention, and pain. During that admission, she underwent palliative radiation therapy to the spleen, abdominal paracentesis (with 4L of fluid removed), and had IV cefepime given for persistent low grade fevers. Her pain was controlled with lidoderm patches, oxycontin and po oxycodone. 

During her current admission, she reported not feeling well and was noted to have a BUN/Cr of 41/2.5. She was admitted for dehydration and acute renal failure. Her past medical history includes two cycles of R-CVP (rituxan, cytoxan, vincristine, prednisone) as part of her initial treatment for CLL. She also had a bone marrow biopsy, which confirmed her known history of CLL. She also had a lymph node biopsy of the right supraclavicular node in order to rule out transformation of her disease, which was also consistent with CLL without any evidence of transformation. However, there was note of caseating granuloma concerning for TB. She was ultimately started on TB medication regimen with rifampin, INH, ethambutol, and pyrazinamide. 

Upon admission, her physical exam showed a very distended but compressible abdomen, with +ttp diffusely over upper abdomen. No R/G. Typanic to percussion. +ascites. HSM difficult to assess given abdominal distention. Her lab results showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, RDW, Plt Ct, UreaN, Creat, Na, K, HCO3, AlkPhos, UricAcd, TSH, and Free T4. Her urine test showed abnormal RBC. Imaging reports showed no hydronephrosis or nephrolithiasis but moderate ascites. No evidence for renal artery stenosis was found. 

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with acute renal failure, likely due to prerenal etiology: decreased po intake, medication effect (allopurinol, HCTZ), and third spacing of ascites after recent paracentesis and removal of 4L fluid. She was also treated for CLL, anemia, and hyperuricemia. Her medications upon admission included Levothyroxine, Lorazepam, Acyclovir, Allopurinol, Folic Acid, Oxycodone, Prochlorperazine Maleate, Ergocalciferol (Vitamin D2), Pentamidine, and Hydrochlorothiazide.
label: controls

summary:

She has also had recurrent c.diff colitis and was hospitalized for PCP pneumonia, which was complicated by a readmission for aseptic meningitis thought to be related to Bactrim. She was previously admitted for neutropenic fever complicated with SIRS, during which no source of infection was found. She was most recently discharged after being admitted for recurrence of fevers, severe abdominal distention, and pain. During that admission, she underwent palliative radiation therapy to the spleen, abdominal paracentesis, and had IV cefepime given for persistent low grade fevers. Her pain was controlled with lidoderm patches, oxycontin and po oxycodone. She was hospitalized again with acute renal failure and ascites. 

She has a history of extrapulmonary TB, hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis, C. difficile, and recurrent ascites. She has undergone ERCP with sphincterotomy and cholecystectomy. She has also completed two cycles of R-CVP (rituxan, cytoxan, vincristine, prednisone) as part of her initial treatment for CLL. She has remained with massive splenomegaly and bulky lymphadenopathy both above and below the diaphragm following this treatment. She has also had a bone marrow biopsy and a lymph node biopsy of the right supraclavicular node, both of which confirmed her known history of CLL. She was started on a TB medication regimen with rifampin, INH, ethambutol, and pyrazinamide, which was later switched to include moxifloxacin. 

Upon admission, she was on acyclovir, allopurinol, ergocalciferol, folic acid, levothyroxine, lorazepam, and pentamidine. Her physical exam showed distended abdomen, uncomfortable to palpation, and pitting edema bilateral. Her admission labs showed abnormal values for K (5.6), WBC (1.4), Hb (8.3), Hct (26.1), and Plt (53). Her discharge labs showed abnormal values for WBC (0.9), RBC (2.53), Hgb (7.7), Hct (23.3), RDW (20.4), Plt Ct (52), UreaN (27), Creat (1.5), Cl (111), HCO3 (19), Albumin (2.6), Calcium (7.6), and UricAcd (6.6). 

During her hospital stay, she underwent a therapeutic paracentesis which yielded 4 liters of clear brown fluid. A CT scan showed small-to-moderate layering left pleural effusion with underlying left lower lobe atelectasis, unchanged lymphadenopathy of the chest and abdomen, and slightly increased abdominal and pelvic ascites. A CXR showed stable large left pleural effusion with left lower lobe atelectasis. 

Her hospital course was marked by recurrent ascites, acute renal failure, CLL, shortness of breath, anemia, hyperuricemia, and hyperkalemia. Her abdominal discomfort and shortness of breath improved following paracentesis. Her acute renal failure improved with hydration and continuation of allopurinol. Her CLL was treated with palliative radiation therapy and her disease status remained relatively stable. Her anemia prompted transfusion of 1 unit pRBC on the morning of discharge. Her hyperuricemia and hyperkalemia were likely due to tumor lysis post-radiation and resolved on discharge. 

Upon discharge, her condition was stable, afebrile, with vital signs stable, and improved abdominal discomfort. She was on room air. Her discharge medications included acyclovir, allopurinol, folic acid, levothyroxine, lorazepam, and prochlorperazine maleate. She was instructed to call her doctor or return to the hospital if she developed worsening abdominal pain or distention, shortness of breath, worsening of her leg swelling, fevers, chills, or other symptoms that concern her.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female with a history of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) who has undergone multiple prior therapies. She has been experiencing progressive abdominal disease with secondary ascites and was admitted for therapeutic paracentesis. The patient has had multiple hospitalizations over the last 6 months, including for neutropenic fever complicated with Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome (SIRS) and recurrence of fevers, severe abdominal distention, and pain due to potential non-cirrhotic Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis (SBP). She underwent palliative radiation therapy to the spleen and has had repeated therapeutic paracentesis to address continued ascites. The patient has recurrent ascites, believed to be secondary to lymphadenopathy and splenomegaly from CLL. She also has poor marrow reserve and evidence of dyspoiesis on her last bone marrow. She did not respond well to radiation therapy and her most recent CT scan shows stable splenomegaly and other abdominal masses. 

The patient's past medical history includes extrapulmonary TB, hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis, cholecystectomy, history of C. difficile, and ascites. Her family history is noncontributory. 

Upon admission, the patient was experiencing abdominal fullness but without discomfort. She denied nausea/vomiting, abdominal pain, fever/chills, chest pain, rash, muscle aches, diarrhea/constipation, and dysuria. She did report orthopnea, shortness of breath, and bilateral leg swelling. 

Physical examination revealed a distended but soft and non-tense abdomen, with no tenderness to palpation. The spleen was not palpable. There was pitting edema bilaterally. 

Admission labs showed abnormal values for WBC (1.2), RBC (2.85), Hgb (8.9), Hct (26.6), RDW (20.2), Plt Ct (47), Neuts (70.8), and HCO3 (21). AlkPhos was also elevated at 136. Paracentesis report showed successful therapeutic paracentesis with 1.5 liters of serosanguineous fluid removed. CXR showed interval improvement in the moderate left pleural effusion and left lower lobe atelectasis, with no evidence of pneumonia. Discharge labs showed abnormal values for WBC (1.2), RBC (2.76), Hgb (8.6), Hct (26.0), RDW (20.0), Plt Ct (54), HCO3 (20), AlkPhos (119), Albumin (2.6), and Calcium (7.6). The patient tested positive for CMV IgG antibody and negative for CMV IgM antibody. 

During her hospital stay, the patient was given a Neupogen injection due to low WBC count and was treated for febrile neutropenia with cefepime, later narrowed to levaquin. She was also treated for nausea with her home compazine regimen. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Acyclovir, Allopurinol, Folic Acid, Levothyroxine, Lorazepam, and Prochlorperazine Maleate.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to a fever. She has a history of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) complicated by splenomegaly and ascites. She has been refractory to multiple regimens and was most recently started on half dose bendamustine. She also has a history of extrapulmonary TB, hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis for which she underwent ERCP with sphincterotomy and cholecystectomy, C. difficile, and ascites. She has an allergy to Vancomycin.

Upon admission, she was experiencing fevers up to 103.9 one day after receiving bendamustine. She denied chest pain, shortness of breath, dysuria, abdominal pain, hematochezia, melena, joint pains, or rash. In the ED, her vitals were 103.9 95/43 120, 24 99% 2L. A CXR showed possible retrocardiac opacity and labs showed neutropenia. A diagnostic paracentesis was performed and showed 513 wbc's and 1 poly. She was started on cefepime and vancomycin for neutropenic fever and levofloxacin for pneumonia. She developed hives on her forearm after receiving vancomycin and this infusion was stopped. She was given 2L of NS and admitted to BMT.

Her admission labs showed abnormal values for WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, RDW, Plt Ct, Neuts, Lymphs, PTT, Glucose, UreaN, Creat, K, HCO3, AnGap, Calcium, Phos, and Mg. Ascites fluid showed abnormal values for WBC, RBC, Polys, Lymphs, Monos, Mesothe, and Macroph. Discharge labs showed abnormal values for WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, RDW, Plt Ct, Neuts, Lymphs, Monos, AlkPhos, Albumin, Calcium, and Phos. Urinalysis showed a small amount of yeast. Blood and peritoneal cultures were negative. CXR showed moderate left pleural effusion, similar to prior studies. CT Chest showed no evidence of pneumonia, unchanged moderately large left pleural effusion and associated atelectasis, unchanged ascites, and stable lymphadenopathy, consistent with history of CLL.

During her hospital stay, no obvious source of the patient's fever was identified. Her fevers quickly subsided, and the cefepime was stopped. It is likely that the patient was having drug-related fevers from her bendamustine. She received a total of 4 units of packed RBCs while admitted due to anemia likely from her recent bendamustine treatment. She underwent a therapeutic paracentesis for her ascites and was given a prophylactic course of ciprofloxacin at discharge. 

Upon admission, she was on acyclovir, allopurinol, prochlorperazine, Vitamin D, levothyroxine, nebupent, and folic acid. At discharge, she was prescribed acyclovir, prochlorperazine, Vitamin D, levothyroxine, folic acid, ciprofloxacin, and nebupent. She was instructed to stop her allopurinol. Her family history is noncontributory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to anemia and hypotension following a paracentesis procedure where 3.2 liters of fluid was drained. She had a history of refractory chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), recurrent ascites requiring paracentesis, extrapulmonary tuberculosis (completed 6 months of therapy with rifampin, INH, and moxifloxacin), hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis (status post ERCP with sphincterotomy), cholecystectomy, C. difficile infection, and ascites. She was on bendamustine and Rituxan for CLL. 

The patient had no complaints upon presentation but had a temperature of 100.5. She denied experiencing chest pain, abdominal pain, lightheadedness, nausea/vomiting, diarrhea, syncope, melena, hematochezia, or cough. Physical examination revealed splenomegaly but no other significant findings. 

Lab results showed several abnormalities including low white blood cell count (1.3*), low red blood cell count (2.69*), low hemoglobin (9.0*), low hematocrit (26.8*), high mean corpuscular volume (MCV-100*), high mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH-33.4*), high red cell distribution width (RDW-19.8*), low platelet count (Plt Ct-22*), low chloride (Cl-111*), low bicarbonate (HCO3-20*), high alkaline phosphatase (AlkPhos-118*), and low calcium (Calcium-8.1*). 

During her hospital stay, she was treated for likely iatrogenic bacterial peritonitis, a possible consequence of the paracentesis. She was initially started on cefepime and as her neutropenia improved, she was switched to cefpodoxime and linezolid. She completed a 7-day course while in the hospital. Her neutropenia was managed by changing her neupogen from every other day to once daily. 

Upon admission, her medications included acyclovir, ergocalciferol, filgrastim, folic acid, levothyroxine, lorazepam, nystatin, pentamidine, prednisone, prochlorperazine maleate, and magnesium oxide. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of refractory CLL, treated extrapulmonary TB, hypothyroidism, and recurrent ascites. Her mental status was clear and coherent upon discharge.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to abdominal distention and fever. She has a history of chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) and secondary myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS), which has led to recurring ascites requiring paracentesis. She is currently on bendamustine and Rituxan for her CLL. She also has a history of extrapulmonary tuberculosis, hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis for which she underwent ERCP with sphincterotomy and cholecystectomy, C. difficile infection, and ascites. 

During her stay, she underwent a therapeutic paracentesis. Her physical examination revealed tachycardia, ascites, and no signs of acute distress. Her blood work showed abnormal values for WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, Plt Ct, Neuts, Glucose, Calcium, and Phos. Her ascitic fluid analysis also showed abnormal values for WBC, RBC, Polys, and Lymphs. 

A CT scan of her chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed extensive mediastinal lymphadenopathy, a small left pleural effusion, atelectasis, opacities in the left upper lobe, two new pulmonary nodules, splenomegaly, ascites, and multiple enhancing masses within the uterus presumed to be fibroids. 

During her hospital stay, she was empirically treated for peritonitis with IV cefepime and IV flagyl. Her ascitic fluid analysis revealed PMN < 250 and gram stain negative, and SAAG 1.3. She was then switched to PO cefpodoxime and PO flagyl. She was also treated with neupogen daily for her CLL and received one dose of inhaled pentamadine. She was transfused 1 unit of packed red blood cells due to a hematocrit of 23.8. Her hypothyroidism was managed with her home dose of levothyroxine 175 mcg PO daily. 

Her medications upon admission included acyclovir, allopurinol, ergocalciferol, filgrastim, folic acid, levothyroxine, lorazepam, pentamadine, prednisone, and prochlorperazine maleate. She has an allergy to vancomycin. Her family history is noncontributory. She was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and able to ambulate independently.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of refractory Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) complicated by Myelodysplastic Syndrome (MDS) and recurrent ascites, was admitted due to febrile neutropenia and relative hypotension. She had been experiencing fevers, nausea, vomiting, dizziness, and a decrease in blood pressure. She had received etoposide and cytoxan five days prior to admission, which resulted in neutropenia. She also reported a decrease in appetite and an increase in fluid accumulation in her abdomen since her last paracentesis. 

Her past medical history includes extrapulmonary tuberculosis, hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis treated with ERCP and sphincterotomy, cholecystectomy, a history of C. difficile, and recurrent ascites. She has no significant family history.
