Upon admission, she was on several medications including acyclovir, allopurinol, cefpodoxime, ergocalciferol, filgrastim, folic acid, levothyroxine, lorazepam, metronidazole, nystatin, olanzapine, pentamidine, prednisone, prochlorperazine maleate, and magnesium oxide. 

Her physical examination revealed a distended but soft abdomen with palpable splenomegaly. Her vitals were stable with a temperature of 97.5, blood pressure of 90/56, pulse of 87, and oxygen saturation of 97% on room air. 

Her admission labs showed a WBC of 0.3, Hct of 24.6, Plt of 40, ANC of 66, Na of 133, K of 4.6, Cl of 104, HCO3 of 19, BUN of 24, Cr of 0.7, Glu of 123, and Lactate of 1.0. Her discharge labs showed a WBC of 0.7, RBC of 2.64, Hgb of 9.0, Hct of 26.0, MCV of 99, MCH of 34.2, MCHC of 34.7, RDW of 18.4, Plt Ct of 28, Glucose of 98, UreaN of 14, Creat of 0.6, Na of 139, K of 4.0, Cl of 110, HCO3 of 24, AnGap of 9, ALT of 12, AST of 15, LD(LDH) of 116, AlkPhos of 85, TotBili of 0.3, Albumin of 2.8, Calcium of 8.0, Phos of 2.6, and Mg of 1.9. 

Her peritoneal fluid showed a WBC of 75, RBC of 5100, Polys of 2, Lymphs of 78, Monos of 10, Mesothe of 10, TotProt of 2.3, and Glucose of 9. Her blood and urine cultures were negative. Her chest X-ray showed clear lungs with no focal consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. 

During her hospital stay, she was treated with cefepime/metronidazole for possible intra-abdominal infection, and her fevers resolved. She also underwent a therapeutic paracentesis to relieve the fluid accumulation in her abdomen. Her blood pressure stabilized after receiving IV fluids. She continued her chemotherapy regimen and received blood and platelet transfusions as needed. Her discharge diagnoses were neutropenic fever, CLL, and malignant ascites.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of refractory Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) complicated by Myelodysplastic Syndrome (MDS) and recurrent ascites, presented to the clinic with worsening ascites and discomfort. She was also tachycardic and complained of shortness of breath. She has been having frequent paracentesis, with the last one prior to this admission. She was last admitted for febrile neutropenia and was discharged on Cefpodoxime and Flagyl. She reports that her abdomen has been increasing in size rapidly since her last paracentesis and she experiences shortness of breath even while resting. She denies chest pain, palpitations, fever, chills, abdominal pain, diarrhea, or constipation. Her last chemotherapy regimen was CEP - Etoposide and Cyclophosphamide, along with Prednisone. 

Her past medical history includes extrapulmonary TB, hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis, cholecystectomy, history of C. difficile, and recurrent ascites. She has undergone several treatments for CLL including CHOP, CVP, fludarabine-based therapy, Campath, and bendamustine. 

Upon physical examination, her abdomen was protruberant and distended, but soft. She had bilaterally crackles at the bases of her lungs and palpable splenomegaly. Her lab results showed abnormal levels of urea nitrogen, total CO2, alkaline phosphatase, calcium, RBC, HGB, HCT, MCV, MCH, RDW, neutrophils, and platelet count. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent paracentesis and 6L of peritoneal fluid was removed. The fluid did not demonstrate evidence of infection. Her shortness of breath was attributed to the bulk of ascites and compression of the lungs. A TTE showed LVEF >65%. Her tachycardia was due to dehydration and resolved after fluid administration. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including Acyclovir, Allopurinol, Cefpodoxime, Ergocalciferol, Filgrastim, Folic Acid, Levothyroxine, Lorazepam, Prednisone, Prochlorperazine Maleate, and Magnesium Oxide. 

Her discharge diagnoses were primary ascites and secondary CLL/MDS, hypothyroidism. She was instructed to call if she developed a new fever, redness or pain at the paracentesis site or if she had difficulty breathing, became lightheaded or dizzy.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of refractory Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) complicated by Myelodysplastic Syndrome (MDS) and recurrent ascites, presented to the clinic with persistent drainage from her paracentesis site. She has been having frequent paracentesis and was last admitted for aseptic ascites and shortness of breath. She reports chronic nausea and lower abdominal pain, worse with palpation. Her last chemotherapy regimen was CEP - Etoposide and Cyclophosphamide. She denies fevers, chills, vomiting, diarrhea, and shortness of breath. 

Her past medical history includes CLL, extrapulmonary TB, hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis status post ERCP with sphincterotomy, cholecystectomy, history of C. difficile, and recurrent ascites. Her family history is noncontributory. 

Upon physical examination, she was found to have a disproportionately large abdomen for her thin habitus, very tender to palpation in the lower quadrants, and palpable splenomegaly. Her vitals were stable and her abdominal pain largely resolved. 

Abnormal lab results included Urea Nitrogen at 23, ALT at 69, AST at 62, Alkaline Phosphatase at 257, Calcium at 7.8, WBC at 2.3, RBC at 3.31, Hemoglobin at 11.2, Hematocrit at 32.8, MCV at 99, MCH at 33.9, RDW at 20.5, and Platelet count at 56. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent diagnostic and therapeutic paracentesis, and a transvaginal ultrasound. The ultrasound of the abdomen demonstrated no fluid collection, abscess, or hematoma. Blood cultures did not grow any organisms. She was started on Meropenem, and cefpodoxime was discontinued due to suspected infection. Her abdominal pain began to resolve and her peritoneal fluid stopped draining. She was switched to levaquin PO which she did not tolerate due to overwhelming dizziness. She was then started on Augmentin which she tolerated well. 

She was found to be hypotensive, though completely asymptomatic. Her blood pressure remained stable throughout the remainder of her admission. She was started on a course of Campath subcutaneous with the hope of treating CLL and decreasing effusions. She had a petechial, pruritic, and erythematous reaction to the first dose which was somewhat relieved with prednisone. 

A CT scan showed a rim-enhancing mass in the uterus, probably a necrotic fibroid. A transvaginal ultrasound confirmed the presence of multiple large uterine fibroids, one appearing densely calcified. No intervention was felt to be necessary at that time. 

Her medications upon admission included Acyclovir, Allopurinol, Folic Acid, Levothyroxine, Prednisone, Prochlorperazine Maleate, Cefpodoxime, Filgrastim, Ergocalciferol, Pentamidine, and Magnesium Oxide.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of refractory Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) complicated by Myelodysplastic Syndrome (MDS) and recurrent ascites, was admitted due to a low-grade fever of 100.0, abdominal pain, and lower extremity edema. She has a history of extrapulmonary tuberculosis, hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis, cholecystectomy, C. difficile infection, and recurrent ascites. She has undergone multiple treatments for CLL, including CHOP, CVP, fludarabine-based therapy, Campath, and bendamustine. She was recently discharged following a 6L therapeutic paracentesis and a two-week course of augmentin. She was started on Campath subcutaneous treatment with the aim of treating CLL and decreasing effusions. 

During her stay, she underwent a focal left abdominal ultrasound, ultrasound-guided diagnostic paracentesis, and ultrasound-guided therapeutic paracentesis, during which five liters of ascitic fluid were removed. Her abdominal pain was localized at the site of her previous paracentesis. An abdominal ultrasound did not show abscess or hematoma as the cause of her pain. She was switched from oral augmentin to IV cefepime and Daptomycin for neutropenic fever. Her abdominal pain resolved over the course of the hospitalization and her ANC improved to greater than 1000. She remained afebrile on oral augmentin for more than 24 hours. 

Her lower extremity edema was evaluated for DVT due to her cancer and recent hospitalization, but a doppler ultrasound did not show DVT. Her edema resolved over the course of her stay. 

A CT scan showed a fibroid uterus with a left fundal heterogeneously enhancing mass with central areas of hypoattenuation, which was concerning for a necrotic fibroid. However, a GYN consultation confirmed that the fibroid was not the source of her fever and abdominal pain and did not require surgical treatment. 

Her admission medications included Acyclovir, Allopurinol, Folic Acid, Levothyroxine, Prednisone, Prochlorperazine Maleate, Filgrastim, Pentamidine, Magnesium Oxide, Alemtuzumab, and Amoxicillin-Pot Clavulanate. 

Abnormal lab results included low WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, RDW, and Plt Ct, high K and Cl, low Albumin and Calcium, high IgG, high Neuts, low Lymphs, high Glucose, high UreaN, low Na, and high AlkPhos. 

Upon discharge, her primary diagnosis was neutropenic fever, with secondary diagnoses of CLL post CamPath treatment, necrotic uterine fibroid, and recurrent ascites. She was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of treatment-refractory Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL), was admitted due to ascites, nonproductive cough, and shortness of breath. She reported that her ascites had been increasing recently and the cough and shortness of breath had developed in the last two days. She also had a recent fever, but was afebrile upon admission. She had a recent appointment with her doctor where it was decided to move towards comfort care due to the lack of treatment options left for her CLL. She underwent paracentesis with removal of 5.5L of fluid, which showed 140 WBCs. Post-procedure, she reported that her cough was gone and she was no longer short of breath. 

Her past medical history includes CLL, extrapulmonary TB (now post 6 months of therapy with rifampin, INH, and moxifloxacin), hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, status post ERCP with sphincterotomy for gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis, status post cholecystectomy, history of C. difficile, and recurrent ascites. She has undergone multiple treatments for CLL including CHOP, CVP, fludarabine-based therapy, Campath, and bendamustine. 

Upon admission, her physical exam showed decreased breath sounds at bases bilaterally, distended abdomen, and 3+ pitting edema bilaterally to mid thigh. Her lab results showed several abnormalities including low WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, and Plt Ct, high MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, Neuts, Lymphs, Atyps, and UreaN, and low Cl and AlkPhos. Her peritoneal fluid flow cytometry/cytology showed abnormal/lymphoma cells comprising 20% of lymphoid gated events and B cells co-expressing pan B-cell markers CD19 along with CD5 and CD23 (subset). 

During her hospital stay, she required weekly paracenteses and had an intraperitoneal PleurX catheter placed for drainage. The fluid did not reveal evidence of SBP and her cough and SOB resolved following drainage. However, the fluid continued to reaccumulate rapidly. Her daughter was shown how to drain the fluid via the catheter prior to discharge and 1.2L was drained on the day of discharge. 

Her medications upon admission included Levothyroxine, Folic Acid, Acyclovir, Allopurinol, Neupogen, Lorazepam, Prednisone, Prochlorperazine, and Mag Oxide. Her family history was non-contributory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of treatment refractory chronic lymphoid leukemia (CLL), refractory ascites, extrapulmonary tuberculosis (TB), hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis, recurrent C. difficile, and recurrent ascites, was admitted due to hypotension and lightheadedness. She has undergone several treatments for CLL including CHOP, CVP, fludarabine-based therapy, Campath, and bendamustine. She also had a Pleurex catheter placed for reaccumulation of ascites. She has a history of ERCP with sphincterotomy for gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis, and cholecystectomy. She was treated for extrapulmonary TB with rifampin, INH, and moxifloxacin. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including levothyroxine, folic acid, acyclovir, allopurinol, filgrastim, lorazepam, prednisone, prochlorperazine maleate, and magnesium oxide. She has an allergy to vancomycin. 

The patient presented with hypotension and lightheadedness. Her blood pressure was in the 70's systolic, but after receiving approximately 1L of normal saline in the ED, her blood pressure stabilized in the 90's. She denied any fever, chest pain, shortness of breath, or change in chronic abdominal pain. 

Her lab results showed several abnormalities including low WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, and Plt Ct. Her MCV, MCH, RDW, and glucose levels were also elevated. Her albumin and calcium levels were low. Her CXR showed no new acute cardiopulmonary abnormality but did show unchanged mediastinal lymphadenopathy and nodular density projecting over the right inferior hilum. 

During her hospital stay, her hypotension stabilized and she remained asymptomatic. Her ascites was managed by removing 1L of fluid on the day of admission and instructing her to not remove more than 500-750mL per day of fluid. Her CLL was not treated at this time. Her anemia was managed by transfusing 2 units of PRBCs without complication. 

Upon discharge, her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and ambulatory. Her discharge diagnosis included treatment refractory CLL and recurrent ascites. She was instructed to not remove more than 500-750 milliliters per day of fluid from her abdomen if possible and to increase the amount of fluids she drinks. Her medications remained unchanged.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to recurrent hypotension and lightheadedness. She has a history of refractory chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL) and refractory ascites. She has had a Pleurex catheter placed for reaccumulation of ascites and has had several admissions for similar symptoms. She also has a history of extrapulmonary tuberculosis, hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis, cholecystectomy, C. difficile infection, and recurrent ascites. She has undergone various treatments for CLL including CHOP, CVP, fludarabine-based therapy, Campath, and bendamustine. 

During this admission, she reported feeling lightheaded and her blood pressure was around 70/50. She might have briefly lost consciousness at home. She denied fevers, chills, headache, visual changes, chest pain, dyspnea, abdominal pain, diarrhea, and dysuria. She stated that her abdomen was getting more distended again. 

On physical examination, she was found to have bilateral lower extremity edema and a distended, slightly tense, nontender abdomen. Her lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally and her cardiovascular exam was normal. 

Admission labs showed abnormal values for total CO2 (21), alkaline phosphatase (208), calcium (7.8), RBC (3.51), HGB (11.7), HCT (34.4), MCH (33.4), RDW (20.4), neutrophils (71), and platelet count (51). Discharge labs showed abnormal values for WBC (2.7), RBC (3.08), Hgb (10.2), Hct (31.5), MCV (102), MCH (33.0), RDW (19.3), atypical cells (2), glucose (107), urea nitrogen (23), bicarbonate (21), alkaline phosphatase (181), and calcium (7.4). 

A chest X-ray showed low lung volumes, likely due to suboptimal inspiration effort, and a focal opacity at the right infrahilar area, which was unchanged compared to multiple prior examinations. There was no evidence of new pleural effusion or pneumothorax. 

During her hospital stay, she was started on fludrocortisone to attempt to raise her blood pressure with minimal results. She was also started on midodrine. Her blood pressure ranged from low to 105 systolic. Ascitic fluid was removed from the patient via the Pleurex drain as needed, with an attempt to not remove more than 1L daily. Fluid was sent for culture and cell count, with no evidence of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. She did have one low-grade fever and received 2 days of ciprofloxacin, with no signs or symptoms of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. 

Her medications upon admission included levothyroxine, folic acid, acyclovir, filgrastim, allopurinol, lorazepam, prednisone, prochlorperazine, and magnesium oxide. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of chronic lymphocytic leukemia and recurrent ascites. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of end-stage Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL), refractory ascites, recurrent hypotensive episodes, extrapulmonary TB, hypothyroidism, osteoarthritis, gallstone pancreatitis and cholangitis, and a history of C. difficile, was admitted due to cellulitis. She presented with fever and pain around the abdominal catheter site, along with mild dyspnea. She denied experiencing night sweats, recent weight loss or gain, headache, sinus tenderness, rhinorrhea or congestion, chest pain or tightness, palpitations, cough, wheezes, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, changes in bowel or bladder habits, dysuria, arthralgias, myalgias, and numbness/tingling in extremities. 

Upon examination, erythema was noted around the abdominal catheter site. Chest CTA was negative for PE or pneumonia. Abdominal CT revealed known abdominal ascites without abscess. Her ascitic fluid study revealed 1756 RBCs, 525 WBCs with 48% polys, 42% lymphs, 7% monos, amylase 32. She received daptomycin 280 mg IV x 1, metronidazole 500 mg IV x 1, cefepime 2 gm IV x 1. 

Lab results on admission showed abnormal values for WBC (2.3), RBC (3.16), Hgb (10.7), Hct (32.2), MCV (102), MCH (34.0), RDW (18.7), Plt Ct (71), Lymphs (43), Atyps (1), PTT (20.8), Glucose (113), UreaN (26), K (6.6), HCO3 (20), Albumin (2.7), and Calcium (8.1). 

Microbiology results showed the presence of Bacillus species, Enterococcus faecalis, Staph aureus coag +, and Proteus mirabilis in the peritoneal fluid. The patient's urine culture showed yeast, but the blood culture showed no growth. 

Imaging results showed no evidence of intra-abdominal abscess or pulmonary embolism. There was a trace of left-sided pleural effusion, stable hilar lymphadenopathy, decreased mediastinal lymphadenopathy, extensive ascites, splenomegaly, stable retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and a fibroid uterus. 

During her hospital stay, the patient was found to have bacterial peritonitis/ascites, with the catheter being removed and the patient started on Rifaximin. She also showed neurological deficits, with left upper-extremity weakness, bilateral symmetric weakness in the lower extremities, slight tongue deviation, and slow speech. Her hypothyroidism was managed with levothyroxine, and her hypotension was managed with midodrine. 

Upon discharge, the patient's primary diagnoses were bacterial peritonitis and CLL. Her level of consciousness was lethargic but arousable, her activity status was bedbound, and her mental status was clear and coherent. Her medications upon admission included acyclovir, levothyroxine, allopurinol, filgrastim, folate, prednisone, midodrine, lorazepam, prochlorperazine, and magnesium oxide.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to a right open tibial wound. She has a history of breast cancer and hypertension. She has not sought treatment for her breast cancer for several years but now has a treatment plan. She also has a right open tib/fib fracture and exposed hardware for right medial gastroc flap and split thickness skin graft for wound coverage. She cannot receive chemotherapy until the wound is closed. 

During her stay, she underwent several procedures including a washout of the open tibia fracture wound, a gastrocnemius muscle flap to the open tibial wound, and a skin graft to the open tibial wound. Post-operatively, she complained of severe discomfort due to her RLE plaster splint. As a result, the splint was removed and replaced with a prefabricated boot. She was maintained non-weight bearing on her RLE. 

Her post-operative course was stable. She received IV pain medication which was effective in controlling her pain. She was given IV fluids until she was able to tolerate oral intake. Her diet was advanced appropriately and she was started on a bowel regimen. She was also given 2 doses of IV cefazolin post-operatively. Her temperature was closely monitored for signs of infection. She received subcutaneous heparin during her stay and was encouraged to ambulate as early as possible. 

Upon admission, she was on Tamoxifen 20 mg po daily. At the time of discharge, she was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs, tolerating a regular diet, ambulating with crutches, voiding without assistance, and her pain was well controlled. Her RLE was wrapped with an ace wrap and AFO boot in place. 

Her family history is negative for breast, ovarian, or any other types of cancer. Her mother died of drowning and her father died of old age. She has one brother and two sisters who are all still alive. 

She was discharged with a diagnosis of right anterior tibia wound defect. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory but required assistance or aid in the form of crutches.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to pain in her right leg. She has a history of a right open tibial and fibular fracture from a pedestrian versus motor vehicle accident. The fracture was treated with intramedullary nailing. However, she developed a wound complication that resulted in exposed hardware and denuded tibia. This was managed with debridement and closure using a medial gastrocnemius flap and split thickness skin graft. She also has a history of untreated breast cancer and hypertension. 

Upon examination, her right lower extremity wound was clean, dry, and intact with minimal erythema and no edema. She was neurovascularly intact and was weight bearing as tolerated in the right lower extremity. 

During her hospital stay, she was found to have a right tibial infection. She underwent irrigation and debridement, removal of hardware, and placement of an antibiotic spacer. Post-operatively, she was stable and her pain was well controlled with oral medications. She was given perioperative antibiotics and anticoagulation. Her home medications were continued throughout her hospital stay. 

The infectious disease team recommended a 6-week course of nafcillin to treat the infection. At the time of discharge, her vital signs were stable and within normal limits. Her incisions were clean, dry, and intact, and she was voiding and moving bowels spontaneously. 

Upon discharge, she was prescribed acetaminophen for pain as needed, heparin for DVT prophylaxis, and nafcillin for the infection. She was discharged home with services and was instructed to follow up in two weeks. 

Her family history is negative for breast, ovarian, or any other types of cancer. Her mother died of drowning and her father died of old age. She has one brother and two sisters who are all still alive.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to an infection in her right tibia. She had previously sustained an open tibia fracture which was managed surgically with a flap and bone grafting. However, the wound became infected and has been draining intermittently. She was treated with antibiotics and an antibiotic bone spacer. The patient also has a history of breast cancer and hypertension. She has been immunosuppressed due to her cancer treatment. 

During her stay, she underwent a surgical procedure involving irrigation and debridement (I&D) of her right tibia, removal of hardware, and elevation of a gastrocnemius flap with complex closure. Post-surgery, she was stable and was transferred to the floor after recovery from anesthesia. She was initially given IV fluids and pain medications, and progressed to a regular diet and oral medications by post-operative day 1. 

Her physical examination was generally unremarkable, with no acute distress noted. However, her right lower extremity was noted to be in a certain condition, with dressings clean, dry, and intact. A chest x-ray was performed, and two fluoroscopic spot views of the proximal right tibia demonstrated a markedly comminuted fracture of the proximal tibia, with the hardware removed. 

Her lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.85), Hgb (10.2), Hct (30.9), MCV (80), MCH (26.6), and RDW (17.1). 

Upon admission, she was on Lisinopril 20 mg daily, Tamoxifen Citrate 20 mg at bedtime, Cephalexin 500 mg every 8 hours, and HYDROmorphone as needed for pain. 

The patient's family history is negative for breast, ovarian, or any other types of cancer. She is not of a certain descent and is originally from a certain place. Her mother died of drowning and her father died of old age. She has one brother and two sisters who are all still in a certain place. 

The patient was discharged in a clear and coherent mental state, requiring assistance or aid for ambulation. She was alert and interactive. Her discharge diagnosis was an infected right tibia.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of untreated advanced breast cancer, was admitted due to trauma from a motor vehicle accident, resulting in a right ankle injury. The patient underwent several surgical procedures including irrigation debridement of an open right tibia fracture, intramedullary rod fixation of the right distal tibia, closed reduction of the right fibula fracture, and application of a negative pressure dressing. 

The patient's physical examination upon discharge showed stable vital signs and no acute distress. The right lower extremity showed intact sensation to light touch in various distributions and a dorsalis pedis pulse of 1+. The distal extremity was warm and well perfused with a capillary refill of less than 2 seconds. The compartments were soft with no pain on passive range of motion of toes. The incisions were clean, dry, and intact with no erythema or discharge.

Lab results indicated abnormal RBC, Hgb, and Hct values. The patient had acute blood loss anemia post-operatively, but remained asymptomatic except for mild tachycardia. Despite the risks, the patient refused blood transfusion. Her hematocrit stabilized and she was deemed stable for discharge by the senior orthopedic surgery staff. She was started on iron supplementation per her request. 

During her hospital stay, the patient was evaluated for her breast mass and a biopsy was performed to guide further workup and treatment as an outpatient. She was given perioperative antibiotics and anticoagulation per routine. 

Upon admission, the patient was not on any medications. At the time of discharge, the patient was prescribed Acetaminophen, Docusate Sodium, Enoxaparin Sodium, Ferrous Sulfate, Multivitamins with minerals, and OxycoDONE. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of an open right tibia/fibula fracture. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory but required assistance or aid. The patient was discharged home with service.
label: controls

summary:
The male patient was admitted to the neurosurgery department following a fall six weeks prior. His chief complaint was a headache that had been persisting for three weeks. His past medical history includes hypertension, high cholesterol, diabetes, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH). He has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. Upon admission, he was taking Zetia, Lipitor, Metformin, and Omeprazole.

The patient's physical examination was largely normal. He was awake, alert, and cooperative with the exam. His speech was fluent with good comprehension and repetition, and he was oriented to person, place, and date. His cranial nerves were intact, and he had normal motor function and sensation. His extremities were warm and well-perfused.

A CT scan of the head revealed an extensive left hemispheric extra-axial fluid collection with a maximal thickness of about 2 cm. The fluid showed variable attenuation with focal high attenuation in the inferior aspect, suggesting an acute-on-subacute or chronic chronicity. There was considerable mass effect with effacement of the adjacent sulci, distortion of the left lateral ventricle, and midline shift of about 6 mm. However, the gray-white matter differentiation was preserved and there was no evidence of ischemic infarct or intra-axial hemorrhage.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a left-sided burr hole procedure twice for the evacuation of the fluid. He tolerated the procedure well and was transferred to the post-anesthesia care unit (PACU) intubated. There were no complaints of motor weakness, speech difficulty, or facial weakness.

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with a left subdural hematoma. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. His discharge instructions included daily incision checks, pain management, exercise limitations, wound care, fluid and fiber intake, medication instructions, and symptoms to watch for that would require immediate medical attention.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of diabetes mellitus (DM), hypertension (HTN), anemia, constipation, prostatitis, subdural hematoma, benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), aortic stenosis, and bilateral inguinal hernia, was admitted due to abdominal pain and constipation. He had not had a bowel movement for seven days, despite normally having one per day and taking stool softeners at home. He reported passing gas and feeling abdominal distension and discomfort. He denied any fevers, nausea, chills, vomiting, or difficulty with urination, but did note left-sided scrotal soreness. 

The patient had previously undergone a left inguinal hernia repair, aortic valve replacement, and an evacuation procedure. His medications upon admission included amoxicillin, atorvastatin, ezetimibe, finasteride, Lisinopril, metformin, nystatin, omeprazole, oxycodone, tadalafil, tamsulosin, aspirin, Colace, MTV, vitamin E, and iron. 

Physical examination revealed a soft, non-tender, non-distended abdomen, and a left suprapubic incision with mild swelling. His extremities were warm and well perfused. Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.52), Hgb (10.8), Hct (33.2), RDWSD (47.1), and glucose (107). 

Imaging studies revealed a single loop of dilated small bowel in the mid abdomen, moderate to large fecal loading, fluid and gas tracking along the left inguinal canal into the scrotum, scrotal wall edema, renal cysts, and a right inguinal hernia containing fat and fluid. No bowel herniation or obstruction was noted. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was observed by the surgery service. He was started on an aggressive bowel regimen, which resulted in him passing gas, having bowel movements, and experiencing decreased abdominal distension. His pain was well controlled with Tylenol, and he was tolerating a regular diet and voiding normally. He was discharged on the second day of hospitalization. His discharge diagnoses were abdominal pain and constipation.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female with a past medical history of depression and previous suicide attempts. She was admitted to the hospital following an overdose of an unknown quantity of Effexor, acyclovir, and lamotrigine. The patient's boyfriend found her in an altered state and she expressed a desire to end her life. She was somnolent and vomiting. The patient was intubated for airway protection due to her altered mental status and vomiting. She was given 2 liters of IVF and a bicarbonate drip was initiated. 

Upon admission, the patient's vital signs were recorded as 98.5  141  115/60  28  100% Mask. Her labs were significant for a CK level of 427. She was administered several medications including Etomidate, Rocuronium, Midazolam, Morphine Sulfate, and Ondansetron. 

Imaging showed that the endotracheal tube tip projects over the mid trachea and the enteric tube side port projects over the left upper quadrant. There was no evidence of pneumothorax. 

The patient has a history of self-injury via cutting and has had a total of 4 prior psychiatric hospitalizations. She has been on several medications including venlafaxine, lamotrigine, desvenlafaxine, sertraline, bupropion, fluoxetine, citalopram, vortioxetine, and alprazolam. 

Upon admission, her lab results showed abnormal levels of RBC (3.78), Neuts (77.3), Lymphs (15.9), Eos (0.7), AbsLymp (1.15), Glucose (155), HCO3 (20), CK(CPK) (427), Calcium (8.2), Phos (2.1), and Ethanol (46). Her pO2 was also abnormal at 72. 

Upon discharge, her lab results showed an abnormal Glucose level of 105. 

The patient was diagnosed with multiple ingestion overdose, depression, and a suicide attempt. She was discharged to an inpatient psychiatric hospital for further management of her depression and suicide attempt.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female college student, was admitted to the psychiatry service due to worsening depressive symptoms and suicidal ideation without a plan. She has a history of depression since her teenage years, with two previous hospitalizations and one self-harm attempt by cutting. She reported feeling overwhelmed by academic pressures and a recent breakup, leading to feelings of guilt, hopelessness, and difficulty concentrating. She also reported insomnia, anhedonia, and decreased energy and motivation. She has been taking lamotrigine 200mg daily for several years for mood stabilization.

During her hospital stay, her medication regimen was augmented with Abilify for anti-depressive and anti-psychotic effects. Her mood improved over the course of her stay, and she denied any auditory or visual hallucinations, and active or passive suicidal or homicidal ideation at the time of discharge. She also had one episode of pre-syncope with a fall, which occurred after straining during a bowel movement on a day of minimal oral intake and increased anxiety. This was managed with IV fluids and she had no further symptoms during her hospitalization.

Her physical and neurological exams were largely unremarkable, with no significant abnormalities noted. Her laboratory results showed a hemoglobin level of 11.9 and a hematocrit level of 35.8, both of which were marked as abnormal. Her urine drug screen was negative for several substances including benzodiazepines, barbiturates, opiates, cocaine, amphetamines, and methadone.

The patient has a history of self-injury, but denies any recent self-injurious behavior. She has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. She has no significant family history. She was discharged home with instructions to continue all medications as directed and to follow up with all outpatient appointments. Her discharge diagnosis was Mood disorder NOS.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted for a right L5-S1 microdiscectomy due to right S1 radiculopathy. She had been experiencing leg and buttock pain, with sharp stabbing pain in her right thigh and buttocks. She also reported numbness in her calf. The pain was reportedly worse after ESI wore off, but sitting up seemed to alleviate it. She denied any low back pain. 

Her past medical history includes Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA) and Hypertension (HTN). Her family history includes obesity and bipolar disorder. Upon physical examination, she was found to be fully oriented and responsive. Her pupils were normal and her face was symmetric. Her speech was fluent and comprehension was intact. She had continued right calf numbness and numbness between her first two toes. Her surgical incision was healing well with no signs of redness, swelling, open areas, or drainage. There were no signs of CSF leak.

Her lab results showed an elevated WBC count at 13.7. Other lab results including RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, RDWSD, Plt, PTT, Glucose, UreaN, Creat, Na, K, Cl, HCO3, AnGap, Calcium, Phos, and Mg were within normal ranges.

During her hospital stay, she underwent an elective right L5-S1 laminectomy and microdiscectomy. Post-operatively, she was kept in a flat position for 12 hours due to an incidental durotomy. She required CPAP while laying flat due to respiratory concerns. On post-op day 1, she was tolerating a diet, ambulating without issues, and her pain was controlled on an oral regimen. On post-op day 2, she reported no headaches and there was no sign of CSF leakage from the lumbar wound.

Upon admission, she was on Metoprolol Succinate XL 50 mg PO BID. She was discharged with a clear and coherent mental status, alert and interactive level of consciousness, and independent ambulatory status. Her discharge diagnosis was right S1 radiculopathy.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to a cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leak. She had previously undergone an L5-S1 laminectomy and microdiscectomy, which was complicated by an intraoperative CSF leak that was repaired. She later presented with postural headaches and was found to have a lumbar CSF leak on MRI. She was admitted for elective lumbar wound exploration, dural repair, bilateral pedicle musculocutaneous flap with plastics, and placement of a lumbar drain. 

Her past medical history includes obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), hypertension (HTN), and a right L5-S1 microdiscectomy. Her family history includes obesity and bipolar disorder. 

Upon discharge, the patient was awake and resting comfortably. Her physical exam was largely unremarkable, with no signs of respiratory distress or edema. Her incisions on her back were noted, along with 2 JP drains, an LP drain, and a Wound Vac. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent lumbar wound exploration and closure of spinal wound with bilateral pedicle musculocutaneous flap. A lumbar drain was placed intraoperatively by the neurosurgery team, and 2 JP drains and a Prevena wound vac were placed by the Plastics team. The patient was started on Ancef post-op, which was continued while drains were in place. There were two instances where the lumbar drain became disconnected from the drainage setup, but these were promptly addressed. The lumbar drain was clamped on post-op day 3 and removed on post-op day 4 without any worsening of the patient's headache. The patient's foley catheter was removed once the drain was removed. On post-op day 5, the patient's wound vac was changed to prevena, antibiotics were changed from IV to PO, and the PICC was discontinued. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Acetaminophen 500mg-1000mg q6h PRN, Ibuprofen 200mg 4 tabs up to TID, and Metoprolol 25mg bid. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of CSF leak and lumbar wound. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was able to ambulate independently. She was discharged home with services and a plan for close follow-up with plastic surgery and neurosurgery.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of type 2 diabetes complicated by neuropathy and nephropathy, end-stage renal disease (ESRD) on hemodialysis, cirrhosis, COPD, Hep C, atrial fibrillation, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a history of stroke with no residual deficits, was admitted due to concerns of a right foot infection. He has a history of multiple skin and soft tissue infections (MRSA, MSSA, pseudomonas), and a history of above knee amputation of the left leg. He reported a foul odor from his foot and an ulcer on his pinky toe. He also reported frequent falls due to loss of balance when transferring from his bed to his wheelchair. 

Upon admission, the patient was given Acetaminophen 1000 mg, IV Morphine Sulfate 4 mg, and IV Levofloxacin 750 mg. An x-ray of the foot showed probable osteomyelitis involving the proximal phalanx of the pinky toe. Soft tissue gas appears localized to the site of lateral forefoot ulcer. 

The patient's family history includes a brother diagnosed with diabetes and a father who had colon cancer and a heart attack. 

Physical examination revealed a malodorous, large necrotic ulcer which probes to bone on the right foot, multiple black and bleeding scabs along the right lower extremity, and absence of touch sensation bilaterally from mid shin to toes. The patient was also easily agitated and defensive. 

Admission labs showed abnormal values for glucose (118), creatinine (2.9), chloride (93), CK(CPK) (29), phosphate (2.2), CRP (170.1), WBC (15.0), RBC (3.28), HGB (10.0), HCT (29.2), NEUTS (87.2), LYMPHS (5.9), EOS (0.3), AbsNeut (13.04), AbsLymp (0.88), AbsMono (0.84). 

Imaging showed a large soft tissue ulcer along the lateral forefoot with probable osteomyelitis involving the proximal phalanx of the pinky toe. Soft tissue gas appears localized to the site of lateral forefoot ulcer. 

Microbiology results showed mixed bacterial flora, bacteroides, and MSSA. 

The patient's hospital course was complicated by severe infection of the right foot, acute osteomyelitis with concern for associated necrotizing fasciitis, and peripheral vascular disease. He underwent right foot excisional debridement and was started on broad spectrum antibiotics. He clinically improved but left against medical advice before a dose of IV antibiotics. 

The patient's discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (2.82), Hgb (8.1), Hct (25.2), and Creat (4.5), Na (126), Cl (88). 

The patient was discharged home with a plan for additional IV antibiotic course to be given at dialysis. He was prescribed Aspirin 81 mg daily, Atorvastatin 40 mg daily, CeFAZolin 3 g IV POST HD (FR), CeFAZolin 2 g IV POST HD (MO,WE), and MetroNIDAZOLE 500 mg PO Q8H.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a significant past medical history of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) complicated by neuropathy, nephropathy, and End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) on Hemodialysis (HD), presented with a worsening plantar heel ulceration. The patient has a history of multiple amputations due to diabetic wounds. He reported an increase in drainage and foul-smelling odor from the wound. The patient underwent right foot wound debridement down to the bone, angioplasty of the right external iliac, thoracentesis, chest tube placement and removal, and mediastinal lymph node biopsy. 

Upon arrival, the patient was afebrile and his vitals were stable. Labs were within normal limits except for a Creatinine of 4.0, Hemoglobin of 11.1, and INR of 1.4. A foot X-ray showed loss of cortical detail at the distal aspect of the cuboid, potentially indicating osteomyelitis. The patient was started on Linezolid and Ceftazidime. Wound aerobic/anaerobic culture and gram stain were sent, as well as blood cultures. 

The patient's past medical history includes cirrhosis, Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), Hepatitis C, atrial fibrillation, ESRD on HD, T2DM complicated by peripheral neuropathy, nephropathy, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, left above-knee amputation, and a history of cerebrovascular accident with no residual deficits. His family history includes a brother diagnosed with diabetes mellitus and a father who had colon cancer and a heart attack. 

Physical examination revealed a large necrotic ulcer on the right foot which probed to the bone. There was an absence of touch sensation bilaterally from mid-shin to toes. The patient was agitated and upset. 

Admission labs showed abnormal results including RBC of 3.61, Hemoglobin of 11.1, Hct of 34.3, Glucose of 107, Creatinine of 4.0, and pleural fluid with TNC of 1214, RBC of 2999, Polys of 2, Lymphs of 73, and Monos of 16. Flow cytometry immunophenotyping revealed involvement by a CD5 negative/CD10 negative and kappa restricted B-cell lymphoma. 

Imaging studies showed loss of cortical detail at the distal aspect of the cuboid, potentially indicating osteomyelitis. There was also severe multilevel arterial insufficiency, a large volume of right pleural effusion with thickening, and a markedly enhancing left renal lower pole mass. 

Upon discharge, the patient's labs showed abnormal results including WBC of 13.1, RBC of 3.77, Hemoglobin of 11.7, Hct of 35.8, Glucose of 102, Urea Nitrogen of 46, Creatinine of 6.2, Sodium of 130, Chloride of 88, and Calcium of 7.5. 

The patient was diagnosed with a right foot soft tissue infection and B cell lymphoma. He also has peripheral vascular disease and ESRD on HD. The patient was discharged with instructions to continue taking antibiotics for the next 6 weeks and to follow up with his oncologist.
label: controls

summary:
The male patient was admitted for an exostectomy to the right heel due to a persistent ulcer. The patient had a previous infection in the same area that had been incised and drained. The ulcer was complicated by an infection and a prominent plantar heel bone causing continuous pressure to the area. The patient underwent a right ulcer excision and exostectomy, followed by a course of IV antibiotics and rehabilitation.

The patient has a medical history of Type 2 Diabetes, Chronic Renal Insufficiency with a baseline of 2.3, Hypertension, Hyperlipidemia, a Cerebrovascular Accident, Right Charcot Foot, and Peripheral Neuropathy. The patient's family history is non-contributory. Upon physical examination, the patient's right foot had a plantar ulcer, but no erythema or purulent drainage was observed.

Several abnormal lab results were noted during the patient's stay. The patient had consistently low RBC, Hgb, Hct, and MCH values. The WBC count was elevated on multiple occasions, peaking at 13.6. The ESR was also elevated at 59. The patient's Urea Nitrogen and Creatinine levels were consistently high, indicating possible kidney issues. The patient's HCO3 was low on multiple occasions, and the patient's glucose level was high at 114. The patient's calcium level was low on two occasions, and the albumin level was also low at 3.2. The patient's CRP was elevated at 13.7, indicating possible inflammation.

The patient's hospital course was uneventful. The patient was followed at an outside hospital clinic and the surgery was performed without complications. A JP drain was placed within the incision. The patient had been growing MRSA and resistant E. coli, which were sensitive to vancomycin and zosyn. Culture results from this admission showed MRSA. The patient remained afebrile throughout the hospital course. The JP drain was removed on hospital day 3 and the site of the drain was closed with one suture on hospital day 4. The patient was discharged with home services following an uneventful course at the hospital.

Upon admission, the patient was taking Aggrenox, Metoprolol XL, Amlodipine, Lisinopril, Simvastatin, Benicar, folic acid, potassium, Prandin, and Rolaids for low Calcium.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Type 2 Diabetes, Chronic Renal Insufficiency, Hypertension, Hyperlipidemia, a previous Cerebrovascular Accident (CVA), Right Charcot Foot, and Peripheral Neuropathy, presented with acute renal failure and an infected right foot. He had recently experienced a bout of ongoing nausea and vomiting. Upon examination, he was found to have an elevated White Blood Cell (WBC) count, Acute Renal Failure (ARF), and orthostatic hypotension. There was concern for an acute infection of his right heel. He was given Intravenous Fluids (IVF) and transferred to a facility where he is followed by podiatry. 

The patient underwent two debridement procedures on his right foot, with the implantation of antibiotic-coated beads. His wound cultures revealed Gram-Negative Rods (GNRs), Gram-Positive Cocci (GPC), and Bacteroides fragilis. Calcaneal tissue removed from the operating room revealed acute osteomyelitis. Blood cultures were negative. He was started on IV vancomycin and zosyn, renally dosed, and will need to complete a 6-week course of antibiotics. 

The patient was found to be in Atrial Fibrillation (AF) with Rapid Ventricular Response (RVR) to the 140s. He was given a total of 10 mg IV metoprolol with little response. IV diltiazem was then utilized, with the patient coming under good heart rate control after 10 mg IV. 

Lab results on admission showed abnormal values for WBC (20.8*#), Red Blood Cell (RBC) count (3.73*), Hemoglobin (HGB) (11.7*), Hematocrit (HCT) (34.6*), Neutrophils (88.1*), Lymphocytes (6.7*), Platelet count (579*#), Urea Nitrogen (56*), Creatinine (5.3*), Sodium (131*), Total CO2 (10*), Anion Gap (25*), and Troponin T (0.02*). Creatine Kinase (CK) was also abnormal (24*). 

Imaging studies revealed Charcot changes in the right foot with soft tissue swelling and air within the adjacent soft tissues, highly suggestive of infection. A bone scan was recommended to confirm this. There was no acute cardiopulmonary process observed on chest X-ray. Renal ultrasound showed no evidence for obstruction, mass, or nephrolithiasis, and no evidence for renal artery stenosis. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Amlodipine, Aggrenox, Folic acid, Lisinopril, Toprol XL, Olmesartan, Prandin, and Simvastatin. His family history was non-contributory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of fibromyalgia, GERD, hypercholesterolemia, insomnia, and possible hypertension, was admitted to the OB/GYN service due to urinary incontinence and vaginal prolapse. She reported experiencing mixed incontinence for several years, with symptoms worsening recently. She also reported vaginal pressure, palpable prolapse, and constipation. She denied any dysuria, hematuria, UTIs, kidney stones, or pyelonephritis. She had previously been fitted with a pessary, which she wore for approximately 15 months, and was treated with oxybutynin for her urinary incontinence, but her symptoms persisted. 

During her stay, she underwent a robotic assisted laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, sacrocervicopexy, suburethral sling, and cystoscopy. Her post-operative course was uncomplicated, with pain managed by IV Dilaudid and Toradol initially, and then transitioned to oral pain medications (oxycodone, tylenol, ibuprofen). She was also continued on her home medications for fibromyalgia and GERD. Her diet was advanced without difficulty and she was able to void spontaneously. 

Upon admission, her medications included anise tea, chlorthalidone as needed for blood pressure, ciclopirox cream, gabapentin 300mg three times daily, ibuprofen 600mg twice daily, linzess 290mg daily, omeprazole 20mg daily, and align. 

Her physical exam on the day of discharge showed no significant abnormalities. Her vital signs were within normal limits, her abdomen was soft, mildly distended, and mildly tender in the lower quadrants, with no rebound or guarding. Her incisions appeared clean, dry, and intact. 

She was discharged with a diagnosis of cystocele, in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and able to ambulate independently. Her family history was reported as non-contributory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female under the Neurology service, was admitted due to an intraparenchymal hemorrhage. She was found disoriented by a housekeeper and was taken to the nearest hospital. A CT head scan revealed a 3.4 X 3.1 IPH in the left corona radiata, posterior putamen, and posterior limb of the internal capsule. Despite being initially stable, she became unrousable and was given 10 mg of vitamin K, 2 U FFP, and Proplex. A repeat scan showed an increase in the size of the IPH. The patient's family initially decided to make her DNR/DNI, but later she was made CMO.

Her past medical history includes atrial fibrillation on Coumadin, CHF, pneumonia, hypertension, and a compression fracture due to osteoporosis. Her family history is non-contributory. Upon admission, her physical exam showed her to be unresponsive to stimuli, with increased tone in the right leg and arm, and abnormal reflexes. Her cranial nerves showed abnormalities, including a deviated right eye, barely reactive pupils, and weak corneal reflexes and gag reflex.

Abnormal lab results included a low RBC count of 3.31, low HGB of 10.4, low HCT of 31.1, and high glucose of 151. Her PTT was also high at 38.8. A CT head scan without contrast showed an interval enlargement of the left hemispheric hemorrhagic lesion with new mass effect on the left lateral ventricle and a 2 mm rightward shift of midline structures.

During her hospital stay, the patient's condition was managed with comfort measures only, with the involvement of the Palliative Care team. She was discharged to an extended care facility. Her medications upon admission included Alendronate 70 mg, Lasix 40 mg QDay, Potassium 10 mEq, multivitamins, Ostercal, and Coumadin 5 mg QDay. Her discharge diagnosis was intracerebral hemorrhage.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted to the neurosurgery unit with chief complaints of nausea, vomiting, and headaches persisting for a week. He had previously checked himself into a detox program due to hypertension, where he was given clonidine. His symptoms returned after a week, and he was given Decadron and clonidine at an outside hospital (OSH). A CT scan at the OSH revealed hydrocephalus, and he was transferred for further treatment. He denied experiencing blurred vision but reported ataxia. 

The patient's past medical history includes back pain, L5-S1 MD, L4-5 HNP, musculoskeletal disease, bilateral thoracotomy for pneumothorax, Hepatitis C, right keratitis, gastrointestinal upset, and right epididymo-orchitis. His family history was not disclosed. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be comfortable, with a blood pressure of 127/88, heart rate of 56, respiratory rate of 13, and oxygen saturation of 98% on room air. His pupils were reactive to light, and his fundoscopic exam was suspicious for papilledema. His mental status was normal, and he was oriented to person, place, and date. His cranial nerves were intact, and his motor and sensory functions were normal. 

The patient underwent a posterior fossa craniotomy for tumor resection. Post-operative CT scans showed expected changes, and MRI scans revealed small foci of enhancement along the resection margin, likely vascular in etiology. Fluid was found in the resection cavity, extending into the suprajacent posterior soft tissues. 

CT scans from the OSH showed hydrocephalus, tonsillar herniation, and a possible mass. Further CT scans revealed a cerebellar mass, likely a metastasis given the lung mass on concurrent CT torso, and unchanged hydrocephalus. A CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast showed a 7.6 x 7.3 x 6 cm soft tissue mass in the right lower lobe of the lung, concerning for lung cancer. Other findings included a 7 mm right lower lobe nodule and a 6.5 mm right middle lobe nodule, which may represent metastases, a small loculated right pleural effusion adjacent to the large mass, severe emphysematous changes with bullous formation in the lungs bilaterally, 5 mm lytic foci in the left ilium, which raise the possibility of metastatic disease, and a wedge deformity of T12 vertebra, likely chronic due to prior trauma. 

The patient's hospital course included consultation with the Oncology service regarding his lung mass, and he consented to liver function tests and HIV testing. An arm abscess was noted on his right arm, which he reported as a common occurrence due to improper heroin injection. The abscess was drained, and he was started on clindamycin. His MRSA screen was positive, and he was put on precautions. He underwent a sub-occipital craniotomy and resection of the tumor, which was completed without complications. His wound culture from his right elbow grew mixed bacteria, but was sensitive to clindamycin. 

The patient's medications upon admission were not disclosed. His discharge diagnoses included a posterior fossa tumor, hydrocephalus, a lung mass, an iliac crest lesion, and an arm abscess. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory upon discharge.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for hepatocellular carcinoma. His past medical history includes HBV-related cirrhosis and tympanic membrane grafting. The patient underwent a Segment V resection, cholecystectomy, and intraoperative ultrasound. The procedure was performed due to a 3.7 x 3.5 cm macrolobulated mass consistent with hepatocellular carcinoma along the border of segment V and IVB, as revealed by an MRI. The patient's liver biopsy showed moderately differentiated hepatocellular carcinoma. 

The patient's family history includes his mother on HD and his father deceased from 'lung problems'. Upon admission, the patient was on Hepsera 10mg qd. 

Several abnormal lab results were noted during his stay. These include GLUCOSE-111*, K+-2.___*, HGB-11.5*, ALT(SGPT)-57*, AST(SGOT)-97*, ALK PHOS-35*, RBC-2.94*, Hgb-9.6*, Hct-27.4*, MCH-32.6*, RBC-2.88*, Hgb-9.5*, Hct-26.4*, MCH-33.2*, Glucose-116*, and ALT-86*. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was given intermittent doses of IV morphine, which was later switched to PCA morphine for improved pain control. On the fifth day post-operation, the patient complained of abdominal bloating, which was managed with Dulcolax pr. His liver function tests initially increased on the first day post-operation but trended down by the day of discharge. 

The pathology report from the liver resection showed hepatocellular carcinoma, moderate portal mononuclear cell inflammation, septal fibrosis with partial nodules, and moderate iron deposition in periportal hepatocytes. The gallbladder showed marked desiccation artifact but no tumor. 

The patient was discharged in stable condition with instructions to monitor for fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain/distension, jaundice, redness or drainage from the incision, and to continue home medications as previously ordered.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to diarrhea. He had a history of hepatitis B virus related hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) and had undergone a segment V liver resection and cholecystectomy. He was discharged four days prior to this admission and had been feeling well until he experienced nausea and diarrhea after eating soup. He vomited his food and had watery diarrhea approximately 10 times, without any blood. He also reported moderate abdominal pain and intermittent pain in his right flank. 

His past medical history includes hepatitis B virus (HBV) related cirrhosis and tympanic membrane grafting. His mother is on hemodialysis and his father passed away due to lung problems. 

Upon physical examination, his abdomen was mildly tender in the periumbilical area and he had a small lipoma in the left lower quadrant. His incision was healing well without any drainage, induration, or surrounding erythema. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.48), Hgb (11.2), Hct (32.3), MCH (32.2), glucose (134), and ALT (45). He was given IV hydration and stool studies were sent. By the second hospital day, he was tolerating a full diet and had only one loose stool on the day of discharge. 

Upon admission, he was taking Adefovir 10 mg daily and Percocet as needed. There was a question raised about whether Lamivudine should be added to his regimen for Hepatitis B, but no changes were made at this time as his viral load was reported as negative. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of resolved diarrhea. He was instructed to call the doctor's office if he experienced fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, inability to take or keep down medications, or any other concerning symptoms. He was also advised to monitor his incision for redness, drainage, or swelling, and to avoid heavy lifting and driving if taking narcotic pain medication.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for a liver transplant due to hepatocellular carcinoma. His medical history includes HBV related cirrhosis and tympanic membrane grafting. He was on Hepsera (Adefovir) 10mg daily for his suppressed hepatitis B. He reported no recent abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fevers, chills, cough, or changes in urination or bowel habits. He also denied any history of abdominal swelling, vomiting blood, or blood in his bowel movements. He had occasional hematuria, but a cystoscopy showed no abnormalities. His HBV antibody and antigen levels were pending.

During his hospital stay, he underwent an orthotopic liver transplant using the piggyback method. Postoperatively, he was stable and his liver function tests (LFTs) initially increased as expected but later trended down. Ultrasounds showed patent portal vein, hepatic veins, and hepatic artery. A CT scan of the abdomen/pelvis showed a small amount of fluid and air in the porta hepatitis but no organized fluid collection. A 3-mm right middle lobe lung nodule was noted, unchanged from previous scans. 

His vital signs remained stable throughout his stay and he was able to ambulate initially with assistance and later independently. He experienced some hyperglycemia, which was managed with long acting and short acting sliding scale insulin. He was able to self-inject insulin and medication teaching went well. 

Abnormal lab results included a WBC count of 13.6*, RBC count of 3.51*, Hemoglobin level of 11.4*, Hematocrit level of 33.6*, MCH level of 32.4*, PTT level of 19.3*, AST level of 2770*, Direct Bilirubin level of 0.5*, and ALT level of 242* and Alkaline Phosphatase level of 151*. 

The patient's family history includes his mother on hemodialysis and his father deceased from lung problems. He was discharged on postoperative day 8 with home services coordinated. His discharge diagnoses were HBV, hepatocellular carcinoma, hyperglycemia related to steroids, and bile leak. His condition at discharge was good.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of COPD, was admitted due to fever, hypoxemia, and shortness of breath. She has a history of recurrent bacterial and fungal pneumonia, and had recently undergone a left upper lobectomy. She was previously treated with voriconazole, Zosyn, and vancomycin, with the latter two being discontinued after a negative MRSA screen. She was readmitted after developing a non-productive cough and fever, and was found to have new hypoxemia. She also reported some gastrointestinal symptoms, including small volume emesis. 

Upon admission, her temperature was 102.7, but she was hemodynamically stable. Her oxygen saturation was between 91-97% on 3L NC. Lab results showed no leukocytosis, and her lactate levels were normal. Her chest X-ray showed status post left upper lobectomy and fibrosis, but no definite superimposed acute consolidation. The ED team consulted with Infectious Diseases, who agreed with restarting Vanc/Zosyn in addition to continuing home voriconazole.

Her past medical history includes hyperlipidemia, COPD, extensive history of pulmonary infection and cavitation, hyponatremia, peripheral edema, anxiety, and depression. Her family history includes a sister with lung cancer and a father with mesothelioma.

Physical examination upon admission showed the patient to be alert and in no apparent distress. She had moderate air movement, no wheezing, and mild right basilar crackles. Her extremities were warm, with 1+ pitting edema. Neurologically, she was alert and oriented, with symmetric facial movements and no dysarthria.

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (2.81), Hgb (8.2), Hct (26.6), MCHC (30.8), RDW (18.5), RDWSD (63.4), Lymphs (13.8), and Monos (13.9). Her urine test showed trace amounts of protein. Microbiology results showed no growth in blood cultures, no MRSA isolated, and negative for Legionella serogroup 1 antigen. Her urine culture showed mixed bacterial flora, consistent with skin and/or genital contamination. Imaging studies showed no new consolidation to explain the patient's symptoms.

Upon discharge, her WBC was 5.5, RBC was 2.70, Hgb was 7.7, Hct was 25.3, MCHC was 30.4, RDW was 18.4, RDWSD was 62.5, and AnGap was 9. Her discharge diagnosis was viral upper respiratory infection. She was discharged home with services, and her mental status was clear and coherent. She was alert and interactive, but required assistance or aid for ambulation.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) requiring oxygen at home, was admitted due to dyspnea. She had a recent history of bacterial and fungal pneumonia and had undergone a left upper lobectomy. Prior to this admission, she had been treated for dyspnea, hypoxia, and fever, and was diagnosed with a viral upper respiratory infection. After a brief period at home, she returned with worsening shortness of breath, hypoxia on exertion, general weakness, and poor oral intake. She did not report any fevers, chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, or chest pain. She did mention chronic right scapular pain. 

Upon admission, her vital signs were stable and her labs showed a hemoglobin level of 8.9, BNP 805, troponin less than 0.01, normal chemistry panel, normal lactate, VBG 7.47/38, and she tested positive for flu. Her chest x-ray showed stable chronic changes including status post left upper lobectomy changes, chronic blunting of left costophrenic angle, fibrotic changes particularly at the lung bases, and severe pulmonary emphysema. No new focal consolidation was seen. 

She was given nebulizers, IV methylprednisolone 80 mg, IV azithromycin, oseltamivir, guaifenesin, famotidine, voriconazole, and acetaminophen. She reported feeling slightly better upon arrival to the floor and stated that her shortness of breath did not feel like a COPD exacerbation. 

Her past medical history includes hyperlipidemia, COPD, extensive history of pulmonary infection and cavitation, hyponatremia, peripheral edema, anxiety, and depression. Her family history includes a sister with lung cancer and a father with mesothelioma. 

On physical examination, she was alert and oriented, with diminished breath sounds and 1+ pitting edema to mid-calf. Her pupils were non-reactive to light, likely due to bilateral cataract surgery. 

Her admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.10), hemoglobin (8.9), hematocrit (28.8), MCHC (30.9), RDW (18.6), RDWSD (63.1), eosinophils (0.6), absolute eosinophils (0.03), pO2 (46), pH (7.47), and she tested positive for flu. Her discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (2.94), hemoglobin (8.4), hematocrit (28.4), MCHC (29.6), RDW (19.0), RDWSD (66.6), and anion gap (5). 

Imaging showed stable chronic changes in her lungs, including status post left upper lobectomy changes, chronic blunting of the left costophrenic angle, fibrotic changes particularly at the lung bases, and severe pulmonary emphysema. There was no new focal consolidation seen. A CT scan showed an indeterminate nodular opacity in the right upper lobe, severe pulmonary emphysema, and unchanged bilateral pulmonary fibrosis. 

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with flu, dyspnea, hypoxia, pulmonary aspergillosis, and hospital-acquired pneumonia. She continued to lose weight, likely due to pulmonary cachexia and ongoing illness. Her COPD was managed with home oxygen and nebulizers, and her anxiety and depression were managed with citalopram. She was also treated for gastroesophageal reflux disease with ranitidine. 

Upon discharge, she was diagnosed with hospital-acquired pneumonia and failure to thrive, in addition to her secondary diagnoses of COPD, pulmonary aspergillosis, anxiety, depression, and GERD. Her discharge medications included levofloxacin, arformoterol, ascorbic acid, aspirin, budesonide, citalopram, famotidine, guaifenesin, ipratropium bromide, iron polysaccharides complex, multivitamins, thiamine, vitamin D, and voriconazole. She was discharged to extended care.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) requiring home oxygen, chronic cavitary aspergillosis, polymicrobial lung abscess, severe bullous lung disease post left upper lobe (LUL) resection, presumed right upper lobe (RUL) primary lung cancer treated with CyberKnife XRT, and Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD), presented to the emergency department with dyspnea on exertion and worsening hypoxia. She reported progressive shortness of breath requiring over 4L oxygen, yellow productive sputum, worsening productive cough, subjective chills, and palpitations with exertional hypoxia. She denied wheezing, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, sore throat, chest pain, abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. She had been on Augmentin with no improvement in symptoms and had diarrhea after starting the medication. 

Her past medical history includes hyperlipidemia, extensive history of pulmonary infection and cavitation, hyponatremia, peripheral edema, anxiety, and depression. Her family history includes a sister with lung cancer and a father with mesothelioma. 

On physical examination, she was found to have a 2cm soft, mobile left submandibular lymph node. Her lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally. 

Lab results showed abnormal lactate (2.1), pO2 (20), pCO2 (56), pH (7.32), RBC (3.37), MCV (112), MCH (35.3), MCHC (31.5), and RDWSD (55.0). Her eosinophils were also elevated (7.4). 

CTA showed no evidence of pulmonary embolism or acute aortic abnormality. There were postsurgical changes post left upper lobectomy, interval development of well corticated superior endplate deformity at T4 and a well corticated wedge compression fracture of the vertebral body of T8, severe pulmonary emphysema, unchanged bilateral pulmonary fibrosis, and an indeterminate nodular opacity at the right upper lobe. 

Her medications upon admission included Citalopram, Famotidine, Fluticasone Propionate, Mirtazapine, Tiotropium Bromide, Aspirin, Vitamin D, Ferrous Sulfate, GuaiFENesin ER, Vancomycin Oral Liquid, arformoterol, and Budesonide. 

The patient's hypoxia with increased O2 requirement was most likely due to a COPD exacerbation. There were no findings concerning for pneumonia or pulmonary embolism on chest CT. She responded to a 5-day course of prednisone and azithromycin and nebulizer treatments. Her creatinine was 1.0 throughout hospitalization, with a baseline of .6-.7. She had diarrhea for a few days and was started on oral vancomycin as prophylaxis while on azithromycin.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) on 2L nocturnal O2, hyperlipidemia, and depression, was admitted due to shortness of breath. She had been experiencing progressively worsening dyspnea and fatigue, intermittent chills, low-grade fevers, and a decline in functional status. She also reported hoarseness. CT scans revealed a non-resolving left upper lobe (LUL) infiltrate. A bronchoscopy was performed, but it was negative for micro and pathology. 

The patient's family history includes a sister with lung cancer and a father with mesothelioma. Upon admission, her medications included aspirin, atorvastatin, vitamin D, citalopram, guaifenesin ER, albuterol, Symbicort, multivitamins, tiotropium bromide, and prednisone.

Abnormal lab results on admission included a white blood cell count of 15.1, red blood cell count of 2.81, hemoglobin of 8.7, hematocrit of 27.0, and potassium of 3.2. Upon discharge, her white blood cell count was 14.6, red blood cell count was 3.39, hemoglobin was 10.5, hematocrit was 33.1, glucose was 53, sodium was 134, and chloride was 91. 

Microbiology results showed the presence of yeast, Aspergillus fumigatus complex, mixed bacterial flora, Aspergillus species, Prevotella species, and viridans streptococci. A CT scan of the chest with contrast showed a large cavitary infection in the left lower lobe with partial improvement, severe pulmonary emphysema with mild bibasilar fibrosis, and numerous small mediastinal lymph nodes. A transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) showed mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy with normal cavity size and global biventricular systolic function, no clinically significant valvular regurgitation or stenosis, and mild pulmonary hypertension.

During her hospital stay, the patient was initially treated for bacterial pneumonia and a COPD exacerbation. She underwent a bronchoscopy which demonstrated evidence of fungal pneumonia. She was started on antibiotics and antifungal treatment, which led to improvement. Her discharge medications included cefpodoxime proxetil, metronidazole, voriconazole, albuterol, aspirin, guaifenesin ER, multivitamins, prednisone, Symbicort, tiotropium bromide, and vitamin D. 

The patient was diagnosed with acute on chronic hypoxic respiratory failure, acute bacterial pneumonia, acute fungal pneumonia, COPD with an acute flare, a cavitary LUL lesion, hyperlipidemia, anxiety, and GERD. She was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and able to ambulate independently.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a former smoker with a history of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD) on 2L nocturnal oxygen, was admitted due to worsening shortness of breath and hypoxemia. She had a recent admission for acute on chronic hypoxemic respiratory failure secondary to COPD exacerbation, bacterial and fungal pneumonia. She presented with a large left upper lobe (LUL) cavitary lesion and cultures grew strep viridans, prevotella, mixed flora and aspergillus fumigatus. She was discharged on voriconazole, along with cefpodoxime/metronidazole for 2-3 weeks. However, she returned to the hospital with fevers, fatigue, and dyspnea with desaturation on exertion. A repeat CT chest showed worsening fluid in the cavitary lesion. She was switched to vanc/zosyn/voriconazole. 

The patient also presented with serum Na 125 with urine lytes consistent with Syndrome of Inappropriate Antidiuretic Hormone Secretion (SIADH). She had some evidence of volume overload at the time of arrival, and she was diuresed and fluid restricted. Despite these interventions, sodium decreased to 118. Renal was consulted who recommended hypertonic saline and intensive monitoring for hyponatremia, requiring an ICU transfer.

The patient underwent a right PICC line placement, left thoracotomy and left upper lobectomy, mediastinal lymph node dissection, bronchoscopy with lavage, and intercostal muscle buttress to the bronchial stump. 

The patient's family history includes a sister with a history of lung cancer and a father with a history of mesothelioma. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Voriconazole, Albuterol, Aspirin, GuaiFENesin ER, Multivitamins, Symbicort, Tiotropium Bromide, Vitamin D, Cefpodoxime Proxetil, and MetroNIDAZOLE.

The patient's lab results showed abnormal values for WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCHC, RDW, RDWSD, Plt Ct, and Glucose. Her sodium levels were also abnormal, ranging from 120 to 136. Her chest CT showed a large left upper lobe cavitation filled with fluid and increasing surrounding lung parenchymal consolidation with possible additional areas of necrosis, concerning for worsening necrotizing pneumonia and pulmonic abscess. Severe pulmonary emphysema and bibasilar fibrosis were also noted. 

The patient's hospital course included management of hyponatremia, pneumonia, COPD, hoarse voice, peripheral edema, anxiety, and diarrhea. She underwent a left thoracotomy with a left upper lobectomy and decortication along with mediastinal lymph node dissection. She was transferred to the floor on postoperative day 6. Her voriconazole was transitioned to PO and her WBC remained stable. Her wounds were healing well. She was discharged to rehab for no more than a 30-day stay while she works with Physical Therapy from a mobility and pulmonary standpoint and continues IV antibiotic therapy.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female with a significant past medical history of ulcerative colitis, primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) with associated cirrhosis, splenomegaly, portal hypertension, esophageal varices, peripheral polyarthropathy, and osteopenia. She presented to her doctor due to fatigue for a few days. She has a long history of anemia, first diagnosed during her first pregnancy. She denies shortness of breath, hematemesis, hematochezia, melena, or menorrhagia. Her last menstrual period was of medium to light flow. Her labs revealed a hematocrit of 18.1 and she was told to come to the hospital for 2 units of blood. The complete blood count also revealed a hematocrit of 18.0 with low mean corpuscular volume, a platelet count of 126, and an elevated alkaline phosphatase at 421. She also has a skin lesion on her right anterior shin that started as a 'pimple-like lesion' the size of a nickel that she picked at. Now the lesion is a 5 cm by 5 cm ulcerated violaceous lesion with dark purple borders with surrounding erythema. It is painful when touched. She had a similar lesion a few months prior treated with prednisone, antibiotics, and topical steroids.

The patient has no family history of gastrointestinal malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, pancreatic disease, or liver disease. On physical examination, she was found to have splenomegaly with the spleen palpable 5 cm below the left coastal margin and a 5 cm by 5 cm ulcerated violaceous lesion with dark purple borders with surrounding erythema.

Lab results showed glucose at 192, LDH at 89, alkaline phosphatase at 232, albumin at 2.8, calcium at 8.0, iron at 15, ferritin at 11, WBC at 2.6, RBC at 2.47, HGB at 4.2, HCT at 15.7, MCV at 64, MCH at 17.0, MCHC at 26.8, RDW at 22.9, platelet count at 100, reticulocyte manual count at 6.2, sodium at 132, potassium at 3.0, alkaline phosphatase at 243, GGT at 106, albumin at 2.9, WBC at 3.6, RBC at 2.83, HGB at 4.7, HCT at 18.0, MCV at 64, MCH at 16.7, MCHC at 26.1, RDW at 21.6, neutrophils at 75, lymphocytes at 16, and platelet count at 116.

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with severe microcytic anemia and was transfused 4 units of packed red blood cells. Her hemoglobin improved from 4.2 to 8.5 and her hematocrit improved from 15.7 to 26.9. She was also diagnosed with pyoderma gangrenosum, a skin condition, and was treated with topical clobetasol 0.5% applied to the outside edge of the lesion twice a day. She was also started on a 60mg prednisone taper. Her primary sclerosing cholangitis and ulcerative colitis were not active issues during this admission.

Upon admission, her medications included folic acid, furosemide, nadolol, spironolactone, sulfasalazine, ursodiol, calcium carbonate-vitamin D3, and ferrous gluconate.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of ulcerative colitis, cirrhosis, primary sclerosing cholangitis, chronic anemia requiring intermittent transfusions, and diabetes, was admitted due to fatigue. She had a fever of 101.3 and tachycardia. She had previously undergone an EGD and colonoscopy.
