She reported a mild nonproductive cough for 2 days prior to admission, but denied other symptoms such as abdominal pain, chest pain, shortness of breath, diarrhea, constipation, body aches, fevers, chills, headache, sore throat, rhinorrhea, nausea, and vomiting. She also reported small amounts of bright red blood per rectum for many years, last about a week ago, but denied melena. 

Upon admission, her labs were significant for H/H of 2.7/11.7, Na 123 (corrected 133), AG 14, glucose 460. UA was negative for ketones, + for 1000 glucose, 2 WBC. Lactate 1.6. Tbili 1.7. Blood cultures were drawn and flu swab performed, which came back positive. She received 1L IVF and 2 units PRBCs. 

Her past medical history includes ulcerative colitis, primary sclerosing cholangitis with associated cirrhosis, splenomegaly, portal hypertension, and esophageal varices, osteopenia, peripheral polyarthopathy, and pyoderma gangrenosum. She has no family history of gastrointestinal malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, pancreatic disease, or liver disease. 

On physical exam, she was alert, oriented, and not in acute distress. Her lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally, and her abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended. 

During her hospital stay, she was found to be positive for flu and was treated with tamiflu. She received 7 units of pRBCs for her anemia. Her colonoscopy showed severe pancolitis consistent with ulcerative colitis and many pseudopolyps. Iron studies were consistent with iron deficiency. She was discharged on home dose iron, ferrous gluconate 324mg tid. 

Her medications upon admission included folic acid, furosemide, nadolol, spironolactone, sulfasalazine, ursodiol, ferrous gluconate, and calcium 500 + D. 

Her discharge diagnoses included acute anemia, influenza, and hyperglycemia, as well as chronic ulcerative colitis, primary sclerosing cholangitis, and diabetes mellitus.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a significant past medical history of cirrhosis, ulcerative colitis (UC), primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) with associated cirrhosis, splenomegaly, portal hypertension, esophageal varices, osteopenia, peripheral polyarthropathy, and pyoderma gangrenosum, presented with left flank swelling and pain. The pain and swelling began two weeks prior when her nephew fell on her left flank. The pain and swelling progressively worsened over time. She was found to have a hematoma and was given antibiotics and pain medication. Upon admission, she reported severe left-sided flank pain, increased abdominal girth, lower extremity edema, and several episodes of loose stool. She denied fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, hematuria, bloody stool, dizziness, or lightheadedness.

Initial vitals in the ED were notable, and her exam revealed a left flank hematoma with significant tenderness on palpation. Abnormal lab results included sodium 123, bicarb 16, BUN/Cr 34/1.1, WBC 15.7, H/H 8.2/26.1, Plt 104, INR 1.8. A CT scan revealed a large retroperitoneal fluid collection, likely a hematoma, extending from the left kidney inferiorly to the level of the left iliacus muscle and into the posterior abdominal soft tissues and lateral abdominal oblique muscles. It also showed cirrhotic liver morphology with multiple perisplenic varices. Treatments received included 1L NS, 1u pRBCs and 1u FFT, and dilaudid 0.5 mg x2.

During her hospital stay, she underwent an incision and drainage procedure for a large left back abscess. She was also found to have electrolyte abnormalities, including a sodium level of 121, which led to a consideration of SIADH and a subsequent 1.5 liter fluid restriction. Her sodium levels were maintained at 129-132. She was also noted to have episodes of tachycardia, likely related to hypovolemia/pain. Cardiology was consulted and recommended outpatient follow-up. To rule out DVT as a cause of her tachycardia, ultrasounds of the lower extremities were performed, which were negative. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Nadolol, Folic Acid, Sulfasalazine, Ursodiol, Calcium Carbonate-Vitamin D3, Furosemide, and Spironolactone. She has no family history of gastrointestinal malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, pancreatic disease, or liver disease.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of ulcerative colitis, cirrhosis, esophageal varices, osteopenia, peripheral polyarthropathy, pyoderma gangrenosum, and flank hematoma, presented with fevers two weeks after being discharged from a previous hospitalization. During her previous hospitalization, she had been treated for a large flank hematoma, which had been drained and treated with antibiotics and pain medication. Her hospitalization was complicated by hyponatremia, tachycardia, and diarrhea. 

Upon her current admission, she reported intermittent pain at the site of the hematoma, but noted that it had been improving. She also reported a fever of 101 degrees, associated with chills, and decreased oral intake. She denied any headache, neck pain, chest pain, cough, hemoptysis, abdominal pain, diarrhea, blood in the stool, or new rashes. She also denied dysuria, urinary frequency, or urgency. 

Initial vitals in the ED were 97.3 97 74/38 18 99% RA, with a Tmax of 102.4. CT abdomen/pelvis demonstrated no acute intra-abdominal process, but did show soft tissue thickening and a 3.8 cm soft tissue defect along the prior surgical site. There were no drainable collections. Urinalysis was markedly dirty. Labs were significant for a cholestatic pattern, hyponatremia, and a metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. The patient was given 3L NS, 50g albumin at 25% and started on vancomycin and zosyn. 

Upon arrival to the MICU, the patient was awake, alert, and interactive. She had no localizing infectious symptoms beyond what was described above. 

Admission labs showed several abnormal findings, including low RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCHC, RDW, RDWSD, and Plt Ct, as well as high Neuts, low Lymphs, low AbsLymp, low AbsEos, high Glucose, low Na, low HCO3, high AST, high AlkPhos, high TotBili, low Albumin, low Calcium, low Phos, low pO2, low calTCO2, and high Lactate. 

Imaging studies showed a mildly enlarged heart, prominence of the main pulmonary arteries consistent with pulmonary arterial hypertension, no acute intra-abdominal process, cirrhotic liver with sequela of portal hypertension including massive splenomegaly and intra-abdominal varices, small volume intra-abdominal ascites, focal wall thickening along the left rectum, mild intrahepatic biliary duct dilation, and no intra- or extrahepatic biliary ductal dilation. 

Microbiology results showed no growth in blood cultures, but urine cultures were positive for E. coli. Wound cultures showed sparse growth of Staph aureus coag +. 

The patient's hospital course was complicated by septic shock secondary to a urinary tract infection, for which she was treated with broad spectrum antibiotics, vancomycin and zosyn, and then transitioned to oral ciprofloxacin. She also had an elevated bilirubin, likely due to her known PSC, and acute kidney injury, likely due to hypovolemia. Her sodium levels were low on admission, likely secondary to her liver disease, but corrected during hospitalization. 

The patient's family history does not include gastrointestinal malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, pancreatic disease, or liver disease. Her medications upon admission included folic acid, ursodiol, nadolol, sulfasalazine, furosemide, spironolactone, famotidine, pantoprazole, calcium carbonate-vitamin D3, loperamide, and hydromorphone.
label: controls

summary:

The wound had been worsening over the past month, despite a 10-day course of antibiotics and self-initiated prednisone treatment. She was advised to be admitted to the hospital due to the severity of the wound. She also has a history of severe iron deficiency anemia, thought to be due to heavy menses, and received 2 units of pRBC for a Hgb of 6.0. Her blood glucose was 509 on admission. 

Her past medical history includes ulcerative colitis, PSC with associated cirrhosis, splenomegaly, portal hypertension, esophageal varices, osteopenia, peripheral polyarthropathy, pyoderma gangrenosum, and flank hematoma. She has no family history of gastrointestinal malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, pancreatic disease, or liver disease. 

Upon physical examination, a 6-cm ulceration over her left buttock with surrounding erythema at edges and foul-smelling drainage was noted. Her vitals were stable and she was in no acute distress. 

Admission labs showed abnormal Hgb (9.6), MCV (74), MCH (20.6), MCHC (27.8), RDW (22.4), RDWSD (52.4), Glucose (509), Na (128), Cl (88), AlkPhos (339), TotBili (1.6), DirBili (0.9), Albumin (2.8), Calcium (8.2), Phos (2.6), and %HbA1c (7.7). Microbiology results showed polymorphonuclear leukocytes, gram-negative rods, and gram-positive cocci. Wound culture showed mixed bacterial flora and Bacteroides fragilis group with moderate growth. 

Discharge labs showed abnormal WBC (3.2), RBC (3.45), Hgb (8.2), Hct (29.0), MCH (23.8), MCHC (28.3), RDW (23.9), RDWSD (71.4), Plt Ct (97), PTT (24.3), Glucose (362), and ALT (44), AlkPhos (224), and Calcium (7.8). 

The patient was on several medications upon admission, including Clobetasol Propionate, FoLIC Acid, HYDROmorphone (Dilaudid), Nadolol, PredniSONE, SulfaSALAzine, Ursodiol, Ferrous Sulfate, Spironolactone, Furosemide, and Calcium 500 + D. 

The patient was diagnosed with pyoderma gangrenosum and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Other conditions include cirrhosis, primary sclerosing cholangitis, and ulcerative colitis.

summary:
The patient, a female with a significant past medical history of Ulcerative Colitis, Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) with associated cirrhosis, diabetes, anemia, and a recent discharge for a skin condition, was admitted due to worsening of her skin condition. She was recently discharged on 60mg Prednisone and cyclosporine 100mg twice daily. She had an acute worsening in her wound about a week prior to admission and increased her prednisone back to 60mg daily. She was referred to the ED for possible IV steroids and glucose control. 

The patient's symptoms started about 2 months ago as a small lesion on her left buttock. She was evaluated by Dermatology and initiated on cyclosporine and IV methyprednisone during her previous admission. Wound cultures grew Bacteroides fragilis and she was started on a 7-day course of Augmentin. She was also started on insulin during admission due to hyperglycemia in the setting of steroids. 

Upon admission, her vitals were stable but labs were notable for Hgb 5.7, Plt 116, Na 127, K 5.3, AST 50, ALT 74, AP 430, T bili 2.1, Alb 2.3. She was given IV HYDROmorphone (Dilaudid), IV Piperacillin-Tazobactam, IV Dextrose, IV Pantoprazole, IV Acyclovir, and 2 units of pRBC. Dermatology expressed concern for possible wound superinfection and recommended starting Zosyn and IV acyclovir. Various cultures were sent for testing. 

Her past medical history also includes esophageal varices, osteopenia, peripheral polyarthropathy, and flank hematoma. She has no family history of gastrointestinal malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, pancreatic disease, or liver disease. 

Physical examination revealed a large necrotic ulcer with purulent drainage and malordor on her left buttock, and 3+ pitting edema from knees to ankles. 

Admission labs showed several abnormal findings including low RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCH, MCHC, and high RDW, RDWSD, Neuts, and low Lymphs, Monos, Eos, AbsLymp, AbsMono, AbsEos. Other abnormal findings included low Glucose, Na, HCO3, high K, ALT, AST, AlkPhos, TotBili, DirBili, and low Albumin. 

Microbiology results showed Staph Aureus Coag +, mixed bacterial flora, E. Coli, and Enterococcus sp. in the tissue. No anaerobes were isolated. Blood cultures showed no growth. 

An MRI of the pelvis with contrast showed a tiny gas-containing fissure or small sinus tract arising from the left upper anus, focal thickening and hyperemia along the left perineum, extensive subcutaneous and soft tissue edema throughout the pelvis, and a small amount of fluid within the cul-de-sac. 

The patient was treated with pulse dose steroids (methylprednisolone 1 gm), discontinued on her home cyclosporine and given infliximab infusion. She was discharged with oral antibiotics and a plan for wound care. 

The patient's discharge medications included Ciprofloxacin HCl 750 mg PO Q12H, Clindamycin 450 mg PO Q8H, Collagenase Ointment 1 Appl TP DAILY, LaMIVudine 100 mg PO DAILY, MetronidAZOLE Topical 1 % Gel 1 Appl TP BID, PredniSONE 120 mg PO DAILY, Sulfameth/Trimethoprim SS 1 TAB PO DAILY, and 70/30 100 Units Breakfast and 70/30 50 Units Dinner. 

The patient was discharged home with service. Her primary diagnosis upon discharge was her skin condition, Ulcerative colitis, and skin infection. Her secondary diagnosis was Cirrhosis and Anemia. The patient was instructed to follow up with her primary care doctor, dermatologist, diabetes specialist, liver doctor, and gastroenterologist.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a past medical history of Ulcerative Colitis, Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) complicated by cirrhosis, and Pyoderma Gangrenosum, was admitted due to pancytopenia. She reported having symptoms similar to the flu, including a runny nose, sore throat, fever, chills, and weakness, but no myalgias. She also reported feeling weak and lethargic, with significant weakness upon standing and occasional cough. She had been taking Bactrim and ibuprofen for her symptoms. Lab results showed WBC 1.8, HGB 12.8, and Plt 61. The patient denied nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, melena, and hematochezia. 

Previously, the patient was admitted for pyoderma gangrenosum with superinfection and was treated with antibiotics and steroids. She also received an infliximab infusion. During that admission, she was found to have anemia with Hgb 5.7 of unclear etiology and received 4 units of packed red blood cells (pRBCs). She has had pancytopenia in the past, with anemia thought to be due to iron deficiency and hemolysis (spur cell hemolysis due to cirrhosis). Thrombocytopenia was thought to be related to her chronic liver disease and leukopenia was thought to be secondary to splenic sequestration and sulfasalazine use. 

Upon admission, the patient's vitals were notable for tachycardia. Labs showed a WBC of 1.9, H/H of 3.9/16.0, Plt of 68, Na 126, K 4.2, Cl 97, HCO3 22, BUN 8, Cr 0.5, AST/ALT within normal limits, alk phos 378, and TB 1.9. Imaging showed a left lower lobe patchy opacity concerning for pneumonia with a small left pleural effusion. 

The patient's past medical history also includes esophageal varices, osteopenia, peripheral polyarthropathy, and flank hematoma. She has no family history of gastrointestinal malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, pancreatic disease, or liver disease. 

Admission labs showed abnormal values for WBC (1.8), RBC (2.59), Hgb (3.9), Hct (15.9), MCV (61), MCH (15.1), MCHC (24.5), RDW (20.5), Plt Ct (61), AbsNeut (1.19), AbsLymp (0.47), AbsMono (0.13), AbsEos (0.00), AbsBaso (0.00), Na (130), AlkPhos (441), DirBili (0.9), and Albumin (2.4). Notable labs included VitB12, Folate, calTIBC, Ferritn, TRF, Osmolal, Ret Aut, and Abs Ret. 

Imaging showed a left lower lobe patchy opacity concerning for pneumonia with a small left pleural effusion and a coarsened, nodular liver, compatible with known cirrhosis with sequelae of portal hypertension including splenomegaly and perisplenic varices. 

During her hospital stay, the patient was treated for pneumonia with oral levofloxacin and for severe iron deficiency anemia with 5 units of PRBC and 1 IV iron infusion. Her hemoglobin improved and she did not have lightheadedness, dizziness, or symptomatic anemia. 

Upon discharge, the patient's diagnoses included acute on chronic anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, ulcerative colitis, primary sclerosing cholangitis, and cirrhosis. Her discharge medications included levofloxacin, calcium carbonate-vitamin D3, ferrous sulfate, 70/30 insulin, folic acid, nadolol, sulfasalazine, and ursodiol.

summary:
The patient, a female with a past medical history of Ulcerative Colitis (currently on sulfasalazine), Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) with associated cirrhosis, splenomegaly, portal hypertension, esophageal varices, pancytopenia, and Type II Diabetes, was admitted due to hypotension and suspected gastrointestinal bleeding. She presented with symptoms of diarrhea, general malaise, and fatigue for four days, which started after she began taking an expired penicillin prescription for a toothache. She also reported palpable rib discomfort bilaterally and had one episode of vomiting after drinking water. 

Upon admission, she was found to be tachycardic, pale, and unwell appearing. She had mild crackles in the bases bilaterally, tenderness to palpation over lower ribs bilaterally and upper right pectoris muscle, and +1 pitting edema of lower extremities bilaterally. Her labs were significant for leukocytosis, anemia, and hyponatremia. She was given IV fluids, Ondansetron, Albumin, Morphine Sulfate, HYDROmorphone, CefTRIAXone, Esomeprazole sodium, Calcium Gluconate, Piperacillin-Tazobactam, SulfaSALAzine, Ursodiol, and Vancomycin Oral Liquid. She was also transfused 1 unit of PRBCs.

Imaging revealed a large right-sided tension pneumothorax, which was treated with needle decompression and chest tube placement. An esophagram showed no evidence of esophageal leak. A CT scan of the abdomen was attempted but was not diagnostic due to extensive barium artifact. Preliminary images showed no evidence of small bowel obstruction, perforation, or megacolon. 

Consultations were made with Thoracic Surgery, who found no concern for esophageal perforation, and Liver, who were highly concerned for sepsis given her recent antibiotic use, diarrhea, and leukocytosis with bandemia. They recommended starting PO vancomycin to treat empirically for severe cdiff given high WBC, low albumin, and IV zosyn for gram negative and anaerobic coverage. 

Her physical exam revealed a large external skin tag vs thrombosed hemorrhoid at 12 o'clock and a 2cmx2cm malodorous anal fissure vs fistula at 8 o'clock. She also had a 1cm elliptical opening into the skin on her right labia majora with a black protruding foreign body. 

Her labs showed anemia, leukocytosis, hyponatremia, and elevated AlkPhos. Her blood cultures were negative, as were her C. difficile, ova and parasites, and fecal cultures. Her pleural fluid culture was also negative. Her CRP was elevated at 229.7, and her zinc and copper levels were low. 

Her discharge diagnoses included pneumonia, inflammatory bowel disease, transfusion-dependent pancytopenia, hyponatremia, mouth lesion, Primary Sclerosis Cholangitis, pneumoperitoneum, pneumothorax, fistula, acute kidney injury, and Type II Diabetes. She was discharged home with service.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of ulcerative colitis, primary sclerosing cholangitis (associated with splenomegaly, portal hypertension, cirrhosis, and esophageal varices), and chronic pancytopenia, presented with fatigue, malaise, dry cough, and watery stool. She had recently been admitted for colitis and gastrointestinal bleeding, during which she was treated for an Enterococcus faecium urinary tract infection with linezolid. She was found to be significantly anemic, requiring transfusion of 7 units of blood, and was also treated for hyponatremia. Since her discharge, she has been experiencing fatigue, lethargy, and general malaise, along with a dry cough and watery stools. She has not been taking any of her medications for about a week.

The patient's lab results showed a vitamin D level of 7ng/mL, creatinine levels fluctuating between 1.37 and 1.34, a C-reactive protein level of 144.7 on presentation (decreasing to 113.3 on discharge), and a hemoglobin level of 4.0 on presentation (increasing to 7.4 on discharge). Her stool calprotectin was collected twice, with values of ___ and 1113.7. Her free K was 158, free L was 237, and K/L was 0.67. Her ALT/AST were ___, and her total bilirubin was 2.5, trending down to 1.5 on discharge. Her direct bilirubin increased from 0.7 to 1.6 at its maximum. Her serum protein electrophoresis showed IGG 2634, IGM 223, IGA 507, with banding present, which was abnormal. A CT scan showed mild hyperenhancement and thickening of the distal colon, which could reflect infectious or inflammatory colitis, cirrhosis with stigmata of portal hypertension, including prominent varices, splenomegaly, and small volume ascites. Her spleen was enlarged to 20cm.

Upon admission, her hemoglobin was 6.8, and her urinalysis showed large amounts of blood, nitrites, leukocyte esterase, many red and white blood cells, and many bacteria. Her chest x-ray showed no acute cardiopulmonary process. She was given 1 unit of packed red blood cells, 1g of ceftriaxone, 1g of tylenol, and her home medications of sulfasalazine and ursodiol.

Her past medical history includes ulcerative colitis, primary sclerosing cholangitis with associated cirrhosis, splenomegaly, portal hypertension, esophageal varices, peripheral polyarthropathy, pyoderma gangrenosum, and a flank hematoma. She has no family history of gastrointestinal malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, pancreatic disease, or liver disease.

Her admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (2.76), hemoglobin (6.8), hematocrit (22.1), MCV (80), MCH (24.6), MCHC (30.8), RDW (19.3), RDWSD (56.1), neutrophils (73.7), lymphocytes (16.9), glucose (137), creatinine (1.2), sodium (133), potassium (3.3), bicarbonate (19), alkaline phosphatase (281), total bilirubin (1.6), and albumin (2.4). Her discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.07), hemoglobin (7.8), hematocrit (25.0), MCV (81), MCH (25.4), MCHC (31.2), RDW (18.6), RDWSD (56.0), glucose (115), sodium (132), bicarbonate (20), alkaline phosphatase (276), total bilirubin (2.1), and calcium (8.3).

Her urine culture showed Klebsiella pneumoniae and Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and her blood culture showed no growth. Her fecal culture showed no enteric gram-negative rods, salmonella, or shigella. A second urine culture showed Escherichia coli and Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Her chest x-ray showed no acute cardiopulmonary process but mild cardiomegaly without evidence of pulmonary edema. An ultrasound of her liver and gallbladder showed cirrhotic liver morphology, patent portal venous system, trace ascites, marked splenomegaly, a 1.3 x 1.4 x 0.9 cm hypoechoic lesion with an echogenic rim, and scattered areas of intrahepatic biliary prominence. An ultrasound of her abdomen showed coarsened nodular liver parenchyma, no evidence of hydronephrosis or stone in her kidneys, an echogenic focus in the right mid kidney, and significant splenomegaly.

During her hospital stay, she was treated for a urinary tract infection with ceftriaxone. She intermittently had low-grade fevers, and her infectious work-up was negative, including serial blood cultures. An abdominal ultrasound showed no obvious sources of infection. A repeat urine culture showed ceftriaxone-resistant E. Coli and pseudomonas, so she was discharged on a 5-day course of Cipro. She was also found to have acute on chronic anemia, and her hemoglobin was stable from her recent discharge. She received 1 unit of packed red blood cells, and her discharge hemoglobin was 7.8. She was also found to have primary sclerosing cholangitis, cirrhosis, portal hypertension, esophageal varices, splenomegaly, and ulcerative colitis. Her renal function responded to fluids, and her creatinine was 1.0 on discharge. She was prescribed Zofran for intermittent nausea. She was discharged home with instructions to continue all of her medications and follow up with her primary care physician within one week.
label: controls

summary:
The female patient was admitted to the medicine service with a history of Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) treated with sulfasalazine, pyoderma gangrenosum, and primary sclerosing cholangitis, which could be causing cirrhosis, portal hypertension, esophageal varices, and splenomegaly. She presented with fever, right-sided abdominal pain, and increased liver function tests (LFTs). Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) suggested acute cholangitis. There was no clear evidence of choledocholithiasis or cholecystitis. Her bilirubin was elevated at 6.3 with no recent baseline available. She was started on IV zosyn and her fevers resolved. She agreed to a course of Augmentin for 10 days, knowing that it is not ideal coverage. She continued home sulfasalazine, ursodiol for primary sclerosing cholangitis.

Imaging revealed a 3.3 cm soft tissue mass concerning for lymphoma, as well as hypoenhancement in the kidney also suggestive of possible lymphoma. She deferred biopsy to the outpatient setting. Hypoenhancement in the left kidney seen in both MRCP and CT Abdomen, possibly consistent with resolving pyelonephritis vs. lymphoma. Urine culture grew 10,000-100,000 strep viridans. Blood cultures were negative. She had no urinary symptoms on admission. She was treated with zosyn. 

She has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, but is currently not on any hypoglycemic medications. She takes lasix, spironolactone as needed. She has not been taking nadolol or PPI recently. Diuretics were held in the setting of infection and poor oral intake.

Admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.16), Hgb (8.0), Hct (25.9), MCH (25.3), MCHC (30.9), RDW (18.3), RDWSD (54.6), Neuts (82.7), Lymphs (10.8), Monos (4.4), AbsLymp (0.56), Glucose (251), Na (132), AnGap (8), AlkPhos (671), TotBili (5.8), DirBili (4.5), Calcium (8.1), and Albumin (2.1). Discharge labs showed abnormal values for WBC (3.3), RBC (2.99), Hgb (7.6), Hct (24.3), MCV (81), MCH (25.4), MCHC (31.3), RDW (17.0), RDWSD (49.5), Glucose (292), Na (130), AlkPhos (471), and TotBili (4.3).

Upon discharge, her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent. Her discharge diagnosis was cholangitis, primary sclerosing cholangitis, and cirrhosis. She was advised to follow up for a biopsy of the abdominal mass.

summary:
The patient is a female with a past medical history of primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) with cirrhosis, esophageal varices, splenomegaly, cholangitis, pyoderma gangrenosum, ulcerative colitis (UC), osteopenia, peripheral polyarthropathy, flank hematoma, tension pneumothorax, Hep B cAb positive, polyclonal hypergammoglobulinemia, iron deficiency anemia, copper deficiency, and zinc deficiency. She has no family history of liver disease or inflammatory bowel disease. 

She presented with jaundice and weakness, and her recent history includes an episode of cholangitis, pyoderma gangrenosum, and UC. She had abnormal liver function tests and an MRI suggestive of cholangitis. She also complained of poor appetite and weight loss, and was started on mirtazapine for appetite stimulation. She underwent a colonoscopy and an endoscopic ultrasound with para-aortic lymph node biopsy. 

In the emergency department, her vitals were stable and her physical examination was notable for jaundice. Her labs showed anemia (Hgb 7.5), hyponatremia (Na 126), metabolic acidosis (HCO3 17), renal insufficiency (BUN 29, Cr 1.2), hyperglycemia (glucose 146), and abnormal liver function tests (Alk phos 734, T bili 7.4, D bili 6.2, Alb 2.3). Her INR was slightly elevated at 1.5. Her imaging showed a coarsened nodular liver parenchyma, extensive varices, splenomegaly, and a hypoechoic lesion within the right hepatic lobe. Her EKG showed sinus tachycardia with no ischemic changes. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent further workup for her elevated beta-macroglobulin and enlarged para-aortic lymph node. Her colonoscopy showed multiple polyps without obvious malignancy. Her endoscopic ultrasound showed a 3cm soft tissue lesion adjacent to the aorta, which was biopsied. The results of the biopsy were pending at the time of discharge. She was recommended for a feeding tube to assist with nutrition, but she declined this intervention. 

Her discharge labs showed persistent anemia (Hgb 7.9), hyponatremia (Na 133), metabolic acidosis (HCO3 20), and abnormal liver function tests (Alk phos 469, T bili 7.9). Her discharge medications included dronabinol, mirtazapine, cepacol, furosemide, oxycodone, spironolactone, sulfasalazine, and ursodiol. 

The patient was discharged home with instructions to eat as much as possible, take all of her medications as prescribed, and attend follow-up appointments with her primary care doctor and specialists.

summary:

She also had a productive cough with yellow and green colored sputum. She has a history of primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC), ulcerative colitis, liver cirrhosis, esophageal varices, anemia, and failure to thrive. Her appetite improved on mirtazapine, dronabinol and PRN zofran and she declined to have a feeding tube placed during this admission. 

Upon admission, her labs showed abnormal levels of Neuts (80.4), Lymphs (12.3), AbsLymp (0.55), Glucose (101), Na (129), HCO3 (21), AlkPhos (470), TotBili (8.7), DirBili (6.8), and Albumin (2.4). Her PTT was 36.4. Her urine culture showed mixed bacterial flora, consistent with skin and/or genital contamination. Her blood cultures showed no growth. 

Imaging showed diffuse irregularity of the intra- and extrahepatic bile ducts, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, a 2 cm heterogeneously enhancing lesion in the periphery of the left kidney, and iron deposition in the bone marrow. Her liver was cirrhotic with sequela of portal hypertension, with prominent upper abdominal varices and splenomegaly. No ascites were observed. Her chest X-ray showed no relevant changes, with normal lung volumes, cardiac silhouette size, hilar and mediastinal contours. No pneumonia, pulmonary edema, pleural effusions, or pneumothorax were observed.

A biopsy of a periduodenal mass suggested a reactive process, with no definite evidence of morphologic or immunophenotypic involvement by lymphoma in the small specimen. If there is suspicion of lymphoma, a lymph node biopsy would be recommended. 

Her discharge labs showed abnormal levels of WBC (3.8), RBC (2.99), Hgb (7.8), Hct (24.7), MCHC (31.6), Neuts (80.7), Lymphs (11.1), AbsLymp (0.44), Na (134), HCO3 (19), AST (46), AlkPhos (484), TotBili (8.5), and Albumin (2.3).

The patient was discharged with diagnoses of failure to thrive, malnutrition, bronchitis, cirrhosis, primary sclerosing cholangitis, periaortic mass, anemia, hyponatremia, and ulcerative colitis. She was advised to continue taking all her medications and follow up with her doctors at her ___ appointments.

summary:

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to ascites. She has a history of Ulcerative Colitis (UC) and Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) complicated by cirrhosis, portal hypertension, and esophageal varices. She had her first parascentesis with 3 liters taken off, but the ascites quickly re-accumulated. She reported abdominal bloating and shortness of breath with exertion. Her past medical history also includes osteopenia and peripheral polyarthropathy, which may be related to UC or osteoarthritis. She has responded well to steroid therapy in the past. There is no family history of gastrointestinal malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, pancreatic disease, or liver disease.

Upon physical examination, she was alert and oriented, with mild cachexia. Her abdomen was soft, non-tender, distended, with massive splenomegaly. She had 2+ edema to knees bilaterally. Her vital signs were stable.

Lab results showed abnormal values for hemoglobin (9.6), hematocrit (31.3), MCV (71), MCH (21.7), MCHC (30.5), RDW (16.5), platelet count (80), PTT (37.4), glucose (114), sodium (129), ALT (95), AST (135), LDH (264), Alkaline Phosphatase (307), and total bilirubin (2.7). Ascites analysis showed abnormal values for WBC (55), RBC (80), Polys (4), Lymphs (78), Monos (10), Mesothe (2), and Macroph (6). The albumin level in the ascites was less than 1.

During her hospital stay, she underwent a therapeutic parascentesis. Her ascites was likely due to portal hypertension related to worsening portal pressures, falling albumin, dietary indiscretion, and medication confusion. She was also diagnosed with severe protein-energy malnutrition, coagulopathy, and thrombocytopenia. Her UC was managed with sulfasalazine and hemorrhoids with colace. 

Upon admission, she was on folic acid, furosemide, nadolol, spironolactone, sulfasalazine, ursodiol, calcium carbonate-vitamin D3, and ferrous gluconate. Her cytology results were pending at the time of discharge.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of Ulcerative Colitis (UC) and Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) complicated by cirrhosis, portal hypertension, and esophageal varices, was admitted from the clinic due to failure to thrive. She reported significant weakness and poor nutritional intake, eating only soup or a baked potato daily due to painful hemorrhoids. After a botox injection, her rectal pain mostly resolved. She had been previously admitted with a chief complaint of ascites, and had undergone paracentesis twice, with 3L and 5.5L of fluid removed respectively. Her albumin was found to be 1.0, and she received extensive nutrition counseling and supplementation. She reported feeling better after the procedures, and was able to walk longer distances. However, she was found to be lightheaded with high blood pressure and appeared malnourished at her recent appointment, leading to her current admission for nutrition management and supplementation.

Her past medical history includes UC diagnosed by colonoscopy, PSC with associated cirrhosis, splenomegaly, portal hypertension, and esophageal varices, osteopenia, and peripheral polyarthropathy. She has no family history of gastrointestinal malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, pancreatic disease, or liver disease.

On physical examination, she was found to be tachycardic with mild crackles at the right base of her lungs, and her abdomen was soft with shifting dullness. She had no lower extremity edema. Her admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (2.91), HGB (7.5), HCT (23.3), MCV (80), MCH (25.7), RDW (22.2), glucose (165), sodium (126), albumin (2.0), calcium (7.1), ALT (49), AST (85), ALK PHOS (220), and total bilirubin (2.7).

During her hospital stay, she required nutritional optimization prior to being considered for transplantation. A tube was placed for feeding after she initially expressed reluctance due to discomfort, nausea, and negative associations with her mother's end-of-life care. Her albumin on admission was less than 2.0. She was also managed for cirrhosis secondary to sclerosing cholangitis, which is secondary to underlying ulcerative colitis. She was continued on her home regimen of sulfasalazine and ursodiol, and maintained on a low sodium diet. Her diuretics were initially held due to low blood pressures and hyponatremia, but her pressures improved during the course of hospitalization and were thought to be secondary to poor oral intake. Her sodium improved with water restriction. An attempt was made to tap her ascites prior to discharge, however, ultrasound did not show ample space for a tap. She was set up with outpatient radiology for an ultrasound-guided tap. She was discharged on half dose diuretics.

Her medications upon admission included Lasix, Folate, Nadolol, Phytonadione, Spironolactone, Sulfasalazine, Ursodiol, Calcium carbonate, Vit D3, and Ferrous gluconate. She was discharged with hepatology and radiology follow-up scheduled. Her discharge diagnoses were Ulcerative colitis and sclerosing cholangitis, poor nutrition, and cirrhosis secondary to sclerosing cholangitis.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a homeless woman with a history of PTSD, bipolar affective disorder (BPAD), hypothyroidism, and chronic pain. She presented to the emergency department after attempting suicide by running into traffic. This was reportedly her second suicide attempt that day, the first being an attempt to jump onto subway tracks. The patient reported feeling depressed and tearful for several weeks, exacerbated by the recent deaths of loved ones and the anniversary of her godmother's death. She also reported chronic pain and anxiety about her living situation. She admitted to being non-compliant with her medication for the past 5-6 days, and reported symptoms of insomnia, fatigue, weight loss, and concerns about a possible brain tumor. She denied any hallucinations or delusions, but reported feeling the presence of her deceased godmother in a comforting way.

The patient has a history of substance abuse, including alcohol and cocaine, and reported drinking and smoking marijuana in the days leading up to her hospitalization. She has a history of multiple suicide attempts and self-harm, including self-cutting. Her past medical history includes epilepsy, recurrent seizures, hypertension, laryngopharyngeal reflux, lumbago, lumbar facet syndrome, lumbar radiculopathy, myofascial pain syndrome, and spinal stenosis. She has been hospitalized multiple times, including for cocaine dependence and following a gang rape.

Her family history includes a father with alcohol dependence, a sister with PTSD and depression, and a brother who was hospitalized for psychiatric reasons after burning his arm. The patient's physical exam revealed multiple healing incisions from self-cutting on her forearms and inner thighs. Her psychiatric exam showed signs of depression, circumstantial thought process, and poor judgment and insight. Her speech was slurred, and she was oriented to Deac 4 but not aware of the date.

Lab results showed an elevated TSH level of 9.1. Other lab results, including glucose, urea, creatinine, sodium, potassium, chloride, total CO2, anion gap, ALT, AST, LD, alkaline phosphatase, total bilirubin, vitamin B12, folate, WBC, RBC, HGB, HCT, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, neutrophils, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils, and platelet count were all within normal ranges. The patient tested negative for ASA, ethanol, acetaminophen, benzodiazepines, barbiturates, and tricyclics.

Upon admission, the patient was taking alprazolam, gabapentin, levothyroxine, tramadol, metoprolol, oxycodone-acetaminophen, hydrochlorothiazide, and oxcarbazepine. During her hospital stay, she developed bilateral leg edema, which was treated with levothyroxine, compression stockings, and lasix. She also experienced constipation, likely due to narcotic use and hypothyroidism, which was treated with colace. Her epilepsy was managed with a lower dose of oxcarbazepine, and her hypertension was controlled without metoprolol. Her chronic pain was managed with a lower dose of gabapentin, along with her home doses of oxycodone-acetaminophen, tramadol, and acetaminophen. She was also found to have a shortened PR interval on ECG, and was advised to avoid sympathomimetics, specifically cocaine. Her laryngeal reflux was stable and managed with omeprazole.

The patient's bipolar disorder was managed with a switch from olanzapine to risperidone, which improved her emotional regulation, mood, and suicidal thoughts. She was also provided with a long-acting injectable formulation of risperidone. Her anxiety was relatively stable and managed with alprazolam. She reported a recent sexual assault, and was visited by a counseling team for support. The patient was discharged in a stable and improved condition, with no evidence of psychosis or suicidality. She was advised to continue taking her medications regularly, abstain from illicit substances, and follow up with her outpatient appointments.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to nausea and vomiting. He had undergone an exploratory laparotomy for bowel obstruction and was recovering well until he experienced these symptoms after a large meal. The patient vomited four times over two days, initially expelling partially digested food which later turned into clear-yellow fluid. Accompanying these symptoms were abdominal distension and pain, which he reported to have subsided after vomiting. He also reported passing a small bowel movement and a small amount of flatus.

The patient's past medical history includes sleep apnea, polyneuropathy which could be due to mild diabetes mellitus and B12 deficiency. He has undergone several surgical procedures in the past including sigmoid resection, tonsillectomy, appendectomy, finger surgery, rhinoplasty, rotator cuff surgery, and eye surgery to correct esotropia. His family history is negative for cancer.

Upon physical examination, the patient was awake, alert, and oriented. His abdomen was soft, mildly distended, and nontender. His incisions were healing well and the staples had been removed. There were no hernias or masses detected.

Lab results showed a neutrophil count of 71.3*, glucose level of 121*, urea nitrogen level of 30*, and creatinine level of 1.3*, all of which were abnormal. The patient's platelet count was 299, and his WBC, RBC, HGB, HCT, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, sodium, potassium, chloride, total CO2, and anion gap were all within normal ranges.

During his hospital stay, the patient was given an NGT for decompression and was started on IV fluids for hydration. He began to pass gas and watery stool on the second and third day of his hospital stay. He was advanced to a regular diet on the third day and tolerated it well. His abdominal wound staples were also removed at that time. He was discharged home in stable condition with arrangements for prompt follow-up in clinic.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including azelastine, bupropion, citalopram, gabapentin, mirtazapine, cyanocobalamin, ibuprofen, oxycodone-acetaminophen, tamsulosin, zolpidem tartrate, zaleplon, mometasone, and docusate sodium.

The patient was diagnosed with post-operative ileus. He was instructed to stay hydrated due to diarrhea and to contact his surgeon or return to the emergency room if the diarrhea persists for more than a few days.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted due to persistent diarrhea. His medical history includes sleep apnea, polyneuropathy (possibly due to mild diabetes mellitus and B12 deficiency), and a sigmoid resection performed several years ago. His surgical history includes tonsillectomy, appendectomy, finger surgery, rhinoplasty, rotator cuff surgery, and eye surgery to correct esotropia. The patient has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. His family history is negative for cancer.

During this admission, the patient underwent an exploratory laparotomy with lysis of adhesions. He had been experiencing persistent diarrhea since his return from a trip abroad, where he had surgery for small bowel obstruction (SBO). A CT scan revealed multiple loops of dilated bowel and pneumatosis intestinalis. The patient denied any abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or fever. 

Physical examination upon discharge showed the patient was alert and oriented, with no signs of distress. His abdomen was soft and tender, with a midline abdominal incision covered with steri-strips. He had edema in both upper and lower extremities, more pronounced in the right upper extremity. 

Imaging results showed unchanged small bowel findings compared to a previous CT examination, with pneumatosis of small-bowel loops in the right upper quadrant and multiple dilated loops of small bowel. There was no evidence of mesenteric or portal venous gas, suggesting small bowel obstruction. There was also cholelithiasis without evidence of cholecystitis. A pathology report from a peritoneal nodule excision showed no malignancy. An ECG revealed a new right bundle-branch block. 

During his hospital stay, the patient experienced a period of disorientation, during which he became violent and required restraints and administration of IV haldol and lorazepam. His mental status gradually returned to baseline by the time of discharge. He also experienced hypotension on post-operative day 1, which was managed with a 1L bolus of normal saline in conjunction with IV albumin. A right-sided power PICC was placed for venous access. An upper extremity duplex series revealed a nonocclusive thrombus in the right basilic vein and possible chronic thrombus in the left brachial vein. 

The patient's medications upon admission included bupropion, citalopram, gabapentin, mirtazapine, and tamsulosin. Throughout his hospitalization, he had persistent diarrhea, for which he received intravenous metronidazole. Stool cultures were negative. He also had a positive urinalysis growing coag positive staph, which was treated with intravenous doxycycline and oral Macrobid. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with recurrent partial small bowel obstruction. He was alert, interactive, and able to ambulate independently.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to recurrent episodes of small-bowel obstruction (SBO) over the past several years. His symptoms included intermittent abdominal distention and vomiting, with some episodes lasting approximately 3 days, but generally less than 24 hours. He has had multiple investigations including CT scans and an MR enteroscopy, which showed a slightly abnormal loop of small bowel in the left mid abdomen. However, attempts at visualization with push enteroscopy were unsuccessful. A capsule enterography was performed which showed no bleeding and no definitive abnormalities. The patient has not experienced significant weight loss.

His past medical history includes sleep apnea, polyneuropathy (possibly due to mild DM and B12 deficiency). He has also undergone several surgeries in the past, including sigmoid resection, tonsillectomy, appendectomy, finger surgery, rhinoplasty, rotator cuff surgery, and eye surgery to correct esotropia. His family history is negative for cancer.

During his stay, the patient underwent an exploratory laparotomy and lysis of adhesions. Post-operatively, he did well, with his diet advanced and tolerated without nausea or vomiting. He was passing flatus, his pain was controlled on oral pain medications, he was ambulating well, and his foley was taken out without problems.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including acetazolamide, bupropion, citalopram, gabapentin, mirtazapine, tamsulosin, azelastine nasal spray, zaleplon, zolpidem, mometasone (nasonex), cyanocobalamin ER, and a multivitamin.

Lab results showed a decrease in WBC from 9 to 6.6, a slight decrease in Hct from 40 to 39.4, and an increase in Plt from 198 to 224. Chemistry was within normal limits.

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with adhesions. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of anxiety, was admitted due to Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA). He reported symptoms of nausea, vomiting, fatigue, malaise, polydipsia, and polyuria over the last two days. He denied any preceding illness but mentioned stress due to college finals. His family history includes a grandfather with adult onset diabetes. Upon admission, he was not on any medications.

During his stay, he was given 2 L of fluid, morphine, and Ativan, and was started on a bolus of 10 units IV insulin followed by a drip at 5 units/hr. A chest X-ray was performed which was reported as negative. His initial vitals in the ED were 98.9 116 114/63 16 100% RA and upon transfer to the MICU, his vitals were 97 92/50 18 100% RA. 

Abnormal lab results upon admission included PO2-117*, PCO2-26*, PH-7.18*, TOTAL CO2-10*, BASE XS--17, GLUCOSE-242*, TOTAL CO2-9*, ANION GAP-23*, CALCIUM-7.9*, PHOSPHATE-2.1*, PO2-47*, PCO2-21*, PH-7.11*, TOTAL CO2-7*, BASE XS--21, GLUCOSE-316*, CREAT-1.3*, CHLORIDE-111*, TOTAL CO2-5*, ANION GAP-25*, WBC-20.1*, NEUTS-83.6*, LYMPHS-11.2*, and URINE GRANULAR-13*. 

Upon discharge, abnormal lab results included WBC-3.8*, RBC-4.18*, Hgb-12.8*, Hct-35.9*, MCHC-35.6*, and Glucose-269*. 

The patient was diagnosed with DKA, evidenced by hyperglycemia, ketonuria, and anion gap metabolic acidosis. His A1c was 15.7%. He was started on insulin and his anion gap closed. He was also seen by a nurse educator who performed insulin teaching. On the day of discharge, his glucose levels were 179, 237, 257, 250. He continued to feel well and was tolerating oral intake without nausea or vomiting. 

He was also diagnosed with volume depletion secondary to aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, which normalized his creatinine levels. Pending labs including anti GAD and c-peptide will be followed by his outpatient provider. His CBC should be rechecked as an outpatient to ensure the cell lines are recovering. 

Upon discharge, the patient was alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was ambulatory and independent. He was instructed to continue taking his prescribed insulin.
label: controls

summary:
The male patient was admitted to the orthopedics service due to back pain following an incident where he jumped from a two-story balcony while intoxicated. He did not lose consciousness or hit his head during the incident. Upon examination, he was found to have a compression fracture of L3 with a 7 mm retropulsion into the spinal canal. He was neurovascularly intact and hemodynamically stable. The patient denied experiencing numbness, weakness, headache, visual changes, chest pain, shortness of breath, or neck pain. 

The patient underwent a major surgical procedure where a Thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO) brace was fitted. He was kept on bed rest until the brace was fitted. He experienced urinary retention, for which a Foley catheter was placed and later removed once the brace was fitted and the patient was able to get out of bed and ambulate. He was voiding freely with control. The patient's initial pain was managed with medication and his diet was advanced as tolerated. Physical therapy was consulted for mobilization out of bed to ambulate. His hospital course was otherwise unremarkable. 

The patient has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. His past medical history is unremarkable and he has no known family history of any conditions. Upon admission, the patient was on Sertraline 50 mg PO daily. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was mildly uncomfortable but alert and oriented. His cranial nerves II-XII were grossly intact. He had tenderness to the midline L spine. His sensory and motor functions were intact, as were his reflexes. Babinski sign was negative and there was no clonus. Perianal sensation was intact per patient and rectal tone was not tested as the patient denied stool incontinence. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with an L3 burst fracture. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory but required assistance or aid. He was discharged home with instructions on activity limitations, diet, brace usage, and medication management. He was also given instructions for follow-up appointments and when to seek immediate medical attention.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female under the OBSTETRICS/GYNECOLOGY service, was admitted due to a shortening/dilated cervix. She was diagnosed with mono di twins during a routine visit and was noted to have a short cervix on ultrasound. She denies contractions, vaginal bleeding, loss of fluid, fevers/chills, recent illness, headaches, vision changes, chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, abdominal pain, and recent falls or abdominal trauma. She confirms active fetal movement. 

Her past medical history includes prenatal care with normal labs for Rh, Abs, Rub, RPR, HBsAg, HIV, and GBS. She tested negative for CF carrier screening and had normal Hgb electrophoresis. She had an elevated T21 risk (1:130) but declined follow-up testing. Her ultrasound showed normal AFI x 2, stomach and bladder, and normal MCA dopplers. She denies any abnormal Pap or cervical procedures, STIs, including HSV. She has no significant family history. 

Upon physical examination, she was alert and oriented, comfortable, with a soft, gravid, non-tender abdomen. No calf tenderness was noted. Her blood pressure was 138/74, and her temperature was 98.2°F. 

Lab results showed abnormal WBC at 13.3, RBC at 3.45, Hgb at 10.7, Hct at 32.4, and RDWSD at 52.2. Urine tests showed a pH of 6.0, Leuks-SM, Urobiln-2, and Ketone-10. All drug tests were negative. She tested negative for Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Group B Beta Strep, and bacterial vaginosis. A yeast vaginitis culture showed rare growth. 

During her hospital stay, she was treated with indomethacin for preterm contractions, which improved significantly. She had no further bleeding and fetal testing was reassuring. She completed a course of betamethasone. She remained clinically stable without any evidence of preterm labor. Her cervical exam was unchanged at the time of discharge. 

Upon admission, she was taking prenatal vitamins. Her discharge diagnosis was preterm contractions. She was alert, clear, coherent, and independently ambulatory at the time of discharge.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a male with a history of coronary artery disease (CAD) post stent placement, inguinal hernia, and thyroid cyst. He was admitted due to persistent back pain and urinary retention following a recent fall. He reported that the pain, described as 'like a shock', shoots down the side of his thigh to his knee. He was previously seen by his primary care physician (PCP) who prescribed Motrin and Flexiril. He was also given a 5-day course of Prednisone by an orthopedic doctor, which initially relieved his pain. However, the pain returned after the course was completed. He also reported a weak urinary stream and feeling like he was not fully emptying his bladder for about two weeks. 

Upon admission, the patient was found to have urinary retention, with 1600cc of urine released when a Foley catheter was placed. An MRI showed disc herniation. The patient's neuro exam was intact, with no saddle anesthesia, normal rectal tone, and no weakness. He continued to have urinary retention, failing voiding trials. However, he was able to void 400cc of urine on his own after the catheter was removed. 

The patient's lab results showed a hemoglobin level of 12.2* and a hematocrit level of 37.3*, both of which are abnormal. His urea nitrogen level was also abnormal at 21*. An MRI of the thoracic and lumbar spine showed a disc extrusion at L2-3, multilevel degenerative changes at other levels in the lumbar spine, and bilateral neural foraminal narrowing at L4-5 and L5-S1. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Simvastatin 80mg daily, baby aspirin, Plavix 75mg daily, Metoprolol 50mg daily, and Vicodin as needed for pain. 

The patient was evaluated by Orthopedics, who recommended no surgical intervention at this time. He was scheduled for an epidural steroid injection by the pain service. His Plavix was held for one week for the procedure, with his PCP's approval. He was discharged on Dilaudid as needed and Gabapentin for pain control. 

The patient's discharge diagnosis was L2-L3 disc herniation. He was alert and interactive upon discharge, and was able to ambulate independently.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, was admitted to the cardiology service after presenting to the emergency room with exertional angina. He had a cardiac catheterization and intraaortic balloon pump placed and was then referred for coronary artery bypass grafting. The patient underwent coronary artery bypass grafting times four, with a left internal mammary artery to left anterior descending artery, reverse saphenous vein graft to obtuse marginal artery, reverse saphenous vein graft to ramus artery, and reverse saphenous vein graft to posterior descending artery. 

Post-operatively, the patient experienced several episodes of atrial fibrillation, which were treated with Beta blockade and amiodarone. He was also started on oral anticoagulation with coumadin. The patient was transfused two units of packed red blood cells for a hematocrit of 21%. He initially had some urinary retention and the foley catheter was re-inserted. After two days of treatment with Flomax, the foley was discontinued and the patient voided without difficulty. 

The patient's lab results showed abnormal glucose (114), urea nitrogen (24), creatinine (1.5), and HbA1c (6.0). His RBC count (3.85), hemoglobin (13.0), hematocrit (35.7), and MCH (33.7) were also abnormal. His blood glucose (131), urea nitrogen (50), and creatinine (1.4) were also elevated. 

The patient's echocardiography report showed normal left atrium and right atrium cavity sizes, normal left ventricular wall thickness, cavity size, and global systolic function, and normal right ventricular chamber size and free wall motion. The aortic valve leaflets were mildly thickened but aortic stenosis was not present. The mitral valve appeared structurally normal with mild mitral regurgitation. 

Upon admission, the patient was on toprol xl 100', lisinopril/hctz, asa 81', lovaza, simvastatin 40', and NTG prn. The patient's father had a myocardial infarction in his past. The patient was discharged to rehab in good condition.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to asymptomatic high-grade left carotid stenosis. This was discovered during a routine physical examination where a cervical bruit was found. A carotid ultrasound and CT angiogram were performed, which suggested a critical left carotid stenosis. The patient underwent a left carotid endarterectomy. He has no history of transient ischemic attack (TIA), amaurosis fugax, or stroke. However, he has a history of coronary artery disease and underwent a coronary artery bypass procedure last year due to anginal variant symptoms and significant three-vessel coronary artery disease. He also had a chronic left hemothorax, which required removal by thoracoscopy some months later.

His past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a tonsillectomy. His father had a myocardial infarction. Upon physical examination, he appeared well with a heart rate of 79, blood pressure of 199/90, and respiratory rate of 19. A very loud left cervical bruit was noted, and carotid pulsations were strongly palpable. 

Lab results showed abnormal levels of RBC (3.03), Hgb (9.6), Hct (28.4), Glucose (162), UreaN (26), and Creat (1.4). A CTA showed a calcific atherosclerotic plaque and left carotid bifurcation with near occlusion of the lumen shortly after the bifurcation. A carotid duplex showed left carotid artery with a peak systolic velocity of 352 and an end-diastolic velocity of 118, putting in a ratio of 3.4, putting it in 80-99% range. The right side has minimal stenosis.

During his hospital stay, the patient developed a small hematoma overnight, which was determined to be stable and required no intervention. His medications upon admission included Lisinopril-Hydrochlorothiazide 20 mg-12.5 mg, Metoprolol Succinate 100mg, Simvastatin 40mg, and Aspirin 81mg. Upon discharge, his condition was stable. His discharge diagnosis was critical asymptomatic left carotid stenosis, status post left carotid endarterectomy.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted to the neurosurgery department due to back pain and leg pain that she has been experiencing since a car accident last year. The pain is located in her upper back and radiates to both legs, increasing with any activity and even standing. She has tried various forms of conservative management such as chiropractic treatment, acupuncture, LESI, etc., but none have been successful. She denies any bowel, bladder, or sexual issues. 

Her past medical history includes a motor vehicle accident, hypothyroidism, ACL surgery, and an appendectomy. She has no known family history of any significant medical conditions. She is allergic to amoxicillin and codeine. Upon admission, she was taking Levothyroxine 112mg every morning.

During her hospital stay, she underwent a T10-L2 posterior spinal fusion. The procedure was well-tolerated and she was transferred to the floor after satisfactory recovery from anesthesia. Initially, she was given IV fluids and IV pain medications, and progressed to a regular diet and oral medications by the second day post-operation. Pain control was initially an issue but by the third day post-operation, she was more comfortable. She was given antibiotics and anticoagulation as per routine. Her home medications were continued throughout the hospitalization. 

Upon discharge, her pain was well controlled with oral medications, her incisions were clean, dry, and intact, and she was voiding and moving bowels spontaneously. Her discharge diagnosis was T11-12 kyphosis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent. 

Her discharge medications included Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO Q6H, Bisacodyl 10 mg PO/PR DAILY, Cepacol (Sore Throat Lozenge) 1 LOZ PO Q4H:PRN sore throat, Diazepam 5 mg PO BID:PRN pain, Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID, OxyCODONE (Immediate Release) ___ mg PO Q4H:PRN Pain - Moderate, Senna 8.6 mg PO BID, and Levothyroxine Sodium 112 mcg PO DAILY.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, depression, fibromyalgia, hypothyroidism, gastroesophageal reflux disease, diabetes mellitus, seasonal allergic rhinitis, restless leg syndrome, and Clostridium difficile infection, was admitted due to jaundice. She had noticed skin changes four days prior to admission and also reported abdominal distension and a significant weight loss of 30 pounds over two months. She had no fever, nausea, vomiting, or abdominal pain. She did report some loose stool, which she attributed to starting metformin for her newly diagnosed diabetes. 

During her stay, she underwent an ERCP with sphincterotomy and stent placement. A CT scan of her abdomen and pelvis revealed biliary tract dilation, mild pancreas duct dilation, pancreas glandular atrophy, a probable fibroid uterus, calcific aortic ASVD, hiatal hernia, and mild constipation. No large or bulky pancreas head mass or gallstones were seen. 

Her lab results showed several abnormal values: RBC count of 3.46, hemoglobin of 10.8, hematocrit of 33.9, glucose of 145, potassium of 3.1, ALT of 335, AST of 167, AlkPhos of 878, TotBili of 3.6, and magnesium of 1.4. Her total bilirubin was 6.5, direct bilirubin was 3.9, AST was 315, ALT was 512, and Alk Phos was 1033. 

The brushings from her common bile duct were positive for malignant cells, consistent with adenocarcinoma. A CT scan of her abdomen with and without IV contrast showed a biliary stent in the common hepatic duct, intrahepatic and extrahepatic biliary duct dilation, and enhancement of the intrahepatic bile duct walls. There were also prominent porta hepatis lymph nodes and a region of hypoattenuation in the uncinate process of the pancreas, measuring 16 mm, which was concerning for a pancreatic neoplasm. 

Her ERCP revealed a 1 cm tight, malignant appearing stricture in the distal common bile duct, and cytology samples were obtained. A 7 cm biliary stent was placed successfully across the stricture. 

Her medications upon admission included carisoprodol, amlodipine, irbesartan, atorvastatin, pramipexole, duloxetine, polyethylene glycol, levothyroxine, folic acid, vitamin D, acetylcysteine, omeprazole, lactobacillus acidophilus, saccharomyces boulardii, metformin, and acetaminophen. 

The patient was discharged with a primary diagnosis of bile duct obstruction and biliary stricture/pancreatic mass. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent. Pending studies on discharge included biopsy results and a CT scan read.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted for a surgical procedure due to a diagnosis of ductal adenocarcinoma. She underwent several procedures including exploratory laparoscopy, laparoscopic biopsies of segment 4 and segment 3, exploratory laparotomy, pylorus-sparing radical pancreatoduodenectomy, end-to-side pancreaticojejunostomy, cholecystectomy with end-to-side ductal mucosa hepaticojejunostomy, placement of gold fiducials, antecolic duodenojejunostomy, and placement of a transgastric feeding jejunostomy tube. 

The patient's past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, depression, fibromyalgia, hypothyroidism, gastroesophageal reflux disease, diabetes mellitus, seasonal allergic rhinitis, restless leg syndrome, and Clostridium difficile infection. She has no family history of early coronary artery disease or gastrointestinal cancer. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking several medications including polyethylene glycol, omeprazole, irbesartan, lactobacillus acidophilus, folic acid, metformin, pramipexole, carisoprodol, acetylcysteine, vitamin D, levothyroxine sodium, amlodipine, ondansetron, duloxetine, saccharomyces boulardii, and oxycodone. 

The patient's physical examination revealed a subcostal abdominal incision with a wound VAC dressing on to 120 mm Hg suction. Her vital signs were stable. Abnormal lab results included a low red blood cell count (3.14), low hemoglobin (9.5), low hematocrit (30.3), high glucose (243), and low calcium (8.0) and phosphorus (2.6) levels. 

The patient's stool tested positive for toxigenic C. difficile. A wound culture revealed sparse growth of Escherichia coli and gram-positive cocci. Pathology examination of the liver, lymph node, bile duct, and pancreatic duct showed no malignancy. However, the pancreas and duodenum showed moderately differentiated pancreatic adenocarcinoma. 

During her hospital stay, the patient developed wound infection and C. difficile colitis. She was treated with oral Flagyl, Keflex, and Vancomycin. Her blood sugar levels were monitored and managed with subcutaneous insulin. She was discharged home with a wound VAC in place and oral Vancomycin for treatment of C. difficile colitis. Her discharge diagnoses included ductal adenocarcinoma G2T3N0, chronic pancreatitis, wound infection, and C. difficile colitis.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to a wound infection. She has a history of pancreatic adenocarcinoma and had undergone a Whipple procedure, which was complicated by a wound infection. She had completed chemoradiation and presented with erythema of her abdominal wound. She noticed increased swelling on the right side of her incision, which later became red and started to drain fluid. The patient denied experiencing fevers, chills, and malaise. The infection cultures from her previous infection grew strep milleri sensitive to vanco and e.coli sensitive to meropenem and zosyn.

Her past medical history includes hypertension, hyperlipidemia, depression, fibromyalgia, hypothyroidism, gastroesophageal reflux disease, diabetes mellitus, seasonal allergic rhinitis, restless leg syndrome, and Cdiff. She has no family history of early coronary artery disease or gastrointestinal cancer.

Upon physical examination, her old subcostal incision was well healed, with the right lateral aspect open about 1.5 inches and packed with a moist-to-dry dressing. Erythema was significantly improved from prior examinations, with no drainage. Her vital signs were stable and she was afebrile.

Abnormal lab results included a red blood cell count of 3.11, hemoglobin of 9.3, hematocrit of 29.8, glucose of 183, urea nitrogen of 21, sodium of 129, bicarbonate of 21, and alkaline phosphatase of 184. 

During her hospital stay, a bedside incision and drainage procedure was performed to fully drain the collection from her wound. The wound was subsequently packed with a wet to dry dressing. She was started on vancomycin and her dressing was changed twice daily. She remained afebrile and hemodynamically stable throughout her hospitalization. Upon discharge, she was tolerating a regular diet, had normal bowel function, was ambulating, was voiding without difficulty, and was without pain.

Her medications upon admission included Avapro, Duloxetine, Folic Acid, Levothyroxine Sodium, Omeprazole, Polyethylene Glycol, Vitamin D, Metformin, Docusate Sodium, Hydromorphone, Florajen, Amlodipine, Acetaminophen, Mirapex, Florastor, NAC, Ondansetron, Vancomycin Oral Liquid, Morphine SR, needle, and Glargine.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a significant past medical history of fibromyalgia, hypertension, depression, and pancreatic adenocarcinoma, was admitted due to fevers, chills, and tachycardia. She had previously undergone a Whipple procedure and chemotherapy for her pancreatic adenocarcinoma, which was complicated by a malignant right pleural effusion. She presented with a fever of 101.4, chills, general malaise, a non-productive cough, shortness of breath, and dyspnea on exertion. She also reported suprapubic tenderness, dysuria, and urgency for the past week. Her daughter noted progressive erythema surrounding the bandage site from the PleurX catheter placement. 

Initial lab results showed abnormal BUN/Cr, H/H 9.1/30.1, positive U/A (+ leuk, + bacteria, + WBC), WBC 7.1, and lactate 2.9. A CXR showed a small right pleural effusion with medial right lower lung opacity. The patient received 2L IVF NS, Vanc 1g, and Zosyn 4.5g. 

The patient's family history includes her mother who passed from lung cancer, her father who had a history of a cell tumor, and a maternal aunt who had a history of breast cancer. 

Physical examination on admission showed faint crackles at lung bases R>L, coarse breath sounds on the right side, a well-healed epigastric scar, a large reducible ventral hernia, and suprapubic tenderness. 

Abnormal lab results included RBC-3.00*, Hgb-9.3*, Hct-30.1*, MCV-101*, MCHC-30.9*, PTT-37.7*, Glucose-135*, Creat-1.2*, HCO3-21*, Lactate-2.9*, ALT-49*, AST-69*, AlkPhos-779*, ALT-43*, AST-69*, AlkPhos-858*, GGT-875*, WBC-3.9*, RBC-2.79*, Hgb-8.6*, Hct-27.7*, MCV-99*, Glucose-103*, HCO3-20*, ALT-36*, AST-49*, AlkPhos-762*, and Calcium-8.3*. 

Imaging showed a new 2.2 x 3.3 cm segment VIII hypodensity with interval increase in intrahepatic biliary dilatation, concerning for obstruction and possible disease recurrence. A small right pneumothorax, small to moderate left non-hemorrhagic pleural effusion, and increased intra-abdominal ascites were also noted. 

The patient was found to have E. Coli bacteremia of unclear source. She was initially covered broadly with Vanc/Meropenem. Blood culture from grew E. Coli sensitive to Meropenem. Vanc was discontinued following sensitivities. The patient most likely developed her acute blood stream infection from an abdominal source in the setting of recurrent pancreatic cancer, inflammation, and obstruction. 

The patient's chronic issues include chronic diarrhea in the setting of a history of C. Diff, fibromyalgia, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, hypothyroidism, and depression. Her medications upon admission included Ondansetron, irbesartan, Diphenoxylate-Atropine, Prochlorperazine, Benzonatate, Lorazepam, Oxazepam, MetFORMIN XR, carisoprodol, Creon, Amlodipine, Guaifenesin ER, HYDROmorphone, L.acidoph-B.lactis-B.longum, Omeprazole, Cetirizine, Duloxetine, Levothyroxine Sodium, Cyanocobalamin, FoLIC Acid, Glargine, and Insulin SC Sliding Scale using HUM Insulin.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a retired elementary school worker, was admitted with altered mental status (AMS), lethargy, and weakness. She has a history of insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (IDDM), hypertension (HTN), fibromyalgia, depression, and metastatic pancreatic adenocarcinoma. She had previously undergone a Whipple procedure and chemotherapy with radiation therapy (chemoXRT) for her pancreatic cancer, which was complicated by malignant right pleural effusions. She had a pleurX catheter placement for the effusions. 

The patient's daughter reported that her mother had been very tired recently, with suppressed appetite, and had become increasingly lethargic, taking in less food and spending most of her time in bed. The patient's urine had been darker than usual, and she had been experiencing fevers, mainly at night. In the emergency department, she received 2L of normal saline, 1G of intravenous vancomycin, and 1G of meropenem. 

The patient's past oncology history includes presenting with symptoms of abdominal fullness and early satiety, worsening abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. She was found to have elevated liver function tests (LFTs) and a CT scan showed biliary ductal dilatation with mild pancreatic duct dilatation and pancreatic glandular atrophy. An ERCP procedure demonstrated a 1 cm malignant-appearing biliary stricture in the distal common bile duct, and a pathology report confirmed adenocarcinoma. 

The patient's family history includes her mother passing from lung cancer, her father having a history of a cell tumor, and a maternal aunt with a history of breast cancer. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented to person and place, with reduced breath sounds at the right base. Lab results showed elevated white blood cell count (15.3), low red blood cell count (3.17), low hemoglobin (9.4), low hematocrit (30.5), low mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (30.8), high red cell distribution width (16.3), high glucose (184), high urea nitrogen (37), high creatinine (1.4), low sodium (127), low bicarbonate (16), high alanine aminotransferase (47), high aspartate aminotransferase (63), and high alkaline phosphatase (1342). 

A CT scan showed new areas of hypoattenuation in the right hepatic lobe with central cystic necrotic areas, concerning for hepatic abscesses. There was also increasing abdominal ascites, stable portal venous thrombosis, and bilateral small pleural effusions. A right upper quadrant ultrasound showed an ill-defined hypoechoic focus in the right lateral lobe of the liver, concerning for inflammatory change, and a small to moderate amount of ascites. A chest x-ray showed a small right pleural effusion. 

During her hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with polymicrobial sepsis and acute kidney injury. She was started on IV vancomycin, meropenem, and flagyl, which led to clinical improvement. A CT scan showed multiple extensive hepatic abscesses not amenable to drainage. The patient and her family elected not to undergo any procedures but to continue the antibiotic therapy with suppressive augmentin therapy while receiving palliative hospice care. 

The patient's medications upon admission included benzonatate, cetirizine, creon, duloxetine, guaifenesin ER, hydromorphone, levothyroxine sodium, omeprazole, ondansetron, oxazepam, carisoprodol, L.acidoph-B.lactis-B.longum, prochlorperazine, vancomycin oral liquid, metformin XR, and methylphenidate.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to pain and swelling of the right perineum. She had a history of a right Bartholin gland cyst, which was incised and drained years ago. Recently, she experienced swelling of the right labia and was prescribed Bactrim DS BID for a Bartholin gland abscess. However, her condition worsened, leading to an incision and drainage procedure and the placement of a Word's catheter. Post-procedure, she experienced significant bleeding and pain, which impaired her daily activities. 

Her obstetric history includes a spontaneous vaginal delivery complicated by cervical incompetence with rescue cerclage, and a cesarean section at 34 weeks due to preterm premature rupture of membranes, resulting in twins. Her gynecological history includes an abnormal Pap smear with no follow-up and a history of abnormal Pap smears post-LEEP. She denies any history of sexually transmitted infections. She has no chronic medical issues and her family history is not contributory.

Upon admission, her physical examination revealed an edematous, fluctuant, and erythematous right labia minora, which was spontaneously draining purulent fluid. A suture was in place at the inferior labia minora, effectively closing the incision site. No bleeding was noted. Her vital signs were stable.

Her lab results showed a mild leukocytosis and a drop in hematocrit from 32.3 to 29.0, likely due to blood loss from the initial incision. Other abnormal findings included a white blood cell count of 17.1, red blood cell count of 3.18, hemoglobin of 8.8, hematocrit of 29.0, MCHC of 30.3, RDWSD of 47.2, absolute neutrophil count of 12.10, absolute lymphocyte count of 3.78, absolute monocyte count of 1.11, eosinophils of 0.3, and a bicarbonate level of 21. Her urine was hazy in appearance with moderate blood, 30 protein, trace ketones, large leukocytes, 160 red blood cells, 103 white blood cells, and few bacteria.

During her hospital stay, she was switched from Bactrim to IV clindamycin and ampicillin-sulbactam. She underwent a successful incision and drainage in the operating room, after which she was transitioned to oral amoxicillin-clavulanate and clindamycin. Her pain was initially managed with ketorolac and hydromorphone, and later with oral acetaminophen, ibuprofen, and oxycodone. She was discharged in stable condition on the third day of her hospital stay.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of HIV/AIDS (CD4 200), non-compliance with antiretroviral therapy, herpes, and syphilis, presented with a 10-day history of a gradually worsening left-sided posterior headache, daily left-sided epistaxis, and generalized fatigue. He also reported waking up with blood on his face and pillow from his nose, with episodes lasting up to 15 minutes. He has tried a humidifier without improvement. He also noted clusters of red rash on his side, boils in his groin, and denied having similar headache or symptoms previously. He has not been taking his antiretroviral medication due to denial and depression. He is sexually active with his HIV positive fiancée. He has no previous history of migraine or tension headache. His father has a history of alcoholism, but no other known family medical history was reported.

Upon admission, the patient was given 1G of acetaminophen and a nicotine patch. A lumbar puncture was performed, which showed 6 WBC, 89% lymphs, protein 38, glucose 32. Gram stain was negative. His urine analysis showed few bacteria, negative nitrate, and negative leukocytes. His chest X-ray showed no acute intrathoracic process and a CT scan of his head showed no acute intracranial process. His blood glucose level was slightly elevated at 122. His cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) showed an elevated RBC count at 415 and 32. His MRI showed minimal mucosal thickening in the right maxillary sinus and a small mucous retention cyst. 

The patient was admitted to medicine for further workup of his headache and to rule out cryptococcal meningitis. His CD4 count was 178, viral load and phenotype studies were pending at the time of discharge. His headache was not associated with significant leukocytosis or elevated protein levels to suggest bacterial or viral meningitis. His CSF cryptococcal antigen was negative. His MRI was negative for acute intracranial abnormality, significant for minimal right maxillary sinus inflammation. He was encouraged to trial standing Tylenol for his headache, with naproxen as needed.
