His epistaxis did not improve with humidifier use, and he was given nasal saline twice a day without occurrence of epistaxis. His skin rash was non-specific and erythematous, with no evidence of vesicles to suggest HSV or erythema, induration, or warmth to suggest cellulitis. His RPR was negative. He also had non-tender swelling in his groin that could suggest lymphadenopathy. His RPR was negative and urine gonorrhea and chlamydia were pending at the time of discharge. 

The patient was not taking any medications upon admission. He was discharged home and instructed to follow up with his primary care doctor. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. His activity status was independent and ambulatory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of cortical blindness from birth, was admitted following a right temporal hemorrhage. She was initially presented with a headache and speech difficulties, which were noticed by her mother over a phone call. The patient was taken to an outside hospital where a head CT scan revealed the hemorrhage. She was then transferred for further neurological workup. An MRI/MRA was performed to rule out arteriovenous malformation (AVM), but the results were inconclusive. An angiogram was recommended by the neurology service.

The patient's condition declined, leading to elective intubation. She was hyponatremic and exhibited left hemiparesis. A craniectomy was performed, after which her condition remained stable. Post-operative head CT showed a decrease in midline shift and stable right temporal hemorrhage with an evolving right middle cerebral artery (MCA) infarction. The patient was difficult to wean off the ventilator and was transferred to rehab with ventilator support.

The patient had a dermatologic reaction resulting in a linear IgA bullous eruption, leading to a diagnosis of vancomycin allergy. She also developed cellulitis in her right posterior thigh. Continuous EEG monitoring was ordered to assess for seizure activity, which showed some bifrontal activity. Her medication, Keppra, was increased to 1000mg BID. She was also trached and PEGed.

Lab results showed abnormal values for WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCH, RDW, Plt Ct, Neuts, Bands, Monos, Glucose, UreaN, Cl, %HbA1c, eAG, Cholest, LDLcalc, pO2, pCO2, and calTCO2. The patient was not on any medications upon admission. 

The patient's mother is living and healthy. There is no mention of any other family history. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with right temporal hemorrhage, subfalcine herniation requiring right hemicraniectomy, right MCA infarct, left distal infarct, respiratory failure, dysphagia, cellulitis right posterior thigh, cortical blindness from birth, linear IgA bullous dermatoses, vancomycin reaction resulting in linear IgA bullous dermatosis, and a new vancomycin allergy. She was discharged to extended care.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female known to the neurosurgery service, was admitted for an elective cranioplasty and replacement of her bone flap. This was due to a previous ruptured arteriovenous malformation (AVM) which required a craniectomy for control of intracranial pressure and cerebral edema. She had been discharged to a rehab facility for three months prior to this admission, where she showed improvements in daily functioning. She was moving her right side fully, but her left side remained paretic. Her past medical history includes cortical blindness from birth. She has allergies to Vancomycin and Cephalosporins. Her mother is living and healthy.

Upon physical examination, she was alert and oriented, followed commands, and had reactive pupils. She had rotary nystagmus at rest, symmetric face, midline tongue, and intact sensation to light touch in all extremities. Her right side and left lower extremity showed full strength, but her left upper extremity was paretic.

Her lab results showed an elevated glucose level of 116*, a high platelet count of 469*, and a high white blood cell count of 16.1*. Her hemoglobin was 13.1, and her hematocrit was 39. Her CT/CTA head pre-op showed stable herniation of the brain through the right hemicraniectomy defect, along with regional cystic encephalomalacia due to a prior large right MCA infarct. Post-op CT showed status post right cranioplasty with reduction of previous brain herniation, postoperative changes including a small amount of pneumocephalus and small foci of blood products in the right temporal lobe, and placement of ventriculostomy catheter via left frontal approach.

During her hospital stay, she underwent a right-sided cranioplasty and placement of a left frontal external ventricular drain (EVD), both of which she tolerated well. She was transferred to the ICU for further care post-operatively. Her intracranial pressure (ICP) was continuously monitored, and she had no complaints. Her neurological exam remained at her baseline. A subdural drain was placed at the time of her surgery and remained in place with mild bloody drainage. She developed a morbiliform rash, which was thought to be a reaction to Ancef. She was started on triamcinolone cream and sarna as needed.

Upon admission, she was on several medications including Amlodipine, Heparin (Porcine), Lansoprazole, Levetiracetam, Levothyroxine, Methenamine Hippurate, Bisacodyl, Cyanocobalamin (Vitamin B-12), Ferrous Sulfate, Multivitamin, and Saccharomyces Boulardii.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to left breast cancer which was discovered during an annual mammogram screening and confirmed with a stereotactic-guided core biopsy that showed invasive ductal carcinoma grade ___. The patient's HER-2/neu receptor was negative. She has no family history of breast or ovarian cancer. Her past medical history includes a ___ trimester TAB ___ years ago, relatively regular cycles, no history of abnormal paps or STDs, and stress-induced asthma (no intubations or hospitalizations). She has no past surgical history. 

During her stay, she underwent a skin-sparing left total mastectomy, left axillary sentinel node mapping and biopsy followed by a left transverse rectus abdominis musculocutaneous flap using ipsilateral rectus muscle. The patient tolerated these procedures well. Post-operation, the patient was able to get out of bed to the chair without difficulty and her pain was controlled with a morphine PCA. Her diet was advanced, which she tolerated well, and her pain medication was transitioned to oral medications. She was tolerating a regular diet, ambulation and her pain was controlled with oral pain medication. 

Upon admission, the patient was on albuterol and omeprazole. Her physical examination showed a rapid heart rate, regular rhythm, normal S1, S2, clear lungs, and a mildly distended abdomen that was appropriately tender to palpation. Her left breast flap was warm and well perfused. Her extremities were warm, well perfused, and showed no calf tenderness. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of left breast cancer. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was able to ambulate independently. Her discharge instructions included personal care instructions for her bilateral breast dressings and drain sites, activity restrictions, and medication instructions. She was advised to call the office immediately if she experienced any signs of infection, a large amount of bleeding from the incision(s) or drain(s), fever greater than 101.5 oF, or severe pain not relieved by her medication.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the neurosurgery service due to a two-week history of dizziness and confusion. A CT scan revealed a left occipital mass with surrounding vasogenic edema. The patient also reported a new rash along his right armpit. In the ER, he had a temperature of 100.7 and elevated blood pressure. His past medical history includes hypertension and an old retinal injury. There is no known history of cancer or tumors in his family.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to have anisocoric pupils with the right larger than the left. His motor functions were intact, and he had sensation to light touch. His wound was clean, dry, and intact with staples in place. 

Lab results showed abnormal levels of WBC (17.0), RBC (4.22), Hct (39.5), MCH (32.5), RDWSD (49.7), PTT (24.0), Glucose (136), and UreaN (21). 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent an MRI that confirmed the left occipital mass. He was started on Dexamethasone 4mg Q6. A CT torso was completed and showed liver cirrhosis and a possible liver mass. There was also a lucency at the right iliac crest, leading to a recommendation for a bone scan. The patient underwent a stereotactic biopsy, which was uncomplicated. Post-operative NCHCT showed expected post-operative changes. He was started on a 7-day course of Keppra for seizure prophylaxis. His Dexamethasone dose was adjusted to 4mg Q8 prior to discharge. 

A liver MRI revealed findings consistent with cirrhosis and portal hypertension with no evidence of a lesion. Labs were sent to assess liver function. The patient reported a rash below his right armpit down his flank, which was treated with Ketoconazole 2% cream BID x 4 weeks.

Upon admission, the patient was on HCTZ 25mg QD. His discharge diagnoses included a left occipital mass, cerebral edema, liver cirrhosis, and a rash. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was discharged home with service.
label: controls

summary:
The male patient was admitted to the neurosurgery service due to an acute change in mental status. He had previously undergone a stereotactic brain biopsy, which revealed a grade 4 glioblastoma (GBM). His past medical history includes hypertension and an old retinal injury. There is no known history of cancer or brain tumors in his family.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be confused but awake, alert, and cooperative. He was oriented to person and place, but not date, and was unable to perform recall tasks. His language was limited and confused. His pupils were asymmetric, with the right pupil larger than the left. His extraocular movements were intact bilaterally without nystagmus, and facial strength and sensation were intact and symmetric. His motor function appeared normal, but he did not participate in a formal motor exam. There was a small palpable fluid collection at the site of his incision.

During his hospital stay, the patient presented with acute mental status changes. A CT scan of his head revealed an acute left subdural empyema and worsening cerebral edema. He underwent a left hemicraniectomy for decompression and evacuation of the empyema. His white blood cell count was elevated, suggesting a possible infection. He also required platelet transfusions to maintain a platelet goal of 50k.

There was suspicion of seizures, and EEG monitoring confirmed seizure activity. He was treated with Keppra and valproic acid. Despite this, he continued to have electrographic seizures. He was also intubated for surgery and remained so postoperatively, as he was unable to be weaned from pressure support.

The patient was diagnosed with a left subdural empyema, for which he was treated with vancomycin. Blood cultures showed gram-positive cocci in clusters. Palliative care was consulted, and the patient was made DNR. The family decided to pursue comfort measures, and the patient was extubated. He passed away with his family present at his bedside.

Upon admission, the patient was taking dexamethasone, famotidine, insulin, ketoconazole, and hydrochlorothiazide.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female of undisclosed age, presented with severe pelvic pain. She has a recent history of diagnostic laparoscopy, diagnostic hysterescopy, and insertion of a Mirena IUD due to severe dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, irregular menstrual cycles, and significant pelvic pain. No evidence of endometriosis or other pathology was found during surgery. She had previously left the ED before a full evaluation could be undertaken due to the long wait. She has been under significant stress at work and has agreed to seek psychiatric care for her strong anxiety but has not yet done so. 

Her past medical history includes GERD, asthma, fibromyalgia, and suspected anxiety disorder & PTSD. She has no known allergies or adverse drug reactions. Her family history is non-contributory. 

Upon physical examination, she was found to be in no acute distress, comfortable, and ambulatory. Her abdomen was soft, non-tender, with no rebound/guarding, and positive bowel sounds. She had minimal spotting on pad and was voiding spontaneously. Her extremities were non-tender and non-edematous. 

Her blood work showed a WBC of 8.9, RBC of 4.52, Hgb of 13.1, Hct of 39.8, MCV of 88, MCH of 28.9, MCHC of 32.9, RDW of 12.5, and Plt of undisclosed value. A transabdominal and transvaginal sonography was performed to assess IUD placement. The IUD was found to be satisfactorily placed within the endometrial cavity. A simple cyst measuring 5.6 x 3.6 x 3.1 cm was found in the right ovary. The left ovary appeared normal with follicular activity. There was no evidence of torsion or free fluid. 

During her hospital stay, her vital signs remained stable and her WBC was normal. An ultrasound showed a properly placed IUD and a simple, 5 cm right ovarian cyst. There was no evidence of torsion. Serial abdominal exams were benign. Multiple potential etiologies for pain were discussed with the patient, including onset of bleeding for the first time after recent surgery with IUD in place, simple cyst on right ovary, possible cyst rupture/leak leading to pain, and potential for microscopic disease. 

Upon admission, she was on omeprazole, naproxen, Percocet, and Mirena IUD. She was admitted overnight for serial abdominal exams to rule out acute intra-abdominal process. Her pain improved after IV toradol and she was able to rest comfortably overnight. In the morning, she was transitioned to PO naproxen and acetaminophen. Her pain continued to be well controlled. She was discharged home with close outpatient follow-up scheduled. Her discharge diagnosis was pelvic pain and right simple ovarian cyst.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a medical history of hypertension (HTN), hyperlipidemia (HLD), type 2 diabetes mellitus (DM2), nephrolithiasis, lower back pain (LBP), and multiple sclerosis (MS), presented with a one-day history of fevers, chills, dysuria, and bilateral flank pain. He reported feeling like he was 'losing control' when urinating, experiencing pain and a sense of incomplete voiding. He also experienced abdominal pain, bilateral flank pain, and a fever. His primary care physician suspected a urinary tract infection (UTI), pyelonephritis, or prostatitis and sent him to the emergency department (ED). 

In the ED, his vital signs were recorded and he was found to have bilateral flank tenderness and a non-tender prostate. His labs showed a white blood cell (WBC) count of 23.4 (N:87.2), a hemoglobin (Hgb)/crit of 17.2/49.7, and a lactate level of 3.5. He was given two 1000 mL boluses of normal saline (NS), blood cultures were drawn, and he was administered acetaminophen and ceftriaxone. 

Upon admission, the patient reported continued nausea but denied vomiting. He had difficulty taking deep breaths without pain and experienced small, minimal but frequent attempts at urination with continued dysuria. He denied constipation/diarrhea. He also reported mild burning of his eye a few days prior to admission, which he said usually resolves on its own. 

His physical exam upon admission showed tenderness along the lower flanks, more on the right than the left. His discharge exam showed mild tenderness along the lower right flank. 

Lab results showed an elevated WBC count, neutrophils, lymphocytes, glucose, and lactate levels. His urine culture showed the presence of Escherichia coli. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's condition improved both clinically and on labs; his hemoconcentration resolved and his white count eventually downtrended. His urinary hesitancy and burning dysuria improved with the help of pyridium and tamsulosin. His hypertension was largely controlled during his inpatient stay. 

Upon admission, the patient was on MetFORMIN, GlyBURIDE, Losartan Potassium, Oxybutynin, and Lumigan.
label: controls

summary:
The male patient, with a history of hypertension (HTN), hyperlipidemia (HLD), type 2 diabetes mellitus (DM2), nephrolithiasis, lower back pain (LBP), and multiple sclerosis (MS), was admitted due to fever. He had previously presented with abdominal pain, bilateral flank pain, fever, and urinary urgency. Initial concerns were urinary tract infection (UTI), pyelonephritis, or prostatitis. He was treated with antibiotics and discharged with a prescription for oral ciprofloxacin. Despite compliance with the medication, the patient continued to experience fevers, urinary urgency, and dysuria, leading to readmission. 

Upon readmission, the patient had a temperature of 101.4 and was given ceftriaxone and tylenol. Lab results indicated dehydration and a dirty urinalysis. The patient's mother has a history of HTN, but there is no family history of coronary artery disease, cerebrovascular accident, or diabetes. 

Abnormal lab results included glucose at 265*, sodium at 131*, chloride at 93*, lactate at 2.3*, neutrophils at 81.2*, lymphocytes at 11.0*, and urine red and white blood cells at 4* and 62* respectively. Urine culture showed over 100,000 organisms/ml of Escherichia Coli. Imaging revealed prostatitis with several fluid collections in the prostate, consistent with prostatic abscesses, splenomegaly, hepatic steatosis, and stable bibasilar pulmonary nodules. There was no evidence of pyelonephritis. 

The patient underwent ultrasound-guided drainage of the prostate abscess, with 2 cc of hemo-purulent fluid obtained and sent for microbiology analysis. The fluid showed no growth. The patient's hospital course involved treatment for prostate abscesses and poorly controlled diabetes. He was initially treated with IV ceftriaxone and underwent image-guided drainage of the largest abscess. He was transitioned to oral levofloxacin 24 hours prior to discharge. His diabetes was managed by resuming metformin and adding Glucotrol XL. His hypertension was managed by continuing half of his home dose of losartan due to lower blood pressures on admission, but he was transitioned back to his normal dose upon discharge. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Lumigan (bimatoprost) 0.01 % ophthalmic hs, Ciprofloxacin HCl 500 mg PO Q12H, GlyBURIDE 2.5 mg PO BID, Losartan Potassium 100 mg PO DAILY, Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO HS, and MetFORMIN XR (Glucophage XR) 1000 mg PO BID. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of prostate abscess, secondary to diabetes mellitus and hypertension. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent upon discharge.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a male with a history of relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (RR-MS), insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (IDDM), prostatic abscesses, and a recent dental abscess. He presented with a 10-day history of fever, chills, bilateral weakness and paresthesias in his legs and arms, and malaise. He had a dental abscess and molar extraction 9 days prior to admission and was prescribed oral amoxicillin, which he reportedly did not take as prescribed. He also reported a 2-week history of night sweats but no unintentional weight loss. His physical examination was notable for clear lungs bilaterally, normal heart sounds, a soft/non-tender abdomen, no CVA tenderness, warm and well-perfused extremities, and strength in all extremities.

Lab results showed an elevated WBC count of 13.8, and a glucose level of 110. Urine studies showed trace leukocytes, negative nitrites, 3 WBCs, no bacteria, and less than 1 epithelial. Chest X-ray demonstrated no acute cardiopulmonary abnormality. ECG showed a rate of 72bpm, sinus rhythm, left axis deviation, normal intervals, poor R wave progression, left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH), ST slurring in I/aVL, and T wave inversion in III/aVR. 

The patient was given IV Cefepime, IV Vancomycin, Pregabalin 150mg, Oxycodone 10mg, and Acetaminophen 1000mg. His condition improved in terms of strength and malaise. 

His past medical history includes four flares of RR-MS, neuropathy, urinary hesitancy, incomplete voiding, fatigue, snoring, witnessed apneic events, dragging of left foot at baseline, nephrolithiasis, prostatic abscess/prostatitis, resection of basal cell carcinomas and removal of a hemangioma from his lip, diabetes mellitus type II, hypertension, psoriasis, obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) on CPAP, and low back pain. His family history includes hypertension in his mother, with no history of coronary artery disease, cerebrovascular accident, or diabetes in the family.

Admission labs showed an elevated HbA1c of 7.5 and eAG of 169. Other abnormal findings included glucose of 172, WBC of 13.5, neutrophils of 75.9, lymphocytes of 11.7, absolute neutrophils of 10.20, and absolute monocytes of 1.00. Imaging findings showed no acute cardiopulmonary abnormality, no drainable fluid collections or foci of abnormal enhancement to suggest residual dental abscess, mild symmetric left ventricular hypertrophy with normal cavity size and regional/global biventricular systolic function, stable 1 mm nonobstructive calculus in lower pole of the right kidney, and small area of ground-glass opacity in the right lung base compatible with infectious etiology. Microbiology results showed no growth in urine and blood cultures. 

The patient was discharged with a primary diagnosis of community-acquired pneumonia and relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis, and secondary diagnoses of type 2 diabetes mellitus, chronic pain, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. His discharge medications included Cetirizine 10 mg, Levofloxacin 750 mg, Atorvastatin 10 mg, Ducodyl (bisacodyl) 5 mg, HYDROcodone-Acetaminophen (5mg-325mg), Glargine 26 Units, Losartan Potassium 100 mg, MetFORMIN (Glucophage) 500 mg, Pregabalin 150 mg, Triamcinolone Acetonide 0.1% Cream, Victoza 2-Pak (liraglutide) 1.2 mg, and Vitamin D 5000 UNIT.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), insulin-dependent diabetes type II, diabetic peripheral neuropathy, obstructive sleep apnea (OSA), kidney stones, and a prior prostatic abscess, presented with a fever and dysuria. He had been experiencing these symptoms for three days, along with reddish-tinged urine, fatigue, flank pain, and burning during urination. He had been prescribed ciprofloxacin for acute complicated cystitis but continued to have fevers. He also reported a loss of appetite and a weight loss of 60 lbs. 

Upon admission, his vitals were stable, and he was alert and in no apparent distress. His physical exam was largely unremarkable, with no signs of distress or acute illness. However, he had decreased sensation to light touch in both lower extremities and a small area of erythema on his right second toe. 

Lab results on admission showed an elevated white blood cell count (27.6*), neutrophils (83.6*), and glucose (197*), along with decreased lymphocytes (5.6*), sodium (134*), chloride (94*), bicarbonate (21*), and an elevated anion gap (19*). His lactate level was also elevated at 2.3*. On discharge, his white blood cell count had decreased to 14.0*, and his glucose was still elevated at 183*. Other notable labs included an elevated C-reactive protein (276.6*) and prostate-specific antigen (PSA, 34.2*). 

Microbiology results showed 10,000-50,000 CFU/mL of Enterococcus faecalis in his urine. Imaging studies, including a chest X-ray and CT of the abdomen/pelvis, did not reveal any acute processes. However, the CT did show a focus of ground-glass opacity in the posterior right lower lobe of the lung, which was unchanged from previous imaging, and prostatomegaly. A prostate ultrasound showed an enlarged prostate consistent with BPH but no evidence of prostatic or periprostatic abscess or drainable fluid collection. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with sepsis, which resolved, and an Enterococcus urinary tract infection. His leukocytosis improved, and his diabetic neuropathy/neuropathic pain was managed. His type 2 diabetes mellitus required higher doses of Lantus insulin while inpatient due to the unavailability of Victoza. His hypertension was managed with Losartan, and his hyperphosphatemia improved without intervention. His constipation improved with PRN Bisacodyl, Senna, and Miralax, and his high-density lipoprotein (HDL) was managed with atorvastatin. His elevated INR was stable and considered a chronic problem. His depression was noted, and his wife planned to have him see a psychiatric nurse. His hyponatremia and anion gap metabolic acidosis resolved. 

Upon discharge, the patient was prescribed a 21-day course of Augmentin for his urinary tract infection, among other medications. He was instructed to follow up with his primary care doctor one week after discharge. He was also scheduled for home physical therapy to assist with walking. His discharge diagnoses included sepsis, Enterococcus urinary tract infection, leukocytosis, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, neuropathic pain, depression, and anion gap metabolic acidosis.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a middle-aged female, was admitted due to a severe headache, which she described as similar to her previous migraines but worse. She has a history of chronic migraines, chronic lower back pain, and endometriosis. She also reported experiencing muscle spasms and episodic upper back pain. The patient's mother is blind due to a rare genetic disorder. 

Upon admission, the patient was in distress and her vital signs were recorded as 98.2 76 ___ 98%. She was given Toradol, Reglan, Zofran, and Dilaudid in the ED, but her pain did not significantly improve. An hour after receiving Dilaudid 1mg IV, her pain was reduced and she was able to eat dinner. Her nausea was well controlled and she was not vomiting. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Acetaminophen-Codeine, Magnesium/Feverfew/Riboflavin, Naratriptan [Amerge], Ondansetron, and Zonisamide. She was also taking over-the-counter Lactobacillus Rhamnosus GG [Probiotic], Multivitamin-Iron-Folic Acid [Centrum Ultra Women's], and Omega-3 Fatty Acids-Fish Oil [Omega 3 Fish Oil].

Lab results showed abnormal values for Hct-34.9*, MCV-80*, MCHC-38.2*, RDW-15.7*, Neuts-85.7*, Lymphs-12.1*, Monos-1.6*, and Glucose-104*. The patient was found to have microcytic anemia, likely due to iron deficiency. 

During her hospital stay, the patient's headache was treated with a 6-day prednisone taper and Depakote 500mg IV x1. Within 12 hours of this therapy, her headache was gone and she was drastically improved and ready to go home. She was discharged with a diagnosis of intractable migraine headache. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and able to walk independently.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to post-procedural pain and nausea following a Y-90 ablation of cholangiocarcinoma. She was found to have a liver mass during a previous admission for urosepsis and renal dysfunction. The liver mass was confirmed to be cholangiocarcinoma through a liver MRI and ultrasound-guided targeted biopsy. The patient has a history of heavy alcohol consumption but has never been diagnosed with liver disease or cirrhosis. She tested negative for hepatitis C and B. She reported feeling generally well with no signs or symptoms from her disease. 

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, alcoholism, benign positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, and osteoporosis. She has undergone several surgical procedures including tubal ligation, gastric bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. 

The patient's family history includes her mother being diagnosed with lung cancer and her father dying from gastric cancer and lung cancer. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Ondansetron, Lisinopril, Hydrocortisone cream, Lamotrigine, Lorazepam, Diphenhydramine, Ferrous Sulfate, Pantoprazole, Calcium Carbonate, Hydrochlorothiazide, Folic Acid, Pyridoxine, Riboflavin, Cyanocobalamin, Ascorbic Acid, Vitamin D, and Bupropion. 

The patient's physical examination revealed mild tenderness in the abdomen. Her lab results showed abnormal values for RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, RDWSD, AlkPhos, Calcium, and Phos. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent a Y-90 ablation for her cholangiocarcinoma. Post-procedure, she complained of severe abdominal pain, which was managed with IV dilaudid. By the time of discharge, she was tolerating a regular diet, mobile, and only required oral dilaudid for pain control. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of cholangiocarcinoma post Y-90 ablation complicated by pain/post embolization syndrome. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. She was advised to follow up with her gastroenterologist and the tumor clinic for further management of her condition.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female with a history of underlying liver disease, recent diagnosis of poorly differentiated cholangiocarcinoma, and recent treatment with Y90 radioembolization and portal vein embolization. She was admitted due to an episode of syncope. She reported feeling dizzy and lightheaded before blacking out and waking up on the floor. She had a laceration on her left chin, which was sutured. She had no chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath prior to the fall. She had been experiencing severe dehydration and lightheadedness due to a recent episode of colitis, which had resolved. She had also been experiencing poor appetite, abdominal pain when trying to eat, and significant weight loss.

Upon admission, her vitals were 98.6 91 138/64 24 96% RA. She appeared dehydrated and had right-sided abdominal tenderness. Labs showed WBC 4.4, H/H 7.7/23.9, Plt 191, Na 133, BUN/Cr ___, ALP 1103, albumin 2.6, lactate 1.0, INR 1.0, and bland UA. A CT scan showed diffuse anasarca with ascites and pleural effusions, as well as wall thickening of the cecal and descending colon. A CXR showed small bilateral pleural effusions. 

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, alcoholism, BPPV, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, and several surgeries including tubal ligation, RNY gastric bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. Her family history includes lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Her medications upon admission included bupropion, lamotrigine, lorazepam, ondansetron, and acetaminophen. 

Abnormal lab results included WBC 2.9*, RBC 2.26*, Hgb 7.7*, Hct 24.0*, MCV 106*, MCH 34.1*, RDW 19.5*, RDWSD 75.2*, AlkPhos 1047*, GGT 306*, and Albumin 2.3*. Imaging showed diffuse anasarca, large ascites, bilateral moderate nonhemorrhagic pleural effusions, subcutaneous edema, wall thickening of the cecum and mid to distal descending colon, and a large known liver mass. 

Conditions and symptoms that have been ruled out include acute ischemia, hemorrhage, new masses, acute colitis, and intrathoracic metastasis. The patient's cancer progression or metastasis remains uncertain.

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to weakness, malnutrition, and failure to thrive. She has a primary diagnosis of T1N0Mx cholangiocarcinoma and has undergone Y90 radioembolization and portal vein embolization. She reported an inability to eat, which has led to marked weakness and nearly complete failure to thrive. She also reported increasing abdominal bloating, nausea, and pain over the last several days. She has shortness of breath, but no cough or chest pain. She also reported some urinary incontinence due to inability to get to the commode. 

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. She has a family history of lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including Acetaminophen, BuPROPion, LamoTRIgine, FoLIC Acid, HYDROmorphone, Multivitamins, Thiamine, Ondansetron, and LORazepam. 

Her lab results showed several abnormalities including low WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, and high RDW, RDWSD. Her AST, AlkPhos, Albumin, Calcium, Ferritn, VitB12, and TSH levels were also abnormal. Her imaging results showed bilateral layering pleural effusions, a large liver mass, and wall thickening of the cecum and mid to distal descending colon. 

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with severe protein-calorie malnutrition, anasarca with ascites and pleural effusions, frequent bowel movements, right upper quadrant pain, anemia, and depression/bipolar disorder. She was given 1u RBC for her low Hgb level. Her TPN was initiated and she was discharged with TPN. She was also given remeron for appetite stimulation and ritalin during daytime to augment mood. 

She was discharged to rehab for physical therapy. Her discharge diagnosis included failure to thrive, anasarca, severe protein-calorie malnutrition, T1N0Mx cholangiocarcinoma, and major depression.

summary:
The patient was admitted for a liver biopsy due to a 6.8 cm hepatic mass suggestive of cholangiocarcinoma. The patient had a history of intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. The patient's family history includes lung cancer in the mother and gastric cancer in the father.

During the hospital stay, the patient experienced complications including a left-sided pleural effusion, likely hepatic hydrothorax, causing significant dyspnea. This required chest tube placement. The patient also experienced significant pain, which was partially alleviated with the removal of the chest tube. Blood cultures on admission were positive for S. epidermidis, likely due to contamination from a PICC line.

The patient's past oncologic history includes urosepsis and renal dysfunction, during which a liver mass was discovered. A liver MRI showed a 6.8 cm right hepatic mass. An ultrasound-guided targeted biopsy of the liver mass was consistent with a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma. The patient underwent Yttrium-90 radioembolization but declined neoadjuvant chemotherapy prior to planned surgical resection. The patient was admitted with syncope and weakness in the setting of recent colitis and dehydration. Noted to be anasarcic with new pleural effusions and ascites, ascitic fluid did not show malignant cells. Upcoming surgical resection was deferred due to nutritional status, weakness, and further evaluation with oncology.

Abnormal lab results include RBC-2.59*, Hgb-8.7*, Hct-26.5*, MCV-102*, MCH-33.6*, RDW-16.5*, RDWSD-61.1*, UreaN-25*, AST-62*, AlkPhos-1067*, TotProt-5.2*, Albumin-2.1*, and Calcium-7.5*. Cytology results were negative for malignant cells in both peritoneal and pleural fluid. Liver biopsy showed preserved architecture and features suggestive of nodular regenerative hyperplasia, minimal steatosis, minimal portal and lobular mononuclear inflammation, sinusoidal and focal mild portal fibrosis, and minimal iron deposition in hepatocytes. Microbiology results showed Staphylococcus epidermidis in the blood culture, resistant to Clindamycin, Erythromycin, Levofloxacin, and Oxacillin, but sensitive to Gentamicin, Rifampin, and Vancomycin. No growth was found in the pleural fluid culture.

The patient's medications upon admission included Bupropion, Ipratropium-Albuterol Neb, Acetaminophen, Hydromorphone, Lamotrigine, Lorazepam, Methylphenidate, Mirtazapine, and Loperamide. The patient was allergic to Celebrex, Norflex, Tylenol-Codeine #3, and nitroglycerin.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted for surgery due to cholangiocarcinoma. She underwent a diagnostic laparoscopy, right hepatic trisegmentectomy, and intraoperative cholangiography. The patient had previously been treated for intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma with Y90 embolization and right portal vein embolization. However, surgery was initially deferred due to liver decompensation and concerns for malnutrition. Her condition improved and she was deemed suitable for resection. Her last staging MR showed no evidence of extrahepatic disease and the tumor was mainly confined to the right lobe of the liver with some encroachment on segment 4. 

The patient's past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. She has a family history of lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including bupropion, dronabinol, folic acid, hydromorphone, lamotrigine, Viokace, sodium bicarbonate, lorazepam, acetaminophen, vitamin D, loperamide, and milk of magnesia. 

During her hospital stay, she required a unit of prbc for hct of 24.5 that increased to 27.7 after the transfusion. She reported pain post-operation and was lethargic and mildly confused on postop day. She developed dysuria and a urine culture grew > 100,000 Klebsiella, sensitive to Bactrim. She was hypertensive with SBP in the 170s and was given hydralazine. Her LFTs initially increased as expected and bilirubin was as high as 3.7 ALT and AST increased following the second surgery to 1287 and 2331 respectively. These were decreasing daily, as well as the bilirubin slowly decreasing, and down to 3 by the day of discharge. 

The patient was having difficulty with maintaining oral intake due to no interest in food, lack of appetite. A feeding tube was inserted and she was started on tube feeds. She was evaluated by physical therapy who recommended she go to rehab in the post-op period. Her JP drain was removed prior to discharge. Her home regimen of medications was restarted, Ativan dose was decreased due to the liver surgery, and narcotic pain medication was stopped to help with mental status. 

Abnormal lab results include WBC-10.3*#, RBC-2.66*#, Hgb-8.8*#, Hct-26.9*#, MCV-101*, MCH-33.1*, MCHC-32.7, RDWSD-54.5*, Glucose-151*, Cl-109*, HCO3-21*, Calcium-8.0*, RBC-2.87*, Hgb-9.2*, Hct-26.7*, MCH-32.1*, RDW-18.4*, RDWSD-59.9*, Glucose-108*, Na-131*, ALT-151*, AST-72*, AlkPhos-683*, TotBili-3.0*, Albumin-2.9*, and Calcium-8.2*. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with cholangiocarcinoma, UTI, bipolar disorder, and moderate malnutrition. She was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, but required assistance or aid for ambulation. She was discharged to a rehab facility.

summary:
The patient is a female known to our service for a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma. She has undergone Y90 embolization, right portal vein embolization, and trisegmentectomy. She was last seen in the outpatient clinic where she reported an improved appetite and denied any abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or fevers. A follow-up MRCP demonstrated a 9.9 cm postoperative collection with a small 1.4 cm component along its posterior aspect, likely seroma versus biloma. She was admitted for percutaneous drainage.

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. Her family history includes lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Upon admission, her labs showed abnormal RBC count (2.99), Hgb (9.9), Hct (30.6), MCV (102), MCH (33.1), RDW (15.6), RDWSD (58.7), Glucose (112), ALT (61), AST (61), and AlkPhos (1436). At discharge, her labs showed abnormal RBC count (3.12), Hgb (10.2), MCH (32.7), RDWSD (49.8), UreaN (4), AlkPhos (1011), and Calcium (8.3).

During her stay, she underwent ultrasound-guided drainage of the collection along the resection margin, ERCP, and EUS Directed transgastric ERCP with stent placement. The ERCP was terminated due to difficulty in cannulating the CBD due to her history of Roux-en-Y gastric bypass. The EUS Directed transgastric ERCP revealed a high-grade bile leak, and an 8mm x 60mm uncovered Wallflex metal stent was successfully placed in the common bile duct. She experienced significant abdominal pain following the procedure, and her lipase was found to be 5395. She was kept NPO and on IV fluids for the next two days, and her pain resolved and lipase returned to normal.

Her medications upon admission included bupropion, dronabinol, folic acid, hydromorphone, lamotrigine, and lorazepam. A CT scan prior to discharge showed a significant decrease in the size of the fluid collection along the trisegmentectomy margin, and the CBD stent was in place. She was discharged to home with assistance for the pigtail catheter flushing, monitoring, and dressing changes.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy, was admitted due to a bile leak. She had previously undergone a right trisegmentectomy and had been discharged with a drain in place. However, she presented with several days of malaise and increased drain output, along with symptoms of poor appetite, fatigue, pain around the drain, weight loss, shortness of breath, subjective fevers, and dizziness when standing.

During her hospital stay, she underwent several procedures including the placement of a biliary drain, a Dobhoff tube, and multiple upsizing of the PTBD due to leakage. Her lab results showed several abnormalities including elevated WBC, RBC, MCH, RDWSD, Plt Ct, Neuts, Lymphs, Monos, Eos, UreaN, Creat, Na, K, Cl, HCO3, AnGap, Calcium, AST, AlkPhos, and Lipase. Her bile culture showed moderate growth of gram-negative rods and sparse growth of Clostridium perfringens. 

The patient's hospital course was complicated by persistent nausea and vomiting, poor appetite, and high drain output. She was treated with IV fluids, Vancomycin, Zosyn, Zofran, and supplements. Her LFTs showed a decrease in alk phos from 1000 on admission to 600s. She also had a dobhoff tube placed and jevity tf started with a goal rate of 45cc/hour continuous. Despite these interventions, she continued to have drainage around the PTBD insertion site. 

The patient also experienced increased depression due to her prolonged hospital stay and multiple procedures. She was started on Remeron 7.5mg at HS with improvement in mood and sleep. She also fell out of bed without sustaining any injury but experienced slight dizziness with shaky legs and felt her legs buckle. Her pain was managed with Tylenol, Neurontin, and Fentanyl patch with break thru oral dilaudid. 

Her family history includes lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father. Her medications upon admission included bupropion, dronabinol, folic acid, hydromorphone, lamotrigine, and lorazepam.

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to weakness and leakage around her percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (PTBD) site. She has a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, for which she underwent a right trisegmentectomy. This was complicated by a bile leak from the cut liver edge, requiring ERCP/stenting and percutaneous drainage. She has had multiple admissions for increasing drain output and has had her PTBD replaced three times. She has been on tube feeds and taking Augmentin since her last discharge. She denies experiencing fevers, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, jaundice, pruritis, or dysuria.

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. She has also undergone multiple ERCP/PTBD placements and exchanges. Her family history includes lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Upon physical examination, she appeared tired and had tenderness to palpation at the midline PTBD drain site with minimal surrounding erythema. Her dressing was saturated with clear bilious output. Her subhepatic drain site was clean, dry, and intact with minimal thick cloudy output. She had no rebound or guarding and no palpable masses. Her vitals were stable.

Her admission labs showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDWSD, and glucose levels. Her AlkPhos was also elevated. Her discharge labs showed similar abnormalities, with a decrease in her Hgb level.

During her stay, she underwent a cholangiogram which showed a decompressed biliary tree and no evidence of a bile leak when the percutaneous biliary drain was injected. However, a small persistent leak was noted upon injection of the subhepatic drain. She had successful exchanges of her PTBD catheters. Despite this, she continued to have significant drainage around the PTBD, and wound/ostomy was called for assistance with placement of an ostomy device around the PTBD for skin preservation. This started to leak under the appliance within about 12 hours.

Her medications upon admission included Acetaminophen, Multivitamins W/minerals, amLODIPine, BuPROPion, Docusate Sodium, Dronabinol, Gabapentin, HYDROmorphone, LamoTRIgine, LORazepam, Mirtazapine, Pantoprazole, Polyethylene Glycol, Senna, and Ursodiol.

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, was admitted due to a persistent bile leak following a right trisegmentectomy. The bile leak had resulted in dehydration and difficulty with nutrition. She reported constant pain from her drain and frequent leaking of bile around the right drain and the percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (PTBD). She denied experiencing fever, chills, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or dysuria.

Her past medical history includes intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. She has also undergone multiple ERCP/ PTBD placements and exchanges. Her family history includes lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

During her hospital stay, she underwent an exploratory laparotomy with extensive lysis of adhesions, Roux-en-Y hepaticojejunostomy to intrahepatic bile duct, removal of previous pigtail drain and percutaneous biliary drain, and placement of a new Silastic transhepatic biliary drain. Postoperatively, she experienced low urine output and hypotension, which improved with IV fluid and IV Albumin administration. She also experienced a decrease in hematocrit, which was managed with PRBC transfusion.

Her lab results showed an increase in liver function tests, with total bilirubin reaching up to 6.3. She also tested positive for Enterobacter Cloacae in her blood cultures and Clostridium Difficile in her stool. Her blood work also showed abnormal values for RBC (2.56*), Hgb (8.1*), Hct (24.8*), RDW (21.2*), RDWSD (73.1*), Glucose (112*), UreaN (14), and HCO3 (27). Her ALT, AST, LD(LDH), AlkPhos, and TotBili were also elevated at various points during her stay.

She was treated with Ciprofloxacin and Flagyl for the Enterobacter Cloacae bacteremia, and oral Vancomycin for the C.diff infection. She also developed myoclonus and choreiform-like leg movements, which were managed by decreasing her Gabapentin and Dilaudid doses. Her discharge diagnoses included bile leak, malnutrition, incision cellulitis, C.diff, Enterobacter cloacae bacteremia, depression, and resolved myoclonus.

Upon admission, her medications included amLODIPine, Amoxicillin-Clavulanic Acid, BuPROPion XL, FoLIC Acid, Gabapentin, HYDROmorphone (Dilaudid), LamoTRIgine, LORazepam, Mirtazapine, Ursodiol, Acetaminophen, Vitamin D, Multivitamins W/minerals, Polyethylene Glycol, and Pantoprazole.

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to fevers and bile leakage from her midline incision. She has a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, which was treated with Y-90 embolization and portal vein embolization, followed by right liver trisegmentectomy. She experienced a persistent bile leak, which required several months of percutaneous treatment and ultimately surgical intervention. She underwent a Roux-en-Y jejunostomy to an exposed segment bile duct. Her post-operative course was marked by C. difficile infection, Enterobacter cloacae bacteremia, malnutrition, and incision cellulitis. She presented with ongoing bile drainage from the drain site, swelling of the anterior portion of her midline incision, associated erythema and warmth, fevers at home, and generalized malaise. She reported poor oral intake due to feeling unwell, but denied nausea/emesis. She reported ongoing non-bloody diarrhea and was completing a course of oral antibiotics for C.diff. She denied chest pain, shortness of breath, dysuria, or urinary frequency. 

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, tonsillectomy, diagnostic laparoscopy, right hepatic trisegmentectomy, intraoperative cholangiography with takeback for hemorrhage, and multiple ERCP/ PTBD placement and exchanges. Her family history includes lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Upon admission, her vitals were T101.8 HR116 BP128/80 RR18 96% RA. Her physical exam showed erythema, fluctuance with central dehiscence and active drainage of bile from her midline incision, and bile draining from JP site left lateral to midline incision, staining skin. Her labs on admission showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, RDWSD, Na, Cl, ALT, AST, AlkPhos, TotBili, DirBili, and Albumin. A CT scan showed a 5.2 x 1.6 x 6.6 cm collection of gas and bile in the anterior abdominal wall, which appears contiguous with the entry site/tract of the previously removed percutaneous biliary drain, which may represent the source for bile leak. The liver did not show evidence of biliary dilatation.

Her medications upon admission included bupropion, ciprofloxacin, furosemide, gabapentin, hydromorphone, lamotrigine, lorazepam, mirtazapine, pantoprazole, spironolactone, ursodiol, vancomycin oral liquid, acetaminophen, vitamin D, and multivitamins with minerals. 

She was discharged with a diagnosis of wound dehiscence and wound infection. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was instructed to call her doctor for any concerning symptoms, to shower but not apply lotions or powders to the incision areas, to cover the incision with a dry gauze and change as needed, not to lift more than 10 pounds, and not to drive if taking narcotic pain medication.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, was admitted due to drainage from the sub-xiphoid area. She had previously undergone Y-90 embolization, portal vein embolization, and R liver trisegmentectomy, and had been dealing with a persistent bile leak. She also had microwave ablation of metastatic lesions in her remnant liver. She developed skin breakdown at the subxiphoid puncture site with bilious drainage over the past 6 weeks, which was consistent with a biliocutaneous fistula. She reported tenderness around the wound and some yellow/green drainage on the gauze sponge, but denied fever, chills, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea.

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. She has also undergone diagnostic laparoscopy, right hepatic trisegmentectomy, intraoperative cholangiography with takeback for hemorrhage, and multiple ERCP/ PTBD placement and exchanges. Her family history includes lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Upon admission, her medications included amlodipine, bupropion, folic acid, furosemide, hydromorphone, mirtazapine, ursodiol, vitamin D, and multivitamins with minerals. Her physical exam revealed a quarter-sized opening at the superior aspect of her midline incision with yellow/green drainage on gauze sponge, mildly tender to palpation around the wound, with some skin irritation surrounding.

Lab results showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, RDW, RDWSD, and glucose levels. Her AlkPhos and Calcium levels were also abnormal. A swab from her abdominal wound showed 1+ polymorphonuclear leukocytes and gram-positive cocci, with sparse growth of Enterococcus sp. 

During her hospital stay, she was started on IV Vancomycin. Her drainage became less bilious and the PTBD was cancelled. A wound culture was obtained that isolated sparse enterococcus felt to be a contaminant. A VAC dressing was placed that was removed the next day as the drainage was minimal and purulent that was too thick for the VAC. Drainage was thick/very light yellow/green. Normal saline wet to dry dressing changes were done. She declined tube feeds and preferred to try to eat as well possible and drink the Ensure Clears. MVI with minerals twice daily was started.

Upon discharge, her diagnosis included an enterocutaneous fistula, cholangio CA, and malnutrition. She was discharged to home in stable condition.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, was admitted due to shortness of breath. She had previously undergone Y-90 embolization, portal vein embolization, and right liver trisegmentectomy, and had been managing a biliocutaneous fistula of the subxiphiod region at home. She also reported recent diarrhea and loss of appetite. Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. She has a family history of lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam revealed clear breath sounds in the left chest, absent breath sounds in the right hemithorax, and tenderness around the biliary cutaneous fistula. Her vitals were stable, and she was alert and oriented. Her medications upon admission included amlodipine, bupropion, folic acid, furosemide, mirtazapine, hydromorphone, ursodiol, vitamin D, and multivitamins with minerals.

During her hospital stay, the patient underwent a right thoracentesis, which improved her shortness of breath. Her blood tests showed abnormal values for RBC (2.75*), Hgb (10.1*), Hct (29.4*), MCV (107*), MCH (36.7*), RDWSD (60.2*), ALT (69*), AST (124*), AlkPhos (643*), TotBili (2.0*), and TotProt (6.0*). Her urine culture showed mixed bacterial flora, consistent with fecal contamination. Her pleural fluid culture showed no growth.

The patient was discharged in her typical state of health with a diagnosis of right pleural effusion, right diaphragmatic hernia, and cholangio carcinoma. She was instructed to follow up with thoracic surgery regarding the possible repair of her diaphragmatic hernia.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted for a diaphragmatic hernia. She has a history of cholangiocarcinoma, which was treated with Y90 embolization, portal vein embolization, and right trisegmentectomy. She had recurrent disease in the remnant liver treated with microwave ablation, which was complicated by biliary cutaneous fistula that has since healed. She underwent a right hepatectomy followed by complications that required additional abdominal surgery. On serial imaging, she was noted to have a large hernia with the majority of the small bowel and colon, within her right chest. She presented with a symptomatic right diaphragmatic hernia with associated shortness of breath on mild exertion and lying flat. She underwent a right thoracotomy, repair of diaphragmatic hernia with mesh.

Her past medical history includes intracranial meningioma, bipolar disorder, depressive disorder, anxiety, prior alcoholism, benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, hypertension, left ventricular hypertrophy, chronic back pain, osteoporosis, tubal ligation, gastric RNY bypass, total abdominal hysterectomy, and tonsillectomy. She has a history of multiple ERCP/ PTBD placement and exchanges. She has a family history of lung cancer in her mother and gastric cancer in her father.

Upon admission, she was on amLODIPine, BuPROPion, Calcium Carbonate, Furosemide, Omeprazole, Ursodiol, Mirtazapine, and Meclizine. Her physical exam showed no abnormalities. However, her lab results showed abnormal levels of WBC-3.2*, RBC-3.20*, Hgb-10.5*, Hct-32.6*, MCV-102*, MCH-32.8*, MCHC-32.2, RDWSD-47.9*, Plt Ct-94*, and AlkPhos-360*, and Phos-2.3*.

During her hospital stay, she had no nausea or vomiting, and her pain was well controlled. She experienced intermittent bouts of tachycardia when out of bed ambulating but remained asymptomatic without chest pain, shortness of breath or palpitations. EKG was within normal limits and CXR was stable with small bilateral pleural effusions. She was discharged in stable condition - pain controlled, tolerating diet, passing gas and was instructed to follow-up in clinic for her scheduled postoperative visit.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the orthopaedics service due to an infection in his right distal tibia. His chief complaint was an infection in the right distal tibia. He had previously suffered a right open distal tibia fracture after falling off a platform. Post-operatively, a wound developed on the lateral aspect of his ankle, which has persisted. He reported to the emergency department due to increasing pain and fever/chills over the last few days. 

During his hospital stay, he underwent two Incision and Drainage (I&D) procedures with Vacuum-Assisted Closure (VAC) placement for his right distal tibia fracture. A Peripherally Inserted Central Catheter (PICC) line was also placed for long-term antibiotics. His physical examination revealed a 1 cm wound over the lateral malleolus with necrotic tissue that probes to bone, surrounded by erythema extending above with blisters. His range of motion was limited to plantar flexion at the ankle. 

His lab results showed several abnormal findings including decreased Red Blood Cell (RBC) count, Hemoglobin (Hgb), Hematocrit (Hct), Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH), and Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin Concentration (MCHC). His White Blood Cell (WBC) count was also elevated, indicating a possible infection. His neutrophil and lymphocyte counts were also elevated. 

During his hospital stay, he was initially started on vancomycin until cultures from the operating room grew Methicillin-Sensitive Staphylococcus Aureus (MSSA). The vancomycin was then stopped and nafcillin was started. He was also evaluated by the infectious disease service for infection management. His hospital stay was otherwise uneventful with his lab data and vital signs within normal limits. 

Upon admission, the patient denied taking any medications. His discharge diagnosis was an infected distal tibia pilon fracture in the right lower extremity. His mental status was clear and coherent, but he required assistance or aid for ambulation. His discharge instructions included wound care, activity restrictions, dietary instructions, medication instructions, and physical therapy. He was advised to follow up with his primary orthopaedic surgeon.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a middle-aged male, was admitted due to bilateral lower extremity weakness, gait instability, balance difficulties, and right upper extremity weakness and loss of coordination. He reported progressive difficulties with gait/balance and intermittent bilateral lower extremity weakness and buckling over the past few weeks, leading to frequent falls. He also experienced increased right upper extremity weakness and loss of coordination with his dominant right hand, causing an inability to write or use his hand for fine motor activities such as typing. He described his lower extremity weakness as unpredictable and intermittent, causing frequent loss of balance. He also reported some recently developing parasthesias in his left palm. He denied bowel/bladder incontinence or saddle anesthesia, although he noted increased urinary urgency. 

The patient underwent a C4-7 Anterior Cervical Discectomy and Fusion (ACDF) procedure due to the progression of his neurologic symptoms and high risk of paralysis or severe cervical spinal cord injury if he continued to sustain frequent falls. His past medical history includes lumbar radiculopathy status post L4-5 fusion. He works as a professional but has been unable to work for the past 2 weeks due to his progressive neurologic symptoms. He denies tobacco, alcohol, and illicit drug use. 

On physical examination, the patient was found to be mildly hyper-reflexic in the bilateral lower extremities. His lab results showed abnormal values for WBC (13.5), RBC (4.36), Hgb (13.3), Hct (37.9), and Glucose (132). 

During his hospital stay, the patient was taken to the Operating Room for the above procedure. The surgery was without complication and the patient was transferred to the PACU in a stable condition. His initial postoperative pain was controlled with oral and IV pain medication. His diet was advanced as tolerated. Physical therapy and Occupational therapy were consulted for mobilization out of bed to ambulate and activities of daily living. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Gabapentin, Motrin, and Tylenol. His discharge diagnoses included cervical disc herniation, cervical stenosis, and cervical myelopathy. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. However, he required assistance or aid for ambulation.
label: controls

summary:
The male patient was admitted due to general malaise and weakness. He had recently been admitted for myelopathy, with an MRI showing C4-5 disc protrusion with canal compromise, cord impingement, and edema on T2 sequences, which was likely indicative of myelomalacia. There were also less severe cord contour irregularities noted from moderate disc bulges at C5-6 and C6-7. He underwent a C4-7 Anterior Cervical Decompression and Fusion (ACDF) and was discharged home. However, he presented again with difficulty ambulating due to bilateral lower extremity knee buckling/weakness and feelings of dizziness/lightheadedness when standing up. He also reported not having a bowel movement in 5 days despite taking stool softeners and some nausea which limited his oral intake. He denied chest pain, shortness of breath, difficulty breathing or swallowing, and any issues with his incision site. 

His past medical history includes lumbar radiculopathy status post L4-5 fusion. He works as a professional but has not been able to work for the past 2 weeks due to his progressive neurologic symptoms. He denies tobacco, alcohol, and illicit drug use. 

Upon physical examination, he appeared well and comfortable. His upper and lower extremities showed some strength limitations. His incision was open to air and clean/dry/intact. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for WBC (10.2), RBC (4.07), Hgb (12.4), Hct (36.3), Lymphs (18.5), AbsNeut (8.22), AbsMono (1.11), and Glucose (102). 

During his hospital stay, he was managed medically with IV fluids and physical therapy for evaluation to return home. His diet was advanced as tolerated and he was tolerating oral thin liquids and foods well. He reported feeling much better with good strength and ability to mobilize well with the walker without weakness or instability. 

Upon admission, he was on Gabapentin, Motrin, and Tylenol. His discharge diagnosis was dehydration. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive, but required assistance or aid for ambulation. He was discharged home with service.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the Orthopaedics service with the chief complaint of lumbar radiculopathy. An MRI obtained in the office showed spinal stenosis at the L4 level. The patient's past medical history includes lumbar radiculopathy status post L4-5 fusion, as reported by the patient. The patient has an allergy to docusate. 

A major surgical procedure, an L4 laminectomy, was performed without complications. Postoperative pain was managed with oral and IV pain medication. The patient's diet was advanced as tolerated. Physical therapy and Occupational therapy were consulted for mobilization out of bed to ambulate and perform activities of daily living. The patient's hospital course was unremarkable. On the day of discharge, the patient was afebrile with stable vital signs, comfortable on oral pain control, and tolerating a regular diet. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed as status post lumbar L4 laminectomy. The patient's mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. However, he required the assistance of a walker for ambulation. 

The patient's discharge medications included Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO Q8H, Morphine SR 15 mg PO Q12H, and OxyCODONE (Immediate Release) as needed for pain. 

The patient was discharged home with service. His discharge instructions included not lifting anything greater than 10 lbs for 2 weeks, walking for recovery, limiting lifting, eating a normal healthy diet, wearing a brace when walking, and wound care instructions. He was also instructed to resume his normal home medications and was given additional medications to control his pain. 

The patient was advised to follow up in 2 weeks for an incision check, baseline X-rays, and possibly the start of physical therapy. He was also advised to call the office if he had a fever over 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit and/or drainage from his wound. 

The patient's physical therapy included weight bearing as tolerated and using a walker for ambulation. He was instructed to follow discharge instructions for wound care.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted to the orthopedics service due to severe progressive difficulty with walking. This was found to be related to spondylolisthesis with severe stenosis at both L3-L4 and L4-L5. She elected to undergo surgical treatment for this condition. The major surgical procedure performed was an L3-5 lami and instrumented fusion. 

Her past medical history includes diabetes mellitus, asthma, hypertension, and a rotator cuff condition. She has an allergy to penicillins. Upon admission, she was on several medications including Losartan Potassium, Memantine, Furosemide, Donepezil, Omeprazole, Potassium Chloride, and Aspirin.

The physical examination revealed normal respiratory effort, regular heart rate and rhythm, and a dressing that was clean, dry, and intact. Sensory and motor exams were within normal limits. However, her blood work showed several abnormal results including elevated white blood cell counts (WBC-10.8*, 14.0*, 10.4*), low red blood cell counts (RBC-3.09*, 3.25*, 3.40*), low hemoglobin (Hgb-9.0*, 9.3*, 9.9*), and low hematocrit (Hct-25.4*, 26.6*, 28.4*). Her glucose levels were also elevated (Glucose-136*, 110*), and she had low calcium (Calcium-8.3*) and phosphorus (Phos-2.5*) levels. Her urine test showed the presence of protein (Protein-30*) and elevated white blood cells (WBC-16*), with few bacteria (Bacteri-FEW*).

During her hospital stay, she underwent surgery without complications and was transferred to the PACU in a stable condition. Postoperative pain was managed with oral and IV pain medication. Her diet was advanced as tolerated and her Foley was removed on postoperative day 2. Physical therapy and occupational therapy were consulted for mobilization and activities of daily living. Her hospital course was unremarkable and she was discharged in a stable condition, requiring assistance for ambulation.

Upon discharge, her diagnosis was spondylolisthesis with severe stenosis at L3-L4 and L4-L5. She was discharged to an extended care facility. Her discharge instructions included activity restrictions, wound care instructions, medication management, and follow-up appointments.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted for an incisional hernia. She had previously undergone a bowel resection due to diverticulitis and was now presenting with increased abdominal pain in the middle of her incision, accompanied by a small bulge. She also reported experiencing nausea and vomiting. A CT scan at an outside hospital revealed a small loop of bowel within the incisional hernia. Her pain and nausea/vomiting symptoms have since resolved, but she still experiences occasional discomfort at the site. 

The patient's past medical history includes fibroadenoma of the right breast, diverticulitis with a subsequent left colectomy, colonic polyps (with a negative colonoscopy), angioedema potentially due to Flagyl or ACE inhibitors, hypertension, drug allergies, left hip pain, and a latex allergy. Her family history includes colon cancer in her father.

During her hospital stay, the patient underwent a laparoscopic incisional hernia repair with mesh and lysis of adhesions. Post-procedure, she was kept NPO with IV fluids overnight. Her diet was advanced in the morning and she was able to tolerate a regular diet. Her pain was well controlled with oral pain medications and she was ambulating. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking Hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ) 25mg daily and Spironolactone 100mg daily. No abnormal lab or test results were reported. The patient was discharged in good condition with instructions to return to the ER if she experienced vomiting, shaking chills, fever greater than 101.5 (F) degrees or 38 (C) degrees, increased redness, swelling or discharge from her incisions, chest pain, shortness of breath, or any other troubling symptoms. She was also advised to resume all regular home medications and take any new medications as ordered.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted for an elective ileostomy takedown. She has a history of chronic ulcerative colitis, which has not responded to medical therapy, and endometriosis. She was presented with various surgical options, including panproctocolectomy with permanent ileostomy to ileoanalp pouch, but a single stage operation was deemed unwise due to her interest in childbearing. 

Upon admission, the patient was on oral contraceptive pills. During her hospital stay, she underwent the ileostomy takedown procedure. Post-operation, she remained NPO, and her pain was managed with a striker pain pump sq and a PCA pump. A foley catheter was initially removed but had to be replaced due to failure to void. Over the course of her stay, her diet was gradually advanced from sips to a regular diet, and her pain was managed with oral medication. 

Her physical examination upon discharge showed no acute distress, with stable vital signs, regular heart rate and rhythm, clear lungs, and a soft, non-tender, non-distended abdomen. Her wound was clean, dry, and intact. 

Lab results showed abnormal RBC count at 4.16*, Hemoglobin at 11.3*, Hematocrit at 34.4*, and Urea Nitrogen at 3*. Other lab results, including WBC, Glucose, Creatinine, Sodium, Potassium, Chloride, Bicarbonate, and Anion Gap, were within normal ranges. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of postoperative ileostomy takedown, ulcerative colitis, and prior total colectomy with ileal-anal pouch. Her condition upon discharge was good. She was instructed to contact her doctor or return to the ER for any significant changes in her symptoms or any new symptoms that concern her, including chest pain, vomiting, dehydration, blood in vomit or bowel movement, yellowing of skin or eyes, worsening pain, fever, or any serious change in symptoms. She was also given instructions for incision care. Her family history was non-contributory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of Ulcerative Colitis (UC) and a total proctocolectomy with J pouch, presented with abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. She had a history of three prior partial obstructions, one of which required hospitalization. She was also recently post In Vitro Fertilization (IVF) implantation. Upon admission, her abdominal pain was still present but less intense, and her abdominal distention had improved. She had passed a small amount of liquid stool since being in the emergency department (ED).

Her past surgical history includes a J pouch, tonsillectomy, and foot surgeries. She has no significant family history. She is allergic to Cipro. Her medications upon admission included Estradiol 2 mg PO TID, Aspirin EC 81 mg PO DAILY, and Crinone (proGESTerone micronized) 8% vaginal BID.

Physical examination revealed a mildly distended and tender abdomen, particularly in the suprapubic area. There was no rebound or guarding. Her vitals were stable. An MRI revealed distended small bowel loops up to 4 cm leading to a transition point in the pelvis, consistent with obstruction.

Abnormal lab results included a decrease in RBC (3.79), Hgb (11.1), and Hct (32.9), an increase in WBC (13.2, 16.3), Neuts (75.9, 88.6), AbsNeut (10.01, 14.45), Glucose (104, 115, 111), UreaN (1, 2, 5), and AnGap (8, 9, 19), and a decrease in Lymphs (16.1, 7.2), Monos (3.2), Eos (1.0, 0.0), AbsLymp (2.12, 1.18), AbsMono (0.53), AbsEos (0.00), HCO3 (21), K (3.4), Calcium (8.2, 8.0, 8.2), Phos (2.1, 1.2), and Mg (1.5).

During her hospital stay, she was managed with bowel rest, nasogastric tube, intravenous fluids, and serial abdominal exams. Her pain was controlled with Tylenol and IV morphine. She was closely monitored for signs of infection, bleeding, and cardiovascular and pulmonary stability. Her nasogastric tube was removed once output was low, and she was slowly advanced to a regular diet. She was voiding without difficulty and her HCG levels were monitored. She had no post-surgical complications or social issues causing a delay in discharge. She was discharged with a diagnosis of small bowel obstruction, tolerating a regular diet, passing flatus, voiding, and ambulating independently.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female of undisclosed age who is currently seven weeks pregnant. She has a past medical history of ulcerative colitis and has undergone a total proctocolectomy with ileoanal J-pouch. She was previously complicated by small bowel obstruction. She was admitted due to several days of abdominal bloating, vomiting, and absence of bowel movements, which spontaneously resolved. She was able to tolerate diet without issue and was discharged home.

Upon admission, her labs showed an elevated WBC count of 18.4, RBC of 5.32, Neuts of 85.6, Lymphs of 8.1, Monos of 5.0, Eos of 0.4, AbsNeut of 15.77, AbsMono of 0.92, and Glucose of 101. Her urine culture showed no growth. An MRI of her abdomen and pelvis without contrast showed borderline dilated small bowel loops containing fluid and some fecal material tapering toward the J-pouch without a transition point to suggest obstruction. Findings could reflect an ileus. A short segment of narrowing near the prior obstruction could reflect peristalsis or a small stricture. Trace abdominopelvic ascites were also noted. An intrauterine pregnancy was better assessed on the same day pelvic ultrasound, which showed a single live intrauterine pregnancy with size equal to date and a small subchronic hemorrhage.

During her hospital stay, she presented with several days of abdominal pain, nausea/vomiting, similar to prior episodes of small bowel obstruction. However, her symptoms completely resolved in the ED and immediately following admission, she was able to tolerate the advancement of diet over 24 hours without issue. She was passing flatus and having bowel movements. Her WBC count improved throughout her admission, with a discharge WBC of 12.4. She had recent outpatient treatment for a UTI with Augmentin. Her urine culture subsequently returned with no growth. She continued prenatal vitamins, prednisone 5mg daily, endometrin 1000mg BID, estradiol patch 0.2mg every other day, and IM progesterone 50mg daily.

She also has chronic GERD and allergies, for which she takes famotidine 20mg BID and loratadine 10mg daily:PRN respectively. Her medications upon admission included loratadine 10 mg PO DAILY:PRN allergies, PredniSONE 5 mg PO DAILY, proGESTerone 50 mg/mL injection 1ml daily, Famotidine 20 mg PO BID, Prenatal Vitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY, Endometrin (proGESTerone micronized) 100 mg vaginal BID, and estradioL 0.1 mg/24 hr transdermal Two patches every other day.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to right leg pain following a fall in a snow bank while cleaning her car. She reported being unable to move her leg due to the pain. She has a history of right total knee arthroplasty and was found to have a right distal femur fracture above the right total knee after the fall. She was placed in a knee immobilizer. She also reported that she was scheduled for left hip surgery the following day. She denied any numbness, tingling, or pain elsewhere. 

Her past medical history includes right knee arthroplasty, Type II Diabetes Mellitus managed with insulin, gastric bypass with post abdominoplasty, left hip surgery, anemia, arthritis, history of amnesia, transient ischemic attack managed with Aspirin 325mg, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, depression, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and a heart murmur. She denies smoking or alcohol use.

Upon physical examination, her right lower extremity was dressed and in a brace. She had +2 pulses distally.
