She underwent a right distal femur open reduction internal fixation, which she tolerated well. Post-operation, she was given IV fluids and IV pain medications, which were later transitioned to a regular diet and oral medications. Her home medications were continued throughout her hospital stay. 

Her medications upon admission included Aspirin 325 mg, Metformin 1000 mg, Lisinopril 2.5 mg, and Bupropion XL 300 mg. 

She was discharged with a diagnosis of right periprosthetic distal femur fracture. At the time of discharge, her pain was well controlled with oral medications, her incisions were clean, dry, and intact, and she was voiding and moving bowels spontaneously. She was advised to bear weight on the right lower extremity and was discharged on Lovenox for deep vein thrombosis prophylaxis. She was also given written instructions concerning precautionary instructions and the appropriate follow-up care.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female with a history of endometriosis, for which she underwent a total abdominal hysterectomy and left oophorectomy. She also has a history of colon polyps, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), fibroids, depression, chronic bright red blood per rectum (BRBPR) with diverticulosis and polyps on colonoscopy, and a history of an eating disorder. She carries the HbS trait. She presented with lower abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting (N/V) and diarrhea (D). The abdominal pain was similar to her typical pain with endometriosis but was more severe, requiring her to take more naproxen. She also reported having bloody stools since her teen years and is undergoing a gastrointestinal evaluation. She had a left chest ache that self-resolved and has not recurred. 

In the ER, her vitals were stable and her EKG and chest X-ray were unremarkable. However, a CT scan of her abdomen and pelvis showed an increase in the size of her right adnexal and right gluteus lipoma. Her urine analysis was consistent with contamination. Her complete blood count (CBC) was notable for a white blood cell count of 1.3 and an absolute neutrophil count (ANC) of 700 with no prior history of leukopenia or neutropenia. She was given cefepime and morphine for pain, which improved her condition. She also received 2 L of normal saline intravenous fluids and was admitted to Medicine. 

Her lab results on admission showed an abnormal white blood cell count of 1.3 and her vitamin B12 level was 1093. On discharge, her lab results showed an abnormal white blood cell count of 2.7, hemoglobin of 11.9, hematocrit of 35.3, neutrophils of 37, lymphocytes of 43, eosinophils of 5, and atypical cells of 9. Her stool culture results were negative for salmonella, shigella, campylobacter, yersinia, E.coli 0157:H7, and clostridium difficile toxin A & B. Her viral culture showed no virus isolated so far. 

Imaging showed a 5x4 cm right adnexal cyst and a left gluteal lipomatous lesion with internal septations that had increased in size, measuring 5.8 x 2.8 cm. Her upper EGD showed normal mucosa in the stomach with a biopsy showing chronic and mild active gastritis and organisms consistent with H. pylori. Her colonoscopy showed semi-solid and liquid stool scattered in the whole colon, diverticulosis of the sigmoid colon, and otherwise normal colonoscopy to the cecum. 

During her hospital stay, her abdominal pain was controlled with oxycodone and protonix was started. Her nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea resolved without intervention. She was found to be leukopenic with moderate neutropenia, most likely due to a viral infection. Her anemia was stable and her iron studies were normal. She was instructed to follow up for a CBC check to ensure improvement in WBC as she cleared the viral infection. She was also instructed to follow up with her primary care physician and gynecologist for a repeat pelvic ultrasound in 6 weeks for her adnexal cyst and for further imaging and possible biopsy of her lipoma. Her depression was managed with continued citalopram and trazodone. 

Her medications upon admission included citalopram 10mg, trazodone 25mg at bedtime, Vicodin as needed, and Naproxen twice daily as needed. She has allergies to penicillin, compazine, tigan, azithromycin, and percocet. 

Her family history includes no inflammatory bowel disease, an uncle who died of skin cancer, another uncle with end-stage renal disease, and a family history of diabetes mellitus.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female under the orthopaedics service, was admitted due to a mass in her left hip area, which was incidentally found during a prior workup and has been enlarging over several years. The mass was presumed to be a lipoma. She underwent a major surgical procedure for the excision of the mass. The final pathology results are still pending.

Her past medical history includes fibroids and endometriosis, for which she underwent myomectomy, hysterectomy, and left oophorectomy. She also has a history of depression, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), chronic bright red blood per rectum (BRBPR) with diverticulosis and polyps found on colonoscopy, an eating disorder, and hemoglobin S (HbS) trait.

The patient has allergies to penicillins, Compazine, Tigan, Azithromycin, and Percocet. Upon admission, she was on Pantoprazole, Trazadone, and Citalopram.

Her family history is significant for an uncle who died of skin cancer, another uncle with end-stage renal disease (ESRD), and a family history of diabetes mellitus (DM).

During her hospital stay, she tolerated the surgical procedure well and her post-operative pain was managed with oral analgesics. She was eager to be discharged home on post-operative day 1. Her physical examination showed a dressing on her left lateral hip, proximal to the left greater trochanter. She was afebrile with stable vital signs and was distally neurovascularly intact. 

Upon discharge, her diagnosis was a presumed lipoma, pending pathology. She was advised to bear weight as tolerated and use walking aids as needed. Her discharge instructions included leaving the dressing on until a specified date, then leaving the wound open to air, leaving steri-strips in place, and not soaking in a tub while showering. She was instructed to call for a follow-up appointment with her doctor.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of endometriosis and chronic pelvic pain, presented to the emergency department with severe pelvic pain that had been worsening over the past three days. The pain was not relieved with Vicodin, a medication she uses at home for pain management. She reported mild nausea, decreased appetite due to pain, painful bladder symptoms when her bladder is full, incontinence, and chronic constipation. She denied having fever, chills, hematuria, dysuria, or abnormal vaginal discharge. 

In the past, she had been treated with Progestin and oral contraceptive pills (OCPs) for her endometriosis, but these treatments led to significant depression. She has never been treated with Lupron. She has been evaluated by both urology and GI in the past. Her past medical history includes depression, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), an eating disorder, and a history of bright red blood per rectum (BRBPR) with diverticulosis and polyps on colonoscopy. She has also undergone a supracervical hysterectomy (SCH) and left salpingo-oophorectomy (LSO) for endometriosis. 

Upon admission, she was on Vicodin, Citalopram 10mg, and Trazodone 25mg qhs. Her physical examination revealed diffuse lower abdominal tenderness, moderate to palpation, with no rebound or guarding, and no costovertebral angle tenderness (CVAT). Her pelvic exam was deferred at her request. Her lab results were all within normal limits. A pelvic ultrasound showed a normal-appearing right ovary with arterial and venous waveforms and normal-appearing follicular activity.

During her hospital stay, she was admitted to the GYN service for IV pain medication and serial exams. Her severe pelvic pain was thought to be an exacerbation of her chronic pain likely due to extensive endometriosis. She was initially on IV toradol/dilaudid, then transitioned to oral pain medications on hospital day 2. She was also started on gabapentin and tizanidine for her chronic pain. 

Her family history was noncontributory. She was discharged on hospital day 3 in stable condition, with her pain moderately controlled on oral medications. She had close follow-up arranged prior to discharge and verbally agreed to a pain contract. Her discharge diagnoses were endometriosis and acute on chronic pelvic pain.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of endometriosis and chronic pelvic pain, presented to the ED with an exacerbation of her usual pain. She has a past medical history of depression, IBS, an eating disorder, and a history of bright red blood per rectum with diverticulosis and polyps found on colonoscopy. She has had previous surgical history of SCH and LSO, abdominal MMY, and LTCS. She has no significant family history. 

The patient's chief complaint was severe pelvic pain. She denied any new symptoms or fevers and chills. She has been on a pain control regimen, including Lupron, as per the Chronic Pain Service. She had a similar admission for pain control in the past. During her current visit, she received oral Dilaudid in the ED, which improved her condition. However, she had an episode of emesis and returned to the ED. After receiving Zofran and Tylenol, she felt better but requested admission for observation due to anxiety about managing her symptoms at home. 

On physical examination, her vitals were normal and her abdomen showed mild tenderness in the lower quadrants. No imaging was performed as her vital signs and exam were not worrisome for an acute process. Her labs showed a WBC count of 3.3*, RBC count of 4.14*, HCT of 35.4*, MCHC of 35.1*, NEUTS of 36.9*, and LYMPHS of 55.0*. Urine UCG and UA were negative. 

During her hospital stay, her pain decreased with medication and there was no evidence of infection or acute abdominal process. She received her scheduled dose of Lupron while in the hospital. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including Buspirone, Citalopram, Famotidine, Gabapentin, Hydrocodone-Acetaminophen, Hydromorphone, Ibuprofen, Leuprolide, Omeprazole, Tizanidine, and Trazodone. 

She was discharged with a diagnosis of chronic pelvic pain and endometriosis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. She was instructed to call or return to the hospital if she experienced increased pain, nausea and vomiting, fevers, chills, or had any other concerns.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female under the OBSTETRICS/GYNECOLOGY service, was admitted due to pelvic pain. She has a history of interstitial cystitis, endometriosis, and chronic pelvic pain. The patient described the pain as an acute exacerbation of her chronic pain, which is midline and suprapubic. She reported that her pain was not responsive to Vicodin, relaxation exercises, and warm compresses, and persisted despite her usual management of Elmiron, gabapentin, and tizanidine. She recently stopped taking Lupron due to decreased libido and believes this may be causing her pain. She also reported increased intercourse, which she believes triggers her pain. 

Her past medical history includes depression, irritable bowel syndrome, a history of eating disorder, a history of bright red blood per rectum with diverticulosis and polyps on colonoscopy, leukopenia, and interstitial cystitis. She has undergone a supracervical hysterectomy, left salpingo oopherectomy, abdominal myomectomy, and C-section. She has been on Lupron since an unspecified date and has no history of sexually transmitted infection. She had an ASCUS pap in the past and underwent a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and left salpingo-oophorectomy with transcervical morcellation.

Upon admission, her physical exam showed no acute distress, a soft abdomen with diffuse tenderness to palpation, no rebound/guarding, no masses, and non-tender extremities. Her discharge physical exam was similar, with mild tenderness to palpation in lower quadrants and back.

Her lab results showed abnormal values for WBC (2.8* and 3.6*), RBC (3.89* and 4.08*), Hgb (11.4*), Hct (33.9*), and Cl (110*). Urine culture results were negative for a urinary tract infection.

During her hospital stay, she was continued on her home pain regimen including Vicodin, gabapentin, tizanidine, and elmiron. She was also started on pyridium and given IV Toradol and Dilaudid for breakthrough pain. She was advised to start a continuous OCP to manage pain secondary to endometriosis and was started on Yaz. She was discharged home in stable condition with adequate pain control on oral medication.

Her medications upon admission included Citalopram, Gabapentin, Hydrocodone-Acetaminophen, Leuprolide, Omeprazole, Pentosan Polysulfate Sodium, Trazodone, and Calcium Carbonate-Vitamin D3. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of acute pain, endometriosis, and interstitial cystitis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a female with a history of interstitial cystitis (IC), endometriosis, and irritable bowel syndrome (IBS). She had undergone an interstim stage II procedure. She presented to the emergency department with pelvic pain due to an IC flare. The patient reported that her pain initially improved after the interstim was placed, but she experienced severe pain from bladder spasms and a strong urge to void the day before her presentation. Her usual medication regimen, including Tylenol #3, did not significantly relieve her pain. She also adjusted her interstim settings without any effect. She denied any worsening discomfort at the surgical site and reported only soreness. She also reported not taking her oral contraceptive pills for her endometriosis due to nausea.

Upon admission, the patient appeared uncomfortable and was intermittently writhing in bed during what she described as spasms. Her abdomen was soft and tender suprapubically, with no rebound or guarding. Her urine analysis showed positive nitrites and bacteria. However, a repeat urine analysis was negative for these. 

During her hospital stay, her pain was controlled with dilaudid, toradol, and vicodin. She has previously responded well to Lupron, but it was discontinued due to decreased libido. She has also tried several oral contraceptive pills, which caused depressive symptoms and nausea/vomiting. After a discussion, she decided to return to Lupron for control of her presumed endometriosis pain. She was also prescribed estrogen add-back therapy in the form of a patch. She was treated with a 3-day course of macrobid due to the initial urine analysis results. 

Her medications upon admission included gabapentin 300 TID, celexa, elmiron, tylenol #3 as needed, and trazodone. She was initially on vicodin for pain control but was switched to oral dilaudid due to nausea/emesis. By hospital day 3, her condition had improved and she was discharged in good condition. 

The patient's family history was non-contributory. Her discharge diagnoses were endometriosis and interstitial cystitis.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of endometriosis and interstitial cystitis, was admitted due to worsening pelvic and vaginal pain over the last three days. She has been managing her chronic pain with Tylenol with codeine, gabapentin, elmeron, and tizanidine. She also takes Lupron for her endometriosis, with her last dose given during a recent hospitalization. She reported feeling nauseous and having occasional vomiting, as well as dysuria. She denied any fever, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, diarrhea, or constipation. It is important to note that she has had multiple admissions for this pain in the past.

Her past medical history includes depression, irritable bowel syndrome, gastritis, leukopenia, and endometriosis. She has a gynecological history of endometriosis, for which she has been on Lupron, and an ASCUS pap, which was never followed up for colposcopy. She underwent a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and left salpingo-oophorectomy with transcervical morcellation. She has no history of sexually transmitted infections and is sexually active with her husband. Her obstetric history includes one spontaneous vaginal delivery, one cesarean section, three therapeutic abortions, and one spontaneous abortion. Her surgical history includes a cesarean section, myomectomy, laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and LSO, and InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant.

Upon physical examination, she was found to be in no acute distress, with a regular heart rate and rhythm, clear lungs, and a soft abdomen that was diffusely tender on palpation with voluntary guarding. Her extremities were non-tender, and her pelvic exam revealed normal appearing external genitalia, vaginal mucosa, and cervix without lesions or discharge.

Her urine tests showed a dark amber, hazy appearance with a positive nitrite and a urobilinogen level of 2*, which is abnormal. 

During her hospital stay, she was managed for pain in the setting of endometriosis and interstitial cystitis. She was initially given IV dilaudid overnight and pyridium. Chronic pain was consulted for further recommendations for outpatient pain regimen. She was started on notryptline, lidocaine patches, and tramadol, and was subsequently transitioned to PO dilaudid. This pain regimen adequately controlled her pain for 24 hours. 

Upon admission, she was on gabapentin, tylenol#3, tizanidine, elmiron, colace, and omeprazole. Her family history is non-contributory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of endometriosis and interstitial cystitis, was admitted due to worsening chronic pelvic pain. She described the pain as radiating from her lower abdominal quadrants to her lower back and groin, and it was exacerbated by activity. The pain was similar to her usual interstitial cystitis pain exacerbations. She also reported chronic bowel and bladder incontinence. She had been previously hospitalized for pelvic pain and was treated with Lupron (22.5 mg), which she did not find effective. Her current medications included elmiron, tylenol with codeine, gabapentin, and tramadol for pain management.

Her past medical history included depression, irritable bowel syndrome, gastritis, leukopenia, and a gynecological history of endometriosis, no sexually transmitted infections, an ASCUS pap that was never followed up for colposcopy, and a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and left salpingo-oophorectomy with transcervical morcellation. She had also undergone a Cesarean section, myomectomy, and InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant. She is sexually active with her husband.

Upon admission, her vital signs were stable. Her abdomen was soft, non-distended, and tender to palpation in the right and left lower quadrants, with voluntary guarding but no rebound tenderness or organomegaly. She also had tenderness in her lower back. Her urine test results were normal.

During her hospital stay, her pain was managed with toradol, gabapentin, tizanidine, nortriptyline, and a lidocaine patch. She was transitioned from IV Dilaudid to PO Dilaudid taper. Her pain improved on this new medication regimen, although she continued to experience early morning pain.

Her medications upon admission were gabapentin 300 TID, tylenol#3 as needed, elmiron 100 TID, colace, omeprazole 20', tramadol 50 TID as needed, nortriptyline 10 QHS, celexa 40', estradiol patch weekly, and tizanidine ?4mg QHS. During her stay, several changes were made to her medication regimen. Celexa was discontinued as nortriptyline was increased. Gabapentin was increased to 600 TID. Tramadol was held inpatient and resumed after 2 days of dilaudid taper. A Lidoderm patch was started inpatient, to be used around the clock. Tizanidine was added in the morning and continued at the current pm dose. Tylenol#3 was continued after 2 days of dilaudid taper completed, along with elmiron 100 3x/day, colace, and omeprazole 20'. She also received IV dilaudid and toradol inpatient and was discharged with 2 days of dilaudid taper and 2 days of intranasal toradol.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of endometriosis and interstitial cystitis. She was alert, interactive, and ambulatory at the time of discharge. She was instructed to follow up for a TENS trial and is considering a diagnostic laparoscopy to assess for other etiologies and progression of her endometriosis. Her family history was non-contributory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of endometriosis and interstitial cystitis, was admitted due to worsening chronic pelvic pain. She had been previously hospitalized for a 7-day inpatient stay for pain control, during which her medications were adjusted and she was seen by the chronic pain service. However, she reported little resolution of symptoms. She described severe spastic bladder pain and had been taking oral dilaudid, which she reported made her feel depressed. 

Her past medical history includes endometriosis, for which she has been on Lupron, and a history of ASCUS pap, which was never followed up for colposcopy. She also has a history of depression, irritable bowel syndrome, gastritis, interstitial cystitis, endometriosis, and leukopenia. Her gynecological history includes a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and left salpingo-oophorectomy with transcervical morcellation. She has also had a cesarean section, myomectomy, and InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant. Her obstetric history includes one spontaneous vaginal delivery, one cesarean section, three therapeutic abortions, and one spontaneous abortion.

Upon physical examination, she was afebrile with vital signs stable. Her abdomen was soft, non-distended, with tenderness to palpation in the right and left lower quadrants, and voluntary guarding, but no rebound tenderness or organomegaly. She also had tenderness to palpation in the lower back. Her pelvic exam was deferred. 

Her lab results showed a white blood cell count of 3.9*, red blood cell count of 4.16*, hematocrit of 34.4*, and mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration of 35.1*#. 

During her hospital stay, she continued to experience pelvic and lower back pain, most severe in the early morning and interfering with sleep. She was started on anti-spasmodic agents levsin and flexeril, which provided some improvement in pain. A foley catheter was placed and provided some relief. She was seen by psychiatry due to her report of suicidal ideation with dilaudid, who felt she was not depressed. 

Upon admission, she was on gabapentin, tylenol#3, elmiron, colace, omeprazole, tramadol, nortriptyline, estradiol patch, and tizanidine. 

Her discharge diagnosis included interstitial cystitis, endometriosis, and chronic pelvic pain. She was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted to the OB/GYN service due to an exacerbation of her chronic interstitial cystitis, which she described as 'bladder spasms'. She has a history of severe endometriosis and interstitial cystitis. She denied experiencing fever, chills, or any other symptoms inconsistent with an interstitial cystitis exacerbation. 

Her past medical history includes endometriosis, for which she has been on Lupron, depression, irritable bowel syndrome, gastritis, interstitial cystitis, and leukopenia. She has no history of sexually transmitted infections. She had an ASCUS pap in the past but never followed up for colposcopy. She underwent a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and left salpingo-oophorectomy with transcervical morcellation. She also had a Cesarean section, myomectomy, and InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant. Her obstetric history includes G6 P2, with one spontaneous vaginal delivery, one Cesarean section, three therapeutic abortions, and one spontaneous abortion.

Upon admission, her vital signs were stable and her physical examination was unremarkable, except for tenderness to touch in the lower quadrants. She declined a pelvic exam, stating that it exacerbates her pain. Her blood work showed a white blood cell count of 3.5* and neutrophils at 38.3*, both of which are below the normal range. Her lymphocytes were at 52.3*, which is above the normal range.

Her home pain regimen includes tramadol, gabapentin 600 TID, lidocaine patches, elmiron TID, and tizanidine. During her hospital stay, she was given two doses of IV morphine 1mg and IV toradol, percocet every three hours, pyridium (elmiron was held), continued on lidocaine patches, and flexeril. On the third day of her hospital stay, she reported a significant improvement in her pain and was discharged home with instructions to follow up.

Her medications upon admission included Lupron, an estradiol patch (which she had not used for several weeks due to cost), gabapentin 600, tizanidine 4mg at bedtime, tramadol 50 several times per day as needed, lidocaine patches, elmiron 100, celexa 40, and omeprazole 20. Her family history was non-contributory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of endometriosis, interstitial cystitis, depression, irritable bowel syndrome, gastritis, and leukopenia, was admitted due to lower abdominal and bladder pain. She has a gynecological history of endometriosis, for which she has been on Lupron therapy, and has undergone a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and left salpingo-oophorectomy with transcervical morcellation. She also has an InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant for her interstitial cystitis. She has no significant family history.

The patient's chief complaint was worsening bladder spasms and lower back pain, which she described as similar to her usual interstitial cystitis flare-ups. She also reported rectal incontinence, which began two months ago, stopped, and then recurred a few times in the past few weeks. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Gabapentin, Acetaminophen with Codeine, Citalopram, Estradiol Transdermal Patch, Leuprolide Acetate, Lidocaine Patch, Pantoprazole, Elmiron, Tizanidine, and Tramadol. 

During her hospital stay, her pain was managed with her home medications, with an increase in her Gabapentin, Tizanidine, and Tramadol dosages. She was also given Hyoscyamine, Pyridium, and a prescription for a Valium/Baclofen/Lidocaine suppository. She initially required IV Morphine and Toradol as well. However, the Hyoscyamine was stopped prior to discharge due to concerns of worsening urinary retention. 

The patient's physical examination upon discharge revealed mild diffuse lower abdominal pain but was otherwise normal. She was discharged with a diagnosis of an interstitial cystitis flare-up. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female under the OBSTETRICS/GYNECOLOGY service, was admitted due to urinary retention and abdominal pain. She has a history of interstitial cystitis, endometriosis, and irritable bowel syndrome (IBS). On the day of admission, she experienced acutely worsened bladder pain and was unable to urinate since morning despite the urge to do so. The pain was described as a dull ache with intermittent sharp bouts, similar to her usual interstitial cystitis pain. She had her last bowel movement the previous day and reported no nausea, vomiting, fever, chills, or back pain.

Her past medical history includes depression, gastritis, leukopenia, and a history of sexually transmitted infection. She has been on Lupron for endometriosis. She had an ASCUS pap that was never followed up for colposcopy. She underwent a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and left salpingo-oophorectomy with transcervical morcellation. She also had a Cesarean section, myomectomy, and InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant. Her obstetric history includes G6 P2, SVD x1, C-section x1, TAB x3, SAB x1.

Upon admission, her physical examination showed a temperature of 98.2, BP 118/71, HR 66, RR 16. Her abdomen was soft, with no rebound or guarding, but very tender supra-pubically. No edema or tenderness was noted in her extremities.

During her hospital stay, a foley catheter was placed due to urinary retention, which resulted in approximately 400cc of urine output. She was managed with IV morphine for breakthrough pain and continued her home medications. Her pain improved by the second day of her hospital stay, and she was discharged with the foley catheter in place.

Her medications upon admission included Acetaminophen w/Codeine, Citalopram, Elmiron, Gabapentin, Lidocaine 5% Patch, Tizanidine, and TraMADOL. She has allergies to Penicillins, Compazine, Tigan, Azithromycin, and Amoxicillin. Her family history was reported as non-contributory.

Upon discharge, her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. Her discharge diagnosis included interstitial cystitis and endometriosis. She was instructed to follow up with her doctor the next day to discuss catheter removal.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of interstitial cystitis, endometriosis, irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), depression, gastritis, chronic pelvic pain, leukopenia, history of bulimia, H/pylori gastritis, osteopenia, history of colonic polyps, and a history of several surgical procedures including cesarean section, myomectomy, laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy and LSO, and InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant, was admitted due to a rash and fevers. She had been receiving monthly leuprolide injections for her endometriosis. The patient reported severe bilateral leg cramps, and the development of itchy and painful bumps under her chin that spread to her face, arms, legs, chest, and back. She also reported chills and night sweats. She has a history of cold sores and has taken valacyclovir in the past. She denied any new medications, detergents, or foods, and no one else at home was sick. She has never had chicken pox. 

Upon admission, her vitals were: 103.1 113 114/71 16 99%. She had vesicular lesions with crusting on her face, back, and arms. Labs showed leukopenia of 2.7 with 49% neutrophils. Her baseline leukopenia is around 3.5. Other labs were unremarkable with Hb12 and Cr 0.6. 

Admission labs showed abnormal WBC-2.7*, RBC-4.19*, Hct-34.8*, MCHC-35.3*, Neuts-49*, Baso-3*, and Cl-109*. ALT-162* and AST-159* were also elevated. Interval labs showed ALT-119* and AST-47*. Discharge labs showed abnormal RBC-4.14*, Hct-35.1*, Neuts-35.5*, Lymphs-54.1*, and ALT-81*. 

Imaging results from a CXR and MRI of the head were normal. Microbiology results showed no growth in blood cultures and VZV culture. VZV IgG was negative, VZV DFA was positive, and HSV DFA was negative. CSF Gram Stain was negative, fluid culture showed no growth, and viral culture is pending. CSF VZV PCR is also pending. 

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with primary Varicella Zoster infection, neutropenia, headache, and transaminitis. Her varicella was treated with oral acyclovir. She developed severe headaches while receiving acyclovir, which were controlled with toradol, oxycodone, and fioricet. Her LP and MRI brain were obtained to evaluate for cause of headache and were negative. Her headaches improved at the time of discharge. Her neutropenia was thought to be secondary to her current viral infection and she was no longer neutropenic at the time of discharge. Her transaminitis was thought to be secondary to viral infection and was trending down at time of discharge. 

Her medications upon admission included Gabapentin, Tizanidine, Estradiol Transdermal Patch, Acetaminophen w/Codeine, Escitalopram Oxalate, pentosan polysulfate sodium, and Leuprolide Acetate. 

The patient's mother has a history of hypertension and her uncle has a skin condition. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of Primary Disseminated Varicella Zooster. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent upon discharge.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of chronic pelvic pain, endometriosis, and painful bladder syndrome, was admitted due to an acute flare of her painful bladder syndrome. She has previously undergone a cesarean section, myomectomy, laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy, and LSO, and has an InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant. Her other past medical conditions include depression, irritable bowel syndrome, gastritis, leukopenia, and osteopenia. She has been on a regimen of Gabapentin, Escitalopram Oxalate, Elmiron, Tizanidine, Acetaminophen with Codeine, Emoquette, and Lidocaine patch.

Upon admission, the patient appeared uncomfortable and was in pain. Her abdomen was soft and tender to touch diffusely, but there was no rebound, guarding, or masses. She denied vaginal discharge but noted two days of vaginal spotting. She also noted a slight urinary retention but was able to void. Her urine test results were normal, but her blood test showed abnormal levels of neutrophils (44.2), lymphocytes (48.7), and RBC (4.02), and HCT (34.2), and Chloride (110).

During her hospital stay, she was managed for her painful bladder syndrome, which was likely triggered by increased caffeine intake. She was given IV morphine for breakthrough pain, in addition to her home regimen for pain control. Her pain improved by the second day of her hospital stay, and she was discharged in good condition. She was advised to follow up with her primary GYN to discuss optimization of treatment for her endometriosis and painful bladder syndrome.

The patient's family history includes her mother with hypertension and an uncle with a skin condition. She has allergies to Penicillins, Compazine, Tigan, Azithromycin, and Amoxicillin.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of endometriosis and interstitial cystitis, was admitted due to two days of pelvic pain. She has a history of chronic pelvic pain from these conditions, which she usually manages with a combination of medication, Interstim, and pelvic floor therapy. Her medications upon admission included Lupron, gabapentin, Elmiron, tizanidine, and Tylenol 3. She also has a history of depression, irritable bowel syndrome, gastritis, leukopenia, and osteopenia. Her surgical history includes a cesarean section, myomectomy, laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy, and LSO, and InterStim stage II sacral nerve implant. 

The patient described her pain as constant, throbbing, and more intense on the right side of her pelvis. The pain radiated to her sacrum and down her thighs. She also reported a sore throat, cough, and fever for the last two days, but no nausea or vomiting. She had some dark brown vaginal spotting the day before, which she has experienced in the past since her hysterectomy. She reported normal bowel movements and no recent constipation or diarrhea. She had a colonoscopy recently which revealed internal hemorrhoids but no other abnormalities. 

The patient's mother was recently diagnosed with bladder cancer and her ex-husband's mother recently passed away. She reported feeling frustrated at being unwell as she wanted to support her mother more. 

Upon physical examination, the patient appeared fatigued and in pain. Her abdomen was soft with mild tenderness in both lower quadrants. Her pelvic exam revealed normal female external genitalia, diffuse tenderness on bimanual exam including CMT, and no palpable adnexal masses or fullness. 

Lab results showed an elevated neutrophil count. All other results, including glucose, urea nitrogen, creatinine, sodium, potassium, chloride, total CO2, anion gap, calcium, phosphate, magnesium, WBC, RBC, HGB, HCT, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils, and platelet count were within normal ranges. Urine tests were also normal. 

During her hospital stay, the patient's pelvic pain was managed with her home regimen of pain medications and toradol. She was able to void spontaneously and had a viral upper respiratory infection which was treated with hydration and supportive care. By the second day of her hospital stay, she was tolerating a regular diet, ambulating independently, and her pain was controlled with oral medications. She was discharged in stable condition.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted to the Obstetrics/Gynecology service due to pelvic pain. She has a history of chronic severe pelvic pain, urinary incontinence, interstitial cystitis, endometriosis, depression, bulimia, vitamin D deficiency, neck pain, otalgia, gastroesophageal reflux, fecal incontinence, rectal bleeding, colon polyps, pelvic floor dyssynergy, atypical nevi, constipation, abnormal pap smear, and H. pylori gastritis. She has had several surgical procedures in the past, including an InterStim Stage II sacral nerve implant, InterStim Stage I with fluoroscopy C-arm, left hip tumor removal, supracervical hysterectomy, cesarean section, myomectomy, dilation and curettage, and oral surgery. 

Upon admission, the patient was visibly uncomfortable but had no respiratory distress. Her abdomen was soft with no tenderness or guarding, and her calves were non-tender. Her discharge physical exam showed mild suprapubic tenderness on palpation, but no rebound or guarding. 

The patient's urine test showed a few bacteria and a urobilinogen level of 2*, which is abnormal. During her hospital stay, she was given IV toradol, IV dilaudid, PO Tylenol, in addition to her home regimen of gabapentin, Effexor, and pyridium for pain control. The chronic pain service recommended increasing the nightly dose of gabapentin to 1200mg. She was maintained on a regular diet throughout admission and was voiding independently without issue. On the third day of her hospital stay, she noted some increased vaginal burning. A repeat urinalysis and urine culture were sent. The urinalysis showed leukocytes and nitrites, and she was treated presumptively for a urinary tract infection (UTI). 

The patient's medications upon admission included codeine sulfate, fluticasone, gabapentin, hydrocortisone acetate, lidocaine HCL, norethindrone acetate, omeprazole, phenazopyridine, sumatriptan succinate, tizanidine, tramadol, valacyclovir, venlafaxine, acetaminophen, calcium carbonate-vitamin D3, cholecalciferol (vitamin D3), and lidocaine HCL. 

The patient was discharged home on the third day of her hospital stay with improvement in her pain. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and able to walk independently.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a female of undisclosed age, was admitted to the inpatient psychiatric unit due to expressing thoughts of overdosing on pain medication to her husband. This was in the context of multiple social stressors, including chronic pain following a hysterectomy a year ago, loss of employment, the death of her father, and marital issues. She has a history of childhood trauma, depression, bulimia, and a previous suicide attempt. She reported symptoms of depressed mood, guilt, low self-worth, poor sleep, and recurrence of purging behavior for several weeks. She denied symptoms of mania, psychosis, homicidal ideation, alcohol, or substance abuse. 

Her past medical history includes a hysterectomy and left oophorectomy. Her family history is significant for her father's alcohol and opiate dependence, who died from a fentanyl overdose. 

Upon physical examination, she was found to be cooperative and pleasant, with a mood described as 'relieved and hopeful'. Her thought process was linear, with no suicidal ideation, paranoia, or delusions. 

Lab results showed abnormal neutrophils at 48.2* and lymphocytes at 44.9*. All other lab results, including ALT, AST, ALK PHOS, TOT BILI, CALCIUM, PHOSPHATE, MAGNESIUM, TSH, GLUCOSE, UREA N, CREAT, SODIUM, POTASSIUM, CHLORIDE, TOTAL CO2, ANION GAP, WBC, RBC, HGB, HCT, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, MONOS, EOS, BASOS, and PLT COUNT were within normal ranges. Urine tests for various substances including benzodiazepine, barbiturates, opiates, cocaine, amphetamines, methadone, ASA, ethanol, acetaminophen, and tricyclics were all negative. 

During her hospital stay, she was started on Celexa 10mg Qam and Trazodone for sleep, which she tolerated well. She also met with a social worker and her family to identify stressors and alternative ways of managing them. She was on Lutera for hormone replacement but this was discontinued at her request as she felt it may have contributed to her depressed mood. 

Upon discharge, her mood was described as 'relieved and hopeful'. She was diagnosed with Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) and bulimia, with stressors at home with her husband. Her discharge instructions included keeping all follow-up appointments, contacting her therapist or psychiatrist if she noticed an increase in depressive symptoms, and seeking immediate help if she had thoughts of hurting herself. Her medication upon admission was Lutera.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the Orthopaedic service due to a crush injury to his right hand and forearm. His chief complaint was a crush fracture injury to his right distal radius and right hand. His past medical history includes hypertension. No family history was provided. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was comfortable, with a temperature of 98.1, heart rate of 83, blood pressure of 152/82, respiratory rate of 18, and oxygen saturation of 100%. He had 2+ radial pulses with intact capillary refill, and a large dorsal laceration with visible tendon ruptures. Neurological examination showed normal sensation.

The patient underwent several surgical procedures without complications. These included open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) of the forearm, exploration of right proximal thumb wound, exploration of right mid forearm dorsal wound, exploration of right dorsal hand wound with repair of several tendons, and complex wound repair measuring approximately 14 cm of right upper extremity/hand.

Lab results showed abnormal neutrophils at 80.3*, lymphocytes at 13.2*, and white blood cell count at 11.9*. All other lab results, including platelet count, red blood cell count, hemoglobin, hematocrit, calcium, phosphate, magnesium, glucose, urea nitrogen, creatinine, sodium, potassium, chloride, total CO2, anion gap, and lactate were within normal ranges.

The patient was not on any medications upon admission. His hospital course was uneventful and he was discharged home in stable condition. His discharge diagnosis was a right hand and forearm crush injury. At the time of discharge, his mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male of unspecified age, was admitted with lower extremity weakness and cauda equina syndrome, likely secondary to metastatic lung cancer that has spread to the L5 spine. He also presented with a perforated bowel. His medical history includes probable metastatic lung cancer, a history of low back pain, hepatitis C, and a history of alcohol use. His mother had lung cancer. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Duragesic, Senokot-S, Flexiril, Decadron, Reglan, Lactulose, Zofran, and Dilaudid. He reported a 20 lb weight loss, progressive low back pain, leg weakness, and numbness in his feet. He was wheelchair bound due to pain and denied loss of bowel or bladder function. Neurological evaluation showed normal pin sensation of lower extremities, but subjective numbness of the feet. No focal weakness of the extremity muscles was noted. 

Work-up revealed a destructive lesion in L5, likely representing metastatic disease, and severe foraminal narrowing at L5-S1 with cauda equina compression. A cavitary lesion in the right upper lobe of the lung, suspicious for nonsmall cell lung cancer, was also found. Biopsy of the L5 lesion was performed with results pending at the time of transfer. The patient was started on Reglan and external beam XRT. 

Upon arrival, the patient reported worsening pain in his lower back, weakness and numbness in his lower extremities, constipation, and abdominal distention. He also reported numbness in his buttocks and lack of full control over his bowel movements. He denied upper extremity weakness or numbness, cough, hoarseness, chest pain, and shortness of breath. 

Physical examination revealed a cachectic appearing male, awake, alert, with no acute distress. His abdomen was soft, distended, and tympanic to percussion, without tenderness to palpation, rebound, or guarding. Neurological examination showed no ability to plantar/dorsiflex bilaterally, and decreased sensation in large toes bilaterally. 

Lab results showed abnormal Amylase at 109 and T. bili at 4.6. Other pertinent results include a mild pathologic compression fracture of L5, a cavitary lesion in the right upper lung, and a large amount of free intraperitoneal air. There was no evidence of pulmonary embolism, but there was multifocal ground-glass opacity and consolidation superimposed upon diffuse centrilobular emphysema. 

The patient underwent an exploratory laparotomy, right colectomy, ileostomy placement, tracheostomy, and percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy. He was maintained on a ventilator throughout most of his hospitalization and was able to come off the vent and have good O2 sats on a trach mask by the day of discharge. He was covered broadly for his lung abscess and post-operative prophylaxis. He was discharged in fair condition to extended care.
label: controls

summary:
The patient is a male with a history of probable metastatic lung cancer, low back pain, and hepatitis C. He was recently discharged from a prolonged hospital stay during which he developed a perforated bowel secondary to metastatic lung cancer. He initially presented with worsening back pain and was diagnosed with cauda equina syndrome from metastatic lung cancer. During spinal fusion surgery, he became hypotensive and his abdomen was noted to be rigid. An upright chest X-ray demonstrated free air under the diaphragm and he underwent emergent laparotomy and right hemicolectomy and ileostomy. Attempts at weaning him from the ventilator were unsuccessful, and he underwent tracheostomy and G-tube placement. He was diagnosed with a lung abscess during his stay. His abdominal fluid grew out Pseudomonas and E. coli. Multiple sputum cultures grew out MSSA, and one grew out yeast and another grew out E. coli. He was discharged to rehab on vancomycin, pip-tazo, metronidazole, ciprofloxacin, and fluconazole. 

He was transferred back from rehab for fever. He was afebrile on transfer to the rehab facility but by the night of his admission, he spiked to 103.3. He was seen by the infectious disease consultant at which time ceftriaxone/flagyl and the prior antibiotics were stopped. His vent settings for the 3 days prior to transfer were CPAP/PS at 50%. He has a portex tracheostomy. In the ED, his initial vital signs were 99.9, 10, 100%. A chest X-ray was done as well as CT torso (with contrast). He received vancomycin/zosyn as well as dilaudid and toradol for pain. He received 4L normal saline. 

On arrival to the MICU, he denied shortness of breath, worsening secretions, chest pain, dysuria, or change in ostomy output. He has a small amount of pain in his abdomen in the right lower quadrant. His mother had lung cancer. His physical exam was notable for a thin, cachetic male, with a tracheostomy in place, and muscle wasting. His labs were notable for WBC-25.5*, RBC-3.79*, HGB-10.5*, HCT-33.0*, NEUTS-86.5*, LYMPHS-7.8*, MONOS-5.5*, PLT COUNT-961*, GLUCOSE-126*, UREA N-25*, TOTAL CO2-20*, and LACTATE-1.5. His sputum culture showed sparse growth oropharyngeal flora and Stenotrophomonas maltophilia. His urine culture showed yeast. His CT torso showed a very limited evaluation for intra-abdominal abscess, questionable focus of fluid collection in the right paracolic gutter, slight decrease in size of cavitated right upper lobe lesion, interval worsening of centrilobular ground-glass nodules throughout both lung fields, consistent with multifocal infection, lytic L5 vertebral lesions with pathologic compression fracture, and increase in conspicuity of hypodense hepatic parenchymal lesions. 

His medications on admission included Heparin 5,000 SC tid, Acetaminophen 650mg q4hrs prn, Oxycodone ___ mg q4hrs prn, Metoprolol Tartrate 50 tid, ceftriaxone 1g q24, flagyl 500 mg q8, Lorazepam 1 mg q4hrs, Insulin (regular insulin), lansoprazole 30 mg daily, chlorhexidine 15mL BID, and promote with fiber goal 50cc/hr. His discharge diagnoses included metastatic lung cancer, perforated bowel status post partial colectomy, right paracolic fluid collection, right lung loculated abscess, and L5 metastatic lesion with cord compression.
label: controls

summary:
The patient, a male with a past medical history of schizophrenia and retinal detachment post-surgery, was admitted to the medicine service due to persistent nausea and vomiting. The patient has had multiple emergency department admissions for the same symptoms. The patient's symptoms include nausea lasting for hours, daily vomiting episodes for the past two weeks, abdominal pain, and a weight loss of 6lbs. The patient denies any changes in diet, dysphagia, toxic habits, or changes in medications in the past years. The patient's initial vital signs in the emergency department were within normal limits. The patient's urine analysis showed few bacteria, protein 30, and positive ketones. The patient was given 2L NS, Zofran, Maalox, Donnatol, and Viscous Lidocaine.

The patient's physical examination upon admission and discharge were largely unremarkable, with the exception of a left eye scleral injection and a surgical pupil unresponsive to light. The patient's lab results upon admission were within normal limits, with the exception of a magnesium level of 2.4. The patient's discharge labs showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, MCH, MCHC, and Phos levels.

The patient underwent several imaging studies, including a CT of the abdomen/pelvis, a CT of the head, a barium swallow, an EGD, an ECHO, and an MR of the head. The CT of the abdomen/pelvis showed a probable hemorrhagic cyst in the left renal upper pole, but no findings to explain the patient's abdominal pain. The barium swallow showed significant dynamic movement of the esophagus in correlation with the heart rate, but no gastroesophageal reflux, narrowing, or hiatal hernia. The EGD did not identify a clear cause for the patient's nausea. The ECHO showed a mildly dilated aortic root, but no clinically significant valvular regurgitation or stenosis. The MR of the head showed no intracranial abnormality.

The patient's hospital course was marked by persistent nausea and vomiting, with no organic source identified. The patient's symptoms were managed with compazine and ativan. The patient was also started on Losartan 25mg for prophylaxis. The patient's medications upon admission included atomoxetine, haloperidol, bupropion, atropine sulfate ophthalmic, polymyxin B-trimethoprim ophthalmic, prednisolone acetate ophthalmic, and apraclonidine ophthalmic.

The patient's family history was non-contributory. The patient was discharged home with a primary diagnosis of vomiting and a secondary diagnosis of schizophrenia. The patient's mental status upon discharge was clear and coherent, and the patient was ambulatory and independent. The patient was instructed to eat as slowly as possible to help with the nausea and to follow up with his primary doctor and a cardiologist.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a male with a history of schizophrenia and a past medical history of retinal detachment, which was surgically repaired. He was admitted to the Medicine service due to dehydration and recurrent emesis, which has led to a 14lb weight loss. He had previously been admitted for similar complaints and had undergone a complete GI work-up, including a barium swallow, EGD, and MRI, all of which were negative for H.Pylori (stool antigen). Psychiatry was consulted to evaluate if the symptoms were related to psychosis or medication side-effects, but they recommended no further medication changes. 

The patient's recurrent emesis continues, and he was admitted after presenting to the ED with uncontrolled emesis. He denies any feelings of food poisoning or any reason not to keep down the food. He reports having an appetite and eating fine, with no dysphagia. However, he experiences nausea and emesis after eating, though not every time. 

The patient has minimal vision in his left eye, which is not a new symptom and has been present since his most recent eye surgery. He had a retinal detachment and it was not surgically repaired until later. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Losartan Potassium, atomoxetine, Atropine Sulfate Ophth, BuPROPion (Sustained Release), Polymyxin B -Trimethoprim Ophth Soln, PrednisoLONE Acetate Ophth. Susp., Haloperidol, Haloperidol Decanoate, apraclonidine, and Prochlorperazine. 

The patient's physical exam revealed a thin man with a regular heart rate, clear lungs, and a soft abdomen with no obvious organomegaly. His left eye had significant conjunctival erythema and he was unable to read anything with it, though he could differentiate light from dark. He had a flat affect and a paucity of speech, but denied psychosis related to food. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for glucose (105*), RBC (4.32*), HGB (13.8*), HCT (39.3*), MCHC (35.2*), RBC (3.80*), Hgb (12.0*), Hct (36.0*), and Phos (4.6*). 

The patient's problems include recurrent emesis of unclear etiology, schizophrenia, and recent retinal surgery for detachment. The cause of the emesis is thought to be psychogenic or a side effect of medication, as a GI work-up was negative. The patient was seen by an ophthalmology consult during this admission who adjusted his topical medications. 

Upon discharge, the patient's condition was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. His discharge diagnosis was nausea and vomiting. The patient was instructed to follow up with his medical appointments as well as his regular outpatient mental health providers.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of schizophrenia and possible ADHD, was brought to the ED due to concerns of worsening paranoia and delusion. He reported experiencing distracting thoughts, some of which made him laugh, and others that made him feel threatened. He also reported symptoms of poor sleep, anhedonia, feelings of guilt, hopelessness, helplessness, poor energy, poor concentration, and poor appetite. He denied any intention, plan, or action to harm anyone. He was previously on halodol, which was switched to Zyprexa due to perceived ineffectiveness. 

The patient has a past medical history of aortic dilation, scoliosis, kyphosis, and retinal surgery. He denies any history of head trauma or seizures. He has been hospitalized four times in the past and has been on medication trials of haldol dec, haldol PO, wellbutrin, and stratera. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be very thin, with no rashes or bruises. His lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally, and he had a tachycardic heart rate with a normal rhythm. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended. 

Lab results showed a glucose level of 92, urea nitrogen of 11, creatinine of 0.7, sodium of 141, potassium of 3.8, chloride of 104, total CO2 of 27, and an anion gap of 14. His WBC count was 4.9, RBC count was 4.20*, hemoglobin was 13.1*, hematocrit was 41.4, MCV was 99*, MCHC was 31.6*, and RDW was 11.8. His AST was 14, LDH was 151, Alk phos was 108, and total bilirubin was 0.6. His TSH was 0.94. His EKG showed a heart rate of 94, Qtc of 411, and diffuse ST elevation. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's tachycardia improved. His hallucinations and homicidal ideation towards imagined people began to decrease in intensity after a few days on increased antipsychotic medication. His olanzapine was increased to 15 mg PO BID. He remained withdrawn, isolative, apathetic, and was found self-dialoging in his room. 

The patient has a history of aortic dilation, which remained asymptomatic and clinically stable during his hospital stay. His tachycardia was managed with metoprolol 25 mg PO BID, which was later changed to metoprolol succinate XL 25 mg PO daily. His decreased PO intake at home improved while in the hospital. He also has a history of retinal detachment and recent right eye surgery. 

Upon discharge, the patient's visual hallucinations and homicidal ideation towards imagined people were less intense and less frequent. He was calm and cooperative, though appearing withdrawn and somewhat apathetic. His medications upon admission were Olanzapine 10 mg PO QHS, Olanzapine 2.5 mg PO BID: PRN anxiety/agitation, BuPROPion (Sustained Release) 150 mg PO BID, and atomoxetine 60 mg oral DAILY.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted to the psychiatry service due to worsening hallucinations, increasing paranoid delusions, and feelings of unsafety. His chief complaint was that he was seeing images that appeared more real to him. He has a history of schizophrenia and a genetic disorder characterized by features such as retinal detachment, mild aortic enlargement, sclerosis, and kyphosis. He has had several past psychiatric hospitalizations and has been on various medications including olanzapine, perphenazine, haloperidol, buproprion, and stratera. He denies self-injury, harm to others, and access to weapons. 

The patient's symptoms included increasing forgetfulness, distractibility, progressive delusions of believing he is a rap artist, and paranoia that people in the community are out to get him. He reported vomiting the day prior to admission, which he thought could be related to gagging on a new antipsychotic. He also reported seeing images of people who are trying to hurt him and getting increasingly angry at these thoughts. He denied feeling depressed, anhedonic, anxious, or having symptoms of mania. 

Physical examination revealed a thin man, sitting comfortably, with no abnormal findings. Neurological examination was within normal limits, with the exception of mild psychomotor retardation. His mood was described as 'good', but his affect was blunted. He denied suicidal ideation but sometimes had homicidal ideation towards imagined people he believed were out to get him. 

Lab results showed a glucose level of 77, urea nitrogen of 16, creatinine of 0.8, sodium of 138, potassium of 4.1, chloride of 104, total CO2 of 23, and anion gap of 15. Urine tests for various substances including benzodiazepines, barbiturates, opiates, cocaine, amphetamines, oxycodone, and methadone were all negative. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's schizophrenia was managed with adjustments to his medication regimen. His home doses of Perphenazine, Olanzapine, and Benztropine were continued, and Clozapine was started and slowly uptitrated. His heart rate and blood pressure became elevated as the Clozapine dose was increased, so his metoprolol dose was increased and losartan was started. His hemoglobin was mildly decreased on admission but remained stable throughout his stay. 

The patient's father visited often during his hospitalization and was in agreement with the plan for discharge. The patient was discharged on metoprolol succinate XL 100 mg daily, clozapine 300 mg daily, glycopyrrolate 2 mg twice daily, losartan potassium 25 mg daily, and omeprazole 20 mg daily. His discharge diagnosis was schizophrenia. His condition at discharge was improved, with stable improvement in his 'bad thoughts'. He was instructed to follow up with all outpatient appointments, continue all medications as directed, avoid abusing alcohol and drugs, and contact his outpatient psychiatrist or other providers if he had any concerns.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to abdominal pain. He reported experiencing vague abdominal pain six days prior to admission, which increased in severity over the next 24 hours. The patient reported persistent, dull, lower abdominal discomfort, despite the pain lessening. He has been tolerating oral intake but with a decreased appetite. He has been constipated and has been taking MiraLax with good effect. He reported no nausea or vomiting, but did report subjective fevers and chills. The patient has a history of hypertension and a known allergy to walnuts. His family history is noncontributory.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be well, alert and oriented. His cardiovascular and respiratory systems were normal. His abdomen was soft and non-distended, with minimal right lower quadrant tenderness to deep palpation. There was no rebound or guarding.

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (4.43), Hgb (13.0), Hct (39.5), Neuts (75.6), Lymphs (13.4), and Eos (4.5). Other lab results, including WBC, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, PTT, Glucose, UreaN, Creat, Na, K, Cl, HCO3, AnGap, and Lactate were within normal ranges.

The patient was diagnosed with acute appendicitis. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis revealed a dilated appendix with hyperemic thickened walls and a surrounding adjacent 2.5 cm organizing fluid collection at its base, suggesting a ruptured appendicitis. The scan also revealed an extensive infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm with dissection in the distal abdominal aorta, extending through the right common iliac artery into the proximal portion of the right external iliac artery. 

The patient was managed non-operatively with antibiotics for appendicitis. A consultation for possible drainage of the fluid collection was declined as it was deemed too small and not ideally accessible. Vascular surgery recommended tight blood pressure control with a systolic blood pressure less than 130, and future repair of the aneurysm. A cardiac echo was performed in preparation for the surgery.

Upon admission, the patient was on Atenolol 25 mg, Atorvastatin 40 mg, and Aspirin 325 mg daily. The patient was discharged home with a diagnosis of appendicitis. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for an elective repair of a known abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA). His past medical history includes AAA and hypertension (HTN). He has also previously undergone an appendectomy. The patient's family history is notable for a possible paternal grandfather with a similar habitus and cardiac death, as well as a history of strokes and diabetes. There are no other reported myocardial infarctions in the family.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be a tall, thin male in no apparent distress. His cardiovascular system was regular rate and rhythm, lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally, and his abdomen was soft and non-tender. His extremities were warm and well perfused, with mild bilateral edema.

The patient underwent an open abdominal aortic aneurysm repair. Post-surgery, he was transferred to the vicu and was seen by the acute pain team. An epidural was used for pain control, which was later discontinued and replaced with oral pain medications. The patient was found to be independent and stable for discharge home on post-operative day 5.

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.50), Hgb (10.6), Hct (32.0), Calcium (7.9), and Phos (2.2). A MRSA screen was conducted using a nasal swab, and no MRSA was isolated.

Upon admission, the patient was on the following medications: Atenolol 25 mg daily, Simvastatin 20 mg daily, Aspirin 325 mg daily, Multivitamins 1 tablet daily, Fish Oil (Omega 3) 1000 mg daily, and Polyethylene Glycol 17 g daily as needed for constipation.

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of abdominal aortic aneurysm and hypertension. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was able to ambulate independently. The patient was discharged home with instructions for post-surgery care and activities.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a male who was admitted due to musculo-skeletal pain. He has a history of mitral valve (MV) repair and atrial septal defect (ASD) closure. He presented with a week-long history of left-sided chest pain. A CT scan ruled out aortic dissection but revealed an ascending aortic aneurysm measuring 4.6 cm involving the aortic root. The patient had a similar episode of chest pain previously, during which a CT scan showed the aortic root to be 3.5 cm, but an echocardiogram showed it to be 4.4 cm. The aorta measured 4.3 cm when he underwent MV repair and ASD closure. The patient reported consistent mid-chest discomfort. 

The patient's family history includes three first-degree relatives who have died from ruptured aortic aneurysms. Upon physical examination, the patient was found to have a marfanoid appearance, with no acute distress. His pulse was 61, respiratory rate was 12, and oxygen saturation was 100% on room air. His blood pressure was 110/56 on the left. He had pain with palpation to the left lower ribs, reproducible with palpation, extending around to his back. His sternum was stable with well-healed sternal incision and chest tube sites.

Lab results showed a glucose level of 83, urea nitrogen of 12, creatinine of 0.8, sodium of 143, potassium of 4.3, and chloride of 109, which was marked as abnormal. His total CO2 was 27 and anion gap was 11. His white blood cell count was 6.9, red blood cell count was 4.80, hemoglobin was 14.1, hematocrit was 43.0, MCV was 90, MCH was 29.3, MCHC was 32.7, and RDW was 13.8. His neutrophils were 49.2, marked as abnormal, monocytes were 4.7, eosinophils were 6.9, marked as abnormal, and basophils were 1.5. His platelet count was 242.

An echocardiogram showed a mildly dilated aortic root and moderately dilated ascending aorta. The aortic root at the sinus level was 4.4 cm, marked as abnormal, and the ascending aorta was 5.3 cm, marked as abnormal. The mitral valve E wave deceleration time was 285 ms, marked as abnormal, and the TR gradient was less than or equal to 203 mm Hg, marked as abnormal. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's flank pain was determined to be musculo-skeletal as it was reproducible with pressure applied to his flank. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of aortic root aneurysm. His condition upon discharge was good. He was instructed to call if he experienced chest or back pain, to continue taking his medications as prescribed, and to avoid lifting heavy weight. His medication upon admission was Atenolol 25 mg PO daily.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to recurrent chest pain which he described as sharp and radiating to the back and stomach, accompanied by nausea. He also reported a heavy feeling but denied shortness of breath. The patient has a history of mitral valve repair and atrial septal defect closure. He has been taking Atenolol 25 mg daily. The patient's family history includes three first-degree relatives who have died from ruptured aortic aneurysms.

Upon admission, the patient's vitals were recorded as 98.8 74 118/79 18 100% RA. Initial ECG showed borderline ST-elevation in inferior leads, but was deemed non-diagnostic by the cardiology fellow. The patient's blood pressure in both arms were discordant, but left was greater than right. A CT angiogram did not show dissection on preliminary read. The patient was taken to the cath lab where his coronaries were found to be clean. His lactate level was slightly elevated at 2.4. 

The patient's physical exam on admission showed mild tenderness to palpation over the left chest and mild tenderness to palpation over the left upper quadrant/ribs. On discharge, the patient's physical exam showed moderate tenderness to palpation over the left chest and moderate tenderness to palpation over the left upper quadrant/ribs.

Lab results showed a lactate level of 2.3* and a glucose level of 101*. The patient's CT angiogram of the chest/abdomen showed a stable size in appearance of ascending aortic root aneurysm with no evidence of aortic dissection nor intramural hematoma. No acute abdominal or pelvic pathology was found. Cardiac catheterization showed no angiographically apparent coronary artery disease.

During the hospital course, the patient's chest pain, which he has had chronically, was more severe and he had associated abdominal and back pain which was new for him. An EKG showed new ST-elevation in the inferior leads. He was found to have clean coronaries in the cath lab. A CTA showed stable size of his known aortic aneurysm. His chest pain is thought to be musculoskeletal as cardiac causes have been ruled out at this time. There was also some concern for GERD as a cause of his chest pain as the patient complained of throat burning during his hospitalization. His cardiac surgeon was informed of his hospitalization.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted to the Cardiac-Thoracic unit with a chief complaint of left-sided chest pain. He has a known history of an ascending aortic aneurysm, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and lactose intolerance. He has previously undergone mitral valve repair and atrial septal defect (ASD) closure. The patient has been experiencing recurrent chest pain and has been to the emergency room multiple times. A CT scan ruled out aortic dissection but revealed a stable ascending aortic aneurysm measuring approximately 4.6-4.9 cm involving the aortic root. This led to a referral for surgery.

During his stay, the patient underwent a redo sternotomy and valve-sparing aortic root repair with a 32mm & 24mm gelweave double graft. Post-surgery, he was transferred to the CVICU for recovery and monitoring. He was extubated on postoperative day 1 and weaned off inotropic and vasopressor support. His hematocrit dropped due to chest drainage, and he was transfused 2 units of packed red blood cells. He was diuresed towards his preoperative weight and started on a beta blocker. He developed edema and pain in his left arm near a prior peripheral IV site and was started on Keflex for suspected phlebitis. An ultrasound of the left upper extremity ruled out deep vein thrombosis. 

His lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.25*), Hgb (10.1*), Hct (28.8*), MCHC (35.1*), Glucose (119*), and HCO3 (33*). His physical examination revealed a tall, thin male in no acute distress with dry and intact skin. His chest incision had keloid scarring, and wires were palpable. His lungs were clear bilaterally, and his abdomen was soft, non-distended, and non-tender. His extremities were warm and well-perfused. 

The patient's family history is significant with his father, brother, and sister having an undisclosed condition. His father, brother, and sister have all passed away at undisclosed ages. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Atenolol 25mg daily and Omeprazole 20mg daily. By the time of discharge, he was ambulating freely, his wound was healing, and his pain was controlled with oral analgesics. He was discharged home in good condition with appropriate follow-up instructions.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male of undisclosed age, was admitted due to complaints of left arm pain and weakness. He has a history of an ascending aortic aneurysm, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and lactose intolerance. He has undergone a valve-sparing aortic root replacement with a 32 mm Valsalva Dacron graft and ascending aortic replacement with a 24 mm Gelweave tube graft. He also had a mitral valve repair and atrial septal defect closure in the past. 

The patient's family history includes his father, brother, and sister, all of whom have passed away at undisclosed ages due to undisclosed conditions. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Acetaminophen, Docusate Sodium, Omeprazole, Cephalexin, Oxycodone, Senna, and Atenolol. 

The patient's chief complaint was left arm pain and weakness, which had been present for three days. He also reported persistent poor appetite and nausea. The pain was described as starting at his right ear and radiating down across his back to his left arm. 

Physical examination revealed that the patient was alert and oriented, with no edema and 2+ radial pulses bilaterally. His sternal incision was clean, dry, and intact, with no sternal click. 

Lab results showed abnormal RBC count (3.44*), Hemoglobin (10.3*), Hematocrit (30.6*), and Glucose (104*). A chest X-ray showed left-sided persistent pleural effusion and opacification of the left lung base as well as patchy right basilar opacification, all suggesting atelectasis. The cardiac, mediastinal, and hilar contours appeared stable. 

A CT angiogram showed a focal absence of opacification in the distal and radial artery of the left upper extremity, which may be due to a thrombus. There was reconstitution of flow distal to this, likely from retrograde flow through the palmar arch. The subclavian, axillary, brachial, and ulnar arteries were patent without evidence of stenosis or dissection. A hematoma surrounding the ascending aorta was noted, which may be postoperative in nature. There was no evidence of dissection or thrombus. 

During his hospital stay, the patient remained hemodynamically stable. The vascular service determined that his radial clot was minimal and did not require anticoagulation. He was started on neurontin and motrin for pain control. The neurology service suggested that his continued weakness might be related to possible spasm or rotator cuff injury. 

Upon discharge, the patient was alert and oriented, with a steady gait. His incisional pain was managed, and his incisions were healing well with no erythema or drainage. His right upper extremity was warm, with +2 pulses, good motor strength, and intact sensation. His discharge diagnosis was left arm pain and weakness.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted with a chief complaint of achalasia. He has a history of an ascending aortic aneurysm, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and lactose intolerance. He has previously undergone aortic root replacement, mitral valve repair, and atrial septal defect closure. His symptoms included difficulty swallowing food, chest pain during oral intake, sensation of food getting stuck in his chest, nausea, emesis, and regurgitation of undigested food. He reported an 11 lb weight loss in recent months, which he managed to regain through conscious efforts to eat full meals despite his symptoms. He has been evaluated via esophageal manometry and a barium swallow test, both of which confirmed the diagnosis of Type 2 achalasia. 

The patient also reported intermittent chest pain, for which he sometimes visits the emergency department. He underwent an exercise stress test that showed no signs of myocardial ischemia. He also experiences profuse epistaxis, for which he has been prescribed nasal sprays by his primary care physician. He denies any fevers/chills, severe abdominal pain, shortness of breath, or dysuria. 

The patient's family history includes his father, brother, and sister, all of whom passed away at different ages. 

Upon admission, the patient was on amoxicillin, atenolol, gabapentin, and omeprazole. His physical examination was largely unremarkable, with no signs of distress or edema. However, his blood tests showed abnormal levels of red blood cells (4.53), hemoglobin (13.5), hematocrit (39.4), glucose (119), and phosphorus (4.9). 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a myotomy and partial fundoplication. He was stable throughout his hospitalization, with no signs of infection or bleeding. He was initially kept nil per os (NPO), then started on a clear diet, and finally advanced to a mechanical soft diet, which he tolerated well. He was afebrile and hemodynamically stable at the time of discharge, tolerating a mechanical soft diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and his pain was well controlled. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of achalasia. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was able to ambulate independently. His discharge instructions included activity restrictions, pain management guidelines, wound care instructions, and medication instructions. He was advised to contact his surgeon if he experienced any severe or worsening pain, chest pain, cough, shortness of breath, wheezing, fever, nausea and vomiting, inability to tolerate fluids, food, or medications, or any other concerning symptoms.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a right-handed male with a past medical history of shingles affecting his legs, presented with symptoms of vertigo, blurry vision, neck pain, and headache. He reported waking up in his usual state of health and going for a run, which is not a common activity for him. During the run, he started feeling lightheaded and noticed blurry vision. His symptoms persisted even after resting. He also experienced a severe room spinning sensation and had to hold on when walking to prevent himself from falling. He reported neck stiffness and pain that radiated up to the base of his skull and then to the vertex and behind his eyes. He described the pain as constant and 'tight' but non-throbbing. He also reported tinnitus earlier in the day. He has no history of recent neck trauma or whiplash injuries and does not get any neck manipulations done. His primary care physician referred him to the emergency department for further evaluation.

The patient's family history includes a father with a history of vertigo and a maternal grandfather with a history of stroke. He has no known allergies to medications. His physical examination on admission was largely unremarkable, with normal vital signs and neurological examination. However, he reported seeing small, white dots in his field of vision bilaterally upon discharge.

Laboratory results on admission showed elevated neutrophils (70.9%) and glucose (119 mg/dL). Imaging studies, including a CT angiogram of the head and neck and an MRI/MRA of the neck with and without contrast, revealed a diminished caliber with a linear intraluminal filling defect of the right V3 segment of the vertebral artery, raising concern for arterial dissection and/or intraluminal thromboembolus. There were also punctate foci of restricted diffusion within the right greater than left cerebellar hemispheres and the left greater than right occipital lobes, compatible with acute to subacute foci of ischemia, likely from an embolic source. A transthoracic echocardiogram showed a mildly dilated aortic root at the sinus level.

The patient was found to have a right vertebral dissection and thrombus. He denied any recent trauma or overexertion of the neck, but had recently moved to a new house, so trauma from lifting heavy objects may have caused the dissection. He was started on heparin bridge to coumadin during his hospital stay. His discharge diagnosis was right vertebral artery dissection.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, presented with a chief complaint of palpitations that had been ongoing for 10 hours.
