He has a history of mechanical aortic valve replacement (AVR), brachial embolism, sinusitis, fibromyalgia, and migraines. He reported similar symptoms a few years ago when he was in rapid ventricular response (RVR). He has chronic mild dyspnea on exertion (DOE), which has not changed. He had a recent ECHO/MRI with a normal aorta. The patient also reported increased reflux with belching but no bitter taste in his mouth, not alleviated with TUMS or sitting up. He feels his abdomen is distended, he notes decreased flatus but had a normal formed bowel movement and distention decreased. No abdominal pain. He denies recent trauma. He has been under stress recently planning a charity event. 

In the ER, vitals were T98.4 HR 135 BP 142/104 R 16 99% RA. On exam he was comfortable, lungs clear, irregularly irregular HR with mechanical valve sounds. EKG showed atrial fibrillation with HR 130s. He spontaneously converted around to NSR in ___ with just fluids at 1400. CXR negative except small R pleural effusion. CTA negative for PE or dissection. CE negative x1. He was given aspirin. Labs unremarkable.

On evaluation on the floor, the patient is comfortable. He denies chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations. Still with reflux. He still has some collar bone pain/neck stiffness. He denies any prior history of stroke, TIA, pulmonary embolism, bleeding at the time of surgery, cough, hemoptysis, black stools or red stools. He denies recent fevers, chills or rigors. He denies exertional buttock or calf pain. All of the other review of systems were negative. 

The patient's family history includes a mother with osteoarthritis, a father who passed away, and a maternal uncle with a myocardial infarction. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Amitriptyline 12.5 mg a day, Atenolol 125mg a day, Omeprazole 40mg daily - just changed to Protonix 40mg daily, and Warfarin 5mg daily - checks INR at home and adjusts doses himself. 

Abnormal lab results include PTT-37.9, Glucose-110, cTropnT-0.16, cTropnT-0.17, RBC-4.59, Hgb-13.5, Hct-39.0, and PTT-37.4. Imaging showed A-fib and A-flutter with HR to 130s, then converted to NSR 60 bpm. EKG on admission NSR ~65, LAD, slightly prolonged PR (204) with 1st degree AV block, with new TW flattening V3, TWI in V4; with TWI V5-V6 unchanged from ___. CXR showed no evidence for pneumonia. Small right pleural effusion. No mediastinal widening. CTA Torso showed no evidence of aortic dissection or pulmonary embolism. Right upper lobe pulmonary nodule, unchanged, and no further imaging follow up is needed. Right upper pole renal cyst. Sacral dural ectasia. 

The patient was diagnosed with atrial fibrillation/atrial flutter with rapid ventricular rate. Other conditions ruled out include pneumonia, aortic dissection, and pulmonary embolism.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to a left frontal brain mass. He had been experiencing confusion, headaches, and slurred speech over the past three days. A head CT scan at an outside hospital revealed the brain mass with edema, leading to his transfer for further evaluation. He received a loading dose of Dexamethasone and Keppra prior to transfer. The patient's wife reported that she had noticed confusion and a slowing of his speech over the past few days. The patient denied any blurred vision, double vision, nausea, or vomiting. 

His past medical history includes atrial fibrillation (Afib) for which he takes daily Coumadin, asthma, several cardioversions at the outside hospital, atrial flutter, mitral valve prolapse, aortic valve insufficiency, and osteoporosis. He has no family history of cancer. 

Upon admission, his physical exam showed that he was awake, alert, and cooperative. His speech was slow but with good comprehension and repetition. His cranial nerves were intact and symmetric. His motor skills were normal with full power throughout his left upper extremity and bilateral lower extremities. Sensation was intact to light touch bilaterally. 

Lab results showed an abnormal PTT value of 42.1. His urine culture showed mixed bacterial flora, consistent with skin and/or genital contamination. Imaging results from an MRI of the head with and without contrast suggested a primary brain neoplasm likely glioma. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed possible cholelithiasis and hepatic and renal hypodensities that were too small to characterize. No evidence of malignancy was found in the abdomen, pelvis, or thorax. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a left craniotomy for tumor resection. Postoperative imaging showed no evidence of hemorrhage and expected postoperative changes. He remained neurologically intact and was transferred to the floor. 

Upon discharge, the patient was alert and oriented, with no pronator drift and full strength. His discharge diagnoses were a left frontal brain lesion and cerebral edema. His medications upon admission were Warfarin, Albuterol, Flaxseed, and Calcium.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the MICU due to multiple bilateral pulmonary emboli, tachycardia, and back pain. He has a history of atrial fibrillation, for which he was previously on warfarin, and a recently diagnosed glioblastoma multiforme (GBM). He also has a history of deep vein thrombosis (DVT), diagnosed two days prior to his current admission. His past medical history includes left frontal glioblastoma, atrial fibrillation, asthma, cardioversions, atrial flutter, mitral valve prolapse, aortic valve insufficiency, osteoporosis, esophageal reflux, colonic adenoma, solitary pulmonary nodule, impaired fasting glucose, bilateral cataracts, thinning of the retina, dyslipidemia, right wrist fracture, eczema (resolved), pneumonia, and scoliosis and kyphosis. He has no family history of cancer.

Earlier in the year, the patient experienced confusion, headache, and slurred speech. A CT scan revealed a left frontal mass, and he underwent a left frontal craniotomy and excision of a brain tumor, with pathology showing GBM. He was started on Dexamethasone/Keppra during his initial hospitalization for resection. As an outpatient, he was started on Temodar and an experimental drug protocol. He later experienced left lower leg swelling and increasing right eye floaters. An ultrasound in the clinic revealed a left popliteal and left peroneal DVT, and he was started on Lovenox 80mg BID. 

Upon admission, his vitals were 98.9 105 130/88 19 96%. His CBC, coags, and chem 7 were normal. A CTA showed multiple bilateral pulmonary emboli in the right upper lobe, right lower lobe, left lower lobe, and lingula. There were also multiple ground-glass and reticular opacities, predominantly in the bilateral lower lobes, which could represent a combination of atelectasis versus developing infarcts. A small right non-hemorrhagic pleural effusion was also noted. 

His admission labs showed elevated neutrophils (85.4*), lymphs (7.9*), glucose (112*), cTropnT (0.02*), and proBNP (496*). His urine test results were normal. 

During his hospital stay, he was started on a heparin drip and later switched to Lovenox for his pulmonary embolism. His GBM was managed with PLX 3397 on trial, Keppra, and dexamethasone. His atrial fibrillation was managed with anticoagulation for his PE and metoprolol for rate control. 

His medications upon admission included Acetaminophen, Bisacodyl, Famotidine, LeVETiracetam, Dexamethasone, Enoxaparin Sodium, temozolomide, Prochlorperazine, and Albuterol Inhaler. 

Upon discharge, his mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent. His discharge diagnoses included pulmonary emboli, DVT, atrial fibrillation and flutter with rapid ventricular response, and GBM.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of left frontal Glioblastoma post-resection, Atrial fibrillation, Mitral valve prolapse, Aortic valve insufficiency, Asthma, Osteoporosis, Esophageal reflux, Colonic adenoma, Solitary pulmonary nodule, Impaired fasting glucose, Bilateral cataracts, Thinning of the retina, Dyslipidemia, Right wrist fracture, Eczema (resolved), Pneumonia, Scoliosis and kyphosis, was admitted due to Pancytopenia. He presented with epistaxis and increased swelling on his scalp for two days. He was found to have platelets <5 and pancytopenia. He denied any black stools but admitted to brown diarrhea for several days. He denies any dizziness, shortness of breath, or chest pain. 

Upon admission, his temperature was 97.9, heart rate was 126, blood pressure was 110/56, respiratory rate was 18, and oxygen saturation was 100%. He received 1L NS bolus and was transfused 1U PRBC and 1U PLT. His stool was brown and faintly guaiac positive. 

Abnormal lab results included WBC-2.4*, RBC-3.09*, Hgb-9.8*, Hct-25.8*, MCHC-38.0*, Glucose-121*, Iron-206*, LD-252*, Ret Aut-0.7*, ALT-49*, LD(LDH)-268*, calTIBC-233*, Ferritn-854*, and multiple instances of Plt Ct-<5*. 

Imaging studies showed no acute intracranial abnormality, no acute intraabdominal or pelvic abnormality, patchy peripheral lower lobe predominant ground-glass opacities, no evidence of aspiration or penetration, and non-occlusive thrombus in the left popliteal vein with improved flow compared to prior study. Persistent occlusive thrombus was found in the left posterior tibial and left peroneal veins. No deep vein thrombosis was found in the right lower extremity veins. 

The patient's hospital course was complicated by neutropenic fever, hematuria, anemia, and atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. His pancytopenia was attributed to study drug/temozolamide. His thrombocytopenia persisted and was relatively refractory to transfusion. His neutropenic fever was treated with aztreonam and cipro for neutropenic fever due to penicillin allergy. He had frank hematuria with clots, which was managed by urology with a foley. His anemia was most likely chemotherapy-related. His atrial fibrillation was managed with metoprolol and diltiazem. 

Upon discharge, his mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive but required assistance or aid for ambulation. His discharge medications included acetaminophen, albuterol inhaler, bisacodyl, acyclovir, aluminum-magnesium hydrox.-simethicone, diltiazem, folic acid, levofloxacin, tramadol, voriconazole, lorazepam, and metoprolol succinate XL. He was advised to return to the ER immediately if he developed bleeding of any kind or if he developed fevers or chills.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to right-sided weakness, falls, and failure to thrive. He has a history of glioblastoma, for which he underwent resection, chemotherapy, and cyberknife treatment. The patient's condition has been deteriorating over the past few weeks, with increasing instability, falls, and right-sided weakness. He also exhibited personality changes, decreased speed of processing/thinking, and episodes of incontinence. His wife expressed concern about caring for him at home and showed interest in palliative care or home hospice.

The patient's past medical history includes left frontal glioblastoma, atrial fibrillation, asthma, cardioversions, atrial flutter, mitral valve prolapse, aortic valve insufficiency, osteoporosis, tonsil removal, esophageal reflux, colonic adenoma, solitary pulmonary nodule, impaired fasting glucose, bilateral cataracts, thinning of the retina, dyslipidemia, right wrist fracture, resolved eczema, pneumonia, and scoliosis and kyphosis. He has no family history of cancer.

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam showed irregular rhythm, no edema, clubbing, tremors, or asterixis, and diminished bilateral patellar reflexes. His admission labs showed abnormal values for WBC (2.9), RBC (3.04), Hgb (10.9), Hct (32.3), MCV (106), MCH (35.9), RDWSD (54.1), Plt Ct (76), Monos (13.1), AbsLymp (0.75), and Glucose (121), UreaN (22). His urine culture showed mixed bacterial flora. Imaging showed no evidence of pneumonia, but trace bilateral pleural effusions and a 10 mm nodular opacity within the right upper lobe were noted. His brain CT and MRI showed increased mass effect with worsening effacement of the left lateral ventricle and new 5 mm shift of normally midline structures to the right.

During his hospital stay, the patient's weakness improved. His family decided to try another round of chemotherapy (temozolomide). He was also treated for constipation, pancytopenia, hypertension, and a pulmonary embolism. A 1-2 mm nodule was found in the left lower lobe of his lung, but as a never smoker, he is considered low risk for lung cancer. He was started on oral calcium and vitamin D supplementation for an age indeterminate compression fracture.

Upon admission, the patient was taking Albuterol Inhaler, Diltiazem, FoLIC Acid, LORazepam, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Acetaminophen, Bisacodyl, Zolpidem Tartrate, Calcium Carbonate, and Alendronate Sodium.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of left frontal glioblastoma, was admitted due to low blood counts. His past medical history includes glioblastoma, pulmonary embolism, marrow failure, atrial fibrillation, asthma, mitral valve prolapse, aortic valve insufficiency, osteoporosis, esophageal reflux, colonic adenoma, impaired fasting glucose, dyslipidemia, pneumonia, scoliosis and kyphosis, bone marrow failure, renal failure, peripheral neuropathy, and sleep apnea. He has had a history of deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism, and has been treated with chemoradiation, which led to bone marrow suppression and disease progression. He has also had a history of confusion, headache, and slow speech, and has undergone resection of the left frontal tumor. 

Upon admission, the patient was fatigued and slow to find words but was oriented and alert. He denied having new complaints or symptoms other than gradually worsening fatigue. His physical exam revealed pitting edema halfway up to his shins bilaterally, petechiae, and a non-inflamed port site. His wife reported that he urinates frequently. 

His lab results showed abnormal levels of WBC (3.9), RBC (1.36), Hgb (5.0), Hct (15.2), MCV (112), MCH (36.8), RDW (18.2), Plt Ct (19), Neuts (80), Lymphs (12), Metas (2), Myelos (1), NRBC (2), Plasma (1), AbsNeut (3.24), AbsLymp (0.47), Glucose (198), UreaN (40), Creat (0.9), ALT (54), LD(LDH) (489), Albumin (3.1), Iron (214), calTIBC (231), Hapto (268), Ferritn (2897), and TRF (178). 

During his hospital stay, a port was placed for future chemotherapy. His hemolysis labs were unremarkable and he did not show signs or symptoms of blood loss. His acute on chronic anemia is likely related to marrow suppression from chemotherapy. He responded well to pRBC transfusion, requiring 3 transfusions before discharge. His platelet count was always >10 and on discharge, it was 31. 

The patient's family history includes his father's death due to an aortic aneurysm. His medications upon admission included acetaminophen, albuterol inhaler, bisacodyl, calcium carbonate, diltiazem, folic acid, metoprolol succinate XL, zolpidem tartrate, dexamethasone, omeprazole, vitamin D, alendronate sodium, lorazepam, furosemide, and oxycodone. 

The patient was discharged to extended care with a primary diagnosis of aplastic anemia and aplastic thrombocytopenia, and secondary diagnoses of glioblastoma, atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and lower extremity edema. His mental status was noted as confused but alert and interactive, and he was independently ambulatory. His discharge instructions included restarting chemotherapy per his oncologist's recommendations, continuing weekly blood checks, and continuing to take his medications as prescribed.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a male with a past medical history significant for glioblastoma, deep vein thrombosis (DVT), pulmonary embolism (PE), bone marrow failure, atrial fibrillation, asthma, mitral valve prolapse, aortic valve insufficiency, osteoporosis, esophageal reflux, colonic adenoma, solitary pulmonary nodule, impaired fasting glucose, bilateral cataracts, dyslipidemia, right wrist fracture, eczema, pneumonia, scoliosis and kyphosis, renal failure, peripheral neuropathy, and sleep apnea. He has a history of tonsil removal and cardioversions. He was admitted to the neurology service due to fevers and encephalopathy. 

During his stay, he underwent a port removal procedure. He had a history of glioblastoma multiforme (GBM) and was previously treated with Temozolamide, which led to severe bone marrow suppression. He was not on anticoagulation due to a history of DVT/PE. He was transferred from his rehab facility due to a fever of 103 and altered mental status. He was found to be in rapid atrial fibrillation with a heart rate in the 130s-150s and was initially hypotensive. His initial vitals were 97.6, 150, 99/61, 18, 97%RA. 

His physical exam was notable for abdominal petechiae and guaiac positive brown stool. His labs showed a WBC of 2.6 (90% PMNs), H&H 6.5/20.5, 10 platelets, lactate 6.4, pH 7.52, pCO2 23. His glucose was 306, UreaN 34, HCO3 19, ALT 66, LD(LDH) 604, and Hapto 321. His imaging showed no acute changes in the head CT and an unremarkable chest X-ray. 

During his stay, he was given IV Digoxin, IVF NS, IV CefePIME, IV Vancomycin, IV Metoprolol Tartrate, PO Metoprolol Tartrate, and PO Acetaminophen. He was also transfused 1 unit of pRBCs but developed a fever during the transfusion, suggesting a possible transfusion reaction. 

His blood cultures were positive for Staphylococcus aureus, suggesting bacteremia. His stool was negative for C. difficile. His port-a-cath tip culture was also positive for Staphylococcus aureus. His urine culture showed no growth. 

His hospital course was complicated by severe sepsis, MSSA bacteremia, altered mental status, and atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. The source of the bacteremia was thought to be the recent port placement or an elbow abscess. His mental status changes were thought to be due to toxic metabolic encephalopathy or psychiatric illness. His care was transitioned to home with hospice following family discussions. 

His family history is notable for his father dying of an aortic aneurysm. He has two healthy sisters. His social history was not provided in the note. His medications upon admission included Acetaminophen, Folic Acid, Lorazepam, Dexamethasone, Cyanocobalamin, Omeprazole, Calcium Carbonate, Alendronate Sodium, Vitamin D, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Polyethylene Glycol, and Senna.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to a subarachnoid hemorrhage following a traumatic fall caused by syncope. She has a history of exertional syncope, mitral valve prolapse, aortic aneurysm (though contradicted by a CTA), and depression. She experienced a number of episodes of syncope during pregnancy, which led to her diagnosis of mitral valve prolapse. 

On the day of admission, she was shoveling and salting the sidewalk when she began to feel palpitations, tachycardia, lightheadedness, body shakes, and heaviness in both arms. She then experienced an unwitnessed loss of consciousness (LOC), during which she lacerated the right side of her head. She also reported a severe frontal headache and photophobia. She has a history of similar exertional episodes, the first of which occurred during childbirth. Her father, paternal grandmother, and eldest son have had similar symptoms.

Upon admission, her vitals were stable. A CT scan showed a small subarachnoid hemorrhage. She received 5 staples to the left parietal head laceration and a tetanus shot. Her physical exam on admission and discharge showed no significant changes. 

Lab results showed abnormal levels of WBC (12.3*), MCHC (35.9*), Neuts (86.6*), Lymphs (8.0*), and RBC (4.17*), Hct (35.8*), and MCHC (36.0*). A CT head scan confirmed a small hemorrhagic focus in the left frontal lobe with no surrounding edema. An echo showed mild posterior leaflet mitral valve prolapse with mild mitral regurgitation. 

During her hospital stay, she had 3 witnessed episodes of syncope, during which she was orthostatic with SBP 138->98. Telemetry review showed no arrhythmia during these episodes, and her EKG was normal. Further cardiac enzymes were within normal limits and echo after the first episode only showed mild mitral valve prolapse and tricuspid regurgitation. 

She was discharged with a primary diagnosis of syncope and subarachnoid hemorrhage. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. Her only medication upon admission was magnesium oxide.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to chest pain. She has a history of mitral valve prolapse and depression. She also reported a history of aortic aneurysm, but this was contradicted by a CTA. The patient underwent a cardiac catheterization during her stay. 

Upon admission, the patient reported experiencing sudden onset midline, sharp chest pain radiating to her left neck while shoveling snow. This was associated with a tingling sensation in her left hand and lips. The pain improved after taking 2 baby ASA. She denied shortness of breath, cough, fever, or chills. In the ED, her vitals were stable and labs showed a rise in troponin from 0 to 0.08. Her CBC, chem 7, and UA were normal. She was given ASA, a GI cocktail, and SL NTG. 

Her family history includes her father and paternal grandmother, and her maternal father had a myocardial infarction. 

On admission, her physical examination was largely normal. On discharge, she appeared anxious and had trace pitting edema to her shins. 

Her lab results showed elevated Neuts (85.7), Lymphs (9.7), and CK(CPK) (300). Her cardiac labs showed elevated cTropnT levels, peaking at 0.82, and elevated CK-MB levels. 

A TTE showed mild regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction with hypokinesis of the inferior septum, mild mitral valve prolapse, and mild late systolic mitral regurgitation. A cardiac catheterization showed no significant CAD, with minimal mid disease in the LAD and normal LMCA, circumflex, and RCA. 

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with NSTEMI due to elevated troponins and no EKG changes. She was started on a heparin drip, beta blockers, atorvastatin, and aspirin. However, the beta blocker was discontinued and she opted not to take the statin. She agreed to take low dose aspirin and Amlodipine 2.5mg daily. 

Her medications upon admission included CarBAMazepine, Calcium, Vitamin D, Vitamin B Complex With C, olive leaf extract, and a probiotic. 

A 3mm lung nodule was incidentally noted on a CTA of the chest, which was new compared to previous imaging. 

Upon discharge, her primary diagnosis was NSTEMI. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent. She was instructed to follow up with a cardiologist and her primary care provider.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to epistaxis and recurrent sinus infections. She has a history of a mechanical replacement procedure, IgG deficiency for which she receives IVIG infusions, bronchiectasis, depression, and frequent sinusitis and epistaxis. She also has a family history of lung cancer and Alzheimer's disease. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including Atenolol, Losartan, Kariva, Aspirin, Adderall, Trazodone, Omeprazole, Warfarin, Clonazepam, Lomotil, Mucinex, Albuterol nebulizer, Vitamin B, Cal/vit D, Mg citrate, IVIG, Flonase, and Sumatriptan. 

The patient's chief complaint was epistaxis and she was scheduled for an ENT surgery. Prior to the surgery, she was put on heparin bridging. She had a significant bout of sinusitis that required hospitalization and was treated with antibiotics including Levaquin. A CT scan showed significant sinus disease and nasal septum abnormalities. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent a successful ENT procedure without complications. Post-operation, she had some oozing from the nose which was treated supportively. She was prescribed Augmentin for prophylaxis and Oxycodone for pain management. Her heparin treatment was successful and Warfarin was resumed post-operation. Her depression was managed with continued Trazodone and Clonazepam. 

Lab results showed an abnormal PTT value of 36.7 and a low WBC count of 3.5. Other lab results including PLT count, RBC, HGB, HCT, MCV, MCH, MCHC, RDW, estGFR, GLUCOSE, UREA N, CREAT, SODIUM, POTASSIUM, CHLORIDE, TOTAL CO2, ANION GAP were within normal ranges. Nasal tissue results were pending. 

Upon discharge, her condition was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and she was ambulatory independent. Her discharge diagnosis included chronic sinusitis, bronchiectasis, and mechanical valves.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female with a complex medical history, presented with epistaxis (nosebleeding) which was more severe on the left side. This occurred after bilateral sinus surgery for recurrent sinusitis. She was on warfarin, which was switched to Lovenox by her cardiologist. She had nasal packing in the left nasal cavity in the emergency department (ED) and was sent home. However, she continued to have profuse epistaxis with clots. She was admitted under cardiology with a plan to anticoagulate with IV dextran and close observation until the epistaxis stopped. She denied lightheadedness, syncope, or chest pain but reported feeling cold. Initial vital signs in the ED were stable, but labs showed a drop in hematocrit from 38-40 to 31.9. She was started on a dextran drip, and since admission, she has not had any further epistaxis.

Her past medical history includes mild IgG deficiency, bronchiectasis, ADHD, migraine headaches, narrow angle glaucoma, depression, hemorrhagic ovarian cysts, left thumb dislocation requiring fusion, right iliac artery aneurism, SVT with cardiac ablation, vertebral compression fractures, pituitary adenoma, dural ectasia, multiple thyroid nodules, enlargement of the aortic arch, pelvic organ prolapse with hysterectomy, thoracic outlet syndrome, osteopenia, left wrist radial head fracture and carpal bone fractures, and GERD. She also had an ascending aortic aneurism with aortic valve insufficiency and underwent surgery with aortic and mitral valve replacements and an aortic conduit implant.

Her family history is significant for the same genetic disorder in her brother, father, sister, and nephews. There is also a history of lung cancer and Alzheimer's in the family.

On physical examination, she was alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. Her vitals were stable, and her physical exam was largely unremarkable. Admission labs showed a decrease in hematocrit and hemoglobin, and an increase in PTT. Discharge labs showed a further decrease in hematocrit and hemoglobin, and an increase in glucose. A chest X-ray showed increased prominence in the region of the left hilum, but the aortic arch region was unchanged.

During her hospital stay, she was taken off all anticoagulation and started on IV dextran. Her hematocrit dropped to 26.5, but she experienced no further epistaxis and her hematocrit stabilized around 28. She restarted aspirin and enoxaparin for bridging to warfarin. She also had soft blood pressures, so her home atenolol and losartan were held and then restarted. She had a low-grade fever with no clear source of infection. She also had chest and back pain, thought to be due to emotional stress and musculoskeletal issues. She was treated with oxycodone, quetiapine, and alprazolam as needed.

Her medications upon admission included Adderall, Albuterol, Aspirin, Atenolol, Clonazepam, Diphenoxylate-Atropine, Guaifenesin ER, Kariva, Losartan Potassium, Omeprazole, Trazodone, Warfarin, Sodium Chloride Nasal, Calcium with D, Magnesium Citrate, Vitamin B Complex, Acetaminophen, and Enoxaparin Sodium.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a postmenopausal woman, was admitted due to postmenopausal bleeding and a diagnosis of endometrial cancer. She underwent a laparoscopic hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, total pelvic lymphadenectomy, and periaortic lymph node resection. The patient had been experiencing several weeks of dark red blood staining but denied any symptoms suggestive of local disease extension. An ultrasound revealed an abnormal vascular polypoid lesion within the endometrium, measuring 2.6 cm in greatest dimension. A subsequent hysteroscopy, polypectomy, and dilation and curettage were performed, revealing a vascular polyp extending from the left uterine wall. Pathology revealed a low-grade endometrioid component (grade 1) and a separate but equal volume of high-grade clear cell carcinoma within the endometrial curettings.

The patient's past medical history includes hypertension, mitral valve prolapse, migraines, depression, and hypoglycemia. She has no history of asthma, thromboembolic disorder, diabetes, or cancer. She is up-to-date with mammograms, colonoscopies, and bone density evaluations. The patient has an allergy to aspirin. Her family history includes a brother with CML, a mother with pancreatic and renal cell carcinoma, an aunt with renal cell carcinoma, a father with lung cancer due to asbestos exposure, and several cousins with breast cancer.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be normocephalic, atraumatic, with no evidence of thrush, masses, adenopathy, spinal or CVA tenderness, palpable masses, hepato or splenomegaly, clubbing, cyanosis, or edema. Her cervix appeared normal and smaller than usual, with no visible mass. A bimanual exam revealed a mobile uterus with no parametrial nodularity.

Pre-operative MRI showed no evidence of metastatic disease or lymphadenopathy. The visualized portion of the thyroid, liver, gallbladder, spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, kidneys, small and large intestine, aorta, portal vein, IVC, bladder, and rectum appeared unremarkable. The uterus exhibited some areas of abnormal enhancement consistent with the patient's diagnosis of endometrial cancer. No aggressive-appearing lytic or sclerotic lesions were seen in the visualized portion of the skeleton.

During her hospital stay, the patient was maintained on her outpatient blood pressure medications and received subcutaneous Heparin three times daily for thrombosis prophylaxis. Her post-operative course was unremarkable. Upon admission, she was taking lisinopril, Actonel, fluticasone, and Calcium 500 + D (D3).
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted to the emergency department after being struck by a moped. She reported losing consciousness after the incident. Her past medical history includes hypertension, ovarian cancer, mitral valve prolapse, migraines, and depression. She has undergone a salpingo oophorectomy for ovarian cancer. She has allergies to Aspirin, Percocet, and Vicodin. 

Her family history includes a brother with chronic myeloid leukemia (CML), a mother with pancreatic cancer and renal cell carcinoma, an aunt with renal cell carcinoma, a father with lung cancer due to asbestos exposure, and a cousin with colon cancer. Several other cousins have had breast cancer.

Upon physical examination, she was found to have right scalp and forehead lacerations with scabbing, ecchymosis surrounding orbits bilaterally, and ecchymoses over the right chest. She had a right leg orthopedic cast in place and was able to move her toes. 

Lab results showed abnormal RBC count (2.71), Hgb (8.6), Hct (25.3), WBC count (14.8), Na (134), Cl (94), AnGap (9), Phos (1.7 and 2.4), and Calcium (8.0). Urine culture showed mixed bacterial flora consistent with skin and/or genital contamination. 

Imaging studies revealed no evidence of cervical spinal fracture or traumatic malalignment, mild degenerative changes, fractures of the right posterior second and third ribs, large right frontal scalp hematoma, smaller right parietal scalp soft tissue hematoma and laceration, no evidence of calvarial fracture or acute intracranial hemorrhage, a hypodensity in the right cerebellum with mild mass effect upon the fourth ventricle, right scapular, right mid clavicular, and multiple right rib fractures, a retroperitoneal mass to the left of the aortic bifurcation, subcentimeter hypodensities in the pancreatic body and tail, distended stomach with possible gastritis involving the antrum of the stomach, fractures of the right scapula, right mid clavicle, and multiple right-sided ribs, comminuted fractures of the distal tibia and fibula, mildly displaced medial malleolar fracture, and a right internal auditory canal mass suggestive of vestibular schwannoma. 

During her hospital stay, she remained stable from a cardiovascular and pulmonary standpoint, was able to tolerate a regular diet, and her pain was managed with a multi-medication approach including Tylenol, tramadol, and a lidocaine patch. She was discharged to rehab for further work with her right lower extremity. 

Upon admission, she was taking amlodipine, Reclast, Acetaminophen, calcium carbonate-vitamin D3, and Docusate Sodium.

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted to the orthopedics service due to a right distal tibia and fibula fracture. This injury was sustained earlier in the month when she was struck by a moped. She had been treated without surgery initially and was in rehab prior to this admission. She reported feeling better overall and was hopeful to be discharged from rehab soon. 

Her past medical history includes hypertension, ovarian cancer, mitral valve prolapse, migraines, and depression. She had previously undergone a salpingo-oophorectomy for ovarian cancer. She has allergies to Aspirin, Percocet, and Vicodin. 

The patient's family history is significant for various types of cancer. Her brother developed Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML). Her mother had pancreatic cancer and was recently diagnosed with renal cell carcinoma. The patient's aunt, her mother's sister, also had renal cell carcinoma. Her father had lung cancer due to asbestos exposure. A paternal cousin had colon cancer and several other cousins had breast cancer.

During her hospital stay, she underwent an Open Reduction and Internal Fixation (ORIF) of the right tibial fracture. Post-procedure, she was stable and recovered satisfactorily from anesthesia. She was initially given IV fluids and IV pain medications, which were later transitioned to a regular diet and oral medications. She was also given antibiotics and anticoagulation (Lovenox 30mg). Her home medication, amlodipine 5mg daily, was continued throughout her hospitalization. 

Upon discharge, her pain was well controlled with oral medications and she was non-weight bearing in the right lower extremity. She was prescribed Aspirin for DVT prophylaxis. Her discharge condition was stable with clear and coherent mental status. She was ambulatory but required assistance or aid. 

No abnormal lab or test results were mentioned in the clinical note.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to a left pelvic mass. She underwent an exploratory laparotomy and resection of the mass. The patient has a history of stage 1A endometrial carcinoma, hypertension, ovarian cancer, mitral valve prolapse, migraines, and depression. She had previously undergone a salpingo oophorectomy for ovarian cancer. 

The patient's family history is significant for various types of cancer. One of her brothers developed Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML). Her mother had pancreatic cancer and a recent diagnosis of renal cell carcinoma. The patient's aunt, her mother's sister, also had renal cell carcinoma. Her father had lung cancer due to exposure to asbestos, and her father's nephew had colon cancer. Several of her cousins had breast cancer.

Upon admission, the patient was on amlodipine 7.5 mg daily. During her hospital stay, she was closely monitored for signs of infection and bleeding, none of which were observed. She was initially kept NPO, but her diet was gradually advanced to a regular diet, which she tolerated well. She was encouraged to ambulate early and maintain good pulmonary hygiene. Her pain was managed with oral medication once she was able to tolerate a diet. 

The patient was alert and oriented throughout her hospitalization. Her vital signs remained stable, and she did not experience any adverse events during her surgery. At the time of discharge, she was afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and able to ambulate, void, and manage her pain without assistance. Her surgical wound was clean with a sterile dressing in place. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of an incidental left pelvic mass. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was instructed to call her doctor or return to the Emergency Department if she experienced any new or worsening symptoms, such as chest pain, cough, shortness of breath, vomiting, dehydration, blood in vomit or stool, burning during urination, blood in urine, discharge, unimproved or worsening pain, fever, or any other concerning symptoms. She was advised to resume all regular home medications and take any new medications as prescribed. She was also instructed on incision care and advised to avoid lifting heavy weights, driving, or operating heavy machinery while on pain medications.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a female who was admitted for the resection of an acoustic neuroma, which was incidentally discovered after she was struck by a moped. She has a history of hypertension, ovarian cancer, mitral valve prolapse, migraines, and depression. She has previously undergone a salpingo-oophorectomy for ovarian cancer. 

The patient's family history is significant for various types of cancer. Her brother developed chronic myeloid leukemia (CML), her mother had pancreatic cancer and renal cell carcinoma, her aunt also had renal cell carcinoma, her father had lung cancer due to asbestos exposure, and her father's nephew had colon cancer. Several of her cousins had breast cancer.

Upon discharge, the patient was alert and interactive, with clear and coherent mental status. She was ambulatory and independent. Her physical exam revealed a dense right facial droop from the forehead down, slightly slurred speech due to facial nerve palsy, and decreased hearing on the right side. Her sensation was intact to light touch and her incision was clean and dry with staples.

The patient underwent a right suboccipital craniotomy for the resection of the acoustic neuroma. Post-operatively, she developed a significant dense right facial droop, which was not present pre-operatively. She was started on a course of steroids for inflammation on the right cranial nerve. A post-operative non-contrast head CT showed no evidence of acute bleeding. She was evaluated by speech and swallow due to her facial droop and was cleared for a ground solids diet with thin liquids.

The patient's medications upon admission included Amlodipine 7.5mg PO daily PRN, Tylenol, Calcium PO QD, and Colace PRN. 

The patient was discharged home with services. Her discharge instructions included keeping her incision dry until the staples are removed, avoiding heavy lifting, running, climbing, or other strenuous exercise until her follow-up appointment, and not taking any blood thinning medication until cleared by the neurosurgeon. She was also advised to call her doctor for severe pain, swelling, redness or drainage from the incision site, fever greater than 101.5 degrees Fahrenheit, nausea and/or vomiting, extreme sleepiness, severe headaches not relieved by pain relievers, seizures, any new problems with her vision or ability to speak, or weakness or changes in sensation in her face, arms, or legs.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male with no significant past medical history, was transferred for possible renal infarct vs pyelonephritis. He had developed nausea, diarrhea, sweats, and left upper quadrant (LUQ) pain after eating pizza. Initial treatment with Pepto-Bismol and gingerale did not provide relief. A CT scan of his abdomen and pelvis showed renal infarct versus focal pyelonephritis, although urinalysis was negative. He received Toradol, ondansetron, and 1L IVF which resolved his pain. Upon arrival to the floor, the patient reported no symptoms and was feeling well.

The patient's past medical history includes bilateral hip replacements due to osteoarthritis. His family history is notable for his mother having pancreatic cancer, but there is no history of blood clots. 

Physical examination upon admission and discharge showed the patient to be in no acute distress, with normal cardiovascular, respiratory, abdominal, and neurological findings. 

Lab results showed an elevated white blood cell count (WBC-10.4*), elevated neutrophils (Neuts-73.7*), lymphocytes (Lymphs-15.8*), monocytes (Monos-9.4), and eosinophils (Eos-0.5*). His glucose level was also elevated (Glucose-116*), and LDH was high (LDH-281*). 

A second opinion CT of the torso showed striated hypoenhancement of the left renal interpolar region and lower pole, with differential diagnoses including focal pyelonephritis, renal infarct, or less likely malignancy such as lymphoma. A 9 mm lucency was noted in the right iliac crest without aggressive features, and a 4 mm right middle lobe nodule was also identified. 

A transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) showed normal left atrial size, normal left ventricular wall thickness, cavity size, and regional/global systolic function, and no source of cardiac embolism was identified. 

The patient's hospital course was uneventful, with no additional pain. The differential diagnosis for his renal infarct included A-fib, endocarditis, hypercoagulability, trauma to the renal artery, and connective tissue disorders. His elevated LDH was consistent with a renal infarct. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with a renal infarct and osteoarthritis status post bilateral hip replacements. He was advised to establish care with a primary care physician, schedule urology follow-up for further evaluation of renal infarct and possible hypercoagulable work-up, and follow-up with radiology in 3 months to evaluate for resolution of the kidney issue and the pulmonary nodule. The patient was not taking any preadmission medications and was discharged with a prescription for Simethicone for bloating.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a female with a history of Type A and Type B Dissection, hypertension, asthma, thyroiditis, hyperprolactinemia, loss of vision in the right eye, arthritis, and headaches. She has undergone several surgical procedures including D&C with left salpingo-oophorectomy, HSC polypectomy, and abdominal MMY. She was admitted due to a bilateral palmar rash and elbow rash that developed after taking her first dose of Bactrim for otitis externa. The pain was described as burning and did not subside with benadryl and calamine lotion, leading her to seek further evaluation. 

Upon evaluation, the joint pain was not believed to be septic, but possibly synovitis or an allergic reaction. The patient was given 125 mg methylprednisolone and Benadryl, as well as IV vancomycin for concern of cellulitis. Her vitals at the time of admission were T 98.1, HR 84, BP 181/72, RR 18, satting 100% RA. 

Her admission labs showed abnormal values for Hgb (11.7), Hct (34.7), Lymphs (16.9), Eos (8.3), PTT (35.6), ESR (25), and CRP (14.5). Wrist radiographs showed no abnormalities. 

During her hospital stay, her symptoms improved significantly after receiving vancomycin, prednisone, and benadryl. Her allergy list was updated to include Bactrim. Her hypertension was managed with her home dose of losartan, metoprolol, and triamterene-HCTZ. Her INR was 1.8 on admission and her home dose of coumadin was continued. Her thyroiditis was managed with her outpatient dose of levothyroxine and her GERD was managed with her home dose of ranitidine. Her depression was managed with her home dose of fluoxetine. 

Her medications upon admission included aspirin, atorvastatin, oxycodone-acetaminophen, fluoxetine, losartan, metoprolol tartrate, warfarin, triamterene-hydrochlorothiazid, albuterol sulfate, ranitidine HCl, and levothyroxine. 

Upon discharge, her primary diagnosis was Bactrim hypersensitivity and her secondary diagnoses included hypertension and thyroiditis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and ambulatory. She was instructed to take one more dose of prednisone the day after discharge and to continue taking benadryl until her symptoms resolve.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a female with a complex medical history, including aortic aneurysm with repair of Type A resection, endometriosis, borderline ovarian tumor post left salpingo-oophorectomy, hypertension, and Hashimoto's thyroiditis. She presented with two days of lower abdominal pain, which she described as periumbilical and radiating to the right lower quadrant. The pain was sharp and associated with some nausea, but no vomiting. She also experienced lower back pain and a reduction in appetite. She reported some clots in her urine and thought she might be on her period. She had no fevers, chills, diarrhea, bloody stools, sick contacts, or recent travels. 

Her past surgical history includes a Bentall procedure with coronary artery bypass grafting, dilation and curettage with left salpingo-oophorectomy, polypectomy, and aortic dissection repair. Her family history includes a cousin with aortic dissection and myocardial infarction, and uncles with myocardial infarctions. There is no family history of ovarian or breast cancer.

Upon physical examination, she was alert and oriented, with no acute distress. Her abdomen was soft, with tenderness at the mid-epigastric area and diffusely on the left and right lower quadrants, but no guarding or rebound tenderness. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for neutrophils (77.7), lymphocytes (14.3), white blood cell count (13.0), red blood cell count (4.14), hemoglobin (11.8), hematocrit (34.9), and lipase (61). Her total bilirubin was also elevated (2.2), with direct bilirubin at 0.5 and indirect bilirubin at 1.7. 

The patient's abdominal pain was evaluated with a CT scan, which showed no signs of embolic events, abdominal hernia, pancreatitis, or gallbladder pathology. Her anemia was thought to be dilutional, and the elevation in total bilirubin was attributed to shear stress from the aortic valve. 

Her medications upon admission included albuterol, amlodipine, atorvastatin, etonogestrel, fluoxetine, levothyroxine, cozaar, metoprolol, protonix, triamterene/HCTZ, coumadin, and calcium with vitamin D3.

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to sternal click and pain. She has a history of hypertension, asthma, Hashimoto's thyroiditis, hyperprolactinemia, arthritis, loss of vision in the right eye, borderline ovarian cancer post left salpingo-oophorectomy, endometriosis controlled with Implanon, and depression. She has undergone several surgical procedures including a Bentall procedure with CABG x 1, total arch replacement grafts to the LCCA and the innominate artery, coronary artery bypass grafting with reverse saphenous vein graft from the neo ascending aorta to the LAD, D&C with left salpingo-oophorectomy, polypectomy, and aortic dissection repair. 

She presented with chest pain radiating to the back and asymmetry of the upper extremity BPs. A CT showed Type A aortic dissection and other findings related to the aneurysm. She underwent emergency aortic root replacement with a Bentall procedure, total arch replacement, and coronary artery bypass grafting. She later developed symptoms of light-headedness, shortness of breath, nausea, and sharp back pain. A chest CT showed unchanged morphology of type A dissection with 2 false lumens. 

Her family history includes a cousin with aortic dissection and myocardial infarction, and uncles with myocardial infarctions. There is no family history of ovarian or breast cancer. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including aspirin, atorvastatin, fluoxetine, losartan, metoprolol tartrate, warfarin, triamterene-hydrochlorothiazid, albuterol sulfate, omeprazole, levothyroxine, isosorbide mononitrite, amlodipine, magnesium, and collagen-c. 

Abnormal lab results include RBC-4.08*, Hgb-11.3*, Hct-34.1*, RBC-3.94*, Hgb-10.9*, Hct-32.8*, PTT-66.4*, PTT-42.0*, and TotBili-1.7*. A radiology report showed a prominent descending thoracic aortic contour, compatible with known history of dissection. There are surgical clips at the right upper lung. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent sternal wire removal for costochondral cartilage separation/dislocation. Her hospitalization was prolonged due to waiting for her INR to become therapeutic. Her discharge diagnoses include sternal click post removal of sternal wires, ascending aorta dissection post Bentall and CABG, borderline left adnexal mass followed by GYN, hypertension, Hashimoto's thyroiditis, hyperprolactinemia, loss of vision in the right eye, arthritis, asthma, and pulmonary nodules.
