summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted for surgical management of her thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysm. She had previously undergone repair of type a dissection and had sternal wire removal. She was experiencing abdominal pain several times a week, which was managed with oxycodone. Her past medical history includes hypertension, asthma, thyroiditis, hyperprolactinemia, arthritis, loss of vision in the right eye, borderline ovarian cancer, endometriosis, and depression. She had a history of several surgical procedures including Bentall procedure with CABG x 1, total arch replacement grafts to the LCCA and the innominate artery, coronary artery bypass grafting, D&C with left salpingo-oophorectomy, polypectomy, and aortic dissection repair. 

The patient's family history includes a cousin with aortic dissection and myocardial infarction, and uncles with myocardial infarctions. There is no family history of ovarian or breast cancer. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including aspirin, levothyroxine, oxycodone-acetaminophen, atorvastatin, fluoxetine, losartan, docusate sodium, prilosec, magnesium oxide, triamterene-hydrochlorothiazide, metoprolol tartrate, amlodipine, albuterol inhaler, and coumadin (which was stopped). 

During her hospital stay, the patient underwent repair of a thoracoabdominal aneurysm. Postoperatively, she experienced transient delirium and hallucinations, thrombocytopenia (which resolved), nausea, elevated liver enzymes, and significant epistaxis. She was also diagnosed with a postoperative urinary tract infection. 

Abnormal lab results included elevated WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, RDW, PTT, glucose, ALT, AST, LD, AlkPhos, Amylase, TotBili, DirBili, IndBili, and lipase. Albumin, calcium, and phosphorus were low. 

Imaging studies showed normal left ventricular systolic function, mild dilation of the descending thoracic aorta, a bileaflet aortic valve prosthesis, and mild aortic regurgitation. An abdominal ultrasound showed no evidence for biliary ductal dilation or hepatic arterial compromise. An MRA showed status post thoracoabdominal aortic graft placement with grafting of the celiac artery, SMA, and renal arteries. There was slight mural irregularity at the sites of the anastomoses, however, all of these vessels remain patent. There was also slight heterogeneous perfusion on the left lateral segment of the liver, left-sided pleural effusion/seroma with associated atelectasis, slightly decreased enhancement of the medial left kidney, and proteinaceous or hemorrhagic left renal cysts. 

The patient was discharged in stable condition. Her discharge medications included aspirin, docusate sodium, pantoprazole, atorvastatin, levothyroxine, metoprolol tartrate, fluoxetine, losartan, oxycodone-acetaminophen, warfarin, sodium chloride aerosol, oxymetazoline aerosol, clindamycin HCl, and ciprofloxacin. She was instructed to follow up with ENT to have her nasal packing removed and her coumadin will be followed by her doctor.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a female who was admitted due to abdominal pain and a drop in hematocrit. She had previously undergone repair of a thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysm and was discharged a week prior to this admission. She returned to the ER complaining of incisional pain and a mass in the left upper quadrant, which she stated felt much harder than before. She also reported some vaginal bleeding, which had increased three days prior but had since decreased.

Her past medical history includes hypertension, asthma, thyroiditis, hyperprolactinemia, arthritis, loss of vision in the right eye, borderline ovarian cancer post left salpingo-oophorectomy, endometriosis controlled with Implanon, and depression. She has undergone several surgical procedures including aortic dissection repair, coronary artery bypass grafting, polypectomy, and laparoscopic lysis of adhesions.

The patient's family history includes a cousin with aortic dissection and myocardial infarction, and uncles with myocardial infarctions. There is no family history of ovarian or breast cancer.

Upon physical examination, a palpable mass of 10cm diameter was noted at the left costal margin, suspected to be a seroma. Her pulse was 73, respiratory rate was 16, and oxygen saturation was 100% on room air. Her blood pressure on the right was 136/49.

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.26), Hgb (9.1), Hct (27.8), Neuts (74.5), Lymphs (11.6), Eos (6.8), PTT (89.3), LD (262), AlkPhos (109), Amylase (132), and TotBili (2.6). Lipase was also elevated at 149.

Imaging results showed no evidence of leak about the descending aortic graft. There was increased simple fluid tracking around the descending aortic graft and increased moderate, simple-appearing left pleural effusion. The ascending aortic graft had a complex appearance with patent origins of bilateral coronary arteries and ascending aorta-to-left coronary arterial system bypass graft. Persistent dissection was noted in the implanted right brachiocephalic artery to the origin of the right common carotid artery and within the remaining native distal aortic arch. There was no acute intra- or retroperitoneal fluid to suggest hemorrhage.

During her hospital stay, the patient was admitted for abdominal pain and reported anemia. However, her hematocrit was 24.6 on arrival. She had increased vaginal bleeding after each heart surgery with significant heparinization. She was also on coumadin for a mechanical aortic valve. A scan did not show a new source of bleeding. She was transfused 2 units of PRBCs with a slow increase in hematocrit.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including aspirin, docusate sodium, pantoprazole, atorvastatin, levothyroxine, metoprolol tartrate, fluoxetine, losartan, oxycodone-acetaminophen, warfarin, sodium chloride aerosol, and oxymetazoline aerosol.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a middle-aged female with a history of type A aortic dissection, which was previously treated with aortic root replacement (Bentall), Arch repair with partial Vascutek graft, and SVG to LAD. She also underwent repair of a thoracoabdominal aneurysm with a Vascutek graft. She has a history of hypertension, asthma, thyroiditis, hyperprolactinemia, arthritis, loss of vision in the right eye, borderline ovarian cancer post left salpingo-oophorectomy, endometriosis controlled with Implanon, and depression. She has undergone several surgical procedures including Bentall procedure with CABG x 1, D&C with left salpingo-oophorectomy, polypectomy, and aortic dissection repair. 

The patient presented with substernal chest pressure radiating to her right scapular region, which she described as severe and associated with nausea and weakness. The pain was recurring and there was associated dizziness. Upon presentation to the ED, her systolic BP was 204, but quickly came down to the 140s with pain control. She underwent emergent CTA that did not show any change from her prior study. 

Her family history includes a cousin with aortic dissection and myocardial infarction, and uncles with myocardial infarctions. There is no family history of ovarian or breast cancer. 

Upon admission, her medications included Metoprolol Tartrate, Warfarin, Atorvastatin, Albuterol Inhaler, Levothyroxine Sodium, Omeprazole, and Triamterene-Hydrochlorothiazide. 

Her lab results showed cTropnT levels of less than 0.01 at three different times. CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed no significant abnormalities. Cardiac catheterization revealed single vessel proximal LMCA coronary artery disease, patent vein graft to LAD which also fills circumflex with no significant residual coronary lesions, patent renal arteries bilaterally, and systemic hypertension. 

During her hospital stay, acute aortic pathology, acute infarct, and ischemia from coronary artery disease were ruled out as possible etiologies of her pain. Her chest pain was more likely to have been the result of gastrointestinal or musculoskeletal etiology. Her hypertension was sub-optimally controlled on presentation and initial hospitalization. No adrenal nodules were present on CT imaging, and CTA indicated patent renal arteries though with partial right stenosis at the ostium with some post-stenotic dilation. However, this was reportedly unchanged from a recent scan. Additionally, renal artery catheterization showed patent renal artery grafts. By the time of discharge, her hypertension was well-controlled on her new regimen. 

Her chronic diagnoses include St. ___ valve, hypothyroidism, GERD, asthma, hyperlipidemia, and depression/anxiety. At discharge, her primary diagnosis was atypical chest pain and secondary diagnoses were hypertension and multiple aortic surgeries for aortic dissection & aneurysm.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of hypertension, asthma, thyroiditis, hyperprolactinemia, arthritis, vision loss in the right eye, borderline ovarian cancer post left salpingo-oophorectomy, endometriosis, and depression, was admitted due to lower back pain and weakness. She has a history of aortic dissection and has undergone a Bentall procedure with CABGx1, a total aortic root replacement with AVR, grafts to the LCCA and the innominate artery, and CABG with reverse saphenous vein graft from the neo ascending aorta to the LAD. She is on warfarin and has also had a retroperitoneal repair of a thoraco-abdominal aortic dissection and aneurysm. 

The patient presented with buttock pain radiating down both legs, difficulty ambulating, sudden onset mid/lower back pain, fever, chills, and a headache. She denies any bowel or bladder symptoms. She has had chronic leg numbness over the past few months which has not progressed. She also endorses a bifrontal/bitemporal throbbing headache associated with nausea, vomiting, and photophobia. She was recently hospitalized for atypical chest pain, but cardiac catheterization and stress tests were normal. 

In the ED, she was given vanc/cefepime/flagyl for an unclear source of infection, morphine and dilaudid for pain, and zofran/lorazepam for anxiety. Neuro was consulted given concern for cord compression, and did not find focal neurologic deficits. CT chest did not show new dissection or aneurysm. She was intubated for a repeat MRI (with fentanyl and midazolam). Neuro and Spine consultations cleared the patient for discharge, however she remained hypoxic several hours after extubation, and she was admitted to medicine for hypoxia. 

Lab results showed an abnormal PTT of 43.7, abnormal neutrophils of 78.1, abnormal lymphocytes of 11.9, and a low hemoglobin of 11.7. An MRI of the spine with contrast showed a 10 mm x 11 mm intradural extramedullary lesion at the L1 level, potentially partially calcified or with internal blood products. An MRI of the brain showed multiple foci of low signal in the susceptibility sequence which could be seen with multiple calcifications such as cysticercosis versus microhemorrhages versus multiple cavernomas. A CTA of the chest/abdomen/pelvis showed stable appearance of dissections involving the right brachiocephalic, descending thoracic aorta, and bilateral common iliac arteries.

The patient's family history includes a cousin with aortic dissection and myocardial infarction, and uncles with myocardial infarctions. There is no family history of ovarian or breast cancer. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Albuterol Inhaler, Levothyroxine Sodium, Losartan Potassium, Metoprolol Tartrate, Warfarin, Collagen Plus Vitamin C, and Calcium 500 With D.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of type A aortic dissection, hypertension, depression, and multiple abdominal procedures, was admitted due to right lower quadrant abdominal pain. The pain was described as sharp, starting periumbilically and then migrating to the right lower quadrant. It was constant and associated with anorexia and chills, but no fevers. The patient denied melena, hematochezia, urinary symptoms, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. She had previously undergone a Bentall procedure with CABGx1 (total aortic root replacement with AVR) and was on warfarin. She also had a retroperitoneal repair of Type B thoraco-abdominal aortic dissection and aneurysm. 

Upon admission, her vitals were stable and labs showed normal liver function tests, but an elevated white blood cell count of 15 and an INR of 1.9. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed cecal colitis, but the rest of the colon and appendix were normal. An ultrasound to evaluate the aorta was inconclusive. The patient was given morphine, dilaudid, cipro 400 IV, and flagyl 500 IV.

Her past medical history includes hypertension, asthma, thyroiditis, hyperprolactinemia, arthritis, loss of vision in the right eye, borderline ovarian cancer post left salpingo-oophorectomy, endometriosis controlled with Implanon, and depression. She has undergone several surgical procedures including aortic dissection repair, D&C with left salpingo-oophorectomy, polypectomy, and laparoscopic lysis of adhesions. 

The patient's family history includes a cousin with aortic dissection and myocardial infarction, and uncles with myocardial infarctions. There is no family history of ovarian or breast cancer.

On physical examination, the patient was alert and oriented, with tenderness to touch in the right lower quadrant. No other significant findings were noted. Her discharge condition was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and ambulatory independent.

Lab results showed an elevated white blood cell count of 15.3 and an elevated PTT of 41.0. A CT scan showed cecal colitis, unchanged aortic configuration and dissection flaps in the bilateral iliac arteries, cholelithiasis vs. gallbladder sludge, a moderate hiatal hernia, and a fibroid uterus. 

The patient's medications upon admission included albuterol, amlodipine, levothyroxine, losartan, metoprolol, aspirin, cyclobenzaprine, tramadol, warfarin, bupropion, and citalopram. 

The patient was diagnosed with cecal colitis. The differential diagnosis includes ischemic, inflammatory, and infectious causes. Given the patient's leukocytosis that resolved with antibiotics, infectious is likely but the lack of diarrhea is uncommon with bacterial or viral gastroenteritis. A colonoscopy will be needed to fully evaluate the etiology.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to dyspnea. She has a history of aortic dissections and has undergone a Bentall procedure, aortic root and arch replacement, and coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG). She reported a sudden onset of dyspnea, similar to previous episodes but not as severe, and not associated with chest pain, cough, fevers, chills, sputum production, or lower extremity swelling. She has recently been affected by the death of a cousin due to a heart attack. 

Her past medical history includes hypertension, asthma, borderline ovarian cancer post left salpingo-oophorectomy, endometriosis controlled with Implanon, hyperprolactinemia, arthritis, loss of vision in the right eye, depression, and a spinal intradural extramedullary lesion at L1 that has markedly decreased and is thought to be hemorrhagic. She has also undergone a polypectomy and abdominal myomectomy. 

Her family history includes a cousin with aortic dissection and myocardial infarction, and uncles with myocardial infarctions. There is no family history of ovarian or breast cancer. 

Upon admission, her medications included amlodipine, aspirin, levothyroxine sodium, losartan potassium, metoprolol tartrate, tramadol, warfarin, omeprazole, and an albuterol inhaler. 

Her physical exam was generally normal, with the exception of trace edema noted bilaterally. Her vitals were stable except for tachypnea. A CTA of the chest showed stable appearance of dissections involving the right brachiocephalic, origin of the left subclavian, and descending thoracic aorta. 

Lab results showed abnormal levels of neutrophils (73.3), lymphocytes (15.2), eosinophils (5.5), PTT (46.0 and 42.2), glucose (104), and cholesterol (218). Her LDL was also elevated at 143. 

During her hospital stay, it was determined that her dyspnea was likely due to anxiety in the setting of her cousin's recent death. Cardiac tests were performed but were all reassuring. Her blood pressure was slightly elevated, and she was found to have hyperlipidemia. Her warfarin dosage was increased due to a subtherapeutic INR. 

Upon discharge, her primary diagnosis was dyspnea likely due to anxiety. Her secondary diagnoses included aortic valve replacement and coronary artery disease status post coronary artery bypass grafting. Her discharge medications included albuterol, amlodipine, aspirin, levothyroxine sodium, losartan potassium, omeprazole, tramadol, warfarin, metoprolol tartrate, and atorvastatin.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to left wrist swelling. She has a history of coronary artery bypass graft (CABG), aortic valve replacement, aortic and abdominal aneurysm repair, and is on coumadin. She had been experiencing left hand pain and swelling for two weeks prior to admission. She reported severe left wrist pain and decreased range of motion, along with chills, nausea, and emesis. She denied any fevers, rashes, arthralgias or myalagias. Her initial vitals in the ED were stable and she was given morphine for pain, diphenhydramine, and started on a heparin drip. 

Her past medical history includes hypertension, asthma, borderline ovarian cancer, endometriosis, thyroiditis, hyperprolactinemia, arthritis, loss of vision in the right eye, and depression. She has also had a polypectomy and abdominal myomectomy. She has a family history of heart attacks, with a cousin and uncles having experienced myocardial infarctions. 

Upon admission, her physical exam showed mild distress but alert and oriented. She had moderate pain with active wrist extension limiting movement, and mild swelling in the wrist. Her vitals were stable. Her labs showed an elevated PTT of 150, neutrophils of 71.1, lymphocytes of 16.7, MCV of 80, MCH of 26.9, and a glucose level of 104. Her joint fluid analysis showed an elevated WBC of 1550, HCT of 18, and polys of 93. Her blood cultures were negative. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent a left wrist arthrocentesis. The results demonstrated an Hct of 18, which was consistent with hemarthrosis. She did not have an elevated WBC and there was no concern for septic arthritis. She did not have crystals in her joint and her uric acid level was within normal limits. She was placed in a brace and her pain was managed with Tylenol, Vidocin, and Morphine IV PRN. 

Her INR was sub-therapeutic at 1.4 upon admission. She was started on a heparin drip and was restarted on Warfarin 5mg. She was discharged on Lovenox with a goal INR of 2.5-3.5 for AVR. 

Upon discharge, her vitals were stable and her physical exam showed mild pain with active wrist extension limiting movement, and mild swelling in the wrist. Her discharge labs showed an elevated PTT of 80.1, MCV of 81, and a normal INR. Her discharge medications included Warfarin, Enoxaparin Sodium, Amlodipine, Aspirin, Hydrochlorothiazide, Levothyroxine Sodium, Losartan Potassium, Acetaminophen, Hydrocodone-Acetaminophen, Albuterol Inhaler, ascorbic acid-collagen, Calcium+D, Atorvastatin, Senna, and Docusate Sodium. 

She was advised to follow up with the HAND clinic and her primary care physician regarding management of coumadin and INR. Her discharge diagnoses included right wrist hemarthrosis, status post aortic valve repair, and hypercholesteremia.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female with a significant past medical history, was admitted due to supratherapeutic INR and 24-hour history of red, painful hands and epistaxis. Her past medical history includes thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysm with thoracic, ascending, and descending aortic replacement and mechanical AVR, hypertension, asthma, borderline ovarian cancer with left salpingo-oophorectomy, endometriosis managed with Implanon, Hashimoto's thyroiditis, hyperprolactinemia, arthritis, loss of vision in the right eye, depression, and a spinal intradural extramedullary lesion at L1. She had been experiencing an upper respiratory infection for 3 weeks prior to admission, with symptoms such as rhinorrhea, semi-productive cough, congestion, and headache. She also reported chills, decreased appetite, and decreased oral intake. 

Upon admission, her vitals were stable, but her INR was elevated to 10.4 with PTT 119.4. Her platelet count was 109. She was given 25mg IV benadryl, 5mg IV morphine x2, and 1L NS. She was admitted to the medical floor for further management. Her physical exam revealed erythematous, tender areas over her right hand, wrist, and several MCPs in her right hand, left thenar eminence, right elbow, and right groin. 

Her labs on admission showed an elevated Hct of 35.3, MCV of 79, and glucose of 103. Her creatinine increased from 0.8 on admission to 1.3 but subsequently resolved. Imaging showed no evidence of acute cardiopulmonary process but did reveal sinus disease in the left paranasal sinuses. 

The patient's family history includes a cousin with aortic dissection and myocardial infarction, uncles with myocardial infarctions, and a cousin who recently died of a heart attack. 

Her medications upon admission included Losartan Potassium, Metoprolol Tartrate, Warfarin, Amlodipine, Atorvastatin, Calcium Carbonate, Albuterol Sulfate, Citalopram, Hydrochlorothiazide, and Levothyroxine Sodium. 

During her hospital stay, her INR was rapidly reversed to < 2.0 with IV vitamin K. She was started back on warfarin and IV heparin bridge. She was also started on doxycycline for suspected bacterial sinusitis. Her warfarin dose was reduced due to expected interaction. Her discharge dose of warfarin was 1mg. 

Conditions ruled out during her stay include infarction, hemorrhage, edema, or mass in the brain, acute cardiopulmonary process, and ovarian or breast cancer in her family history.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to back pain and hypertension. She has a history of aortic dissection, for which she underwent a Bentall procedure, mechanical AVR, and CABG x1. She also had a thoracoabdominal aneurysm repair. She reported a two-day history of new onset upper back pain, different from the pain she experienced during her previous dissection. She had no other symptoms such as chest pain, abdominal pain, extremity pain, headache, dizziness, fevers, or chills. She was taking aspirin and Coumadin for her mechanical valve. Her blood pressure was found to be elevated during a routine appointment with her primary care physician, leading to her admission to the ED.

Her past medical history includes hypertension, asthma, borderline ovarian cancer post-LSO with a recent normal pelvic ultrasound, endometriosis controlled with Implanon, hyperprolactinemia, arthritis, loss of vision in the right eye, depression, and a spinal intradural extramedullary lesion at L1 that has markedly decreased and is thought to be hemorrhagic. She has a family history of dissection and myocardial infarction in a cousin and uncles, and a recent death due to a heart attack in another cousin. There is no family history of ovarian or breast cancer.

Upon physical examination, her vital signs were stable and she was breathing comfortably. Her sternotomy scar was well healed and she had palpable radial pulses bilaterally. Her abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender. There were no lesions on her extremities.

Her lab results showed abnormal MCHC (31.5), RDW (16.1), RDWSD (48.3), lymphs (15.3), and glucose (113) levels. Her potassium level was also low (3.2). 

During her hospital stay, her blood pressure was regulated in the ICU with an esmolol drip. Further review of scans showed that her thoracic aneurysm and the dissection are chronic. She was asymptomatic and her blood pressure was well controlled, with systolic <140 off the esmolol drip. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including acetaminophen PRN, amlodipine 10', aspirin 81', atorvastatin 40', citalopram 40', HCTZ 25', levothyroxine 112', metoprolol tartrate 50'', albuterol PRN, calcium-vitamin D, warfarin 1', and doxycycline 100'' prior to dental procedures. 

Upon discharge, her diagnosis was carotid, aortic, and iliac artery dissection. She was alert, interactive, and ambulatory. She was instructed to return to the emergency department if she developed worsening chest pain, felt lightheaded, had visual changes or signs of a stroke, or developed pain in her legs.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to shortness of breath. She has a history of chronic type B aortic dissection, aortic root and arch replacements, and coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG). She also has a history of hypertension, asthma, borderline ovarian cancer, endometriosis, hyperprolactinemia, arthritis, vision loss in the right eye, depression, and a spinal intradural extramedullary lesion at L1. She has undergone several procedures including a left salpingo-oophorectomy, a hysteroscopy with polypectomy, and an abdominal myomectomy. 

Upon admission, she presented with dyspnea on exertion and orthopnea. Initial vitals in the emergency department were notable for a temperature of 99.1, heart rate of 106, blood pressure of 132/53, respiratory rate of 26, and oxygen saturation of 96% on room air. Lab results showed a negative troponin, a creatinine level of 0.8, a B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP) level of 7290, and an international normalized ratio (INR) of 2.2. Her hemoglobin and hematocrit were slightly decreased from her baseline. Imaging studies showed no evidence of active bleeding from aortic dissection, bilateral pleural effusions, bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, and moderate hiatal hernia. Her chest x-ray was read as pulmonary edema. 

During her hospital stay, she was given intravenous fluids, ondansetron, furosemide, and morphine sulfate. She reported improvement in her breathing after receiving furosemide. She also complained of nausea and belching but denied any abdominal pain. 

Her family history is notable for a cousin with aortic dissection and myocardial infarction, and uncles with myocardial infarctions. She also had a cousin who recently passed away due to a heart attack. 

Upon discharge, her physical exam showed a well-developed, well-nourished woman with normal vital signs. Her cardiac exam was normal with a regular rate and rhythm, and no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Her lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally. Her abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended with no hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. Her extremities were warm and well perfused with no clubbing, cyanosis, or peripheral edema. 

Her discharge medications included enoxaparin, ferrous sulfate, furosemide, isosorbide mononitrate, lisinopril, metoprolol succinate, warfarin, acetaminophen, albuterol inhaler, aspirin, atorvastatin, calcium 500 + D, citalopram, doxycycline hyclate, and levothyroxine sodium. 

Her discharge diagnoses included a genetic connective tissue disorder, hypertension, and acute systolic congestive heart failure.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to right groin pain. She has a notable medical history of acute type A aortic dissection, for which she underwent an emergent Bentall procedure, aortic arch replacement, and coronary artery bypass graft (CABG). She also has a history of hypertension, asthma, borderline ovarian cancer post left salpingo-oophorectomy, endometriosis managed with Implanon, Hashimoto's thyroiditis, hyperprolactinemia, arthritis, vision loss in the right eye, depression, and a spinal intradural extramedullary lesion at L1. She had a recent hospitalization for shortness of breath, during which she was found to have an ejection fraction of 39% on transthoracic echocardiogram with mild global hypokinesis. A left heart catheterization showed a 65% stenosis at the proximal left main coronary artery with no back flow to the left circumflex artery from her graft, raising concerns for ischemia. However, a stress test did not show any fixed or reversible ischemia, so percutaneous coronary intervention of the left main coronary artery lesion was deferred.

Upon admission, the patient reported ongoing right groin pain radiating down her leg. An ultrasound revealed a 2.8 x 2.4 x 1.9 cm partially thrombosed right common femoral artery and a posterior 9 x 3.5 x 3 cm thrombosed pseudoaneurysm versus soft tissue hematoma. She was given oxycodone and morphine for pain management, but the morphine caused significant nausea. A computed tomography angiogram of the torso showed a 1.0 cm pseudoaneurysm arising from the right common femoral artery, with a surrounding 2.7 cm hypodense area that may be communicating but partially thrombosed or a separate hematoma. There was no significant change to the repaired type A dissection as well as residual areas of thoracic and abdominal aorta dissections. Confluent mediastinal and hilar lymphadenopathy, unchanged since prior study, suggestive of sarcoidosis was also noted.

The patient's family history includes a cousin with aortic dissection and myocardial infarction, uncles with myocardial infarctions, and a recent death of a cousin due to a heart attack. There is no family history of ovarian or breast cancer.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including aspirin, citalopram, levothyroxine sodium, albuterol inhaler, acetaminophen, warfarin, ferrous sulfate, furosemide, isosorbide mononitrate, lisinopril, metoprolol succinate XL, calcium 500 + D, doxycycline hyclate, and atorvastatin.

The patient's physical examination revealed a normal rate, irregular rhythm with frequent premature beats, clear lungs, and a tender mass overlying the femoral pulse in the right groin. Her vital signs were stable, and she was alert and oriented. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was ambulatory and independent at the time of discharge.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to back pain and acute onset abdominal pain. Her past medical history includes hypertension and asthma, and she has undergone two laparoscopic gynecological procedures. She is allergic to penicillins. Upon admission, she was taking Lisinopril 2.5 QD and an unspecified inhaler.

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with a type B dissection and was admitted to the Cardiovascular Intensive Care Unit (CVICU) for bed rest, pain control, and blood pressure control. Her blood pressure medications were adjusted during her stay. Multiple CT angiograms were performed, which showed stability in her condition. Her blood pressure was under control at the time of discharge.

Lab results showed abnormal levels of red blood cells (3.87), hemoglobin (10.9), and hematocrit (32.0). A CT scan of the abdomen revealed no significant change in the extension of the dissection flap, which originates at the level of the subclavian artery origin. The scan also showed prominent bilateral hilar lymph nodes, small bilateral pleural effusions with associated atelectasis, and two 6 mm nodules in the right upper and left lower lobes. There was mild aneurysmal dilatation of the suprarenal abdominal aorta, and a small hernia in the anterior abdominal wall. A chest X-ray showed a slightly larger cardiac silhouette and a new left pleural effusion accompanied by adjacent left basilar atelectasis.

The patient was discharged in stable condition and was instructed to follow up with her primary care physician and a pulmonologist for the lung nodule. She was also advised to have a follow-up CT angiogram in one month. The patient was discharged to home with service.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to chest pain and acute type A dissection. She has a known history of hypertension, asthma, Hashimoto's thyroiditis, hyperprolactinemia, loss of vision in the right eye, arthritis, and dizziness. She has also undergone a dilation and curettage with left salpingo-oophorectomy, HSC polypectomy, and abdominal MMY in the past. 

Upon admission, she was on several medications including Acyclovir, Albuterol PRN, Fluoxetine, Hydrocortisone, Amlodipine 5mg, Lipitor 40, HCTZ 25, Isosorbide 30, Labetolol 200mg po BID, and Losartan 50. She has allergies to Penicillins, Ethyl Alcohol, Erythromycin Base, and Latex Gloves. 

During her stay, she underwent aortic root replacement with a Bentall procedure, total arch replacement, and coronary artery bypass grafting. Postoperatively, she was taken to the intensive care unit with an open chest due to swelling and was transfused for postoperative anemia. She was later returned to the operating room for sternal washout and closure. 

Her physical examination showed a well-nourished female in mild distress with a pulse of 68, respiration rate of 12, and O2 saturation of 99%. Her blood pressure was unequal in both arms, with the right being 60 and the left being 146/69. She had a IV/VI holosystolic murmur. 

Lab results showed an elevated WBC count of 15.1 and a prolonged PTT of 37.5. Her ECHO showed a moderately dilated aortic root at the sinus level, mildly dilated ascending and descending aorta, and severe aortic regurgitation. Post bypass, her LVEF was globally depressed at 35% and mild RV hypokinesis was noted. A CTA revealed a new type A aortic dissection involving the aortic root and extending to the level of the descending thoracic aorta. 

Upon discharge, she was alert and oriented, with steady gait and sternal pain managed with Percocet as needed. She was advised to shower daily, avoid applying lotions or creams to the incisions, weigh herself daily, and avoid driving and lifting more than 10 pounds for a certain period.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to an ICD shock. He has a history of mechanical aortic valve replacement twice, dilated cardiomyopathy with an EF of less than 20%, and left bundle branch block (LBBB). He had a pacemaker/BiV-ICD placed and underwent successful DCCV for atrial tachycardia to sinus rhythm. There was a concern that the lead had migrated and was irritating the left ventricle. The patient denies dyspnea on exertion, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, and swelling. He reports being able to walk without limitation or symptoms.

His past medical history includes aortic regurgitation, dilated cardiomyopathy, left bundle branch block, post-op SVT for which amiodarone was started, hyperlipidemia, allergic rhinitis, and pneumonia. He denies a history of syncope. His family history is noncontributory.

Upon admission, the patient was alert, oriented, and in no acute distress. His physical exam was largely unremarkable, with trace pitting edema bilaterally up to mid shin being the only abnormal finding. His labs showed abnormal PTT values of 56.1, 53.0, 61.6, and 46.3. His EKG showed atrial pacing and ventricular pacing versus left bundle-branch block. His CXR showed a biventricular pacemaker and AICD with all 3 leads now located in their expected positions, stable moderate cardiomegaly, and associated left atrial enlargement.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent an ICD lead revision due to concerns of malpositioned leads. The procedure was completed without complications. His INR was supratherapeutic on admission at 4.6, which was reversed with PO vitamin K. He continued on his home dose of Coumadin post-procedure.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Amiodarone, Captopril, Digoxin, Furosemide, Metoprolol Tartrate, Potassium Chloride, Warfarin, Magnesium Oxide, and Ferrous Sulfate.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a male with a complex cardiac history, including non-ischemic dilated cardiomyopathy, aortic regurgitation, and a history of two aortic valve replacements (AVR). He presented with progressive dyspnea on exertion despite outpatient diuretics. He has also experienced worsening bilateral edema and weight gain. His outpatient doctors have increased his torsemide dose, but he reports minimal increase in urine output. He denies chest pain, palpitations, lightheadedness, dizziness, fevers, chills, cough, and sick contacts. 

His past medical history includes dilated cardiomyopathy, left bundle branch block, post-operative supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) for which amiodarone was started, hyperlipidemia, erectile dysfunction, allergic rhinitis, and cardiac catheterization. He has a history of aortic regurgitation and has undergone two aortic valve replacements. His family history includes congestive heart failure in his father and cancer in his mother. There is no other family history of cardiomyopathy, arrhythmia, premature coronary artery disease, or sudden cardiac death.

Upon admission, his medications included Sacubitril-Valsartan, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Digoxin, Warfarin, Ferrous Sulfate, Torsemide, Potassium Chloride, Cyanocobalamin, Multivitamins, and MagneBind 400. He has an allergy to Iodinated Contrast Media - IV Dye.

His physical examination revealed irregular rhythm, no murmurs, no crackles, wheezes or rhonchi in the lungs, and bilateral pitting edema up to the level of knees. His EKG showed a v paced rhythm, not meeting Sgarbossa criteria for MI. His labs were notable for proBNP 3929, Cr 1.0, INR 2.1. His 2D-Echocardiogram showed a severely dilated left ventricle, severe global hypokinesis, moderately dilated right ventricle with at least moderately decreased systolic function, and mild left and right atrial enlargement.

Abnormal lab results included Hgb-12.8*, MCHC-31.9*, PTT-99.3*, LD(LDH)-281*, proBNP-3929*, Phos-4.6*, TSH-14*, and Digoxin-1.8*. His UreaN-26* and Creat-1.3* were also elevated. 

Conditions and symptoms that have been ruled out include chest pain, palpitations, lightheadedness, dizziness, fevers, chills, cough, and sick contacts.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of severe aortic regurgitation, bioprosthetic aortic valve, mechanical aortic valve replacement, severe dilated non-ischemic cardiomyopathy, left bundle branch block, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation on Coumadin, non-obstructive coronary artery disease, and a recent hospital admission for heart failure exacerbation, presented to the clinic with increasing dyspnea, weight gain, and renal function. He reported worsening orthopnea and dyspnea on exertion over the past few days. He denied experiencing chest pain, light-headedness, dizziness, and palpitations. He was given 120mg IV Lasix and started on a Lasix drip at 20mg/hr. His vitals were recorded as T 97.6, BP 99 / 65, HR 103, RR 20, SpO2 95% RA. 

His past medical history includes hyperlipidemia, a catheterization procedure, two aortic valve replacements due to dehiscence of the first valve, atrial fibrillation, left bundle branch block, and a post-operative stroke requiring craniotomy. He also has erectile dysfunction. His family history includes his father having congestive heart failure and his mother dying of cancer. There are no heart issues reported for his brothers or his children, and no other family history of cardiomyopathy, arrhythmia, premature coronary artery disease, or sudden cardiac death.

Upon admission, the patient's labs showed INR 4.7, Cr 1.8, K 5.1, and proBNP. He was given 120mg IV Lasix and started on a Lasix drip at 20mg/hr. His physical exam showed a JVP of 16 cm, regularly irregular heart rhythm, no chest wall deformities or tenderness, soft and non-tender abdomen, 1+ pitting edema to mid shins bilaterally, and no significant skin lesions or rashes. 

Abnormal lab results included Hgb-12.7, Hct-39.9, MCHC-31.8, UreaN-44, Creat-1.8, AnGap-17, TotBili-3.2, proBNP-6062, Ret Aut-2.7, Abs Ret-0.11, LD(LDH)-363, UricAcd-8.8, Iron-30, Ferritn-415, TSH-13, T3-59, PSA-7.8, RBC-3.43, Hgb-9.4, Hct-29.0, PTT-96.4, UreaN-22, and VitB. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Amiodarone, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Potassium Chloride, Sacubitril-Valsartan, Torsemide, Warfarin, Aspirin, Cyanocobalamin, and Ferrous Sulfate.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to hypotension and blurry vision. He has a history of severe aortic regurgitation, which required mechanical aortic valve replacement twice, mitral valve ring repair for mitral regurgitation, severe dilated nonischemic cardiomyopathy, wide left bundle branch block, atrial fibrillation, and nonobstructive coronary artery disease. He was recently admitted for decompensated heart failure. He also has a history of hyperlipidemia and allergic rhinitis. His family history includes his father having congestive heart failure.

Upon admission, the patient was taking Lisinopril 5mg daily, Spironolactone 25mg daily, and Torsemide 80mg daily. He reported no chest pain, dyspnea, lightheadedness, fevers, or chills. His blurry vision had completely resolved. His weight was down to 196lbs from 197.8lbs the previous day. His creatinine level was 1.6, up from a recent baseline of 1.0. 

Physical examination revealed an irregular rhythm, no jugular venous distension, clear lungs, and palpable distal pulses. His blood pressure was 75/48. Admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.88), Hgb (10.5), Hct (32.9), MCHC (31.9), Neuts (74.2), Lymphs (14.0), PTT (39.4), Glucose (140), UreaN (43), Creat (2.6), HCO3 (20), AnGap (19), and Phos (4.9). His pO2 was also low at 36. 

Imaging showed multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules and nodular opacities, which could indicate septic emboli, nonspecific inflammatory opacities, or less likely metastatic disease. His liver appeared normal on ultrasound. 

During his hospital stay, his heart failure was managed with adjustments to his medication regimen to prevent hypovolemia. His creatinine level returned to baseline, and his anemia was managed with Ferrous Sulfate. His atrial fibrillation was managed with Warfarin and Amiodarone. 

Upon discharge, his diagnoses included acute on chronic systolic heart failure and hypovolemic shock, with secondary diagnoses of atrial fibrillation and acute kidney injury. His discharge medications included Enoxaparin Sodium 150 mg SC daily, Milrinone 0.2 mcg/kg/min IV drip infusion, Torsemide 20 mg PO daily, Amiodarone 200 mg PO daily, Aspirin 81 mg PO daily, Cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg PO daily, Ferrous Sulfate 325 mg PO daily, Lisinopril 5 mg PO daily, and Warfarin 5 mg PO daily. His discharge weight was 93.3 kg, creatinine was 0.8, INR was 1.9, and potassium was 4.0.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for hep bridging for a scheduled colonoscopy. His medical history includes severe aortic regurgitation with two aortic valve replacements, dilated cardiomyopathy with an ejection fraction of 20%, left bundle branch block, post-operative supraventricular tachycardia, hyperlipidemia, and allergic rhinitis. He also has a history of stroke requiring craniotomy and ICU admission. He has been on warfarin, which was last administered at a dose of 4mg. He was recently admitted for a heart failure exacerbation and was discharged on torsemide, which was later increased to 80mg daily. He was also started on spironolactone 25mg daily. 

During his stay, he underwent a colonoscopy where a single sessile 12 mm bleeding polyp of benign appearance was found in the descending colon and was completely removed. His chest X-ray showed a left chest wall biventricular AICD and an enlarged cardiac silhouette. 

His admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.87), Hgb (10.8), Hct (32.9), PTT (41.3), Glucose (119), UreaN (35), Creat (1.3), and AnGap (9). His PSA was also elevated at 8.5. His discharge labs showed similar abnormalities with RBC (3.75), Hgb (10.4), Hct (31.8), PTT (41.7), and Glucose (104). 

His family history includes his father having congestive heart failure. He has no family history of early MI, arrhythmia, cardiomyopathies, or sudden cardiac death. 

His medications upon admission included Milrinone, Torsemide, Amiodarone, Aspirin, Cyanocobalamin, Ferrous Sulfate, Lisinopril, Rosuvastatin Calcium, Spironolactone, and Warfarin. 

The patient was discharged with a primary diagnosis of chronic systolic heart failure with LVAD, routine screening colonoscopy, and atrial fibrillation. His secondary diagnosis was a mechanical heart valve. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory and independent. His discharge medications included Enoxaparin Sodium, Milrinone, Amiodarone, Aspirin, Cyanocobalamin, Ferrous Sulfate, Lisinopril, Rosuvastatin Calcium, Spironolactone, Torsemide, and Warfarin.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a male with a history of severe aortic regurgitation, having undergone mechanical aortic valve replacement twice, once for valve dehiscence and severe aortic insufficiency, and a mitral valve ring repair for mitral regurgitation. He also has severe dilated non-ischemic cardiomyopathy and is on home milrinone, undergoing evaluation for a heart transplant. He has a wide left bundle branch block, atrial fibrillation, and non-obstructive coronary artery disease. He presented to the clinic with three days of nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, and was found to be hypotensive. He had recently been discharged after an elective colonoscopy as part of his cardiac transplant workup. He had not been taking his torsemide due to the vomiting and diarrhea, but his other medications were continued. He was also receiving lovenox and bridging to warfarin after his colonoscopy. 

On arrival to the clinic, his heart rate was 82 bpm and his blood pressure was 62/Doppler. His labs were notable for sodium at 129, potassium at 4.0, creatinine at 7.0, BNP at 4728, white cell count at 9.2, hemoglobin at 10.9, and INR at 4.7. He denied any current nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, chest pain, or dyspnea. 

His family history includes his father having congestive heart failure in his later years, but no family history of early myocardial infarction, arrhythmia, cardiomyopathies, or sudden cardiac death. 

Upon admission, his physical exam was largely unremarkable, with no significant abnormalities noted. His discharge physical exam showed trace bilateral edema present in the lower left extremity. 

His admission labs showed abnormalities in red blood cell count (3.92), hemoglobin (10.9), hematocrit (32.3), urea nitrogen (42), creatinine (7.0), sodium (129), chloride (86), and proBNP (4728). His haptoglobin was less than 10, and his pO2 was 32. His stool and urine cultures were negative for any pathogens. His chest X-ray showed moderate cardiomegaly but no edema, effusion, or pneumothorax. His flexible sigmoidoscopy showed an ulcer at 40 cm with an endoclip, and stool throughout the colon. 

His medications upon admission included milrinone, aspirin, cyanocobalamin, amiodarone, ferrous sulfate, lisinopril, rosuvastatin calcium, spironolactone, torsemide, and warfarin. 

During his hospital stay, he was found to have hypotension likely due to hypovolemia from gastroenteritis and gastrointestinal bleeding. He received resuscitation with IV fluids and 1 unit of packed red blood cells. His blood pressure returned to normal and he was asymptomatic. His creatinine level, which was 7.0 on admission, improved to 1.0 upon discharge. His anemia was managed with a blood transfusion and his hemoglobin level on discharge was 8.6. His INR, which was supratherapeutic at 4.7 on admission, was managed and was 2.3 on the day of discharge. His heart failure was managed with adjustments to his medication regimen. His discharge diagnoses included gastrointestinal ulcer, gastroenteritis, hypotension, chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, and atrial fibrillation with a mechanical aortic valve.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a male with a complex medical history, including severe aortic regurgitation that required mechanical aortic valve replacement twice, severe dilated nonischemic cardiomyopathy, wide left bundle branch block status, atrial fibrillation, and nonobstructive coronary artery disease. His heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) has seen a decline in ejection fraction from 35% to 25%. He has had multiple recent admissions for volume overload, colonoscopy prep with subsequent readmission for bleeding, admission for recent prostate biopsy, and was admitted this time for diuresis and milrinone adjustment. 

The patient's cardiac history began when he was noted to have a murmur during a routine physical. He underwent a transthoracic echocardiogram which demonstrated severe aortic regurgitation. He underwent mechanical aortic valve replacement and did well for many years until he began to notice increased dyspnea and fatigue. An echocardiogram at that time demonstrated dehiscence of the valve, severe paravalvular aortic regurgitation, significant functional mitral regurgitation, and LVEF 25%. He underwent a repeat bioprosthetic aortic valve replacement with mitral valve ring repair. His postoperative course was complicated by stroke and increased intracranial pressure requiring craniotomy. He was continued on warfarin therapy long-term for thromboembolic prophylaxis.

The patient's symptoms progressed further to the point that he was almost exclusively sedentary. His LVEF had dropped to less than 20%. He had a left bundle branch block and was referred for CRT-D implantation. The procedure was complicated by atrial tachycardia requiring cardioversion. He also had early LV lead dislodgement requiring revision. He had improvement in functional capacity but did not have a significant reduction in LV size or significant increase in LVEF. 

The patient reported a significant decline in his exertional capacity over the previous 2 months, lower extremity swelling, and weight gain up to 230 pounds on his home scale. His medication was adjusted and he had vigorous diuresis on torsemide. He completed a cardiopulmonary exercise test which was notable for a peak VO2 of 14.3 mL/KG/minute and VE/VCO2 slope of 35. He also had a decrease in blood pressure toward the end of exercise. 

The patient's weight had increased up to 222 pounds on his home scale and he also began developing a couple weeks of progressive exertional dyspnea that progressed to dyspnea at rest and orthopnea. He was admitted to the hospital with decompensated heart failure. He was diuresed with a Lasix drip and received 1 dose of metolazone. His digoxin was discontinued and he was started on amiodarone with an oral load. His metoprolol was decreased slightly to 25 mg daily. His discharge medications included Entresto 49/51mg twice daily, metoprolol succinate 25 mg daily, amiodarone oral load followed by 200 mg daily, torsemide 80 mg daily, spironolactone 12.5 mg daily, potassium chloride 20 mEq twice daily, aspirin 81 mg daily, and warfarin.

The patient was admitted for acute decompensated heart failure to the inpatient service. He was diuresed with a Lasix drip. His beta-blocker was held due to concern for low output features and his Entresto was held due to relative hypotension. After some diuresis, he underwent a right heart catheterization which demonstrated elevated biventricular filling pressures (RA 11, PCWP 22) with a reduced cardiac index of 1.7 L/min/m². He was initiated on milrinone initially at 0.25 mcg/kg/min which was subsequently uptitrated to 0.375 mcg/kg/min. His diuretics were transitioned to torsemide 80 mg twice daily which was then reduced to 80 mg daily due to concern that he has been over diuresed. His weight at the time of discharge was 90.1 kg. His discharge regimen included milrinone 0.375, lisinopril 5 mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, torsemide 80 mg daily, amiodarone 200 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily, and warfarin.

The patient had admission again for blurry vision, and found to have SBPs in 60--70s. Given these findings the patient was directly admitted to the heart failure service. He appeared volume depleted. Home diuretics were held, PO fluids encouraged. Home milirone was initially held in the setting and restarted at reduced dose of 0.2mcg/kg/min. Torsemide was decreased to 40mg (80 mg home dose) and then to 20 mg daily prior to discharge. Lisinopril was changed to 2.5 mg BID (home dose 5mg daily) but then changed back to 5 mg daily on day of discharge. Weight on discharge was noted to be 93.3 kg. Spironolactone was discontinued.

TSH was noted to be elevated at time of admission, felt likely sick euthyroid with recommendation to repeat in weeks. PSA was also noted to be elevated at 7.8 with further testing recommended as an outpatient.

The patient was admitted for UFH drip as bridge for colonoscopy as part of the pre-transplant evaluation. During the colonoscopy prep it was noted that he had blood in his stool on the day of the colonoscopy. He was found to have a single sessile 12 mm bleeding polyp of benign appearance in the descending colon. Pathology reported it as a tubular adenoma. The polyp was removed with recommendations for repeat colonoscopy in years per GI.

The patient was readmitted with significant hypotension and severe dehydration in the setting of a 3 day GI illness (vomiting/diarrhea) despite holding his diuretics. He also was found to have bleeding from recent polypectomy site (INR supratherapeutic as well most likely due to poor po intake due to GI illness). He was admitted to the CCU where his cardiac medications were held (SBP of 70 and creatinine of 7), he was fluid resuscitated and received 1 unit of PRBC's due to GI bleeding. A flexible sigmoidoscopy was performed once he was hemodynamically stable and found to have an ulcer at the polypectomy site which was cauterized. He was discharged on milrinone 0.2 mcg/kg, lisinopril reduced to 2.5 mg and spironolactone reduced to 12.5 mg daily. His torsemide was left at 100 mg daily.

The patient underwent a successful prostate biopsy with Dr. ___ on ___ and the results of that biopsy are pending. He also went to ___ on ___ for his initial pretransplant evaluation. Apparently he was exposed to someone possible influenza during that visit and was advised
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a male with a complex cardiac history, including severe aortic regurgitation, dilated non-ischemic cardiomyopathy, left bundle branch block, atrial fibrillation, and non-obstructive coronary artery disease. He has undergone two aortic valve replacements and has a history of stroke and increased intracranial pressure requiring craniotomy. He is on home milrinone and has had multiple admissions for volume overload and diuresis. He also has a history of prostate cancer and was admitted for a right heart catheterization and a scheduled prostatectomy. 

The patient's family history includes congestive heart failure in his father and cancer in his mother. There is no family history of cardiomyopathy, arrhythmia, premature coronary artery disease, or sudden cardiac death. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including milrinone, amiodarone, aspirin, cyanocobalamin, ferrous sulfate, multivitamins, potassium chloride, rosuvastatin calcium, spironolactone, warfarin, valsartan, and torsemide. He was also started on Tamiflu prophylaxis due to exposure to the flu from a family member. 

The patient underwent a prostatectomy and right heart catheterization during his stay. His physical exams showed irregularly irregular rhythm, a systolic murmur, and trace edema in the left ankle. His admission labs showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, Hct, Glucose, and UreaN levels. His discharge labs showed a decrease in RBC, Hgb, and Hct, and an increase in PTT. 

The patient's hospital course included management in the CCU where he received diuresis and further medication optimization. His home milrinone was decreased due to low MAPs and his home valsartan and spironolactone were held for low SVRs. He was also bridged with lovenox due to his planned procedure and atrial fibrillation. 

The patient was diagnosed with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, atrial fibrillation, and prostate adenocarcinoma. He also has right carotid atherosclerosis, frequent premature ventricular contractions, and iron deficiency anemia. 

Upon discharge, the patient was prescribed oxybutynin, oxycodone, polyethylene glycol, senna, milrinone, amiodarone, aspirin, cyanocobalamin, enoxaparin sodium, ferrous sulfate, multivitamins, potassium chloride, rosuvastatin calcium, torsemide, and warfarin. His spironolactone and valsartan were held and he was instructed not to restart them until following up with his cardiologist. He was also instructed to complete a Lovenox to warfarin bridge until his INR is therapeutic. 

The patient was discharged home with service and was instructed to follow up with his primary care physician, cardiologist, and urologist.

summary:
The patient is a male with a complex medical history, including severe aortic regurgitation, mechanical aortic valve replacement, and redo mechanical aortic valve due to valve dehiscence and severe AI. He also has a history of mitral valve ring repair for functional MR, severe dilated nonischemic cardiomyopathy (LVEF 10%), left bundle branch block, CRT-D, atrial fibrillation, and nonobstructive coronary disease. He has a history of aortic regurgitation, dilated cardiomyopathy, left bundle branch block, hyperlipidemia, erectile dysfunction, allergic rhinitis, and cardiac catheterization. He has undergone mechanical aortic valve replacement for aortic regurgitation and recurrent severe AI with LVEF 25%, and mitral valve ring repair due to significant functional mitral regurgitation. His family history includes congestive heart failure in his father and cancer in his mother. 

The patient was admitted for right heart catheterization and a plan for left ventricular assist device (LVAD) placement. He underwent a redo sternotomy, redo aortic valve replacement with a 25 mm mosaic tissue valve, HeartMate 3 LVAD implantation, and coverage of ascending aorta with CorMatrix. He also had a percutaneous tracheostomy tube placement and a bedside pericardiocentesis. 

His hospital stay was complicated by profound hypotension/vasoplegia requiring pressors, respiratory failure requiring tracheostomy placement, and septic shock due to Proteus bacteremia/ventilator-associated pneumonia requiring IV antibiotics. His LVAD functioned well after implant. He also had an acute hemoglobin drop suspected to be a GI bleed, which resolved and remained stable. He was able to tolerate increased diet and was transitioned off tube feeds. 

His labs upon admission showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, Hct, UreaN, Creat, and proBNP. His discharge labs showed abnormal WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCHC, RDW, RDWSD, Neuts, Lymphs, Monos, Myelos, NRBC, AbsNeut, AbsMono, and glucose. 

His imaging studies showed severe global left ventricular hypokinesis, severe global right ventricular free wall hypokinesis, a bioprosthetic aortic valve prosthesis, a mitral valve annuloplasty ring, a moderate pericardial effusion, and a severely dilated left ventricular cavity. His CT scans showed severe cardiomegaly, diffuse ground-glass/nodular lung opacity at the lung apices, consolidative opacity at the left lung base, a small right pleural effusion, and a small nonhemorrhagic pericardial effusion. His ultrasound showed a distended gallbladder without other findings of acute cholecystitis, and no acute intra-abdominal findings. 

The patient was discharged to extended care for rehabilitation. His discharge medications included Acetaminophen, Albuterol Inhaler, HydrALAZINE, Levothyroxine Sodium, Mexiletine, Thiamine, Aspirin, Spironolactone, Torsemide, Amiodarone, Ferrous Sulfate, Multivitamins, Rosuvastatin Calcium, and Valsartan (held). His discharge diagnoses were severe dilated non-ischemic cardiomyopathy, LVAD placement, and hypoxic respiratory failure.

summary:

His medical history includes severe aortic regurgitation, mechanical aortic valve replacement, redo mechanical aortic valve for valve dehiscence and severe AI, MV ring repair for functional MR, severe dilated nonischemic cardiomyopathy (LVEF 10%), LBBB, CRT-D, afib, and nonobstructive coronary disease. His surgical history includes mechanical aortic valve replacement, mitral valve ring repair, laparoscopic radical prostatectomy, and HeartMate 3 LVAD placement. 

During his stay, the patient presented with symptoms of a GI bleed due to a supratherapeutic INR. He also reported feeling much better after receiving two units of blood, improved lightheadedness, and left quadrant abdominal pain. His physical exam revealed tenderness in the left quadrants, no rebound tenderness, and no edema. 

His lab results showed abnormal levels of WBC, RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCHC, RDW, RDWSD, PTT, UreaN, Cl, Albumin, and Phos. Imaging revealed findings compatible with gangrenous acute cholecystitis, left greater than right bibasilar atelectasis, trace left greater than right pleural effusions, and new gallbladder hemorrhage without evidence of active extravasation. 

The patient's hospital course was complicated by acute cholecystitis, acute on chronic blood loss anemia, HFrEF, leukocytosis, hypothyroidism, and malnutrition. He was treated with multiple medications including Amoxicillin-Clavulanic Acid, Pantoprazole, Octreotide Acetate, Neutra-Phos, Acetaminophen, OxyCODONE, Torsemide, Mexiletine, Spironolactone, Amiodarone, Ferrous Sulfate, Levothyroxine Sodium, Multivitamins W/minerals, Rosuvastatin Calcium, and Thiamine. 

The patient's family history includes his father having congestive heart failure and his mother dying of cancer. There is no family history of cardiomyopathy, arrhythmia, premature coronary artery disease, or sudden cardiac death. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with acute cholecystitis, acute on chronic blood loss anemia, HFrEF with LVAD placement, atrial fibrillation, hypothyroidism, and right carotid atherosclerosis. His discharge medications included Acetaminophen, Amoxicillin-Clavulanic Acid, Neutra-Phos, Octreotide Acetate, OxyCODONE, Pantoprazole, Torsemide, Mexiletine, Spironolactone, Amiodarone, Ferrous Sulfate, Levothyroxine Sodium, Multivitamins W/minerals, Rosuvastatin Calcium, and Thiamine. He was advised to follow up with his doctors and to take his medications as prescribed.

summary:
The patient is a male with a significant past medical history including severe aortic regurgitation with two mechanical aortic valve replacements, severe dilated nonischemic cardiomyopathy, left bundle branch block, atrial fibrillation, and nonobstructive coronary artery disease. He also has a history of prostate cancer, hyperlipidemia, and erectile dysfunction. He has had a cardiac catheterization showing mild irregularities in the left anterior descending artery, left circumflex artery, and right coronary artery. He has a HeartMate 3 left ventricular assist device (LVAD) and a redo aortic valve replacement with a 25 mm Mosaic tissue valve. 

The patient presented with shortness of breath upon waking from sleep. He reported compliance with all medications, but his torsemide was recently decreased from 100mg to 80mg. He had a decrease in urine output and a weight gain of approximately 2 pounds. He denied chest pain, nausea, vomiting, dizziness, and palpitations. His LVAD had not had any alarms recently. 

Upon examination, he was hypoxic on 4L nasal cannula. His lungs had bibasilar crackles and his abdomen was nontender with a percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (PTBD) in place draining clear green fluid. His labs were notable for a hemoglobin of 7.7 (down from 9.1 the previous week), a white blood cell count of 11.9, and a platelet count of 212. His blood urea nitrogen was elevated at 26. His pro-B-type natriuretic peptide (proBNP) was also elevated at 1548. His chest x-ray showed a stable marked enlargement of the cardiac silhouette and central pulmonary vascular congestion. 

The patient reported no symptoms upon transfer to the floor. He had a melenic bowel movement and given his acute drop in hemoglobin and known arteriovenous malformation (AVM), there was concern for an upper gastrointestinal bleed. He was recently hospitalized for a gastrointestinal bleed in the setting of a supratherapeutic international normalized ratio (INR), and during this hospitalization, he developed acute cholecystitis and had a percutaneous cholecystostomy tube placed. 

His family history is notable for congestive heart failure in his father and cancer in his mother. There is no family history of cardiomyopathy, arrhythmia, premature coronary artery disease, or sudden cardiac death. 

Upon discharge, his hemoglobin was 7.9, his INR was 2.9, and his creatinine was 0.8. His discharge weight was 84.7kg. His discharge diagnoses included acute on chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), dilated nonischemic cardiomyopathy, upper gastrointestinal bleed, normocytic anemia, atrial fibrillation, mechanical aortic valve replacement, and cholecystitis. His secondary diagnoses included hyperlipidemia and hypothyroidism.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to shortness of breath and episodes of large volume bright red blood per rectum (BRBPR) over the past 16 hours. His past medical history is significant for severe aortic regurgitation with mechanical aortic valve replacement (AVR) and redo mechanical aortic valve replacement for valve dehiscence and severe aortic insufficiency. He also has a history of severe dilated nonischemic cardiomyopathy, wide left bundle branch block (LBBB), atrial fibrillation, and nonobstructive coronary artery disease. He has a HeartMate 3 left ventricular assist device (LVAD) and a redo AVR with a 25 mm Mosaic tissue valve. He was recently admitted for acute decompensated heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (ADHFrEF) and melena. He was discharged with warfarin 2.5mg daily, a reduced dose from admission. His discharge INR was 2.9. Repeat INR was 3.5 and dose was decreased to 1mg. 

On the evening prior to admission, he experienced several episodes of maroon colored stools. He had new dyspnea on exertion. He experienced one episode of vomiting with cough, but no other coughing, chest discomfort, lightheadedness, palpitations, abdominal pain, fevers, or chills. He does report rash on his arm that was potentially related to a new fabric pillow. He was also recently hospitalized for GI Bleed in setting of supratherapeutic INR, discharged. Diagnostic work up during this hospitalization with evidence of numerous arteriovenous malformations (AVMs). During this hospitalization he developed acute cholecystitis and had a percutaneous cholecystostomy tube placed, took multiple antibiotics including zosyn and linezolid and ultimately discharged on augmentin 875/125 to continue until chole tube is removed.

Initial vitals in the ED were: T 98.5, HR 90, BP 60 MAP, RR 23, 100% RA. Labs were notable for Hgb 7.1, Hct 22.9, WBC 14.8, INR 4.7, BUN 34, Cr 1.1, proBNP 1567, lactate 2.9. Patient was given IV pantoprazole 40mg, IV octreotride 50mcg/hr gtt, 250 cc NS, and 1U pRBC. 

His family history includes his father having congestive heart failure and his mother dying of cancer. There is no other family history of cardiomyopathy, arrhythmia, premature coronary artery disease, or sudden cardiac death. 

Upon discharge, the patient's primary diagnoses were upper GI bleed, normocytic anemia, acute on chronic HFrEF, dilated nonischemic cardiomyopathy, atrial fibrillation, mechanical AVR, hypokalemia, and cholecystitis. His secondary diagnoses were hyperlipidemia and hypothyroidism. His discharge medications included potassium chloride, spironolactone, warfarin, amiodarone, amoxicillin-clavulanic acid, cyanocobalamin, ferrous sulfate, levothyroxine sodium, mexiletine, multivitamins, octreotide acetate, pantoprazole, rosuvastatin calcium, torsemide, and vitamin B complex. Losartan potassium was held.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a male with a significant past medical history including severe dilated nonischemic cardiomyopathy, severe aortic regurgitation with mechanical aortic valve replacement (AVR) twice, mitral valve ring repair for functional mitral regurgitation, wide left bundle branch block, atrial fibrillation, nonobstructive coronary artery disease, and acute cholecystitis. He has a HeartMate 3 left ventricular assist device (LVAD) and a cardiac resynchronization therapy defibrillator (CRT-D) in place. He also has a history of erectile dysfunction, allergic rhinitis, hyperlipidemia, and prostate cancer. His family history includes congestive heart failure in his father and cancer in his mother. 

The patient presented with a dislodged percutaneous cholecystostomy drain tube. He reported that the tube may have gotten caught on his wheelchair the day prior to presentation and had fallen out overnight. He initially noticed biliary drainage from the site, but this resolved. He reported no abdominal pain or fevers/chills, but did have one episode of nausea and vomiting. 

Upon admission, his vital signs were stable. Physical examination revealed a well-developed, well-nourished male in no acute distress. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended. The percutaneous cholecystostomy site on his right abdomen appeared clean, dry, and intact with no erythema or drainage. 

Lab results showed an elevated white blood cell count of 14.0, low red blood cell count of 3.32, low hemoglobin of 8.5, low hematocrit of 28.3, low mean corpuscular hemoglobin of 25.6, and high red cell distribution width of 16.1. 

An attempt was made to replace the percutaneous cholecystostomy drain, but this was unsuccessful due to the gallbladder compressing readily and a substantial amount of resistance encountered at the gallbladder wall, likely from scarring and fibrosis related to prior cholecystitis. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's INR was found to be subtherapeutic at 1.3. He was bridged with a heparin drip, and his home warfarin dose was increased to 2mg daily. His discharge INR was 1.5. 

The patient was discharged home with services. His discharge diagnoses included chronic cholecystitis, systolic heart failure, and subtherapeutic INR. His discharge medications included warfarin, amiodarone, amoxicillin-clavulanic acid, cyanocobalamin, ferrous sulfate, levothyroxine sodium, mexiletine, multivitamins, octreotide acetate, pantoprazole, potassium chloride, rosuvastatin calcium, spironolactone, torsemide, and vitamin B complex. 

The patient was instructed to follow up with his surgeon for future planning of cholecystectomy and to have a repeat INR drawn to monitor his warfarin level. He was also advised to weigh himself every morning and to call his doctor if his weight increases by more than 3 lbs.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of non-ischemic dilated cardiomyopathy and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), was admitted due to drainage from the driveline site and surrounding skin irritation. He has a Heartmate III LVAD implanted and a CRT-D. He has undergone mechanical aortic valve replacement (AVR) twice due to valve dehiscence, with the second replacement occurring during the same procedure as the LVAD implant. He also has a history of atrial fibrillation and non-obstructive coronary artery disease. 

The patient was recently admitted for an incidentally displaced percutaneous cholecystostomy tube, which was placed to treat acute cholecystitis. The drain was accidentally pulled and fell out completely, leading to a small amount of biliary drainage from the site. The patient was admitted for replacement, but the procedure was unsuccessful. He was transitioned back to Augmentin and was planned to follow-up with surgery as an outpatient for consideration of cholecystectomy. 

The patient's past medical history also includes aortic regurgitation, dilated cardiomyopathy, left bundle branch block, post-op SVT for which amiodarone was started, hyperlipidemia, erectile dysfunction, allergic rhinitis, cardiac catheterization, CRT-D insertion complicated by atrial tachycardia requiring cardioversion, CS lead dislodgement and subsequent inappropriate shock, and prostate cancer. His family history includes his father having congestive heart failure and his mother dying of cancer. There is no family history of cardiomyopathy, arrhythmia, premature coronary artery disease, or sudden cardiac death.

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Amiodarone, Amoxicillin-Clavulanic Acid, Cyanocobalamin, Ferrous Sulfate, Levothyroxine Sodium, Mexiletine, Multivitamins, Octreotide Acetate, Pantoprazole, Rosuvastatin Calcium, Spironolactone, Torsemide, Vitamin B Complex, Warfarin, and Potassium Chloride.

Abnormal lab results upon admission included RBC-4.20*, Hgb-10.8*, Hct-34.8*, MCH-25.7*, MCHC-31.0*, Neuts-76.2*, Lymphs-9.2*, and AlkPhos-134*. A swab from the driveline site showed polymorphonuclear leukocytes, gram-negative rods, and gram-positive cocci in pairs. A CT scan showed skin thickening and minimal stranding in subcutaneous fat at the site of entry of the line, but no evidence for inflammatory change involving the abdominal wall or the intra-abdominal or thoracic course of the line. 

During the hospital stay, the patient had one run of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia (NSVT) lasting 7 beats, for which he was continued on amiodarone and mexiletine and monitored on tele. His discharge diagnosis included driveline site drainage, heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, non-sustained ventricular tachycardia, and a history of cholecystitis.
