summary:
The patient is a male with a complex medical history, including severe aortic regurgitation that required mechanical aortic valve replacement, severe dilated nonischemic cardiomyopathy with an ejection fraction of 15%, wide left bundle branch block, atrial fibrillation, and nonobstructive coronary artery disease. He has undergone multiple procedures, including a HeartMate 3 LVAD implant and a redo procedure with a 25 mm Mosaic tissue valve. He has also had a laparoscopic cholecystectomy. 

The patient has had a significantly complicated medical history over the past year, with six hospitalizations. These included a postoperative course following Heartmate device placement, which involved hypotension, respiratory failure, prolonged intubation, and a GI bleed. He was also readmitted for recurrent GI bleeds, heart failure exacerbation, and concerns for driveline infection. 

Upon admission, the patient was experiencing dyspnea on exertion and volume overload. He had developed a fever and abdominal pain around the driveline exit site. He also reported increased lower extremity edema. His physical exam revealed possible bibasilar crackles, tenderness over the left upper quadrant/epigastrium around the superior edge of driveline dressings, and 1+ edema to midshins bilaterally. 

Lab results showed several abnormalities, including a lactate level of 2.6, a urea nitrogen level of 31, a creatinine level of 1.3, an LDH level of 281, a proBNP level of 7308, a WBC count of 20.3, an RBC count of 3.30, a hemoglobin level of 8.7, a hematocrit level of 28.7, a MCHC level of 30.3, and abnormal neutrophil, lymphocyte, and eosinophil counts. 

Imaging studies showed increased consolidation at the left lung base, suggestive of an infectious process, and increased consolidation at the right lung base, likely representing atelectasis. Trace bilateral pleural effusions were also noted. The patient's urinary bladder wall was mildly thickened, which may represent cystitis. 

The patient's hospital course involved treatment for pneumonia, heart failure exacerbation, and cholecystitis. He underwent cholecystectomy, which was complicated by significant nausea and vomiting post-procedure, as well as multiple episodes of bleeding from the laparoscopic site. His warfarin was held and he was bridged with heparin for the surgery. He was discharged on a regimen of medications including warfarin, amiodarone, clobetasol propionate, cyanocobalamin, ferrous sulfate, levothyroxine, metolazone, mexiletine, multivitamins, octreotide acetate, pantoprazole, potassium chloride, rosuvastatin, spironolactone, and torsemide. 

The patient's family history is notable for congestive heart failure in his father and cancer in his mother. There is no family history of cardiomyopathy, arrhythmia, premature coronary artery disease, or sudden cardiac death.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a male with a complex cardiac history, including severe aortic regurgitation requiring mechanical aortic valve replacement, redo mechanical aortic valve replacement for valve dehiscence and severe AI, mitral valve ring repair for significant functional mitral regurgitation, severe dilated nonischemic cardiomyopathy with EF 15%, wide left bundle branch block, atrial fibrillation, and nonobstructive coronary artery disease. He has a HeartMate 3 LVAD and a 25 mm Mosaic tissue valve. He also has a history of erectile dysfunction and prostate cancer. He recently underwent a laparoscopic cholecystectomy due to concerns of cholecystitis being a possible infectious source. 

The patient presented with purulent drainage from his drive line site and post-operative hematoma in the area of the gallbladder. He reported increased dyspnea on exertion, fatigue, and continued RUQ abdominal pain. Cultures from the drive line grew staph aureus. He denied any fevers, chills, chest pain, N/V, dysuria, or cough. 

His family history includes his father having congestive heart failure and his mother dying of cancer. There are no heart issues for his brothers or his children and no other family history of cardiomyopathy, arrhythmia, premature coronary artery disease, or sudden cardiac death. 

Upon admission, his labs showed abnormal values for RBC (2.72), Hgb (7.0), Hct (23.0), MCH (25.7), MCHC (30.4), Neuts (75.7), Lymphs (10.8), AbsNeut (6.34), AbsLymp (0.90), Glucose (127), UreaN (25), and Creat (1.3). His discharge labs showed abnormal values for WBC (10.9), RBC (3.50), Hgb (9.2), Hct (30.1), MCHC (30.6), Neuts (72.6), Lymphs (9.0), AbsNeut (7.74), AbsLymp (0.96), AbsMono (1.07), AbsEos (0.73), and HAV Ab (POS). 

A CT scan of the abdomen without contrast showed a postoperative hematoma within the gallbladder fossa measuring 5.5 x 3.4 x 3.0 cm, marked cardiomegaly with LVAD in place, and mild ground-glass opacity in the lower lungs concerning for mild edema. A chest X-ray showed no focal consolidation or evidence of pulmonary edema. 

The patient's wound culture was positive for Staph Aureus Coag + with moderate growth. The organism was found to be sensitive to Clindamycin, Erythromycin, Gentamicin, Levofloxacin, Oxacillin, Tetracycline, and Trimethoprim/Sulfa. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Amiodarone, Clobetasol Propionate, Cyanocobalamin, Ferrous Sulfate, Hydrocortisone Cream, Levothyroxine Sodium, Mexiletine, Multivitamins, Pantoprazole, Rosuvastatin Calcium, Spironolactone, Acetaminophen, Metolazone, Potassium Chloride, Octreotide Acetate, Warfarin, and Torsemide. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of line site infection and post-surgical hematoma. His condition upon discharge was clear and coherent, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a male with a significant past medical history including dilated cardiomyopathy (EF 25%) with left bundle branch block (LBBB), aortic valve replacement (AVR) three times, now with a bioprosthetic valve, hyperlipidemia (HLD), nonobstructive coronary artery disease (CAD), anemia with gastrointestinal bleeding (GIB), and a history of stroke and increased intracranial pressure (ICP) post-operation. He presented with a mechanical fall and was found to be volume overloaded and in atrial fibrillation. He also complained of pain at the site of driver insertion. 

Initial vitals in the emergency department were normal, but the patient had a left arm hematoma. Abnormal lab results included hemoglobin (Hgb) 7.3, INR 2.7, creatinine 1.7, ALT 41, AST 48, LDH 256, and proBNP 1621. CT scans of the head and C-spine showed no acute fracture or traumatic dislocation, but extensive encephalomalacia of the bilateral cerebellar hemispheres, frontal, parietal, and occipital lobes, and retrolisthesis of C3 on C4, C4 on C5, and C5 on C6, likely due to degenerative change. 

The patient was given amiodarone 400mg, ferrous sulfate 325mg, losartan 12.5mg, mexiletine 200mg, pantoprazole 40mg, spironolactone 25mg, levothyroxine 100mcg, metoprolol succinate 12.5mg, and doxycycline 100mg. 

The patient's family history includes his father having congestive heart failure and his mother dying of cancer. There is no family history of cardiomyopathy, arrhythmia, premature coronary artery disease, or sudden cardiac death. 

Upon discharge, the patient's creatinine was 1.3 (BUN 15) and his weight was 91.1 kg (200.84 lb). His discharge diagnoses included acute on chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), dilated cardiomyopathy, left bundle branch block, left upper extremity hematoma, acute kidney injury, atrial fibrillation, mechanical fall, recurrent driveline infection, anemia, hyperlipidemia, and hypothyroidism.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a male with a significant past medical history of dilated cardiomyopathy (EF 25%) with left bundle branch block, aortic regurgitation, hyperlipidemia, and nonobstructive coronary artery disease. He has undergone aortic valve replacement three times, the most recent one involving a bioprosthetic valve. He also has a history of atrial tachycardia, stroke, anemia with gastrointestinal bleeding, and recurrent driveline infection. He was admitted due to dyspnea and was found to have low hemoglobin, for which he received 1 unit of red blood cells. There were no signs of active bleeding. His heart failure was managed with a right heart catheterization that showed only mildly elevated filling pressures. He was actively diuresed to euvolemia and discharged on torsemide 100 mg daily.

Upon admission, his lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (2.93), Hgb (7.2), Hct (24.1), MCH (24.6), MCHC (29.9), Neuts (76.1), Lymphs (9.4), AbsLymp (0.69), UreaN (25), Creat (1.8), AST (47), and LD (276). His discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.16), Hgb (7.8), Hct (26.7), MCH (24.7), MCHC (29.2), Creat (1.3), and AnGap (9). 

The patient's dyspnea on exertion was likely due to anemia rather than volume overload. His hemoglobin remained stable throughout admission requiring no additional transfusions. The etiology of anemia was thought to be from hemolysis from the ventricular assist device as there were no overt signs of bleeding or hemolysis on labs. He was evaluated by GI and found with guaiac negative stools and no signs of active bleeding. He was discharged with a plan for outpatient esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD).

The patient's chronic kidney disease was noted with a creatinine level of 1.8 at admission, elevated from a baseline of 1.2-1.7 over the past few months. Creatinine resolved after blood transfusion, suggesting a pre-renal etiology. His history of ventricular tachycardia was stable and rate-controlled. He continued on amiodarone, mexiletine, metoprolol, and warfarin.

The patient's medications upon admission included amiodarone, cyanocobalamin, doxycycline hyclate, ferrous sulfate, levothyroxine sodium, metoprolol succinate XL, mexiletine, multivitamins, octreotide acetate, pantoprazole, rosuvastatin calcium, spironolactone, warfarin, vitamin B complex, metolazone, potassium chloride, torsemide, and clobetasol propionate.

The patient was discharged with a primary diagnosis of iron deficiency anemia, suspected gastrointestinal bleed, and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a male with a history of dilated cardiomyopathy (EF 25%) with left bundle branch block, post-operative atrial tachycardia and CVA, recurrent anemia with gastrointestinal bleeding, aortic regurgitation, hyperlipidemia, and nonobstructive coronary artery disease. He has undergone aortic valve replacement three times and has a bioprosthetic valve. He also has a history of ventricular tachycardia, recurrent drive line infection, chronic kidney disease, carotid artery stenosis, B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, and prostate cancer. He was admitted due to downtrending hemoglobin and hematocrit levels noted on outpatient labs after an unremarkable esophagogastroduodenoscopy. 

During his hospital stay, he required 2 units of packed red blood cells and underwent a colonoscopy and capsule study, which did not demonstrate an obvious source of bleeding. The etiology was felt to likely be oozing in the setting of previously known arteriovenous malformations. His hemoglobin stabilized at 8.2 and his goal INR was changed to 1.5-2. 

Admission labs showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCV, MCH, MCHC, Neuts, Lymphs, Eos, UreaN, Creat, and Glucose. Discharge labs showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCH, MCHC, RDW, RDWSD, Lymphs, PTT, LD(LDH), Albumin, and Calcium. 

The patient's medications upon admission included Cyanocobalamin, Doxycycline Hyclate, Ferrous Sulfate, Levothyroxine Sodium, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Mexiletine, Multivitamins, Octreotide Acetate, Pantoprazole, Potassium Chloride, Rosuvastatin Calcium, Spironolactone, Torsemide, Vitamin B Complex, Warfarin, Clobetasol Propionate Cream, MetOLazone, and Losartan Potassium. 

The patient was discharged with a primary diagnosis of anemia and secondary diagnoses of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, recurrent drive line infection, chronic kidney disease, history of ventricular tachycardia, carotid artery stenosis, B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, and prostate cancer. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive but required assistance or aid for ambulation.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for an asymptomatic aortic aneurysm. He underwent a major surgical procedure involving the resection of the aortic root aneurysm and ascending, as well as aortic valve replacement with a valved 29 mm mechanical conduit via a Bentall procedure. The patient was recently diagnosed with a FBN1 variant gene after his son was diagnosed with the same disorder. An echocardiogram showed a 5.2 cm dilatation at the aortic root with mild aortic regurgitation, and a chest CT measured the aortic root at 4.9 cm. The patient's blood pressure was high and was managed with metoprolol 25mg and losartan 25mg daily.

The patient's past medical history includes aortic root aneurysm, hypertension, hypertriglyceridemia, lumbago, obesity, and plantar fasciitis of the right foot. His family history includes hypertension in both parents and valve disease in his son.

Physical examination showed a well-developed male in no acute distress. His blood pressure was slightly high, and his heart rate was normal. His lungs were clear bilaterally, and his abdomen was non-distended and non-tender. His extremities were warm and well-perfused, and his neurological function was grossly intact.

Post-operative transesophageal echocardiogram showed normal biventricular function, correct positioning of the mechanical aortic valve and tube graft, and normal trans-aortic gradient. There was no paravalvular leak. The patient's chest X-ray showed bibasilar atelectatic changes, more prominent on the right, and possible small bilateral pleural effusions. There was no acute pneumonia.

Discharge labs showed a slightly low RBC count (3.65*), hemoglobin (11.0*), and hematocrit (33.9*). The patient's glucose, urea nitrogen, creatinine, sodium, potassium, chloride, bicarbonate, and anion gap were all within normal ranges. His magnesium level was 2.2.

During his hospital stay, the patient was weaned from sedation and extubated on post-operative day 1. He was weaned from inotropic and vasopressor support, and coumadin was initiated for the mechanical valve. He was also started on a beta blocker and was diuresed toward his preoperative weight. On post-operative day 3, he developed sternal erythema without drainage at the superior pole, for which he was started on Kefzol IV. The erythema improved, and he was transitioned to oral Keflex. By the time of discharge on post-operative day 5, he was ambulating freely, his wound was healing, and his pain was controlled with oral analgesics.

Upon admission, the patient was taking Losartan Potassium 50 mg and Metoprolol Succinate XL 50 mg daily. His discharge diagnosis included aortic root aneurysm post-procedure, hypertension, hypertriglyceridemia, obesity, lumbago, and plantar fasciitis of the right foot. His condition upon discharge was alert and oriented, non-focal, ambulating with a steady gait, and his sternal pain was managed with oral analgesics. His sternal incision showed erythema at the superior pole, but no drainage, and there was no edema.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to dyspnea on exertion. His medical history is complex and includes aortic root aneurysm, ARDS, asthma, bioprosthetic aortic valve stenosis, brachioplexopathy on the left, Boerhaave's esophageal rupture post repair, deep vein thrombosis, gastroesophageal reflux disease, incisional hernia post repair, paravalvular insufficiency of bioprosthetic aortic valve, subclavian artery stenosis on the left, type A aortic dissection, and type B descending aortic dissection post endovascular repair. He has undergone multiple surgical procedures including percutaneous tracheostomy, percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy, left thoracotomy with mediastinal debridement and repair of esophageal perforation, laparotomy and placement of gastric and jejunal feeding tubes, replacement of abdominal aorta with aortobi-iliac graft and right external iliac to superior mesenteric artery and left renal bypass, endovascular repair of descending thoracic and proximal abdominal aorta with modular thoracic stent graft and arteriography, and repair of incisional hernia. 

During this admission, he underwent a third-time redo sternotomy and resection of aortic root aneurysm and redo aortic valve surgery with a composite mechanical valve conduit as a Bentall procedure. He also had an apical pericardiocentesis with drain placement. His preoperative workup was unremarkable and he was cleared for surgery. Postoperatively, he required aggressive diuresis and developed hypernatremia requiring free water boluses. He was extubated without issue and heparin was started for anticoagulation. He received packed red blood cells for post-op blood loss anemia and was started on beta blocker. A moderate pericardial effusion was noted on echocardiogram and was drained in the cath lab. He was placed on cefepime for coverage and diflucan was started for a two week course due to positive cultures for yeast. 

His physical exam showed multiple healed incisions on chest, abdomen, and left axilla. His lungs were clear bilaterally and his heart had a regular rate and rhythm. His abdomen was soft, non-distended, and non-tender. His extremities were warm and well-perfused with trace edema. His neuro exam was grossly intact and no carotid bruit was appreciated. 

His lab results showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCHC, RDW, RDWSD, and PTT. His glucose, UreaN, Creat, and Na were also abnormal. His cardiac catheterization showed no significant coronary artery disease. His transthoracic echocardiogram showed a moderate to large loculated pericardial effusion subtending the right ventricle and apex with no evidence of tamponade. 

Upon discharge, he was alert and oriented, ambulating with a steady gait. His sternal pain was managed with oral analgesics and his sternal incision was healing well with no erythema or drainage. His edema was trace. His discharge medications included acetaminophen, fluconazole, furosemide, potassium chloride, warfarin, aspirin, fluticasone-salmeterol diskus, multivitamins, pantoprazole, and ProAir HFA. He was discharged home in good condition with appropriate follow up instructions. His family history is negative for premature coronary artery disease, sudden cardiac death, and Marfan syndrome.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a male of undisclosed age who was admitted for a surgical procedure due to an ascending aortic aneurysm. His past medical history includes hypertension and gastroesophageal reflux disease. He underwent a resection of the aortic root aneurysm and Bentall procedure with a mechanical valve. The patient reported no change in his clinical condition since his last office visit and denied any chest pain, shortness of breath, dyspnea on exertion, edema, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, or orthopnea. He leads an active life with his family.

The patient's family history is significant for aortic rupture in his father, who passed away at an early age, and a cousin who recently underwent aortic surgery. Upon admission, the patient was on metoprolol succinate and omeprazole. 

Physical examination upon admission and discharge showed no acute distress, with normal findings across all systems. The patient was tall and thin, with dry and intact skin. His heart rhythm was regular, and his abdomen was soft, non-distended, and non-tender. His extremities were warm and well-perfused, with no edema or varicosities. Neurological examination was grossly intact.

Pre-bypass echocardiography showed a moderately dilated aortic root at the sinus level, with dilated sinuses of Valsalva. The mid descending aorta had an opacification concerning for intraluminal hematoma vs. atherosclerotic plaque vs. dissection. Post-bypass echocardiography showed normal systolic function and no wall motion abnormalities. 

Abnormal lab results included a hematocrit of 27.8, red blood cell count of 3.20, hemoglobin of 9.3, hematocrit of 27.5, and a PTT of 50.2. Glucose levels were also elevated at 103 and 109. Anion gap was 9.

During his hospital stay, the patient was extubated, alert, and oriented, and breathing comfortably on post-operative day 1. He was hemodynamically stable and neurologically intact. His Lasix was decreased and eventually stopped due to hypotension, and Albumin was given. He was transferred to the telemetry floor for further recovery. Chest tubes and pacing wires were discontinued without complication. He was evaluated by the Physical Therapy service for assistance with strength and mobility. By the time of discharge on post-operative day 6, the patient was ambulating freely, the wound was healing, and pain was controlled with oral analgesics. He was discharged home with visiting nurse services in good condition with appropriate follow-up instructions.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a male with a history of an ascending aortic aneurysm, which was repaired with a mechanical valve. He is currently on Coumadin. He presented with two episodes of bright red blood per rectum (BRBPR) and three months of intermittent, crampy, left lower quadrant abdominal pain without radiation. His postoperative course was complicated by atrial fibrillation, for which he was started on amiodarone. He denies any chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath. His vital signs were within normal limits upon arrival at the emergency department. 

The patient's labs showed a hemoglobin/hematocrit of 12.1/39.1 and 11.6/36.2, a PTT of 61, and an INR of 3.8. A CT angiogram of the chest showed expected postoperative changes of aortic valve replacement and ascending aortic repair, as well as a left common iliac artery aneurysm measuring 1.9 cm. There were no findings to suggest an aortoenteric fistula or other abnormality based on the arterial phase exam. 

The patient's past medical history includes an aortic aneurysm, gastroesophageal reflux disease, and hypertension. His family history is significant for his father passing away due to an aortic rupture and a cousin who recently underwent aortic surgery. 

Upon arrival to the floor, the patient reported two episodes of painless BRBPR. The blood was coated and mixed with stool. He denied any previous episodes and abdominal pain. 

Admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (4.10), HGB (11.6), HCT (36.2), RDW (15.7), RDWSD (50.4), and PTT (61.1). Discharge labs showed abnormal values for Hgb (12.9), MCHC (31.6), RDW (15.9), RDWSD (51.4), PTT (54.8), and glucose (110). 

The patient's hospital course was notable for stable hemoglobin levels without any additional gastrointestinal bleeding during admission. He was seen by the gastroenterology team and discharged with a plan for outpatient colonoscopy. His INR was 2.9 on discharge. 

The patient's medications upon admission included amiodarone 200 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily, metoprolol tartrate 12.5 mg three times daily, warfarin 2 mg daily, and acetaminophen 1000 mg every six hours as needed for mild pain. 

The patient was discharged home with a primary diagnosis of BRBPR. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. He was instructed to take all of his medications as prescribed, prepare for his colonoscopy with over-the-counter magnesium citrate, and await contact from the clinic to set up the colonoscopy.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to dyspnea on exertion. She has a known history of severe mitral valve prolapse with regurgitation, gastric reflux disease, gestational diabetes mellitus, hypertension during pregnancy, obesity, and has undergone two c-sections, laser eye surgery, cataract surgery, and foot surgery to shorten bone length. Her family history includes her mother having coronary artery disease.

During her stay, she underwent a Mitral Valve Repair procedure. Her physical examination showed normal vitals with a blood pressure of 125/82, heart rate of 71, respiratory rate of 14, and oxygen saturation of 97% on room air. Her height and weight were recorded as 5'9' and 260lbs respectively. The rest of her physical examination was unremarkable.

Her lab results showed several abnormalities. Her white blood cell count was elevated at 12.3 and later increased to 25.5. Her red blood cell count was low at 3.63 and later increased to 3.83. Her hemoglobin was low at 10.0 and later increased to 10.6. Her hematocrit was low at 31.3 and later increased to 32.6. Her glucose was slightly elevated at 106. Her bicarbonate was high at 34. Her LDH was elevated at 290 and her alkaline phosphatase was also elevated at 108. Her albumin was low at 3.1. Her PTT was elevated at 44.5 and her fibrinogen was high at 97.0.

Her echocardiography report showed a normal left ventricular ejection fraction of 50% to 60%, and a normal aorta ascending measurement of 3.2 cm. The report also noted mildly thickened and elongated mitral valve leaflets, moderate to severe mitral valve prolapse, and severe mitral regurgitation. No pericardial effusion was observed.

During her hospital stay, she was monitored in the intensive care unit postoperatively and was extubated several hours later. On postoperative day one, she was transferred to the step down unit for further recovery. She was gently diuresed towards her preoperative weight and the physical therapy service was consulted for assistance with her postoperative strength and mobility. She was ready for discharge home with services on postoperative day four. She was not on any medications upon admission. 

Upon discharge, her condition was alert and oriented x3 nonfocal, ambulating with a steady gait, and sternal pain managed with dilaudid as needed. Her discharge diagnoses included severe mitral regurgitation post mitral valve repair, gastric esophageal reflux disease, history of gestational diabetes mellitus, hypertension with pregnancy, and obesity.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to chest pain. He has a history of Type B aortic dissection and had been medically managed for a few years. However, he ran out of his medications the previous week and presented with a systolic blood pressure of 200 and chest discomfort. A CT scan revealed a Type A aortic dissection. His past medical history includes a vasectomy. His family history is significant for coronary artery disease, with a brother who died from a myocardial infarction.

Upon admission, the patient underwent a Bentall procedure with a 27-mm Freestyle bioprosthesis and replacement of the ascending aorta and at least half of the aortic arch with a Vaskutek Dacron tube graft. Postoperatively, he had high chest tube output and received multiple blood products. He remained intubated due to bleeding and was extubated on postoperative day 2. He was started on Lopressor and Losartan for blood pressure control. He was transferred to the step-down unit on postoperative day 3 and was evaluated by physical therapy for strength and mobility. By postoperative day 5, he was tolerating a full oral diet, his incisions were healing well, and he was ambulating with assistance.

The patient's physical exam upon admission was largely normal, with a pulse of 74, respiratory rate of 18, and O2 saturation of 97%. His blood pressure on the right was 116/65. His skin was dry and intact, and his neck was supple with full range of motion. His lungs were clear bilaterally, and his heart rhythm was regular. His abdomen was soft, non-distended, and non-tender, with positive bowel sounds. His extremities were warm and well-perfused, with no edema or varicosities. Neurologically, he was grossly intact with no deficits.

Pertinent results include a mildly dilated aortic root, moderately dilated ascending aorta and aortic arch, and markedly dilated descending thoracic aorta. An aortic dissection flap was seen in all visible portions of the aorta. Moderate (2+) aortic regurgitation was seen, as well as trivial mitral regurgitation and moderate (2+) tricuspid regurgitation. Postoperatively, the patient had a cardiac output of 7L/min, a bioprosthetic valve in the aortic position with no insufficiency, and a mean gradient of 16mmHg. There was trivial mitral regurgitation, mild/moderate tricuspid regurgitation, and a dissection flap present in the descending aorta. The ascending aorta had been replaced with a tube graft, and biventricular systolic function was preserved.

Abnormal lab results included RBC count of 3.53*, hemoglobin of 11.1*, hematocrit of 32.2*, and glucose of 101*. The patient's medications upon admission were Norvasc 10 daily, HCTZ 25 daily, Lisinopril 40 BID, Propranolol 80 BID, and MVI.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male of unspecified age, was admitted for the surgical correction of an incisional hernia. He had previously undergone emergency cardiac surgery for an acute aortic dissection. Post-surgery, he noticed a bulge in his upper abdomen that had grown and become uncomfortable. He denied any chronic cough, constipation, or urinary difficulty and had no symptoms of intestinal obstruction. His review of systems was otherwise benign. 

His past medical history includes hypertension and a Type B aortic dissection that progressed to a Type A dissection. He has also undergone several surgical procedures in the past, including a vasectomy, aortic repair via median sternotomy incision, tonsillectomy, and right inguinal hernia repair. 

The patient's family history is significant for cardiovascular disease and cancer. His brother, father, and paternal uncle all died from myocardial infarctions at unspecified ages. His mother passed away from metastatic breast cancer. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking several medications, including amlodipine (10 mg daily), citalopram (20 mg daily), lisinopril (40 mg twice daily), propranolol (80 mg twice daily), simvastatin (20 mg at bedtime), aspirin (325 mg daily), and a multivitamin (one capsule daily). 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a laparoscopic incisional hernia repair, which he tolerated well. He was kept overnight for hemodynamic monitoring due to his extensive past cardiac history. The following day, he was deemed fit for discharge as he was keeping down adequate oral intake, urinating, ambulating, and had stable vital signs. 

On the day of discharge, his physical exam was normal. His vitals were stable, and his abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended, with a well-healed median sternotomy scar and a new incision that was clean, dry, and intact. There were no masses, and his neurological exam was intact. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of incisional hernia. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. His discharge instructions included resuming all home medications as prescribed prior to surgery, adhering to his regular home diet, avoiding strenuous activity, and following specific wound care instructions. He was also advised to contact his doctor if he experienced any adverse symptoms, such as fever, redness, pain, wound drainage, incision opening, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain/swelling, vomiting blood, difficulty swallowing, diarrhea, constipation, or bloody/black stool.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to chest pain. She has a significant medical history including a redo sternotomy with aortic root replacement, MVR and hemiarch two weeks prior to her current admission. She described her chest pain as musculoskeletal and more painful than a similar episode she experienced after her operation. She was initially at a rehab facility and was transferred to the hospital after a CT chest was done at a local emergency department due to her being hypotensive. Her blood pressure typically runs low, in the 90's and even lower, without any mental status changes.

Her past medical history includes replacement of ascending aorta for dissection several years ago and resternotomy two weeks ago with aortic root/hemiarch replacement and MVR due to chronic dissection and aneurysm and AI as well as MR. She also has a history of myasthenia, fibromyalgia, GERD, stroke with left hemiparesis, facial dystonia, migraines, anxiety and depression. Her family history includes a brother who died from aortic dissection.

Upon physical examination, she was awake and alert with a hoarse voice and asymmetrical face. Her pulse was 68, respiratory rate was 16, and oxygen saturation was 99%. Her blood pressure was initially 101/47 and later dropped to 86/50. Her sternotomy incision was clean without drainage or erythema.

Her lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.12), Hgb (8.8), Hct (27.2), and UreaN (5). Her PTT was also abnormal at 41.6. Her echocardiography report showed abnormal values for left atrium long axis dimension (4.2 cm), aortic valve peak velocity (2.7 m/sec), aortic valve peak gradient (29 mm Hg), aortic valve valve area (1.7 cm2), mitral valve E wave deceleration time (109 ms), and TR gradient (+ RA = PASP) (75 mm Hg). Her chest X-ray revealed a small right pleural effusion, widening of the right paratracheal stripe indicating widening of the superior mediastinum, and mild cardiomegaly.

During her hospital stay, a chest CT revealed an intact aortic graft. An echo showed an ejection fraction of 55% with well-seated aortic and mitral valves, 1+ tricuspid regurgitation, severe pulmonary artery hypertension, and no pericardial effusion. Her INR on admission was 2.5. She reported that pain medication improved her pain. Her tylenol was increased and she was discharged back to rehab.

Her medications upon admission included clonazepam, fluoxetine, folate, hydroxyzine, keppra, omeprazole, and pyridostigmine. Her discharge diagnosis was musculoskeletal pain following MVR, aortic root/hemiarch replacement. Her mental status was without deficits, her incision was clean/dry/intact with no sternal click, and she was able to ambulate with a walker.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of congestive heart failure (CHF), atrial fibrillation (on Coumadin), and pulmonary hypertension, presented with right lower leg swelling and pain following a fall during a hiking trip. She had a history of paresthesias bilaterally in her feet. She had a mechanical aortic valve replacement (AVR) and a pacemaker placement (PPM) due to her cardiac conditions. She also had a history of hypertension and aortic coarctation. Her Coumadin was temporarily stopped and restarted for a routine colonoscopy two weeks prior to presentation, during which she was bridged with Lovenox.

Upon admission, her vitals were stable and physical examination revealed swelling and significant pain in her right lower extremity. Her initial labs showed an elevated RDWSD at 48.4, PTT at 37.8, and glucose at 109. Urinalysis showed elevated WBC at 10 and hyaline casts at 23. Imaging of her right lower extremity revealed a hematoma in the posterior calf measuring 4.7 x 3.1 cm, but no evidence of deep venous thrombosis or fracture.

During her hospital stay, her right calf hematoma was monitored for potential compartment syndrome. However, orthopedic surgery found her compartments to be full but compressible and neurovascularly intact, thus ruling out compartment syndrome. Her pain was managed with Tylenol and Tramadol. Her Coumadin was restarted with a goal INR of 3.0 (2.5-3.5) and she was bridged with a heparin drip until she reached a therapeutic range. Her INR reached 3.2 by the time of discharge.

Her chronic conditions were managed with her home medications, which included Warfarin, Furosemide, Losartan Potassium, Amiloride HCl, Isosorbide Mononitrate, Metoprolol Succinate XL, Lorazepam, Aspirin, and Potassium Chloride. Her potassium levels were monitored and repleted as necessary due to her chronic hypokalemia, likely secondary to her home Lasix.

Upon discharge, her primary diagnosis was right lower extremity hematoma and secondary diagnoses were subtherapeutic anticoagulation for mechanical heart valve and atrial fibrillation. She was discharged home with services and was instructed to follow up with her primary care physician for INR monitoring.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to acute pain in his left calf, pallor, and coolness. Upon arrival at the ER, he had lost sensation near his knee and motor ability. He was started on a heparin gtt in the ER. Distal pulses in his left foot were not found and doppler signals were only found in the proximal bypass, not at the knee level. The patient has been maintained on coumadin for his mechanical valve with an INR goal of 2.5-3.6. He was urgently taken to the OR for exploration. 

The patient's past medical history includes hypertension, arthritis, benign prostatic hyperplasia, high cholesterol, and low testosterone. He has undergone several surgical procedures including distal SFA to BK pop bypass with RSVG and popliteal artery aneurysm resection, aorto-bi-iliac dacron graft with ligation internal iliacs bilaterally, and Benthal for Type A dissection with a mechanical valve. 

The patient is married, practices law, is a recovering alcoholic, and a former smoker. His physical exam showed him to be alert and oriented, with a blood pressure of 95-110/60s. His carotids were 2+, with no bruits or JVD. His lungs were clear, abdomen was soft and non-tender, and his feet were warm and well perfused. His left upper groin incision was stapled and open to air. 

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.00), Hgb (9.0), Hct (27.1), and PTT (90.5). His glucose level was 102 and UreaN was 25. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a left groin cutdown, repair of left common femoral artery, left below knee popliteal artery cut down, left leg thrombectomy, and 4-compartment fasciotomy. Post-procedure, there was good palpable and Dopplerable flow through the anterior tibial, posterior tibial, and peroneal arteries. 

The patient experienced several complications during his hospital stay. He developed a moderate-sized left groin hematoma where a puncture was made for the angiogram. He also experienced blood loss anemia due to oozing and bleeding from fasciotomies, which required a total of 8 units of prbcs. He had an episode of gross hematuria, which was likely due to foley irritation in the setting of recent anticoagulation. He also had an episode of agitation and confusion, likely due to narcotic pain medication, other meds received intra- and post-operatively, and anemia. 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including Albuterol Inhaler, Atenolol, Atorvastatin, Doxazosin, Finasteride, Losartan Potassium, Warfarin, Viagra, Aspirin, Trazodone, and Venlafaxine XR. 

The patient was diagnosed with Peripheral Arterial Disease and was discharged to extended care. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was ambulatory but required assistance or aid.
label: cases

summary:
The male patient was admitted due to abdominal pain. He has a complex vascular history and has previously undergone a Bentall procedure, repair of left common femoral artery, left leg thrombectomy, and 4-compartment fasciotomy for acute leg ischemia. He was discharged to rehab with heparin gtt and Coumadin. He returned to the ED with new onset right upper quadrant/right flank pain, and nausea/vomiting. He has a history of hypertension, arthritis, benign prostatic hyperplasia, high cholesterol, and low testosterone. He has also undergone distal SFA to BK pop bypass with RSVG and popliteal artery aneurysm resection, aorto-bi-iliac dacron graft with ligation internal iliacs bilaterally, and Benthal for Type A dissection with a mechanical valve. His family history is non-contributory.

Upon admission, he was alert and oriented, with a prominent systolic murmur due to a mechanical valve, and tenderness to palpation in the right upper quadrant. His groin incision was dry, clean, and intact, with no erythema. His medial and lateral fasciotomy sites were healing well with good granulation tissue. His medications upon admission included Albuterol, Aspirin, Atorvastatin, HYDROmorphone, Metoprolol Tartrate, traZODONE, Venlafaxine XR, Docusate Sodium, Gabapentin, Furosemide, Heparin, Viagra, Finasteride, Doxazosin, and Warfarin.

Abnormal lab results included PTT of 90.5 and 94.8, glucose of 102 and 199, urea nitrogen of 25 and 30, platelet count of 449, neutrophils of 86.8, lymphocytes of 6.8, monocytes of 5.8, WBC of 14.0, RBC of 3.55, hemoglobin of 10.5, hematocrit of 31.9, ALT of 101, AST of 117, alkaline phosphatase of 281, total bilirubin of 1.9, and 3 hyaline in urine.

Imaging showed an enlarged and hyperemic gallbladder, raising suspicion for acute cholecystitis. There was also focal dilatation of the native infrarenal abdominal aorta, measuring up to 40 mm. MRCP confirmed acute cholecystitis with a small stone in the gallbladder neck and adjacent hepatic hyperemia. No choledocholithiases were found. A successful percutaneous cholecystostomy was performed in the setting of acute cholecystitis.

During his hospital stay, the patient's liver function tests were upward trending, leading to the cancellation of a planned laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The patient recently underwent repair of the left common femoral artery, left leg thrombectomy, and 4-compartment fasciotomy for acute leg ischemia. He was discharged to rehab with heparin drip and Coumadin. He returned to the emergency department with new onset right upper quadrant/right flank pain, and nausea/vomiting starting at 5 am. He reported vomiting six times since the morning, mostly mucus. His last bowel movement was soft and he had no complaints concerning his left lower extremity. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Aspirin, Atorvastatin, Docusate Sodium, Doxazosin, Finasteride, Venlafaxine XR, Warfarin, Enoxaparin Sodium, Tramadol, Viagra, and Gabapentin. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent an open cholecystectomy without complications. Post-operatively, he was made NPO with IV fluids, had a foley catheter, and was given Dilaudid PCA for pain control. He was hemodynamically stable and his pain was well controlled. His blood sugar was monitored and insulin dosing was adjusted accordingly. His complete blood count was examined routinely; no transfusions were required. He was restarted on Coumadin on post-operative day #3 and he was started on Heparin drip. His PTT was therapeutic and INR upon discharge was 1.9.

His discharge instructions included resuming all regular home medications, getting plenty of rest, ambulating several times per day, drinking adequate amounts of fluids, and avoiding lifting weights greater than a certain amount until follow-up with the surgeon. He was also instructed to avoid driving or operating heavy machinery while taking pain medications.

summary:

His family history includes his mother's death from complications of urosepsis and his father's death from CHF. He has two sons. 

Physical examination revealed a very tall man with a well-appearing marfanoid habitus. He had pale conjunctiva, anicteric sclera, and slightly decreased breath sounds at the right base. His abdomen showed a well-healing scar over the right upper quadrant with no tenderness.

His creatinine level, which was elevated upon admission, returned to baseline with IV fluids, suggesting volume depletion. His anemia was within his baseline and normocytic. His INR was at goal and he continued his home dose of warfarin. 

His medications upon admission included acamprosate, albuterol inhaler, atenolol, atorvastatin, doxazosin, finasteride, hydromorphone, levofloxacin, losartan potassium, sildenafil, testosterone enanthate, testosterone, trazodone, venlafaxine, warfarin, aspirin, docusate sodium, and senna.

His physical examination revealed mild left facial asymmetry and left-sided hemiparesis. His mental status was awake and alert, cooperative with exam, and normal affect. He was oriented to person, place, and date.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a right craniectomy and evacuation of subdural hematoma. Post-operative head CT showed good resection of the subdural hematoma and pneumocephalus. He was extubated and was awake and alert, following commands with all four extremities. However, he developed tachycardia, hypertension, and diaphoresis consistent with alcohol withdrawal and received Ativan. He was found to be restless but somnolent with minimal response on the left side. MRI/MRV was negative for stroke or venous thrombus. He was intubated to tolerate MRI. EEG showed no seizures but indicated areas of focal cortical irritability, underlying structural lesion, focal subcortical dysfunction, and a very mild encephalopathy of non-specific etiology. His hematocrit dropped to 18 and he was given a unit of PRBCs. He remained intubated and was given another unit of PRBCs as his hematocrit continued to trend down. He was extubated in the afternoon and his mental status improved. He was started on Zyprexa and weaned off the precedex drip. He was transferred from the ICU to the SDU. He spiked a fever and became lethargic, not moving left side antigravity or to noxious. He was pancultured and given tylenol. His sputum culture from ___ grew. His lung sounds were crackly bilaterally, a CXR was obtained which showed pulmonary edema and he was given Lasix for diuresis. He was started on cefepime/vancomycin for presumed aspiration pneumonia. His exam continued to wax and wane. He was started on Mannitol 50g IV Q6H x24 hours to help reduce cerebral edema. His delirium completely resolved. He was accepted to ___ and was discharged in good condition. He was re-started on Coumadin upon discharge. His INR was 1.5 in the morning and he will be due to receive a dose at rehabilitation later today. The INR will need to be closely monitored at rehabilitation and the Coumadin dose adjusted as needed.

His family history includes a mother who passed away due to complications of urosepsis and a father who passed away due to undisclosed causes. He has two sons.

Upon admission, the patient was on enoxaparin, Abilify, atenolol, carbidopa-levodopa, Adderall, dorzolamide-timolol, finasteride, folic acid, trazodone, and Effexor XR. His physical exam revealed a right subcostal incision with staples, blanched erythema around the incision, and some fullness around the left aspect. The right lateral part was open and packed with dry gauze dressing with serosanguinous drainage. Multiple lower abdomen bruises were also noted.

Abnormal lab results included RBC count of 3.73*, Hgb of 10.2*, Hct of 34.1*, MCHC of 29.9*, RDW of 19.2*, RDWSD of 63.9*, and glucose of 108*. His Cl was 112*, calcium was 8.1*, and Phos was 2.6*. Urine culture revealed more than 100,000 CFU/mL of Escherichia coli, which was sensitive to several antibiotics including cefazolin, cefepime, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin, gentamicin, meropenem, piperacillin/tazobactam, tobramycin, and trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a successful transduodenal adenoma resection. Postoperatively, he developed delirium, which improved by POD 3. He also developed a urinary tract infection, which was treated with ceftriaxone and then PO Bactrim. On POD 6, he was found to have wound erythema with underlying fluid collection. On POD 7, some right lateral staples were removed and a large amount of old hematoma was evacuated. The wound was packed with 4x4 gauze. His hematological parameters were routinely monitored and he was started on a Heparin drip on POD 3 with PTT goal 45-50, and Coumadin was restarted on POD 5. He remained on the Heparin drip until his INR was therapeutic. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs, tolerating a regular soft diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and his pain was well controlled. His discharge diagnoses included duodenal adenoma with focal high-grade dysplasia, urinary tract infection, and postoperative wound hematoma.

The patient, a male with a history of anxiety and a physical appearance suggestive of a genetic disorder, was admitted due to visual hallucinations and confusion. These symptoms began approximately two days after he stopped drinking alcohol. He had been consuming a significant amount of alcohol daily, with an increase in intake over the past few weeks. His symptoms also included tremors, worsening confusion, difficulty with short-term memory, and at times, an inability to hold a conversation. He had no history of these symptoms and was reportedly a functioning individual at baseline. He had previously been seen for bilateral foot and palatal paresthesias, hypochloremia, and an anion gap of 30, which partially resolved with fluids. 

Upon admission, he was alert but hallucinating, with nystagmus and ataxic gait. His labs showed normal electrolytes and anion gap, but he had leukopenia (3.5), thrombocytopenia (101), and anemia with a hemoglobin/hematocrit of 11.6/33.9. He received 7 mg IV Ativan, 20 mg PO Valium, 5 mg PO Haldol, along with thiamine 100 mg and folate 1 mg. 

His physical exam upon admission showed him to be drowsy but arousable, with no active hallucinations, no horizontal or vertical nystagmus, no focal weakness or sensory loss, and ataxia and gait exam deferred due to drowsiness. His discharge exam showed him to be alert and oriented, with language and thought intact, no active hallucinations, no horizontal or vertical nystagmus, no focal weakness, target dysmetria on finger to nose test, ataxic on walking, and sensory exam notable for intact temperature sensation to cold tuning fork, proprioception, and light touch.

His lab results showed abnormal levels of WBC (3.8), RBC (3.24), hemoglobin (11.6), hematocrit (33.9), MCV (105), MCH (35.8), ALT (130), AST (248), and magnesium (1.5). His discharge labs showed persistently abnormal levels of WBC (3.6), RBC (3.24), hemoglobin (11.5), hematocrit (33.7), MCV (104), MCH (35.6), ALT (203), and AST (458). 

During his hospital stay, he was diagnosed with alcohol withdrawal, bilateral paresthesias, transaminitis likely due to alcohol intake, macrocytic anemia, leukopenia, and thrombocytopenia, all likely due to direct toxicity of alcohol on his bone marrow and folate deficiency. He was started on IV folate and transitioned to oral folate prior to discharge. He declined inpatient or outpatient sobriety options, expressing confidence in maintaining sobriety on an individual basis. 

His family history is negative for psychiatric disease. He was taking Lorazepam upon admission.

summary:
The patient, a male with no significant past medical history, was admitted due to a known aortic Type A dissection. He complained of throat pain and chest heaviness. His family history includes an uncle who passed away due to a dissection. 

During his hospital stay, he underwent several surgical procedures. These included an emergent repair of the Type A aortic dissection with Aortic Root Replacement, Bentall procedure with a size 29 mechanical Valsalva composite graft, Hemiarch replacement with a size 30 Gelweave graft, and Right axillary artery cannulation. He also had an exploration of the left subclavian artery followed by brachial embolectomy after cutdown, stent of axillary and subclavian arteries, and arteriography. Additionally, he underwent a prophylactic left forearm fasciotomy, prophylactic left hand fasciotomy, left open carpal tunnel release, and layered closure of a medial arm wound.

Lab results showed several abnormal findings. His WBC was elevated at 16.7, MCH was 32.6, MCHC was 35.7, and CK(CPK) was 177. His glucose was high at 157, urea nitrogen was 26, creatinine was 1.3, and potassium was 5.4. A later blood test showed decreased RBC at 2.89, Hgb at 8.8, Hct at 26.8, and an increased RDW at 17.3. His PTT was also elevated at 40.2. 

Radiology reports showed no evidence for acute transcortical ischemia, but there was a questionable focus of increased DWI signal within the left cerebellum, which could represent a tiny focus of acute ischemia. There was also bilateral mastoid opacification and under-pneumatization seen, as well as mucosal thickening in the left sphenoid sinus. A chest radiology report showed improved but not completely resolved left lower lobe opacity, mild-to-moderate cardiomegaly, and improved mediastinal widening. 

An echocardiography report showed normal biventricular contractile function and normal appearance of the prosthetic mechanical aortic valve. There was no pericardial effusion. The dissection in the aortic arch and descending aorta was visualized. 

During his hospital course, the patient was treated for postoperative pneumonia, acute renal failure secondary to rhabdomylosis, and postoperative right eye visual deficits. He was also diagnosed with obesity. He was started on ultrafiltrate for volume removal due to decreased urine output. He was intubated and sedated due to volume overload and was on heparin for a mechanical aortic valve. He was started on antibiotics due to an increased white blood cell count. He developed a rash on vancomycin and zosyn, so the vancomycin was stopped and the zosyn was changed to meropenum. The rash progressively resolved, and the meropenum was discontinued after the course was completed. He was extubated, reintubated due to respiratory difficulty and hypoxia, and then extubated again after aggressive diuresis. He was transferred from the ICU to the step down floor and then to rehabilitation. 

Upon admission, the patient was taking multivitamins.

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted to the podiatric surgery service due to an infection and hematoma in his right foot. His medical history includes peripheral vascular disease (PVD), obesity, acute renal insufficiency, and right eye visual deficits. He has undergone several surgical procedures, including an emergent repair of type A aortic dissection with aortic root replacement, hemiarch replacement, right axillary artery cannulation, exploration of left subclavian artery followed by brachial embolectomy after cutdown, stent of axillary and subclavian arteries, arteriography, prophylactic left forearm fasciotomy, prophylactic left hand fasciotomy, left open carpal tunnel release, and layered closure of a medial arm wound. 

The patient's family history includes an uncle who passed away due to aortic dissection. Upon admission, the patient was taking several medications including carvedilol, famotidine, fentanyl patch, dialudid, losartan, gabapentin, clonidine, and simvastatin. 

The patient's chief complaint was an infected hematoma in his right foot. He underwent a right foot debridement and washout procedure. Physical examination revealed diminished gross sensation and significant edema in the right foot, but no pain at the surgical site. 

Lab results showed an elevated mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) value of 33.8 and a prolonged partial thromboplastin time (PTT) of 47.0. Upon discharge, the MCH remained elevated at 33.7, the PTT increased to 53.3, and the glucose level was elevated at 153. 

Imaging of the right foot showed severe first metatarsophalangeal joint osteoarthritis with joint space narrowing osteophytosis, postsurgical changes, and some postoperative soft tissue swelling and gas. No acute fracture or concerning bone lesion was seen. Non-invasive arterial studies showed no evidence of arterial insufficiency in the lower extremities. 

Microbiological analysis of a swab from the right toe revealed the presence of Staphylococcus aureus and mixed bacterial flora. The Staphylococcus aureus was found to be resistant to clindamycin, erythromycin, levofloxacin, and oxacillin, but sensitive to gentamicin, rifampin, tetracycline, trimethoprim/sulfa, and vancomycin. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was started on broad-spectrum antibiotics and remained stable with well-controlled pain. He was discharged on postoperative day 5 with oral antibiotics and a wound vacuum-assisted closure (VAC) device for his right foot. His discharge diagnosis was right foot hematoma/infection.

summary:
The patient, a right-handed male with a history of obesity, was admitted to the neurology service due to a cerebellar infarct. He presented with right arm paresthesia, vomiting, and gait difficulty, and was found to have a right vertebral occlusion and cerebellar stroke. His symptoms began with an instant-onset right-sided headache, sharp in intensity, and accompanied by double vision. The headache returned later in the day, more painful and accompanied by right scalp tenderness, nausea/vomiting, and right hand tingling paresthesia. The following morning, the headache persisted and he was unable to walk in a straight line, listing heavily to the right. His right hand was also numb. He was given 325mg aspirin, Reglan, 1L IVF, and transferred for further management. 

The patient's family history is significant for a cousin on his mother's side with a genetic disorder and mitral valve prolapse, as well as mitral valve prolapse in the patient's mother. His father, aged ___, has had five unprovoked DVTs in the last ___ years. 

Upon admission, the patient's vitals were T:98.5, BP:135/94, HR:90, RR:16, SaO2:97%. Neurological examination revealed increased sensation to light touch on the right face, but no hyperalgesia to pinprick. There was light pins-and-needles paresthesia in the right hand decreasing proximally up to the shoulder. Sensation to light touch and pinprick was intact and symmetric even in this area. 

Admission labs showed abnormal values for glucose (198*), WBC (15.7*), AbsNeut (10.35*), AbsLymp (3.86*), AbsMono (1.12*), and PTT (23.9*). Urine test showed positive for oxycodone (oxycodn-POS*). Pertinent labs showed abnormal values for %HbA1c (7.3*), eAG (163*), and HDL (28*). Discharge labs showed abnormal values for WBC (15.7*) and glucose (134*). 

Imaging revealed loss of vascular flow enhancement within the right V1-V3 segments, consistent with right vertebral artery dissection. No evidence of deep venous thrombosis was found in the right or left lower extremity veins. No spontaneous echo contrast or thrombus was seen in the body of the left atrium or left atrial appendage. No atrial septal defect or patent foramen ovale was seen. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was started on heparin due to concern for hypercoagulability. However, TEE showed no thrombus and no ASD or PFO was visualized. Doppler US of the lower extremities also showed no evidence of deep venous thrombosis. Factor VIII level was within normal limits. Antiphospholipid Syndrome labs were also sent out and are still pending. The etiology of his stroke was thought to be dissection, which was confirmed on MRI neck with fat saturation technique. 

The patient was found to have an elevated LDL for which he was started on Atorvastatin for secondary stroke prevention. His HbA1c was elevated to 7.3 so he was provided with diabetes education and started on Metformin upon discharge.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male with a past medical history of asthma, atypical chest pain, congestive heart failure with a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 45%, plantar warts, presumed gonococcal urethritis, cardiomyopathy, possible spondylitis, osteopenia, and wrist pain, presented with presyncope. He reported several days of lightheadedness and feeling like he might pass out, which was exacerbated by position changes. He denied fevers, chills, chest pain, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, diarrhea, dysuria, or symptoms of anxiety. He did note feeling somewhat tachycardic and had some mild dyspnea, which he attributed to his asthma. 

Upon admission, his vitals were stable and he was given ASA 325. An EKG was performed, which was unremarkable. Laboratory tests were notable for negative troponin. His blood work showed a WBC of 6.1, RBC of 3.75*, Hgb of 12.8*, Hct of 37.9*, MCV of 101*, MCH of 34.2*, and MCHC of 33.9. His UreaN was 11, Creat was 1.2, Na was 141, K was 4.1, Cl was 102, HCO3 was 30, and AnGap was 13. His Mg was 2.1, and his cTropnT was <0.01. 

A chest X-ray showed no evidence of acute cardiopulmonary abnormality. Long-term event monitoring was performed to evaluate his palpitations and rule out significant arrhythmia. His baseline recording showed sinus rhythm at rates 77 to 82 BPM. Four recordings with complaints of 'heart racing,' 'short of breath,' 'chest pain' and 'rapid heart beats' showed sinus rhythm/sinus tachycardia at rates 98 to 161 BPM with 1 VPB. 

An echocardiogram showed normal left atrial size, mildly dilated right atrium, normal left ventricular wall thickness, top normal/borderline dilated left ventricular cavity size, mild global left ventricular hypokinesis (LVEF = 45 %), and normal right ventricular chamber size and free wall motion. The aortic valve leaflets appeared structurally normal with good leaflet excursion and no aortic stenosis or aortic regurgitation. The mitral valve appeared structurally normal with trivial mitral regurgitation. 

The patient's family history includes seizures in his sister and cancer in his father. His mother's medical history is unknown as she has passed away. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Albuterol Inhaler, Fluticasone Propionate, and Lisinopril. 

The patient was diagnosed with presyncope and orthostasis. It was believed that he was hypovolemic and presented with symptoms of orthostatic hypotension and hypovolemia. He received IVF resuscitation with improvement in his symptoms. There was no evidence of acute coronary syndrome, arrhythmia, or neurologic driver given normal EKG, telemetry, and relatively benign history. His symptoms of palpitations and dyspnea may be related to reactive airways disease/asthma. He was also noted to have macrocytic anemia, which will be worked up as an outpatient.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was transferred from an outside hospital due to chest and back pain, which was later identified as a type B aortic dissection. She reported sudden onset of back and chest pain approximately 10 hours prior to admission, along with dyspnea and nausea. She also experienced difficulty walking and parasthesias of the right lower extremity, which have since decreased. She denied changes in vision, arm paraesthesias or weakness, difficulty with speech, and abdominal pain. She also denied pain or parasthesias in her legs or feet. 

Her past medical history includes bilateral retinal detachments. She has undergone bilateral eye surgery for retinal detachments and a C-section. There is no known family history of thrombophilic or bleeding disorders. 

Upon admission, her vitals were recorded as 97.6 71 145/74 20 99% RA. Her right lower extremity was noted to be cool to touch compared to the left lower extremity. All pulses were palpable. 

Her admission labs showed abnormal values for WBC (15.9), RBC (3.73), Neuts (91.1), Lymphs (5.4), Monos (2.8), Eos (0.1), AbsNeut (14.47), AbsLymp (0.85), AbsMono (0.44), AbsEos (0.01), PTT (24.6), Glucose (107), and HCO3 (20). Her calcium level was also low at 8.2. 

Imaging revealed a type B dissection extending into bilateral common iliac arteries with true lumen feeding renal and visceral segments. There was also heterogeneous opacification of the right mid and distal superficial femoral, popliteal artery and posterior tibial, anterior tibial, and peroneal arteries. The aortic dissection flap extends to the left common iliac artery and right common iliac and proximal right internal iliac artery. 

During her hospital stay, she was started on a heparin drip. She began to have chest pressure with corresponding BP in 140s. She also began to have abdominal pain with eating solid food. She was able to tolerate a full liquid diet, with intermittent severe abdominal pain with intake of solid food. She was scheduled for an aortic fenestration procedure. 

Upon discharge, her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was ambulatory and independent. She was not on any medications upon admission.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a Portuguese-speaking woman, was admitted due to chest pain and EKG changes. She has a history of bilateral retinal detachments and has undergone bilateral eye surgery for the same, as well as a C-section. There is no known family history of thrombophilic or bleeding disorders. 

Upon admission, the patient was not on any medications. She underwent a replacement repair of type A dissection with replacement of aortic arch with reimplantation of arch vessels using a 26 mm Gelweave Medusa graft and elephant trunk. She also underwent a Bentall procedure with a 25 mm mechanical valve conduit graft. Post-procedure, she had a mediastinal re-exploration, evacuation of mediastinal hematoma, and repair of mediastinal bleeding at the left coronary button off the previously done Bentall procedure.

CTA demonstrated a retrograde Type A dissection. The patient had a previous Type B dissection. The CTA also showed a persistent aortic dissection extending to the common iliac bifurcations. There was pneumomediastinum and a left pneumothorax without mediastinal shift. There was a new simple pericardial effusion and new, left greater than right simple pleural effusions with associated compressive atelectasis.

Abnormal lab results included a decrease in RBC, Hgb, and Hct, and an increase in WBC. The patient's PTT was also elevated. 

The patient was brought to the Operating Room for repair of Type A dissection with total arch replacement and Bentall procedure. Post-operatively, she was transferred to the CVICU in stable condition for recovery and invasive monitoring. She had high chest tube output and returned to the OR for mediastinal exploration. Hemostasis was achieved and she returned to the CVICU. 

By the time of discharge on POD 7, the patient was ambulating freely, the wound was healing, and pain was controlled with oral analgesics. The patient was discharged home in good condition with appropriate follow-up instructions.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted to the Cardiac Surgery department due to lower back pain and shortness of breath. She has a history of aortic aneurysm, aortic dissection, and bilateral retinal detachments. She had previously undergone repair of type A dissection with replacement of her aortic arch and reimplantation of the arch vessels using a 26 mm Gelweave Medusa graft and elephant trunk, Bentall procedure with a 25 mm mechanical valve. She was taken back to the operating room later that same day for bleeding. The remainder of her post-operative course was unremarkable and she was discharged to her brother's home upon discharge. Anti-coagulation management was arranged via her PCP. 

On the morning of her readmission, she developed worsening upper back pain with ongoing shortness of breath and therefore presented to the BI emergency department for evaluation. She has no known thrombophilic or bleeding disorders in her family history. 

Upon admission, her vitals were stable but she appeared weak. Her physical exam was largely unremarkable, with decreased breath sounds at the right base and trace lower extremity edema. Her admission labs showed several abnormalities including elevated platelet count, neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, WBC, and glucose, as well as decreased RBC, HGB, HCT, MCHC, and total CO2. Her cTropnT was also elevated. 

Echocardiography revealed a large pericardial effusion and a left pleural effusion. The estimated right atrial pressure was normal, left ventricular systolic function was low normal, and right ventricular chamber size was normal with borderline normal free wall function. A bileaflet aortic valve prosthesis was present with no aortic regurgitation seen. Mild mitral regurgitation was seen. 

Radiology report showed minimal interval decrease of the pre-existing left pleural effusion that continues to be moderate in size. The heart is borderline in size. The sternal wires are in stable position. No pneumothorax or pneumonia was noted. 

Microbiology lab results showed Propionibacterium acnes in the pericardial fluid and Aspergillus versicolor group in the fungal culture. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent drainage of the pericardial effusion and placement of a left pleural pigtail to drain her pleural effusion. She was started on an antibiotic and anti-fungal regimen, which was later discontinued as growth was deemed contamination. She was also seen in consultation by the ophthalmology service for a complaint of floaters, but they were felt to be benign. 

Her medications upon admission included Atorvastatin, HYDROmorphone, Miconazole Nitrate Vag Cream, Aspirin, Acetaminophen, Guaifenesin, Metoprolol Tartrate, Valsartan, TraMADOL, Warfarin, Furosemide, and Potassium Chloride. 

Upon discharge, her condition was stable. She was alert and oriented, ambulating with a steady gait. Her sternal pain was managed with oral analgesics and her incisions were healing well with no erythema or drainage. She had trace edema. Her discharge diagnosis was pericardial and pleural effusion, aspergillis in pericardial fluid. She was instructed on appropriate follow-up and discharged to her brother's home with family support.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted to the CARDIOTHORACIC unit with a chief complaint of continuous cough since her previous discharge, along with epigastric and back pain with deep breath. She has a history of aortic aneurysm, aortic dissection, and bilateral retinal detachments. She has undergone surgical procedures including bilateral eye surgery for retinal detachments and a C-section. She has also had a repair of type A dissection with replacement of aortic arch with reimplantation of arch vessels using a 26 mm Gelweave Medusa graft and elephant trunk, and a Bentall procedure with a 25 mm mechanical valve. 

During her stay, she underwent a left thoracostomy tube placement and right pigtail chest tube placement. She was readmitted with shortness of breath and found to have large left effusion and pericardial effusion. A left pigtail was placed and cardiology performed a pericardiocentesis. She was discharged to home but returned with an unrelenting cough since discharge and epigastric and back pain with deep breathing. Over the past few days, she has been experiencing increasing shortness of breath and weakness. 

Upon her readmission, she was found to have a large left pleural effusion and moderate right effusion with no pericardial effusion. She was started on a heparin drip for subtherapeutic INR of 1.2 with a mechanical AVR. Her INR upon presenting to the ED was 1.2. 

Her lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (2.83*), Hgb (8.0*), Hct (26.1*), MCHC (30.7*), RDWSD (48.1*), and PTT (85.2*). Her glucose level was also slightly elevated at 102*. 

Her medications upon admission included Acetaminophen, Aspirin EC, Atorvastatin, HYDROmorphone (Dilaudid), Metoprolol Tartrate, TraMADOL (Ultram), Valsartan, Warfarin, Lidocaine 5% Patch, LORazepam, and Ranitidine. 

Her family history does not include any known thrombophilic or bleeding disorders. Her physical exam showed constant cough since discharge and worse with lying supine, nausea in the past 24 hours with coughing, and retinal detachment. 

Her CTA showed post type A aortic dissection repair with reanastomosis of the great vessels to the proximal aorta, unchanged foci of air surrounding the graft, unchanged appearance of the dissection involving the innominate artery and thoracic aortic dissection extending inferiorly to the internal iliac artery on the right and left common iliac bifurcation, moderate right and large left pleural effusions increased from prior examination with adjacent compressive atelectasis, and near resolution of pericardial effusion. Her cytology report was negative for malignant cells. 

During her hospital stay, she was monitored in the CVICU and received heparin therapy. Her chest tubes were discontinued without incident. She remained in the hospital for several days due to subtherapeutic INR and the need for heparin gtt. Her discharge diagnoses were recurrent bilateral effusions and subtherapeutic INR.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to dyspnea on exertion. He has a known history of mitral valve prolapse with mitral regurgitation and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. He also has aortic insufficiency, non-obstructive coronary artery disease, cardiomyopathy, history of GI bleed x2, dyslipidemia, emphysema (noted on chest CT scan), prostate cancer (preoperative), and type 2 diabetes. He has a history of tobacco use but has quit. His past surgical history includes AICD/pacemaker, appendectomy, right biceps repair, bilateral cataract surgeries, and cystoscopies for scar tissue removal. 

The patient's family history includes his mother who died of a myocardial infarction, his father who had a myocardial infarction, and a brother who also had a myocardial infarction. 

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to have a pulse of 64, respiratory rate of 16, O2 saturation of 97% on room air, and blood pressure of 130/63 on the right and 134/64 on the left. He had no edema, and his neurological exam was nonfocal. 

The patient's lab results showed an elevated glucose level of 154*, elevated urea nitrogen level of 23*, and elevated LDH of 266*. His HbA1c was 7.8* and eAG was 177*. 

Imaging studies revealed less than 40% stenosis on the right carotid and 60-69% stenosis on the left. A CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis showed moderate atherosclerotic changes in the thoracoabdominal aorta, moderate calcification of the abdominal aorta and iliac branches, moderate to severe emphysema, and fatty liver. There were also multiple calcifications in the pancreas consistent with chronic pancreatitis, a small nodule in the left adrenal gland, and bilateral renal cysts. An echocardiogram showed a mildly dilated left atrium, moderately dilated left ventricular cavity, moderately dilated aortic root, and mild dilation of the aortic arch and descending thoracic aorta. There was moderate aortic regurgitation and mild mitral regurgitation. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was admitted for pre-operative work-up and heparin bridge. He was discharged home on Lovenox, which will be discontinued when INR is therapeutic on coumadin. 

Upon admission, the patient was on Warfarin 5mg alternating with 7.5 mg, Fenofibrate 160 qd, Digoxin 0.125 qd, Lisinopril 5 qd, Simvastatin 40 qd, Coreg 12.5 bid, Lasix 10 qd, Glyburide 2.5 bid, and Fish Oil 1000mg QD.

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to dyspnea on exertion. He has a known history of mitral valve prolapse with mitral regurgitation and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. He also has a history of aortic insufficiency, non-obstructive coronary artery disease, cardiomyopathy, dyslipidemia, emphysema, prostate cancer, and type 2 diabetes. He has a history of tobacco use but has quit. His past surgical history includes the placement of an AICD/pacemaker, appendectomy, right biceps repair, cataract surgeries, and cystoscopies for scar tissue removal. His family history includes his mother, father, and brother all having myocardial infarctions at various ages.

During his stay, he underwent a Bentall procedure with a 25-mm mechanical valve conduit and a mitral valve replacement with a 33-mm mechanical valve. Post-operatively, he was stable and was transferred for recovery and invasive monitoring. He was extubated on post-operative day 1 and was hemodynamically stable. He was started on a heparin bridge and coumadin for the mechanical valves. His chest tubes and pacing wires were discontinued without complication. He was evaluated by physical therapy for strength and mobility assistance. His blood sugar was managed with glipizide, metformin, and insulin.

His physical exam showed no significant abnormalities. His pulse was 64, respiratory rate was 16, and oxygen saturation was 97% on room air. His blood pressure was 130/63 on the right and 134/64 on the left. He had no edema and his neurological exam was nonfocal.

His lab results showed abnormal values for WBC (14.1*), RBC (2.93*), Hgb (9.1*), Hct (26.2*), and PTT (ranging from 36.4* to 128.4*). His intra-operative TEE showed a mildly dilated left ventricular cavity with normal systolic function, a moderately dilated aortic root, moderate to severe aortic regurgitation, and myxomatous and severely redundant mitral valve leaflets with moderate/severe bileaflet mitral valve prolapse. Post-CPB, his LV systolic function appeared mildly depressed with an estimated EF of 40-45%.

Upon admission, his medications included Warfarin, Fenofibrate, Digoxin, Lisinopril, Simvastatin, Coreg, Lasix, Glyburide, and Fish Oil. He was discharged in good condition, ambulating freely, with his wound healing and pain controlled with oral analgesics.
