summary:
The patient, a male with a history of poorly controlled Type I Diabetes Mellitus, peripheral neuropathy, recurrent foot infections, left Charcot foot, alcohol abuse, depression, GERD, erectile dysfunction, and a history of cataract removal in the left eye, was admitted due to swelling and redness in his right foot. He reported aching pain in his right shin and flu-like symptoms including weakness, fatigue, chills, diarrhea, decreased appetite, and nausea. He also reported blood on his right sock. The patient has no sensation in either foot below the mid-shin level and does not regularly check his fingersticks. 

Upon admission, his vitals were recorded as T99.6 130/68 HR 88 RR 20 94%RA Wt 212lbs FSBG 397. Physical examination revealed a thin, white male in no acute distress. His right foot was bandaged from foot to just below the knee, with visible and somewhat erythematous toes, shiny skin, and warm to the touch. His left foot showed Charcot deformity and chronic venous stasis dermatitis. 

Lab results showed abnormal WBC count of 12.0*, RBC count of 4.20*, HGB of 12.5*, HCT of 37.9*, NEUTS of 76.4*, LYMPHS of 14.8*, and UREA N of 22*. His right foot x-ray revealed a Lisfranc fracture dislocation, subluxation at the talonavicular joint, and likely underlying diabetic neuropathy. 

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent ORIF on his right foot for Charcot foot/Lis Franc disruption. He was treated with vancomycin and unasyn for possible associated cellulitis, with the regimen later changed to vancomycin, ciprofloxacin, and flagyl. His diabetes was managed with glargine and humalog, and he was continued on his outpatient regimen of lisinopril. He also experienced post-op fevers, which resolved over time. Hematuria was noted on urinalysis, likely due to a foley placed during ORIF. His depression was reported as well-controlled, and he was continued on his outpatient regimen of celexa. He reported alcohol abuse, but showed no signs or symptoms of withdrawal. 

Upon admission, the patient was on NPH, Humalog sliding scale, Celexa, MTV, folic acid, thiamine, lisinopril, and neurontin. His family history includes a mother with diabetes and CVA, a father with rectal cancer, and two healthy sisters.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of poorly controlled Type I Diabetes, peripheral neuropathy, recurrent foot infections, left Charcot foot, alcohol abuse, depression, GERD, erectile dysfunction, and a history of cataract removal in the left eye, was admitted due to increased swelling and erythema in his right foot. This began when he started putting more weight on the foot after transitioning from using a crutch to a wheelchair. He denies any fevers, chills, or trauma, and has not noted any changes in the contralateral foot. He also denies weight change, nausea, vomiting, cough, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, hematemasis, hematochezia, melena, hematuria, and dysuria. His blood sugar was noted to be very elevated (536) upon admission.

The patient's family history includes a mother with diabetes and CVA, a father with rectal cancer, and two healthy sisters. His physical examination revealed a swollen, warm, erythematous right foot compared to the left, but not tender to palpation. There is a 2cm, circular healing lesion on the lower third of the right leg. The left foot has a deformity that is at baseline per patient. 

Lab results showed abnormal RBC count (3.98), HGB (11.5), HCT (34.7), NEUTS (72.9), GLUCOSE (536), SODIUM (132), and POTASSIUM (5.2). X-ray of the left foot showed extensive Charcot joint deformities, gross demineralization of the majority of the more distal foot, bony deformity of the third proximal phalanx, severe degenerative changes at the first interphalangeal joint, and extensive vascular calcifications. The right foot X-ray showed diffuse osteopenia, extensive hardware in the mid foot, probable soft tissue swelling, and relative lucency and slight irregularity at the base of the fifth metatarsal.

During his hospital stay, the patient's foot swelling and erythema improved markedly over 24 - 48 hours with simple elevation. It was suspected that the patient's recent increase in weight-bearing activity, or possibly some occult trauma not detected by the patient due to lack of sensation in the foot, triggered his presentation. His diabetes was poorly controlled, and his insulin regimen was changed from Lantus with a Humalog sliding scale to 70/30 insulin injected at breakfast (50 units) and dinner (35 units). 

Upon admission, the patient was on Citalopram 40 mg daily, Folic acid 1 mg daily, Gabapentin 600 mg QHS, Lisinopril 20 mg daily, Simvastatin 20 mg daily, NPH insulin 28 units QAM, Humalog sliding scale, MVI, and Thiamine 100 mg daily.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to scrotal pain. He has a history of poorly controlled Type 1 diabetes, which has led to complications such as peripheral neuropathy and retinopathy. He also has a history of alcohol abuse, depression, GERD, erectile dysfunction, and has undergone surgery for Charcot foot and cataract removal in the left eye. His family history includes diabetes in his mother and aunt.

Upon admission, the patient presented with scrotal swelling, nausea, and pain. He denied any dysuria, difficulty with urination, penile involvement, fevers, abdominal pain, chest pain, or dyspnea. His initial vital signs were recorded and an examination revealed an erythematous tender right scrotum. His glucose level was notably high at 286. A scrotal ultrasound was performed which showed no torsion. He was given Clindamycin and IVF. 

The patient's physical exam on admission showed an erythematous scrotum, tender but not tense with weeping lesions, and no penile involvement. His extremities showed multiple deformities of both feet. His vital signs were recorded as 96.3 147/83 89 97% RA. 

Lab results on admission showed abnormal levels of RBC (3.84), Hgb (11.5), Hct (33.1), Glucose (286), UreaN (23), Phos (2.4), %HbA1c (9.9), eAG (237), and Lactate (2.8). A scrotal ultrasound showed a moderate-to-large right hydrocele, a large right scrotal pearl, and no evidence of testicular torsion bilaterally.

During his hospital stay, the patient was diagnosed with scrotal cellulitis and was treated with IV clindamycin. His condition improved and he was transitioned to PO clindamycin. His diabetes was managed with his home insulin glargine dose and a sliding scale, and his hypertension was managed with continued Lisinopril. He showed no signs of acute infection or injury in his bilateral Charcot feet.

Upon discharge, his condition was improved with markedly less dramatic erythema of the scrotum. His discharge lab results showed abnormal levels of RBC (3.73), Hgb (11.0), Hct (33.4), and Glucose (105). 

The patient's medications upon admission included Lantus, Humalog Sliding Scale, Aspirin, Simvastatin, Neurontin, Multi-Vitamin Hi-Po Tab, Lisinopril, and Nicotine TP.
label: cases

summary:
The patient is a male with a past medical history of type 1 diabetes, which has been complicated by Charcot Foot, Lisfranc fracture, retinopathy, microalbuminuria, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. He also has an unknown psychiatric condition. He was recently admitted for scrotal cellulitis, which was treated with IV clindamycin and showed good improvement. He was discharged on oral clindamycin for 7 more days. His diabetes has been poorly controlled in the past, with an A1c of 9.9. He has been compliant with his insulin regimen of 40 units of lantus in the morning and humalog with meals. 

The patient presented to the ED from a clinic for evaluation of hyperglycemia, tachycardia, and weakness. He reported feeling weak with difficulty ambulating and had a critically high fingerstick at the clinic. He was sent to the ED for rehydration, insulin administration, and consideration for admission given his difficulty with blood sugar management and risk for further infectious complications. He also complained of nausea, one episode of vomiting, constant dull 'heartburn pain', and 'stomach discomfort'. He denied any overt chest pain, shortness of breath, dyspnea on exertion, fever, chills, diarrhea, cough, or dysuria. 

Upon physical examination, he was alert and had no rashes. His scrotum was non-tender, and he had a right hydrocele. His labs were significant for a glucose level of 438, potassium level of 7.1, and a total CO2 of 20. His WBC count was 11.2, RBC count was 4.33, and hemoglobin was 12.8. His urinalysis showed 10 ketones and was negative for infection. 

During his hospitalization, he was diagnosed with diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) due to medication non-compliance. His anion gap closed and his fasting blood sugar levels improved after administration of insulin in the ED. He had evidence of a left lower lobe infiltrate on chest X-ray, but it was not treated as he did not have clinical signs of pneumonia. His scrotal cellulitis appeared to be resolved. 

His medications upon admission included clindamycin, gabapentin, glargine, lispro, lisinopril, simvastatin, and aspirin. His family history includes his mother and aunt having diabetes.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to right scrotal edema and erythema that had been present for four days. He reported an insidious onset of swelling and erythema with only slight discomfort, and denied any trauma to the area, sharp pain, fever, dysuria, hematuria, dyspnea, chest pain, angina pectoris, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or constipation. He is not sexually active. On the day of admission, he noticed significant scrotal discharge. His primary care clinic noted significant erythema and weeping, with his underwear soaked in exudate and blood, extending to the crural fold on the right side.

The patient has a history of insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (IDDM), complicated by Charcot Foot, Lisfranc fracture, foot ulcers, retinopathy, and microalbuminuria. His most recent HbA1C was 7.3. He also has chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) with an FEV1 of 59%, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, recurrent scrotal cellulitis, and tobacco abuse. His mother and aunt both have diabetes.

Upon admission, his vital signs were stable and he reported feeling comfortable with no pain. His physical exam was largely unremarkable, with the exception of a large, fluctuant, non-tender hydrocoele on the right side, slight erythema, and intertrigonous erythema. His left scrotum appeared normal.

Lab results showed a slightly elevated creatinine level of 1.3 (baseline 0.9-1.2), which resolved the following day after 1L IVF NS. His potassium level was initially high at 9.7, but this was due to gross hemolysis and it was 4.4 on repeat. His glucose level was 147, urea nitrogen was 22, and bicarbonate was 22. His hemoglobin was slightly low at 11.7, as was his hematocrit at 35.7. His red blood cell count was also slightly low at 3.95.

An ultrasound of his scrotum showed a right hydrocoele with a right scrotal pearl, with no evidence of torsion. This was unchanged from a previous study. There were also stable scattered calcifications along the anterior of the right testicle, and a 1.7-cm left-sided large scrotal pearl.

During his hospital stay, the patient's scrotal edema and erythema were thought to be due to the hydrocoele, with a possible recurrence of scrotal cellulitis. However, cellulitis was considered unlikely due to the lack of tenderness, leukocytosis, or fever. He was initially started on vancomycin, cefepime, clindamycin, and fluconazole, but this was later narrowed to clindamycin 300mg every six hours orally. He was also started on clotrimazole cream twice a day for presumed tinea corporis.

His diabetes was managed with his home regimen of lantus 40U daily plus 6 units of humalog with each meal and a humalog sliding scale. His hypertension was managed with his home dose of lisinopril, and his hyperlipidemia was managed with his home dose of atorvastatin. He was also continued on his home dose of aspirin and gabapentin as needed for neuropathic pain.

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with a hydrocoele and possible scrotal cellulitis. He was instructed to take clindamycin four times a day until a specified date for the bacterial infection in his scrotum, and to use clotrimazole cream twice a day until his fungal infection in his groin resolves. He was also advised to keep his groin clean by washing thoroughly in and between the folds.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of uncontrolled insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, was admitted due to scrotal pain, swelling, and possible infection. He has been admitted twice in the past for scrotal cellulitis. The pain had started a month prior and had progressively worsened. The patient also reported swelling of the right scrotum. He has not had fevers, chills, dysuria, or hematuria. Upon examination, mild erythema was observed on the right scrotum. A scrotal ultrasound showed a stable hydrocele in the right scrotum with no evidence of abscess or torsion. The patient was started on IV clindamycin.

The patient's past medical history includes insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus complicated by Charcot Foot, Lisfranc fracture, foot ulcers, retinopathy, and microalbuminuria, COPD, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, recurrent scrotal cellulitis, coronary artery disease, DKA, bilateral PE, GERD, impotence, PPD positive, recovered alcohol abuse, memory loss, and red/green color blindness. His family history includes diabetes in his mother and aunt, and rectal cancer in his father.

Upon admission, the patient was on Albuterol Inhaler, Atorvastatin, Gabapentin, Glargine, Lisinopril, Tiotropium Bromide, Aspirin, and Multivitamins. His admission labs showed abnormal glucose (147*), urea nitrogen (24*), RBC (3.86*), HGB (11.6*), and HCT (34.4*). His discharge labs showed abnormal glucose (65*), urea nitrogen (22*), RBC (3.89*), HGB (11.5*), and HCT (34.7*).

During his hospital stay, the patient's scrotal pain and swelling were managed. His pain was not worsening and he did not require any pain medication. Antibiotics were stopped as active infection was deemed very unlikely. His other medical conditions, including insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, elevated creatinine, and mild anemia, were also managed. The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of testicular swelling and pain. He was instructed to follow up with his urologist for further evaluation of his pain.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male with a significant past medical history including COPD, HTN, HLD, GERD, IDDM, and a history of bilateral PE, presented for wound evaluation. He has been dealing with bilateral heel ulcers for the past six months. He was advised to seek medical attention due to concerns of worsening infection. The patient denied any lower extremity pain but reported nausea, vomiting, decreased appetite, poor oral intake, dark tarry stools, polyuria, and polydipsia over the past four days. He also reported subjective fevers, weakness, and fatigue. However, he denied any chest pain or shortness of breath. 

Upon examination, grade II ulcers were noted on both heels without any surrounding erythema. Initial vitals in the ED were 99.2 107 110/51 20 95% RA. summary:

He has received wound care from different institutions and has been on Aspirin 81mg and Plavix since his left AT angioplasty. During a previous admission, he underwent a bilateral angiogram with no intervention and was discharged with plans to continue antibiotics for six weeks. 

His family history includes diabetes in his mother and aunt, rectal cancer in his father, and stroke in his mother.

Lab results showed a hematocrit level of 26.2* and creatinine level of 1.4*. Angiographic findings revealed that the right popliteal artery is patent, the right posterior tibial artery is occluded, the right anterior tibial artery is patent but has a 3 cm long segment of stenoses in its middle, and the right peroneal artery has stenoses at its proximal aspect. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was observed overnight following his angiogram. He had some oozing from his groin stick, which was managed with a stitch. His PICC line had flipped up into the internal jugular, which was repositioned in interventional radiology.

His past medical history includes coronary artery disease (CAD), IDDM complications such as Charcot Foot, Lisfranc fracture, foot ulcers, retinopathy, and microalbuminuria, diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), hypertension (HTN), hyperlipidemia (HLD), pancreatitis, bilateral pulmonary embolism (PE), gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), positive purified protein derivative (PPD) status post treatment, and alcohol abuse. The patient's family history includes diabetes in his mother and aunt, rectal cancer in his father, and stroke in his mother at an unspecified age. Upon physical examination, the patient was found to have significant flatfoot/Charcot deformity bilaterally, decreased skin turgor, and nonpalpable but dopplerable pulses bilaterally, likely due to edema. He had bilateral calcaneal ulcerations, with the left being larger than the right. There were no acute signs of infection.

The patient underwent bilateral heel debridements with Integra applications. Post-operatively, he remained afebrile with stable vital signs and adequate urine output. He received subcutaneous heparin throughout his admission and worked with physical therapy to learn to get around while being nonweightbearing bilaterally.

His red blood cell count (3.33), hemoglobin (8.8), hematocrit (27.6), and mean corpuscular hemoglobin (26.5) were all low. Upon admission, the patient was taking Albuterol Inhaler, Atorvastatin, Gabapentin, Glargine, Multivitamins, Lisinopril, Tamsulosin, Tiotropium Bromide, and Trazodone. He was discharged home in a stable condition, alert and interactive, and able to get out of bed with assistance to a chair or wheelchair. His discharge diagnosis was bilateral heel ulcerations.

On physical examination, the patient was found to have two large ulcerations on both heels, with the left heel ulcer being much larger and showing signs of erythema at the most distal edge of the ulcer. There was no deep probing or undermining and no bony involvement.

Lab results showed abnormal values for RBC (3.37*, 3.10*, 3.33*, 3.47*), Hgb (8.7*, 8.2*, 8.6*, 9.0*), Hct (26.8*, 24.8*, 26.7*, 28.1*), MCV (80*, 80*, 80*, 81*), MCH (25.8*, 26.6*, 25.9*, 26.0*), WBC (15.6*), Neuts (81.6*), Lymphs (12.4*), Glucose (246*, 226*, 178*, 351*), UreaN (26*, 27*, 27*, 32*), Creat (1.1, 1.2, 1.2, 1.3*), Na (134, 135, 134, 131*), K (4.7, 4.2, 4.9, 5.4*), and CRP (157.9*). summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Insulin-Dependent Diabetes Mellitus (IDDM), Hypertension (HTN), Hyperlipidemia (HLD), Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) with a baseline Creatinine (Cr) of 1.1-1.4, and chronic non-healing foot ulcers, presented with redness surrounding heel ulcers. The patient's ulcers had previously been infected with pseudomonas/MRSA. The patient's visiting nurse was concerned about the erythema on the heel, which was more pronounced on the left. The patient's podiatrist recommended that he come to the emergency department. The patient denied any fever, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, bowel or bladder changes. He also reported no pain in his feet, although he has significant peripheral neuropathy secondary to diabetes. 

The patient was recently admitted to the podiatry service for similar cellulitis and non-healing bilateral heel ulcers. He had a bedside debridement and was discharged with a two-week course of cipro/clinda. He has had multiple admissions for non-healing infected ulcers, each time treated with IV antibiotics. 

In the ED, initial vital signs were: 96.8 100 119/43 16 100% RA. Examination of the left heel ulcer was concerning for infection in the setting of chronic poor healing. Labs were notable for a white blood cell count of 15.5, Hemoglobin/Hematocrit of 8.4/26.1, Cr. 1.5 (baseline 1.1-1.4), and Potassium of 5.7. An X-ray of his foot showed no evidence of osteomyelitis. He was treated with IV Vancomycin and Ceftriaxone while in the ED. Podiatry evaluated the patient in the ED and recommended admission to Medicine for IV antibiotics. 

The patient's past medical history includes IDDM (complicated by Charcot Foot, Lisfranc fracture, foot ulcers, retinopathy, and microalbuminuria), bilateral non-healing ulcer status post multiple debridements; past cultures growing MRSA and pseudomonas, Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), HTN, HLD, CKD, recurrent scrotal cellulitis, Coronary Artery Disease (CAD) status post Myocardial Infarction (MI), Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA), Bilateral Pulmonary Embolism (PE), Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD), Impotence, positive Purified Protein Derivative (PPD) test with clear Chest X-Ray, recovered alcohol abuse, memory loss, and red/green color blindness. 

The patient's family history includes diabetes in his mother and maternal aunt. 

On admission, the patient's vitals were 97.4, 125/54, 90, 18, 100% RA BG 351. The patient appeared thin and chronically ill. Examination of the left lower extremity revealed a large ulcerated area with pink central tissue with some areas of necrosis and surrounding erythema extending onto the lateral foot, with serous drainage onto the bandage. The right heel had a smaller ulceration that appeared more chronic with central eschar and minimal surrounding erythema. Dopplerable pulses were present bilaterally. The patient had 1+ pitting edema on the left greater than the right. 

Lab results on admission showed a white blood cell count of 15.5, red blood cell count of 3.38, Hemoglobin of 8.4, Hematocrit of 26.1, Mean Corpuscular Volume (MCV) of 77, Mean Corpuscular Hemoglobin (MCH) of 24.7, Glucose of 303, Urea Nitrogen of 27, Creatinine of 1.5, Sodium of 133, Potassium of 5.7, Chloride of 98, Bicarbonate of 22, and Anion Gap of 19. The patient's C-Reactive Protein (CRP) was elevated at 150.9. 

Microbiology results showed mixed bacterial flora, including Staphylococcus aureus, Beta Streptococcus Group B, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Sensitivities showed that the Staphylococcus aureus was resistant to Oxacillin and the Pseudomonas aeruginosa was sensitive to Cefepime, Ceftazidime, Ciprofloxacin, Gentamicin, Meropenem, Piperacillin/Tazobactam, and Tobramycin. 

The patient's medications on admission included Albuterol Inhaler, Aspirin, Atorvastatin, Collagenase Ointment, Gabapentin, Lisinopril, Magnesium Oxide, Tamsulosin, Acetaminophen, Clopidogrel, and Insulin Glargine with a sliding scale using Humalog Insulin. 

The patient was discharged with a primary diagnosis of lower extremity cellulitis and secondary diagnoses of chronic neuropathic heel ulcers, Type 2 diabetes mellitus, and Chronic Kidney Disease (stage 3). The patient's mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. He was able to get out of bed with assistance to a chair or wheelchair.

summary:

He has been under the care of the Podiatry and Vascular services and has undergone multiple debridements, most recently for bilateral heel debridements. He has also been receiving hyperbaric oxygen therapy. His other past medical history includes coronary artery disease (CAD) with a myocardial infarction, Charcot arthropathy, retinopathy, microalbuminuria, diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), aortic root dilation, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) with an FEV1 of 59%, hypertension (HTN), hyperlipidemia (HLD), pancreatitis, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and Lisfranc dislocation. His surgical history includes cataract surgery, open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) for Lisfranc dislocation and percutaneous tendon Achilles lengthening, balloon angioplasty of the right peroneal artery, left anterior tibial (AT) angioplasty, multiple bilateral foot surgeries for Charcot foot, possible omphalocele repair in early childhood, penile implant, left calcaneus bone biopsy, and right calcaneal debridement.

The patient's family history includes his mother who had a stroke and type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM), his father who had rectal cancer, and two sisters with obesity. His paternal grandfather had leukemia.

Upon admission, the patient had large bilateral ulcers on both plantar heels, with the right heel showing a central area of necrosis and the left heel showing necrotic, fibrotic base with bone exposed. There was mild hyperemia to the skin noted on the plantar distal skin. Upon discharge, the patient's ulcers showed minimal improvement, with the right heel showing a central area of necrosis and granulation tissue, and the left heel showing a granular base with small areas of fibrosis.

The patient's lab results showed several abnormal findings including a hematocrit of 20.7, white blood cell count of 9.2, red blood cell count of 2.42, hemoglobin of 6.4, mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration of 31.4, red cell distribution width of 19.2, glucose of 68, urea nitrogen of 36, creatinine of 2.0, and calcium of 8.1. His bilateral foot X-ray showed extensive prior surgery with a fixation plate extending along the first metatarsal, medial cuneiform, and navicular bone on the right foot, and collapse of the midfoot with min destruction and fragmentation of the medial cuneiform, medial displacement of the navicular, diffusely mottled appearance to the bone on the left foot.

During his hospital stay, the patient underwent a bilateral heel amputation. Post-operatively, he had a drop in his hematocrit and received 2 units of packed red blood cells (PRBC) with an appropriate response. He also had poor oral intake and a few episodes of hypoglycemia, which were managed by the Diabetes inpatient service. He was continued on doxycycline per the infectious disease recommendations and was placed on ciprofloxacin for a total of a 10-day course. His creatinine increased slightly following surgery and will require monitoring upon discharge.

The patient's medications upon admission included Tamsulosin, multivitamins, Glargine, Gabapentin, Clopidogrel, Doxycycline Hyclate, Aspirin, Atorvastatin, and Albuterol. He was discharged with the same medications, with the addition of Ciprofloxacin HCl. The patient was discharged to extended care with clear and coherent mental status, alert and interactive level of consciousness, and out of bed with assistance to chair or wheelchair activity status. His discharge diagnosis was bilateral foot infection.

summary:

He had been discharged from the hospital after a diagnosis of bilateral diabetic foot infections. After a brief time at rehab, he was discharged home where he had little support with his daily activities. He reported difficulty maintaining his meals and grocery shopping due to foot pain. He also reported feeling anorexic, losing weight, and experiencing diarrhea. He often skipped his insulin injections due to these symptoms. summary:
The patient, a male with a history of insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (IDDM), coronary artery disease (CAD), chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and peripheral vascular disease (PVD), presented with emesis. He was found to be in diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), with suspected coffee ground emesis, troponin leak, and hyperkalemia. The patient reported not taking his insulin or other medications at home due to feeling sick and weak. He also reported episodes of black vomit and dehydration. He denied experiencing chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, diarrhea, dysuria, fever, chills, cough, sore throat, congestion, and rhinorrhea. He did, however, report increased urinary frequency, which he attributed to his water pill. 

The patient had recently been treated for DKA at another facility, where his course was complicated by anemia requiring 7 units of packed red blood cells (pRBCs), and elevated prostate-specific antigen (PSA)/mass on bladder of unknown etiology. In the emergency department, he had one episode of guaiac-positive coffee ground emesis and reportedly yellow stool. Notable labs included a venous blood gas (VBG) of 7.___, blood sugar of 870, bicarbonate of 6, anion gap of 34, potassium of 7.2, creatinine of 5.4 (baseline 4.2), hemoglobin of 9.5 (discharged hemoglobin 9.9), and white blood cell count of 10.4 with 88.6% neutrophils. 

A chest X-ray revealed a hazy right lower lung airspace opacity, which could represent right lower lobe aspiration pneumonitis or pneumonia, or a small layering pleural effusion. An EKG showed ST depression in the inferior/lateral leads, with a troponin level of 0.2 and negative CKMB. 

The patient underwent a diagnostic thoracentesis. He was started on an insulin drip, given 3L of normal saline, ciprofloxacin/clindamycin x 1, and pantoprazole 80mg IV x1 in the ED.

Upon admission, the patient's physical exam revealed decreased breath sounds in the lung bases, tachycardia, decreased bowel sounds, pain with palpation of the epigastric area, erythematous round marks on the extremities, Charcot deformity of both feet, ulcers with skin sloughing, erythema, and some pus on the right side. The patient's troponin level was elevated at 0.20, and his lactate level was also elevated at 2.4. Pleural fluid analysis showed no polymorphonuclear leukocytes or microorganisms, and cultures showed no growth. 

The patient's discharge diagnoses included diabetic ketoacidosis, ___ tear, acute kidney injury, and transudative pleural effusion. His chronic conditions include chronic kidney disease, insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. 

Upon discharge, the patient was alert and interactive, and able to get out of bed with assistance to a chair or wheelchair. His discharge medications included acetaminophen, albuterol, amlodipine, aspirin, atorvastatin, clopidogrel, Dakins, furosemide, sodium bicarbonate, tamsulosin, tiotropium bromide, pantoprazole, gabapentin, multivitamins, and glargine. He was discharged to an extended care facility.

summary:
The patient, a male with a history of Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus complicated by Charcot arthropathy, retinopathy, nephropathy, Coronary Artery Disease post Myocardial Infarction, Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), peripheral vascular disease, and Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) Stage V, was admitted due to weakness, nausea, and hypotension. He reported a productive cough, weakness, and hypotension, which led to his transportation to the emergency department (ED). Initial vitals in the ED showed hypotension and labs revealed elevated BUN, Creatinine, Glucose, and Lactate levels. Urinalysis showed RBC 10, WBC 3, few bacteria, large protein, trace glucose, and negative ketones. Blood cultures were sent. 

Imaging revealed mild pulmonary edema with small to moderate size bilateral pleural effusions and bibasilar atelectasis. The patient was treated with 1L IVF and started on Vanc 1000 mg and Cefepime 2g IV. He reported shortness of breath, chest pain with inspiration, decreased oral intake, and diarrhea with dark stools. He denied abdominal pain, vomiting, constipation, and urinary symptoms. He reported regular wound care of his foot ulcers and recent debridement. 

The patient's past medical history includes CAD with an MI, Insulin Dependent DM with foot ulcers, Charcot arthropathy, retinopathy, microalbuminuria, DKA, aortic root dilation, COPD, HTN, HLD, pancreatitis, GERD, and Lisfranc dislocation. His surgical history includes cataract surgery, ORIF for Lisfranc dislocation and percutaneous tendon Achilles lengthening, balloon angioplasty of right peroneal artery, left AT angioplasty, multiple bilateral foot surgeries for Charcot foot, possible omphalocele repair in early childhood, penile implant, left calcaneus bone biopsy, and right calcaneal debridement. 

Family history includes his mother who had a stroke and Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus, his father who had rectal cancer, and two sisters with obesity. His paternal grandparents are deceased, with his grandfather having had leukemia. 

Physical examination on admission showed the patient to be alert, short of breath with speaking in full sentences, with a few crackles at lung bases, a crescendo-decrescendo murmur at LUSB radiating to carotids, and warm extremities with chronic ulcers and no erythema. Neurological examination was grossly normal. 

Lab results on admission showed abnormal RBC, Hgb, Hct, MCHC, RDWSD, Glucose, UreaN, Creat, and Lactate levels. Imaging showed mild pulmonary edema with bilateral pleural effusions and bibasilar atelectasis. The patient's foot showed increased sclerosis involving the calcaneus, severe changes of Charcot arthropathy in the tarsal bones, and possible chronic infectious process. The lung scan showed low likelihood of pulmonary embolism and ventilation imaging compatible with COPD. No evidence of deep venous thrombosis in the right or left lower extremity veins was found. The echocardiogram showed normal global and regional biventricular systolic function, indeterminate pulmonary pressures, and mildly dilated left atrium. 

The patient's medications upon admission included acetaminophen, albuterol inhaler, aspirin, atorvastatin, clopidogrel, furosemide, sodium bicarbonate, tamsulosin, pantoprazole, gabapentin, multivitamins, glargine, ferrous sulfate, vitamin D, epoetin, amlodipine, trazodone, and oxycodone. 

During his hospital stay, the patient's dyspnea was thought to be due to pulmonary edema, and he was treated with IV lasix. His hypotension improved with IV fluids and he remained hemodynamically stable throughout the admission. His blood cultures were negative and he received empiric vanc/cefepime treatment. His diabetes was managed with adjustments to his insulin regimen. His discharge diagnoses included hypotension and bilateral pleural effusions.

summary:
The male patient was admitted due to respiratory failure. He has a history of insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus (IDDM), chronic kidney disease V (CKDV) with a baseline creatinine of 5, coronary artery disease (CAD) post myocardial infarction (MI), and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). He was transferred from an outside hospital after being found hypoxic in his rehab. He was intubated and placed on empiric vancomycin and Zosyn. His creatinine increased to 5.9 on admission from a baseline of around 5. He had a matured fistula on his right side but had not yet received hemodialysis. His acute kidney injury was thought to be secondary to hypoperfusion in the setting of infection. 

The patient underwent initiation of hemodialysis and bronchoscopy and bronchoalveolar lavage. He was given Fentanyl and midazolam drip for sedation, and 1L NS in ED here, in total several liters. He received vancomycin and zosyn at the outside hospital. His labs were notable for BNP 3084, Cr 5.6. UA with WBC 55, positive leuks, negative nitrites. ABG was also conducted. His CXR showed bilateral infiltrates concerning for ARDS vs pulmonary edema. Superimposed pneumonia cannot be excluded.

The patient has a history of peripheral arterial disease post angioplasties, multiple prior PE/DVT, provoked, insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus with foot ulcers, Charcot arthropathy, retinopathy, microalbuminuria, DKA, aortic root dilation, COPD with an FEV1 of 59%, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, pancreatitis, GERD, and Lisfranc dislocation. He has undergone several surgical procedures including cataract surgery, ORIF for Lisfranc dislocation and percutaneous tendon Achilles lengthening, balloon angioplasty of right peroneal artery, left AT angioplasty, multiple bilateral foot surgeries for Charcot foot, possible omphalocele repair in early childhood, penile implant, left calcaneus bone biopsy, and right calcaneal debridement.

The patient's family history includes his mother who died from a stroke and had type 2 diabetes, his father who died from rectal cancer, and two sisters with obesity. His paternal grandfather had leukemia and his paternal grandmother is deceased.

Upon admission, the patient was intubated and sedated. His physical exam revealed diffusely decreased breath sounds, soft and non-distended abdomen, no edema, and multiple skin ulcers. His admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (2.93), Hgb (8.2), Hct (26.6), MCHC (30.8), RDWSD (51.1), Glucose (290), UreaN (65), Creat (5.6), cTropnT (0.30), proBNP (3084), Albumin (2.4), Phos (5.6), Mg (1.5), pO2 (51), pCO2 (46), and pH (7.34). His microbiology results were negative for C. difficile DNA amplification assay, urine culture, blood cultures, and respiratory viral screen & culture. His diagnostics showed diffuse, bilateral peribronchial consolidative nodular opacities on CT chest, saccular structure within the left popliteal fossa on left lower leg U/S, and regular supraventricular tachycardia on EKG. His TTE showed normal left ventricular wall thickness, cavity size, and regional/global systolic function (LVEF >55%).

The patient's hospital course was complicated by hypoxemic respiratory failure secondary to aspiration pneumonia, acute on chronic kidney injury, supraventricular tachycardia, positive PPD, and nutrition deficits on PO intake. He also had insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, elevated troponin, anemia, and hypertension. He eventually refused hemodialysis despite understanding that he will die without it. He was transitioned to comfort measures only and passed away.

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted to the hospital due to chest pain and shortness of breath. She has a history of coronary artery disease (CAD) and has previously undergone percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) to the right coronary artery (RCA) complicated by in-stent restenosis (ISR). She has had two instances of ISR, the most recent of which was treated with a drug-eluting stent (DES). The patient reported an episode of acute, deep, pressure-like substernal chest pain that woke her from sleep. Upon presentation to the emergency department, her chest pain had resolved. Her troponin levels were negative twice and her EKG showed no changes from prior. 

The patient has been on propranolol for angina and migraines, but her heart rate of 55-60 made it difficult to up titrate. She had previously tried nitrates for angina but could not tolerate them due to headaches. She was started on amlodipine for angina. The patient also takes carbamazepine, which has effectively controlled her seizures over the years. 

Her lab results showed abnormal levels of red blood cell count (3.47), hemoglobin (9.8), hematocrit (30.9), and mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (31.7). Her alkaline phosphatase level was also elevated (110). 

Upon admission, the patient was on several medications including amlodipine, carbamazepine, clonazepam, diclofenac sodium, vitamin D, ezetimibe, fluticasone propionate, furosemide, nitroglycerin, propranolol, rosuvastatin calcium, ticagrelor, aspirin, cetirizine, senna, polyethylene glycol, and docusate sodium. 

The patient was discharged with a diagnosis of stable angina. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory. She was instructed to continue all of her pre-admission medications and to follow up with her cardiologist if her chest pain worsens.

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of obesity, epilepsy, and hyperlipidemia, was admitted due to chest pain. She reported experiencing exertional chest pressure for approximately six months, which had worsened in frequency and severity over the past several weeks. The symptoms were associated with severe chest pressure, left arm pain, jaw pain, radiation to the back, diaphoresis, dyspnea, and intermittent nausea. The symptoms began occurring at rest and were almost continuous, worsening with anxiety or physical exertion. She had been brought to the hospital by ambulance a week prior due to developing severe pain while walking. At that time, EKG, lab work, CTA, and CXR were reassuring and cardiac enzymes were negative. She underwent a pMIBI with normal LV contractile function and equivocal evidence of mild lateral wall ischemia. She was discharged with a recommendation to follow up with a cardiologist as an outpatient to receive a catheterization. 

Upon her readmission, her initial vitals were BP: 133/80; HR: 60; T: 97.8; RA: 97%. EKG showed T wave inversions in AvR and V1, unchanged from prior. Labs were notable for negative trop x2, Chem-7; CBC was unremarkable. She was given ASA 325, Nitro 0.4, and Tylenol. 

Her past medical history includes dyslipidemia, smoking, migraines, obesity, arthritis, and spondylosis. Her family history includes a brother and father with DVTs, a father with coronary artery disease and a possible myocardial infarction later in life, and a mother who had a stroke. 

During her hospital stay, she underwent a coronary angiography with PCI, which revealed single vessel coronary artery disease and successful PCI with a drug-eluting stent of the RCA coronary artery. Her discharge labs showed a decrease in RBC count (3.81). 

Her medications upon admission were CarBAMazepine 200 mg PO TID, Propranolol 20 mg PO TID, and ClonazePAM 0.5 mg PO TID. 

The patient was diagnosed with unstable angina, chest pain, and coronary artery disease. She was discharged with instructions to take all prescribed medications, particularly her new aspirin/Plavix to prevent clots from forming in her stent, and to attend all follow-up appointments.

summary:
The patient, a female with a past medical history of obesity, hyperlipidemia, and coronary artery disease (CAD) post drug-eluting stent (DES) to the right coronary artery (RCA), presented with recurrent chest pain. She reported experiencing crushing, substernal chest pain that radiated across her chest and to her right ear, associated with exertion, diaphoresis, dizziness, nausea, and extreme fatigue. The pain was similar to what she experienced prior to her first stent placement. 

Her past medical history also includes dyslipidemia, smoking, migraines, obesity, arthritis, and spondylosis. She has no history of coronary artery bypass graft (CABG), percutaneous coronary interventions, or pacing/implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD). Her family history includes her brother and father having deep vein thrombosis (DVTs), her father having CAD with a possible myocardial infarction later in life and a clotting factor problem, and her mother having a stroke.

Upon physical examination, she was found to have a new bruise on her right hand where an IV was placed and her right arm was more swollen than her left. Her veins were more visible in her left arm than her right. 

Her lab results showed abnormal red blood cell count (3.62*) and red cell distribution width standard deviation (47.5*). Her cardiac troponin T levels were also elevated (0.13*, 0.14*, 0.06*, 0.03*), and her proBNP was 349*. 

She underwent a percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) on her RCA due to severe in-stent restenosis. The procedure was successful with no residual stenosis, TIMI-3 flow in the distal vessel, and no evidence of residual dissection, embolization, or thrombus. A transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) showed normal biventricular cavity sizes and regional/global biventricular systolic function, no valvular pathology or pathologic flow, normal estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure, and mild aortic regurgitation with mildly thickened leaflets.

Her medications upon admission included carbamazepine, clonazepam, propranolol, aspirin, clopidogrel, nitroglycerin, and rosuvastatin. During her hospital stay, she was switched from clopidogrel to ticagrelor and her rosuvastatin dosage was increased. 

She was diagnosed with in-stent re-stenosis and non-ST elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI). Her mental status upon discharge was clear and coherent, and she was alert and interactive. She was advised to take her medications as prescribed, ice and elevate her arm to help with the mild swelling and pain, call her primary care physician if she experiences chest pain again, and follow a heart-healthy diet.

summary:
The patient, a female with a past medical history of obesity, hyperlipidemia, coronary artery disease (CAD), migraines, arthritis, and spondylosis, was admitted due to recurrent chest pain. She had previously undergone a drug-eluting stent (DES) placement in the right coronary artery (RCA) due to a 70% stenosis. However, she was readmitted with worsening chest pain and was found to have a 90-95% restenosis of the RCA stent. This was treated with balloon angioplasty followed by a cutting balloon and intravascular ultrasound (IVUS), which showed a well-expanded stent. Her medication was switched from Plavix to ticagrelor and she was discharged without chest pain. 

On the day before her current admission, she experienced substernal heaviness, chest pain radiating to her bilateral clavicles and neck, shortness of breath, dizziness, diaphoresis, paleness, and nausea. These symptoms were similar to those that led to her previous hospitalization. 

Her family history includes a brother and father with deep vein thrombosis (DVTs), a father with CAD and a possible myocardial infarction later in life, and a mother who had a stroke. 

Upon admission, her physical exam was largely normal, with the exception of swelling at the right radial access site. Her admission labs showed abnormal red blood cell (RBC) count of 3.62 and red cell distribution width standard deviation (RDWSD) of 47.5. Her cardiac troponin T (cTropnT) was also elevated at 0.07. A coronary angiogram showed no angiographically apparent coronary artery disease and a widely patent RCA stent. 

Her discharge labs showed a decreased RBC count of 3.42, hemoglobin (Hgb) of 10.4, hematocrit (Hct) of 32.1, and an elevated phosphate level of 4.7. 

During her hospital stay, she was diagnosed with non-ST elevated myocardial infarction and her symptoms were believed to be attributable to coronary spasm or microvascular showering from the prior catheterization. Her pain was managed with Tylenol and a lidocaine patch. She was medically optimized with the addition of ezetimibe to her medication regimen, which also included aspirin, carbamazepine, clonazepam, nitroglycerin, propranolol, ticagrelor, and rosuvastatin. 

Upon discharge, she was alert, interactive, and able to ambulate independently. She was instructed to continue taking her medications as prescribed and to seek medical attention if she experienced new or concerning symptoms such as chest pain, shortness of breath, or severe abdominal pain.

summary:
The patient, a female with a past medical history of obesity, hyperlipidemia, coronary artery disease (CAD), migraine, arthritis, and spondylosis, was admitted due to chest pain. She had previously undergone a drug-eluting stent (DES) placement in the right coronary artery (RCA) and a balloon angioplasty for stent restenosis of the RCA DES. Upon admission, she presented with continuous, non-exertional, midline, retrosternal chest pain, and left groin pain since her most recent catheterization. Initial findings included an EKG that showed interval progression in T-wave inversions in inferior leads and a troponin peak of 0.14 before downtrending. She underwent a coronary angiogram which showed a widely patent DES with no other significant coronary artery disease. Her symptoms were attributed to coronary spasm or microvascular showering from prior catheterization. She also experienced neuropathy radiating down her leg post-catheterization, likely related to nerve injury.

The patient's physical exam was notable for small volume hemoptysis, tenderness to touch in the lower abdomen, and a left groin site without bruit, nonpulsatile, no edema/erythema, but very tender to touch. Lab results showed a negative troponin x 2 and a D-dimer of 1660. Studies showed sinus bradycardia T-wave inversion in III and aVF on EKG. A CT scan showed no evidence of an aortic dissection or pulmonary embolism, but there was apparent thickening/soft tissue involving the proximal aorta and pulmonary arteries, which measured slightly hyperdense. The differential for this includes trace hemopericardium, versus inflammation of the aortic arch and pulmonary arteries. Bilateral lower lobe peribronchial nodularity was nonspecific but could reflect aspiration or atypical infection. There was hemorrhage surrounding the left external iliac artery, likely postprocedural, and a probable small right pseudoaneurysm which is 5 mm in length with a neck measuring 3 mm.

The patient was given ASA, ticagrelor, zetia, carbamazepine, clonazepam, and propranolol. She reported progressive joint pains, worse in the past few years, and joint stiffness upon waking for 30 minutes. She also reported not being able to bear weight on her left leg, such as after walking down the block to the bank or to grocery shop. Her level of physical activity has been significantly hampered, leading her to gain weight. She also reported hair loss and a facial rash, with redness especially around her nose. She has no family history of lupus or rheumatoid arthritis, but several family members have multiple sclerosis on both her father and mother's side. She also reported epigastric pain, which came on in the ED after having apple juice. It is also exacerbated when she eats nothing. She denies it is reflux. On further review of systems, she denies fevers or chills, any prior history of stroke, TIA, deep venous thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, bleeding at the time of surgery, myalgias, joint pains, cough, hemoptysis, black stools or red stools. She denies exertional buttock or calf pain. All of the other review of systems were negative.

Her family history includes a brother and father with deep vein thrombosis (DVTs), a father with CAD with a possible myocardial infarction later in life and a clotting factor problem, and a mother who had a stroke. Her admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.18), Hgb (9.8), Hct (30.0), and RDWSD (46.5). Her discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.29), Hgb (10.2), Hct (31.4), and RDWSD (47.2). Her CRP was also elevated at 76.3.

summary:
The patient is a female with a history of coronary artery disease (CAD) post percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) to the right coronary artery (RCA), epilepsy, migraines, dyslipidemia, arthritis, and spondylosis. She has had multiple recent admissions due to recurrent chest pain. She presented with intermittent low-grade chest pain that developed into severe chest pressure radiating to her right shoulder, right ear, and left shoulder blade. In the emergency department, her ECG showed sinus bradycardia with Q wave in III, late R wave progression, and T wave inversion in III and aVF. Troponins were negative twice and other labs were unremarkable. 

Upon admission, her medications included aspirin, carbamazepine, clonazepam, ezetimibe, nitroglycerin, propranolol, rosuvastatin calcium, and ticagrelor. Her family history includes her father having a myocardial infarction in his later years, while her siblings and children are all healthy. 

Her physical exam upon admission and discharge were largely unremarkable, with no significant findings. However, her admission labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.27), Hgb (9.9), Hct (31.4), MCHC (31.5), RDWSD (46.5), and Phos (4.7), and CRP (8.7). Her discharge labs showed abnormal values for RBC (3.54), Hgb (10.7), Hct (33.7), MCHC (31.8), and RDWSD (47.2).

During her hospital stay, she underwent a stress echo and a chest MRA. The stress echo showed average functional exercise capacity with atypical angina symptoms and non-specific ECG changes without 2D echocardiographic evidence of inducible ischemia. The chest MRA showed mild but abnormal wall thickening of the ascending aorta and arch. 

Her chest pain was evaluated for noncardiac/noncoronary causes, and the differential diagnosis included coronary vasospasm vs. cardiac syndrome X. She was started on amlodipine for empiric therapy. Rheumatology was consulted due to concerns about a vasculitic process, but they agreed that it was unlikely to be causing her pain. She was discharged with instructions to continue her medications and to call the heartline or return to the ED if her pain worsened or occurred with exertion.

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of coronary artery disease (CAD) post percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) to the right coronary artery (RCA), epilepsy, migraine, arthritis, and spondylosis, was admitted due to chest pain. She has had multiple hospitalizations for chest pain and a recent unremarkable stress test. The patient reported worsening chest pain, similar to previous episodes, with radiation to the back and down the arms, as well as a headache. The pain was not relieved with nitroglycerin and was associated with shortness of breath. 

During her stay, she underwent coronary angiography which revealed a 90% restenosis of the proximal RCA stent. A 3.5x18mm Onyx drug-eluting stent (DES) was placed. The patient's left ventricular end-diastolic pressure (LVEDP) was found to be elevated and she was started on low dose furosemide. 

The patient's family history includes her father having a myocardial infarction (MI) in his later years. Her siblings and children are all healthy. 

Upon admission, the patient was on aspirin, carbamazepine, clonazepam, ezetimibe, nitroglycerin, propranolol, rosuvastatin calcium, ticagrelor, and amlodipine. During her stay, furosemide was added to her medication regimen. 

The patient's physical exam upon admission and discharge was largely unremarkable. Her lab results upon admission and discharge were not provided in the note. A chest X-ray showed no acute intrathoracic process. 

The patient has had multiple recent admissions for chest pain. During her previous hospitalizations, she had a stress test that showed average functional exercise capacity with atypical angina symptoms and ECG changes without 2D echocardiographic evidence of inducible ischemia. A magnetic resonance angiogram (MRA) confirmed thickening and showed mild hyperintensity of the root of the aorta. 

The patient was discharged home with instructions to continue her medications and to follow up with her primary care provider. The discharge diagnosis was chest pain and restenosis.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of coronary artery disease (CAD) post percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) to the right coronary artery (RCA), presented with worsening cough, central chest discomfort, and mild shortness of breath three days after PCI. She described her chest discomfort as different from her prior chest pain associated with in-stent restenosis (ISR), which was described as substernal crushing pain. The patient's cough was worsening, and she began to feel chest tightness that was limiting her ability to breathe. She has been unable to lie flat due to chest discomfort for a long time. She has a history of epilepsy, migraine, arthritis, and spondylosis. Her family history includes her father having a myocardial infarction in his later years.

Upon admission, the patient was given Aspirin 243mg. Initial vital signs were T 98.1, HR 71, BP 117/68, RR 17, 98% RA. The EKG showed new T-wave inversions in V3 and V4. Lab results showed a hemoglobin level of 10.8, Trop <0.01, and proBNP 39. The chest X-ray did not show any acute processes. 

The patient's past medical history includes dyslipidemia and CAD post PCI to RCA with in-stent restenosis post plain old balloon angioplasty (POBA). The patient's medications upon admission included amlodipine, aspirin, carbamazepine, clonazepam, ezetimibe, nitroglycerin, propranolol, rosuvastatin, ticagrelor, and furosemide.

Abnormal lab results upon admission included RBC-3.60*, Hgb-10.8*, Hct-33.8*, and Phos-5.2*. Discharge labs showed RBC-3.40*, Hgb-10.3*, Hct-32.6*, and MCHC-31.6*, and Phos-5.1*. 

The patient underwent an exercise stress test which showed no significant ST changes noted during exercise or recovery. The patient's coronary status showed 90% ISR to RCA and an ejection fraction of 60% during stress echo. 

The patient's chest pain resolved on discharge. She was continued on her home medications including aspirin, ticagrelor, propranolol, amlodipine, rosuvastatin, ezetimibe, and furosemide. The patient also complained of her abdominal pulse being more noticeable recently, which was very apparent on physical exam. 

The patient's discharge diagnoses included chest pressure, epilepsy, migraine, and coronary artery disease. The patient was discharged home in a clear and coherent mental state, alert and interactive, and ambulatory - independent.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female, was admitted due to right flank pain. She has a history of Ulcerative Colitis, Hypothyroidism, Hypertension, Hyperlipidemia, Anxiety, and Nephrolithiasis. She reported having passed a brown stone in her urine and was experiencing severe right groin pain. She also reported shortness of breath, 'arthritic type chest pain', chills, and multiple episodes of non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting. An EKG showed possible new ST changes and she was given Aspirin 325mg. Her cardiac enzymes were negative. Upon arrival to the floor, she reported feeling better, but was developing a migraine and had right flank pain. She also reported having chills overnight and hematuria. She suspected a colitis flare but denied bloody stools. She reported increased loose yellow stools and abdominal cramping. She has chronic arthritic joint pain, particularly in her right hip/knee.

A ureteroscopy and stent placement in the right ureter were performed. An abdominal CT showed a right proximal ureteral stone with mild right hydro and mild perinephric stranding. She was treated with IVF, Cipro, Toradol, and Zofran. 

Her lab results showed several abnormal findings. Her RBC count was 3.40*, Hgb was 10.0*, Hct was 29.7*, WBC was 13.8*#, Neuts were 85.6* and 93.1*, Lymphs were 8.7* and 5.3*, Monos were 4.8 and 0.2*, Glucose was 135*, Calcium was 7.7*, and Phos was 2.0*. Her urine and blood cultures showed the presence of E. coli. 

A CT of her abdomen/pelvis showed a 4-mm right proximal ureteral calculus, with mild right hydronephrosis and perinephric fat stranding. A CTA of her chest showed no pulmonary embolism or acute aortic pathology, but did show small-to-moderate bilateral pleural effusions, with adjacent focal atelectasis, and small focal airspace consolidation in the right lower lobe. 

She was diagnosed with Acute Pyelonephritis and Proximal Ureterolithiasis on the right side, complicated by E. coli Bacteremia. She was treated with Ceftriaxone for a total 14 day course and underwent ureteroscopy and stent placement in the right ureter. She was ruled out for MI and Acute diastolic CHF. 

Her medications upon admission included Aspirin 325mg daily, Levothyroxine 100mcg daily, Omeprazole 20mg daily, Propanolol 20mg BID, Sulindac 200mg BID, and Tramadol 50-100mg q6hr prn. 

Her family history includes a genetic disorder and CAD. She was discharged to home with service. Her discharge diagnoses included Nephrolithiasis, Pyelonephritis, and Hematuria.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of Schizoaffective disorder, was brought to the ED after being found walking topless outside her apartment. She reported feeling 'on top of the world' for a couple of days, having lots of energy, and not sleeping the night before admission. Initial vital signs were 98.5, 144/72, 116, 16, 98% on RA. She was noted to be laughing inappropriately. Serum and urine tox were negative. CT Head was unremarkable. She was evaluated by Psychiatry who felt her presentation was typical of a maniform psychosis. She had a recent admission for Pyleonephritis due to Ureterolithiasis complicated by E coli bacteremia after Right uretheral stenting. 

Her past medical history includes benign hypertension, hyperlipidemia, ulcerative colitis, hypothyroidism, hematuria, nephrolithiasis, GERD, and osteoarthritis. She has a family history of CAD. 

Upon arrival to the wards, she reported feeling 'heavy,' but would not elaborate further. She denied pain or dyspnea, fever, chills, nightsweats, flank pain. She did report recent diarrhea. No suicidal or homicidal ideation was noted. 

Abnormal lab results include WBC-11.7*, RBC-3.46*, Hgb-10.4*, Hct-30.8*, Neuts-76.5*, Glucose-103*, ALT-44*, VitB12-1011*, TSH-4.7*, Hgb-10.6*, RBC->1000*, WBC-382*, and CastHy-14*. 

The patient was diagnosed with acute psychosis, complicated urinary tract infection, right ureteral stenting, mild transaminitis, anemia, hypothyroidism, and GERD. 

Upon admission, the patient was on aspirin 81 mg Cap, Propranolol 20 mg Tab, Clinoril 200 mg Tab, Omeprazole 20 mg Cap, Ultram 50 mg Tab, Synthroid ___ mcg Tab, and Lidocaine 5 % (700 mg/patch). 

The patient was discharged with the diagnosis of resolved psychosis NOS, unspecified mood disorder, hematuria, pyelonephritis, ureterolithiasis, E coli bacteremia, hypothyroidism, anemia, and gastroesophageal reflux disease.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of ulcerative colitis (UC), diverticulitis, hematuria, hyperlipidemia (HL), hypertension (HTN), hypothyroidism, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), osteoarthritis, and nephrolithiasis, presented with three weeks of diarrhea and mucoid/bloody stool, consistent with prior UC flares. She also reported diffuse abdominal pain, chills, difficulty eating solid foods due to nausea and pain, dizziness, dyspnea on exertion, occasional migraines, and a 16 lb weight loss. She denied fevers and melena. She had a recent episode of nephrolithiasis post lithotripsy and stent placement, and an emergency department visit when she was diagnosed with diverticulitis. 

Upon admission, her medications included aspirin, diphenoxylate-atropine, levothyroxine, omeprazole, oxycodone, propranolol, sulindac, and tramadol. She was given Ciprofloxacin, Flagyl, Morphine Sulfate, and Ondansetron in the ER. 

Physical examination revealed no apparent distress, regular heart rate and rhythm, clear lungs, soft and non-tender abdomen, and alert and oriented neurological status. Abnormal lab results included a lactate level of 3.3 and a platelet count of 473. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed colon wall thickening consistent with colitis, improved mild right hydronephrosis and more homogenous nephrogram, unchanged mild CBD and intrahepatic biliary dilatation, and incomplete contrast opacification of the origin of the celiac axis. 

During her hospital stay, she was initially suspected to have a UC flare and was put on IV antibiotics. However, stool samples tested positive for clostridium difficile toxin, leading to a change in treatment to IV and then oral Flagyl. She responded well to this treatment and was discharged home once she became asymptomatic. 

The patient has a family history of coronary artery disease. She is allergic to Indocin, Zyprexa, and Neoprene Elbow Support.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a middle-aged female, was admitted due to ongoing and worsening diarrhea. She has a history of ulcerative colitis, benign hypertension, hyperlipidemia, hypothyroidism, hematuria, nephrolithiasis, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), and osteoarthritis. She has had multiple emergency department visits and hospital admissions over the past several months, including a recent admission for C. diff colitis, which was treated inpatient with IV flagyl for 5 days and discharged on a 14-day oral flagyl course. 

Upon admission, the patient reported continued/worsening diffuse lower abdominal pain and diarrhea, which improved but never fully resolved while on flagyl, and then worsened once the flagyl course was completed. She also reported feeling worse for the past few days, with symptoms including nausea, vomiting, fatigue/weakness, abdominal pain especially in the left lower quadrant, and 9 bowel movements the previous day. Stools were described as brownish fluid/mucoid gel with occasional blood. 

Physical examination revealed tachycardia and tenderness in bilateral lower quadrants without rebound or guarding. Lab results showed an elevated white blood cell count of 15.3, elevated neutrophils at 67.0, lymphocytes at 16.2, eosinophils at 12.1, and platelet count at 541. The patient's glucose level was also elevated at 105, and her sedimentation rate was high at 60. 

Stool cultures were negative for Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Cyclospora, Microsporidium, E.Coli 0157:H7, and Yersinia. However, the patient tested positive for C. difficile toxin. 

The patient's medications upon admission included aspirin, hydrocortisone acetate, levothyroxine, omeprazole, propranolol, sulindac, tramadol, and vitamin D3. 

The patient's family history includes a syndrome and coronary artery disease. 

During her hospital stay, the patient's diarrhea was attributed to recurrent C. Diff. She was treated with IV flagyl and oral vancomycin, which led to a significant improvement in her symptoms. She was noted to have a mild thrombocytosis during her admission, which was likely reactive. This should be rechecked when she follows-up with her primary care provider. 

Upon discharge, the patient was diagnosed with C. diff colitis. Her mental status was clear and coherent, and she was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a female with a history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, ulcerative colitis, hypothyroidism, hematuria, nephrolithiasis, GERD, and osteoarthritis, was admitted to the hospital due to blood-tinged diarrhea and acute shortness of breath. She was found to be hypoxic and hypotensive upon arrival, and her heart rate had decreased significantly. She was intubated and started on dobutamine, and given 6 mg of epinephrine and 6 mg of atropine for her heart rate. She had a return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) after about 30 minutes of CPR. A bedside echo showed right ventricular (RV) strain. She was empirically given tPA and started on IV heparin, as well as vanc/cefepime. Her oxygenation and blood pressure improved transiently, but she became bradycardic shortly thereafter and lost pulses, leading to another PEA arrest code. She was transferred to the MICU for further care.

Upon physical examination, the patient was found to be intubated and sedated. Her pupils were fixed and dilated to 8cm, with no corneal reflex. Her lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally, and her heart was tachycardic. Her abdomen was mildly distended, with bowel sounds present and no rebound tenderness or guarding. Her extremities were cool, with difficult to appreciate peripheral pulses. She had no withdrawal to pain.

Lab results showed a glucose level of 343, AST of 41, AlkPhos of 118, cTropnT of 0.03, Calcium of 7.4, Phos of 8.3, and D-Dimer of 4990. Post-arrest labs showed WBC of 20.9, RBC of 2.91, Hgb of 8.8, Hct of 28.8, MCV of 99, MCHC of 30.4, PTT of 150, Glucose of 346, K of 6.3, Cl of 114, HCO3 of 16, ALT of 356, AST of 532, LD(LDH) of 976, Albumin of 1.8, Calcium of 7.6, Phos of 6.1, pCO2 of 87, pH of 6.83, calTCO2 of 16, Glucose of 323, and Lactate of 12.6.

Imaging showed an endotracheal tube terminating approximately 4.9 cm above the level of the carina, no focal consolidation, no large pleural effusion or pneumothorax, and a top-normal cardiac silhouette. An EKG showed sinus tachycardia, a new RBB with normal axis, and a widened QRS. A TEE showed right ventricular dilation, free wall hypokinesis, severe tricuspid regurgitation with non coapting tricuspid valve leaflets, and no pericardial effusion.

The patient's medications upon admission included Levothyroxine Sodium, Aspirin, Omeprazole, Sulindac, TraMADOL, Multivitamins, Propranolol, and Lidocaine. The patient passed away during her hospital stay. The cause of her PEA arrest is unknown, but it is presumed to be due to a pulmonary embolism.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted due to pain following an aborted laparoscopic hiatal hernia repair. He has a history of recurrent hiatal hernia and depression. His surgical history includes lap Nissen fundoplication and port site hernia repair twice. He presented to the emergency department with pleuritic left chest pain and right posterior shoulder/scapular pain. He had taken oxycodone, which did not improve his pain. He reported no nausea, vomiting, or difficulty breathing. 

Upon physical examination, his vitals were stable and he was alert and oriented. He had subcutaneous emphysema in the left lower chest. His abdomen was soft, non-tender, and non-distended with positive bowel sounds. His extremities were warm, well-perfused, with 2+ pulses. 

Lab results showed elevated WBC at 11.3, low Hgb at 13.3, and elevated glucose at 122. His ALT was also elevated at 53. A CT scan of the chest and abdomen showed no evidence of pulmonary embolism or aortic dissection. There was no oral contrast extravasation from the esophagus to suggest esophageal perforation, but an occult leak could not be excluded due to the degree of pneumomediastinum and pneumoperitoneum. There was left thoracic wall subcutaneous air likely related to trocar placement and a large hiatal hernia was noted. 

During his hospital stay, the patient was started on IV dilaudid for pain control, which was later transitioned to oral medication. He was also started on a regular diet which he tolerated well. His pain was controlled on oral medications, he was ambulating without assistance, and voiding without difficulty. 

Upon admission, the patient was on wellbutrin sr 450, propranolol 80, trazodone 100, and oxycodone 5 prn. His family history was non-contributory. He was discharged home with a diagnosis of pain control following an aborted laparoscopic hiatal hernia repair. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert, interactive, and independently ambulatory.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male, was admitted for a large recurrent hiatal hernia. He underwent an exploratory laparotomy, repair of paraesophageal hernia with mesh, and Nissen. The patient had previously undergone hernia repair two months prior, which was aborted after initial laparoscopy and lysis of adhesions. He was discharged home without any nausea, vomiting, or difficulty breathing. His past medical history includes depression and he has had a lap Ni___ fundoplication ___ and port site hernia repair x2. His family history is non-contributory.

Upon admission, the patient was on Wellbutrin SR 300mg in the morning, Wellbutrin SR 150mg ___, Propranolol 80', and Trazodone 100' PRN for insomnia. His physical exam showed him to be a well-appearing, slender male with faint heart sounds, good lung aeration, and no wheezing. His abdomen was mildly tender but non-distended, with no rebound/guarding. He was alert and interacting appropriately.

Post-operative labs showed an Hct of 37.4* and a glucose level of 168*. His calcium level was 8.6 and Mg was 1.5*. A chest X-ray showed no evident pneumothorax, normal cardiac size, and no pleural effusion. There was mild elevation of the left hemidiaphragm and bilateral neck subcutaneous emphysema. His last labs prior to discharge showed an Hct of 38.6* and a Phos of 1.5*#.

During his hospital stay, the patient had a thoracic epidural placed for pain control and received Dilaudid + Bupivicaine through the epidural with IV Tylenol. He was stable from a cardiovascular and pulmonary standpoint. Post-operatively, he was given IV fluids and had a nasogastric tube in place. His diet was advanced when appropriate and he passed flatus on POD4. He was also started on a bowel regimen to encourage bowel movement. His temperature was closely monitored for signs of infection and he received subcutaneous heparin during his stay.

Upon discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile with stable vital signs, tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and his pain was well controlled. His primary diagnosis upon discharge was Paraesophageal hernia, s/p re-revisional repair with placement of prosthetic material. His mental status was clear and coherent, and he was alert and interactive. His activity status was independent and ambulatory.
label: cases

summary:
The patient, a male with a longstanding history of major depression, was admitted to the psychiatry service following a self-reported suicide attempt. He had jumped in front of a car after experiencing multiple psychosocial stressors, including a fight with his husband and the risk of losing custody of his children. He reported a depressed mood, feelings of guilt, and two months of anhedonia, poor energy, and poor concentration. He denied current suicidal ideation or plan and expressed a desire to live for his children and repair his relationship with his husband. 

His past psychiatric history includes hospitalization for depression, two prior suicide attempts, and trials of various medications (Paxil, Prozac, Zoloft, Lexapro) with no significant benefit. His past medical history includes an enlarged aorta, transient leg paralysis due to a viral infection, hypertension, a clavicle fracture from a suicide attempt, two abdominal hernia repairs, surgical correction of esophagus, and a head concussion from a motor vehicle accident. He has no history of seizures. 

His social history includes potential alcohol abuse, with increased tolerance and his husband expressing concern over his drinking habits. He denies current drug use but reports past use of marijuana and cocaine. He is currently going through a divorce and has one adopted child and is also taking care of his husband's nephew. 

His family history includes depression in his mother and sister, schizophrenia in his paternal uncle, depression with multiple suicide attempts in his grandmother, and alcoholism in his paternal grandfather and aunts. 

Upon physical examination, he was found to be tall and thin, with full strength and sensory grossly intact. His cognition was intact, with fluent speech and language, and poor judgment and fair insight. His mood was described as 'depressed' and congruent, tearful at times when discussing his suicide attempt. 

Lab results showed a glucose level of 121. Urine tests were negative for various substances including benzodiazepine, barbiturates, opiates, cocaine, amphetamines, and methadone. 

During his hospital stay, he underwent evaluation for electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) as a treatment for his treatment-resistant depression. He experienced some dizziness and 'zaps' running from his back to his head along his spine, which were considered to be related to Effexor withdrawal. He received 11 ECT treatments as an inpatient and resolved to continue a course of ECT as an outpatient. He was continued on his home dose of Wellbutrin and started on Cymbalta at 20mg Daily on the day of discharge. 

He also developed right arm thrombophlebitis, which was treated with Ibuprofen and warm compresses. He was maintained on his home dose of Propranolol for his enlarged aorta and his blood pressure normalized and remained stable throughout the remainder of the admission without further intervention. 

Upon discharge, the patient was future-oriented, committed to continuing with outpatient treatment, and reported significant improvement in his mood and absence of thoughts of self-harm. His discharge medications included Fish Oil, Multivitamins, Zinc Sulfate, Propranolol, Lorazepam, Wellbutrin, Duloxetine, Ibuprofen, and Omeprazole. His discharge diagnosis was major depressive disorder and alcohol abuse.
label: cases
